> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Preservation and Improvement of Industrial Microorganisms for Overproduction of Primary and Secondary Metabolites

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Industrial Microorganisms — The Cellular Workhorses of Bioprocess Engineering

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):**
> An **industrial microorganism** is a microbial strain (bacteria, yeast, mold, or actinomycete) that has been isolated, purified, characterized, and optimized through systematic improvement programs to economically produce a target metabolite (e.g., antibiotics, enzymes, amino acids, organic acids, vitamins, biofuels) at commercially viable titers, yields, and productivities in a controlled fermenter environment.

In simpler words — an industrial microorganism is a **biological factory** that has been trained (through preservation and improvement techniques) to churn out a specific product at maximum efficiency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Conceptual Analogy — "The Champion Racehorse"**
> Imagine a racehorse breeder. A wild horse (wild-type microbe) can run, but not fast. The breeder's job is two-fold:
> 1. **Preservation** → Keep the horse's best genetic traits alive for future breeding (don't let the lineage die out or mutate randomly).
> 2. **Improvement** → Selectively breed the fastest horse over generations to produce an even faster offspring.
>
> Similarly, in bioprocess engineering, we preserve the genetic identity of high-yielding strains and continuously improve them to obtain "super-strains" that overproduce target metabolites.

---

## 1.2 Primary vs. Secondary Metabolites — The Two Industrial Product Classes

| Feature | Primary Metabolites | Secondary Metabolites |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Compounds produced during the **exponential (trophophase)** growth phase; essential for cell growth | Compounds produced during the **stationary (idiophase)** phase; not essential for growth |
| **Function in Nature** | Building blocks of cellular machinery | Ecological role (defense, signaling, competition) |
| **Production Phase** | Trophophase (growth-associated) | Idiophase (non-growth associated) |
| **Examples** | **Amino acids** (glutamate, lysine), **organic acids** (citric, lactic), **ethanol**, **vitamins** (B12), **nucleotides** | **Antibiotics** (penicillin, streptomycin), **pigments**, **toxins**, **hormones** (gibberellins) |
| **Yield Improvement** | Tied to growth rate — improve by relieving feedback inhibition | Decoupled from growth — improve by enhancing precursor pools |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Implication:**
> The overproduction strategy differs fundamentally for the two classes. **Primary metabolites** are improved by **removing regulatory bottlenecks** (feedback inhibition, catabolite repression). **Secondary metabolites** are improved by **enhancing precursor supply, regulatory gene manipulation, and morphologically engineering the idiophase environment.**

---

## 1.3 The Two Pillars of Industrial Strain Management

### Pillar 1 — **Preservation**
Long-term maintenance of a strain's genetic, morphological, and physiological integrity without contamination, mutation, or loss of productivity.

### Pillar 2 — **Improvement**
Systematic enhancement of the strain's genetic capability to overproduce the target metabolite beyond wild-type levels.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why both?** A poorly preserved strain loses its improved traits. A well-preserved but unimproved strain remains economically unviable. Both are equally important and **interdependent**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 PRESERVATION OF INDUSTRIAL MICROORGANISMS

Preservation methods are broadly classified into **short-term (working cultures)** and **long-term (stock cultures)** techniques.

### 2.1.1 Subculturing (Periodic Transfer Method)
- The oldest and simplest method.
- Involves transferring the organism to a fresh medium at regular intervals (typically **1–6 months**).
- **Disadvantage:** High risk of contamination, mutation, and loss of desirable traits over time.
- **Use:** Only for short-term maintenance or as a backup.

### 2.1.2 Refrigeration (4 °C)
- Storage at **4 °C** on agar slants.
- Slows metabolism but does not stop it — subculturing still needed every 2–8 weeks.
- **Suitable for:** Bacteria and yeasts (limited use for molds).

### 2.1.3 Cryopreservation (Ultra-Low Temperature Storage)
- Storage at **−80 °C** (mechanical freezer) or **−196 °C** (liquid nitrogen vapor phase).
- Cells are suspended in a **cryoprotectant** — typically **10–15% (v/v) glycerol** or **5–10% (v/v) dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO)**.
- **Mechanism:** Cryoprotectants prevent intracellular ice crystal formation that would puncture cell membranes.
- **Why −196 °C is the gold standard:** At this temperature, all biochemical reactions, including hydrolytic enzyme activity and free radical damage, effectively cease. Genetic stability is maintained for **decades**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Engineering Point:**
> The **freezing rate** matters. Optimal cooling rate ≈ **1 °C per minute** (controlled-rate freezing). Too fast → intracellular ice. Too slow → osmotic dehydration and cell shrinkage.

### 2.1.4 Lyophilization (Freeze-Drying)
- The process of freezing the culture and then removing water by **sublimation under vacuum**.
- **Steps:**
  1. Suspend cells in a **cryoprotectant** (e.g., 20% skim milk + 7.5% glucose or 10% sucrose).
  2. Pre-freeze at −40 °C to −80 °C.
  3. Apply high vacuum (~10–100 mTorr).
  4. Water sublimes from ice directly to vapor.
  5. Seal the ampoule under vacuum or inert gas (N₂).
- **Shelf life:** **5–20+ years** at room temperature.
- **Used for:** Most bacteria, yeasts, and sporulating fungi.
- **Not suitable for:** Sensitive strains without thick cell walls.

### 2.1.5 Storage in Silica Gel
- Cells are desiccated on anhydrous silica gel crystals.
- Particularly useful for **fungi** (e.g., *Penicillium*, *Aspergillus*).
- Low cost, simple, and effective for **1–4 years** of storage.

### 2.1.6 Storage in Soil/Grain
- Used primarily for **actinomycetes** and spore-forming fungi.
- Spores are mixed with sterile soil or grain, dried, and stored at low temperatures.
- **Shelf life:** Years to decades.

### 2.1.7 Immobilization Methods
- Cells trapped in polymer matrices (alginate, polyacrylamide) or attached to carriers.
- Combined with drying or low-temperature storage for long-term stability.

---

## 2.2 IMPROVEMENT OF INDUSTRIAL MICROORGANISMS

Improvement strategies have evolved from purely **classical (random) approaches** to **rational (targeted) engineering**.

### 2.2.1 Classical (Random) Mutagenesis & Selection

#### Step 1 — Mutation Induction
Common mutagens used in industry:

| Mutagen | Type | Mechanism |
|---|---|---|
| **UV radiation (254 nm)** | Physical | Forms thymine dimers → blocks replication → SOS repair causes errors |
| **X-rays, γ-rays** | Physical | Cause double-strand breaks and base deletions |
| **NTG (N-methyl-N′-nitro-N-nitrosoguanidine)** | Chemical | Alkylating agent → GC → AT transitions (super-mutagen) |
| **EMS (Ethyl methanesulfonate)** | Chemical | Alkylates guanine → base mispairing |
| **Nitrous acid (HNO₂)** | Chemical | Deaminates bases |
| **Acridine orange** | Chemical | Frameshift mutagen (intercalating dye) |

> [!CAUTION]
> **KTU High-Yield Fact:**
> **NTG is the most powerful chemical mutagen** used in industrial strain improvement programs because it induces multiple closely linked mutations — a phenomenon called **"mutational clustering"** — which is ideal for knocking out entire regulatory pathways (e.g., feedback inhibition).

#### Step 2 — Selection of Improved Mutants
Direct screening of every mutant is impractical when the improvement is 1.02–1.05×. Therefore, **selective enrichment techniques** are used:

- **Analog Resistance Selection:** Use toxic analogues of the target metabolite (e.g., **S-2-aminoethyl-L-cysteine** for lysine production in *Corynebacterium glutamicum*) → only mutants with **deregulated, overproducing pathways** survive.
- **Auxotroph Selection:** Use **minimal media + growth factor** to isolate specific auxotrophs that accumulate precursors.
- **Carbon Catabolite Derepression:** Use **2-deoxyglucose** to select for mutants resistant to catabolite repression.
- **High-Throughput Screening (HTS):** Automated robotics + colorimetric/fluorescent assays for rapid phenotype identification.

### 2.2.2 Protoplast Fusion (Somatic Hybridization)

> [!DEFINITION]
> **Protoplast Fusion** is a technique in which the rigid cell wall of two parental strains is enzymatically removed (using **lysozyme** for bacteria, **Novozym 234 / Glucanex** for yeast/fungi) to form protoplasts, which are then fused using **polyethylene glycol (PEG, 30–40%)** or electric field (**electrofusion**). The fused protoplasts regenerate their cell walls on selective media, producing a hybrid with combined or enhanced traits.

**Industrial Significance:**
- Allows **genome combination** between strains that are sexually incompatible.
- Useful when multiple desirable traits (e.g., high yield + fast growth) are present in different strains.
- Pioneered in *Streptomyces* and *Penicillium* programs.

### 2.2.3 Recombinant DNA Technology (Genetic Engineering)

> [!DEFINITION]
> The deliberate insertion, deletion, or modification of specific genes using restriction enzymes, ligases, vectors (plasmids, phages, cosmids, YACs), and transformation/transfection methods to construct a strain with a defined genetic blueprint for overproduction.

**Key Strategies:**

| Strategy | Molecular Basis | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Gene Amplification** | Multiple copies of a key biosynthetic gene on a high-copy plasmid | Increasing rate-limiting enzyme levels |
| **Promoter Engineering** | Replacing native promoters with strong, constitutive, or inducible promoters (e.g., **lac**, **trc**, **tac**) | Decoupling product formation from native regulation |
| **Knockout of Repressor Genes** | Deleting negative regulatory genes (e.g., *brpA* in *Streptomyces*) | Constitutive expression of biosynthetic operons |
| **Feedback-Resistant Enzymes** | Site-directed mutagenesis of allosteric sites | First enzyme in pathway no longer inhibited by end product |
| **Heterologous Expression** | Cloning a pathway from one organism into a more tractable host | E.g., producing artemisinin precursors in yeast |
| **Metabolic Engineering** | Systems-level flux balance analysis (FBA) to redistribute carbon flux toward product | Whole-cell pathway optimization |

### 2.2.4 Genome Shuffling
- A recursive protoplast fusion of multiple improved mutants from a mutagenesis program.
- Generates **diverse recombinant populations** with cumulative beneficial mutations.
- Pioneered by **Maxygen** (Wong et al.) for tylosin-producing *Streptomyces fradiae*.
- **Result:** 6–10× improvement in titers achievable in 1–2 years vs. 10+ years by classical methods.

### 2.2.5 Metabolic Engineering vs. Classical Improvement

> [!NOTE]
> **Metabolic Engineering (Bailey, 1991):** Improvement of cellular activities by manipulation of enzymatic, transport, and regulatory functions of the cell with the use of **recombinant DNA technology**.
>
> **Classical Improvement:** Random mutagenesis + selection — no molecular knowledge needed.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Parameter | Description |
|---|---|---|
| **Specific Growth Rate** | $\mu = \frac{1}{X} \frac{dX}{dt}$ | $X$ = biomass concentration |
| **Doubling Time** | $t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | Time for biomass to double |
| **Product Titer** | $P \; [\text{g/L}]$ | Total product accumulated per unit volume |
| **Product Yield (on substrate)** | $Y_{P/S} = \frac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S}$ | g product / g substrate consumed |
| **Volumetric Productivity** | $Q_P = \frac{P}{t} \; [\text{g/(L·h)}]$ | g product per liter per hour |
| **Specific Productivity** | $q_P = \frac{1}{X} \frac{dP}{dt} \; [\text{g/(g·h)}]$ | g product / g biomass / h |
| **Mutation Rate** | $\mu_m = \frac{M}{N \cdot t}$ | $M$ = mutants, $N$ = total cells, $t$ = time |
| **Survival Fraction** | $S = \frac{N_t}{N_0}$ | After mutagen dose |
| **Kill Curve (Lethal Dose)** | $LD_{90} = \text{dose for 90\% kill}$ | Used to calibrate mutagen exposure |
| **Feedback Inhibition (Hill)** | $v = \frac{V_{max}[S]^n}{K_M^n + [S]^n}$ | $n$ = Hill coefficient; $n > 1$ = cooperative inhibition |
| **Cryoprotectant Concentration** | $\text{Glycerol} = 10\text{–}15\% \; (v/v)$ | Standard for cryopreservation |
| **Lyophilization Vacuum** | $P_{vac} \approx 10\text{–}100 \; \text{mTorr}$ | Required for sublimation |
| **Storage at LN₂** | $T = -196 \; \text{°C}$ (77 K) | Gold-standard genetic stability |
| **Feedback Derepression Index** | $\vert \alpha \vert = \frac{\text{IC}_{50,\text{mutant}}}{\text{IC}_{50,\text{wild}}}$ | Ratio of inhibitor concentration for 50% inhibition |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** When asked to "improve penicillin yield" — the answer must mention **all three** mechanisms: (1) relieve feedback inhibition, (2) increase precursor supply (e.g., phenylacetic acid), (3) morphological differentiation in idiophase.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

## 3.1 Worked Example 1: Calculating Strain Improvement Using Mutation-Selection Data

**Problem:**
A wild-type *Corynebacterium glutamicum* produces **5 g/L of L-lysine** in 72 hours. After UV mutagenesis and analog resistance selection using **S-2-aminoethyl-L-cysteine (AEC)**, mutant strain **AEC-12** is isolated. In a 1-L fermenter with 20 g/L glucose as initial substrate, after 72 h the biomass is 4.5 g/L, residual glucose is 2.1 g/L, and lysine is **22 g/L**.

**Compute (a) Volumetric productivity, (b) Yield on substrate, (c) Specific productivity, (d) Fold improvement.**

### Given Information
- $P_{wt} = 5 \; \text{g/L}$ (wild type titer)
- $P_{mut} = 22 \; \text{g/L}$ (mutant titer)
- $V = 1 \; \text{L}$
- $S_0 = 20 \; \text{g/L}$
- $S_f = 2.1 \; \text{g/L}$
- $X = 4.5 \; \text{g/L}$
- $t = 72 \; \text{h}$

### Step-by-Step Solution

#### Part (a): Volumetric Productivity $Q_P$
Volumetric productivity represents how much product is made per liter per unit time. It is the most critical economic parameter in industrial fermentations because fermenter capital cost scales with $Q_P$.

$$Q_P = \frac{P_{mut} - P_0}{t - t_0} = \frac{22 - 0}{72 - 0}$$

$$Q_P = \frac{22}{72} = 0.3056 \; \text{g/(L·h)}$$

#### Part (b): Yield on Substrate $Y_{P/S}$
Yield measures the stoichiometric efficiency of substrate-to-product conversion.

$$Y_{P/S} = \frac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S} = \frac{P_{mut} - P_0}{S_0 - S_f} = \frac{22 - 0}{20 - 2.1}$$

$$Y_{P/S} = \frac{22}{17.9} = 1.229 \; \text{g/g}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation:** Every gram of glucose consumed yields **1.229 g of L-lysine**. The theoretical maximum yield (from stoichiometry) for lysine from glucose is ≈ **1.34 g/g**, so this strain operates at **91.7% of theoretical maximum** — a near-optimal industrial strain.

#### Part (c): Specific Productivity $q_P$
This normalizes productivity to biomass — it tells us how "hard" each cell is working.

$$q_P = \frac{1}{X} \cdot \frac{dP}{dt} \approx \frac{P_{mut}}{X \cdot t} = \frac{22}{4.5 \times 72}$$

$$q_P = \frac{22}{324} = 0.0679 \; \text{g/(g·h)}$$

#### Part (d): Fold Improvement
$$F = \frac{P_{mut}}{P_{wt}} = \frac{22}{5} = 4.4 \text{-fold}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** A **4.4× improvement** is realistic after a single round of UV + analog selection. Industrial programs run **8–15 sequential rounds** of mutagenesis + selection, often achieving **100–1000× cumulative improvement** (e.g., penicillin went from ~2 mg/L in 1941 to >70,000 mg/L today — a **35,000× improvement**).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example 2: Determining Optimal UV Mutagen Dose from Kill Curve

**Problem:**
A kill curve experiment for *Penicillium chrysogenum* conidia exposed to UV (254 nm) gives the following data. Determine the **LD₉₀ dose** and the **mutation rate** at that dose.

| UV Dose (s) | Surviving Colonies (CFU/mL) |
|---|---|
| 0 | $1.0 \times 10^6$ |
| 30 | $6.2 \times 10^5$ |
| 60 | $3.0 \times 10^5$ |
| 90 | $9.0 \times 10^4$ |
| 120 | $1.1 \times 10^4$ |
| 150 | $5.0 \times 10^2$ |

### Step 1: Compute Survival Fraction at Each Dose

$$S = \frac{N_t}{N_0}$$

| Dose (s) | $N_t$ (CFU/mL) | $S = N_t / N_0$ | $\log_{10} S$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | $1.0 \times 10^6$ | 1.000 | 0.000 |
| 30 | $6.2 \times 10^5$ | 0.620 | −0.208 |
| 60 | $3.0 \times 10^5$ | 0.300 | −0.523 |
| 90 | $9.0 \times 10^4$ | 0.090 | −1.046 |
| 120 | $1.1 \times 10^4$ | 0.011 | −1.959 |
| 150 | $5.0 \times 10^2$ | 0.0005 | −3.301 |

### Step 2: Determine LD₉₀
**LD₉₀** = dose at which 90% of cells are killed → $S = 0.10$ → $\log_{10} S = -1.0$.

From the table, $S = 0.090$ occurs at **90 s**. So **LD₉₀ ≈ 90 seconds**.

### Step 3: Determine Mutation Rate at LD₉₀
Assume at LD₉₀, the number of penicillin-overproducing mutants detected by the velvet-replica + selective plate screen is **45 CFU/mL**.

$$\mu_m = \frac{M}{N_t} = \frac{45}{9.0 \times 10^4}$$

$$\mu_m = 5.0 \times 10^{-4} \; \text{mutants per survivor}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Standard Practice:** Mutation programs are run at **70–90% kill** (LD₇₀ to LD₉₀) because lower doses cause insufficient DNA damage, while higher doses cause multiple lethal hits. **NTG** typically uses even lower doses (~10–30% kill) due to its extreme potency.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — How Each Improvement Method Targets a Specific Bottleneck

| Bottleneck in Metabolite Overproduction | Classical Mutagenesis | Protoplast Fusion | Recombinant DNA | Metabolic Engineering |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Feedback inhibition of first enzyme** | ✅ Analog resistance selection | ⚠️ Indirect | ✅ Site-directed mutagenesis of allosteric site | ✅ FBA + enzyme modification |
| **Catabolite repression** | ✅ 2-DG resistance | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Knockout of *cre* / *crc* genes | ✅ Global regulatory redesign |
| **Precursor supply** | ⚠️ Random | ✅ Combine two partial-pathway strains | ✅ Amplify rate-limiting genes | ✅ Pull-push-block strategies |
| **Efflux / secretion** | ❌ Difficult | ⚠️ Limited | ✅ Overexpress efflux pumps | ✅ Membrane transporter engineering |
| **Morphological differentiation (e.g., pellet formation)** | ⚠️ Random | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ Limited | ⚠️ Partial |
| **Idiophase stability** | ✅ Random screens | ✅ Combine robust strains | ✅ Delay sporulation by *spo* mutants | ⚠️ Limited |

> [!NOTE]
> **Legend:** ✅ = Direct/Effective | ⚠️ = Partial/Indirect | ❌ = Not applicable

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Takeaway:** A complete modern strain improvement program uses **all four** approaches in sequence — classical mutagenesis to generate diversity → screening for improved phenotypes → genome shuffling to combine beneficial mutations → metabolic engineering to fine-tune flux.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Overview of Preservation and Improvement Pipeline

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Wild-Type Industrial Microorganism] --> B[Isolation and Purification]
    B --> C[Identification and Characterization]
    C --> D1[Preservation Phase]
    C --> D2[Improvement Phase]
    D2 --> E1[Classical Mutagenesis - UV, NTG, EMS]
    D2 --> E2[Protoplast Fusion]
    D2 --> E3[Recombinant DNA Technology]
    D2 --> E4[Metabolic Engineering]
    E1 --> F[Screening and Selection]
    E2 --> F
    E3 --> F
    E4 --> F
    F --> G[High-Yielding Mutant Strain]
    G --> H{Strain Stable?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Master Cell Bank - Cryopreserved at minus 196 C]
    H -- No --> J[Further Mutagenesis Cycles]
    J --> F
    D1 --> K1[Short Term: Subculture at 4 C]
    D1 --> K2[Long Term: Lyophilization]
    D1 --> K3[Ultra Long Term: Cryopreservation in LN2]
    D1 --> K4[Alternative: Silica Gel or Soil Storage]
    I --> L[Industrial Fermentation - Overproduction of Primary or Secondary Metabolite]
    L --> M[Downstream Processing]
    M --> N[Purified Industrial Product]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Decision Tree for Choosing Preservation Method

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[New Industrial Strain to Preserve] --> Q1{Strain Type?}
    Q1 -- Bacteria --> B1
    Q1 -- Yeast --> Y1
    Q1 -- Filamentous Fungi --> F1
    Q1 -- Actinomycetes --> A1
    B1 -- Spore former --> SP[Lyophilization plus 20 percent Skim Milk]
    B1 -- Non spore former --> CR[Ultra Low Freezer at minus 80 C with 15 percent Glycerol]
    Y1 --> Y2{Desired Shelf Life?}
    Y2 -- Less than 1 year --> REF[Refrigeration at 4 C on YPD Slant]
    Y2 -- More than 5 years --> LYO[Lyophilization]
    F1 --> F2{Fungal Strain has Spores?}
    F2 -- Yes --> SG[Silica Gel Storage]
    F2 -- No --> CR2[Cryopreservation in LN2 with 10 percent DMSO]
    A1 --> AS[Soil Storage plus Cryopreservation at minus 80 C]
    SP --> Validation[Viability Test and Productivity Assay]
    CR --> Validation
    LYO --> Validation
    REF --> Validation
    SG --> Validation
    CR2 --> Validation
    AS --> Validation
    Validation --> Accept{Viable and Productive?}
    Accept -- Yes --> MCB[Master Cell Bank Established]
    Accept -- No --> Reject[Discard and Reisolate]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Iterative Improvement Cycle (Classical Strain Development)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Parent Strain] --> M1[Mutagenesis Treatment]
    M1 --> Surv{Survival Fraction 10 to 30 percent}
    Surv --> Plate[Plate on Selective Media]
    Plate --> Prim[Primary Screen - Analog Resistance]
    Prim --> Sec[Secondary Screen - Shake Flask Titer]
    Sec --> Stat[Statistical Analysis - Compare to Parent]
    Stat --> Compare{Significant Improvement greater than 1.2x?}
    Compare -- Yes --> NewP[New Improved Parent Strain]
    Compare -- No --> Discard[Discard and Re-mutagenize]
    NewP --> M1
    Discard --> M1
    NewP --> Ind[Industrial Production Strain]
```

## 4.4 Schematic — Pathway Deregulation for Overproduction of a Primary Metabolite

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    S[Substrate - e.g. Glucose] --> E1[Rate Limiting Enzyme - Allosteric]
    E1 -- Normally inhibited by P --> Reg{Feedback Inhibition}
    Reg -- Removed by mutation --> P1[Intermediates]
    P1 --> E2[Pathway Enzymes]
    E2 --> P2[More Intermediates]
    P2 --> E3[Terminal Pathway]
    E3 --> P[Final Product - e.g. L Lysine]
    P -- Overproduced --> Efflux[Efflux Pump - Overexpressed]
    Efflux --> Out[Product Secreted to Broth]
    style E1 fill:#ffe599,stroke:#333
    style Reg fill:#f4cccc,stroke:#333
    style Efflux fill:#b6d7a8,stroke:#333
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The yellow box is the **target enzyme** (to be deregulated by mutation). The red box is the **inhibition** (to be removed). The green box is the **efflux system** (to be amplified) — this prevents intracellular product accumulation from re-inhibiting the pathway.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Quick Recall for Diagram-based Questions:**
> 1. **Preservation** = stability of *existing* trait.
> 2. **Improvement** = generation of *new* trait.
> 3. **Primary metabolite** = derepress pathway + amplify efflux.
> 4. **Secondary metabolite** = enhance precursor pool + delay morphogenesis.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define an industrial microorganism. List any four methods used for the preservation of industrial microorganisms.**
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition (1 mark):** An industrial microorganism is a microbial strain (bacterial, fungal, yeast, or actinomycete) that has been isolated, purified, and genetically improved for the economic overproduction of a commercially valuable metabolite such as an antibiotic, amino acid, enzyme, vitamin, or organic acid in a large-scale fermenter.

> **Preservation methods (2 marks — any four):**
> 1. **Subculturing / periodic transfer** on agar slants at 4 °C.
> 2. **Refrigeration** at 4 °C.
> 3. **Cryopreservation** at −80 °C or in liquid nitrogen (−196 °C) using glycerol (10–15%) or DMSO (5–10%) as cryoprotectant.
> 4. **Lyophilization (freeze-drying)** under vacuum using a cryoprotectant such as 20% skim milk.
> 5. **Storage on silica gel** crystals.
> 6. **Storage in sterile soil or grain** (for actinomycetes and spore-forming fungi).

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between primary and secondary metabolites with two industrial examples each.**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Primary Metabolite | Secondary Metabolite |
|---|---|---|
| **Phase of production** | Trophophase (growth phase) | Idiophase (stationary phase) |
| **Function** | Essential for cell growth | Non-essential; ecological role |
| **Examples (1 each, 1 mark)** | L-Glutamate, L-Lysine, citric acid, ethanol | Penicillin, streptomycin, gibberellin |

> **Note (1 mark):** Primary metabolites are produced in parallel with growth and are essential; secondary metabolites are produced after growth cessation and are not essential for the producing organism's survival.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice (either-or). Below are two complete alternative questions.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**(a) [7 Marks] Describe in detail the various methods used for the preservation of industrial microorganisms. Highlight the principle, procedure, and applications of lyophilization.**

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024, Modified]**

**Model Answer:**

#### Introduction (1 mark)
Preservation is essential to maintain the genetic, morphological, and physiological integrity of an industrial strain over long durations without contamination, mutation, or loss of productivity.

#### Methods of Preservation (4 marks)

**1. Subculturing (Periodic Transfer Method):**
- Inoculation of the strain onto fresh agar slants at regular intervals (1–6 months).
- Stored at 4 °C.
- Simple but high risk of contamination and mutation.

**2. Refrigeration:**
- Storage at 4 °C on agar slants or plates.
- Slows but does not stop metabolism.
- Subculturing every 2–8 weeks.

**3. Cryopreservation:**
- Storage at −80 °C (mechanical freezer) or −196 °C (liquid nitrogen vapor phase).
- Uses cryoprotectants — 10–15% glycerol or 5–10% DMSO — to prevent intracellular ice formation.
- Optimal cooling rate ≈ 1 °C per minute.
- Genetic stability maintained for decades.
- **Gold standard** for long-term preservation.

**4. Lyophilization (Freeze-Drying) — Detailed Below.**
**5. Storage on Silica Gel:**
- Anhydrous silica gel crystals desiccate the cells.
- Particularly useful for spore-bearing fungi.
- Low cost, shelf life 1–4 years.

**6. Storage in Sterile Soil or Grain:**
- For actinomycetes and sporulating fungi.
- Shelf life years to decades.

**7. Immobilization + Drying:**
- Cells trapped in polymer matrices (alginate, polyacrylamide) and dried.

#### Principle, Procedure & Applications of Lyophilization (2 marks)

**Principle:** Removal of water from a frozen suspension by **sublimation** under high vacuum.

**Procedure:**
1. Suspend cells in **20% skim milk + 7.5% glucose** (or 10% sucrose) as a protective medium.
2. Pre-freeze the suspension to **−40 to −80 °C**.
3. Apply high vacuum (~10–100 mTorr).
4. Water sublimes from solid ice directly to vapor.
5. Seal the ampoule under vacuum or inert N₂ atmosphere.
6. Store at 4 °C in the dark.

**Applications:**
- Suitable for bacteria, yeasts, and sporulating fungi.
- Shelf life of **5–20+ years** at room temperature.
- Used in culture collections (ATCC, MTCC) and industry.

#### Conclusion (0.5 mark)
Lyophilization is the most widely used **long-term, room-temperature-stable** preservation method for industrial strains.

#### Diagram (0.5 mark)
*(Refer to the block diagram in Section 4.1.)*

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Explain the classical methods for improvement of industrial microorganisms. Discuss the role of NTG mutagenesis and analog resistance selection with suitable examples.**

**Model Answer:**

#### Introduction (1 mark)
Classical strain improvement relies on **random mutagenesis followed by selection** for superior phenotypes, without prior molecular knowledge of the pathway. It has been the backbone of industrial strain development since the 1940s.

#### Classical Mutagenesis (3 marks)

**Mutagens used:**
- **Physical:** UV radiation (254 nm), X-rays, γ-rays.
- **Chemical:** NTG (N-methyl-N′-nitro-N-nitrosoguanidine), EMS, nitrous acid, acridine dyes.

**Mechanism of action (briefly):**
- UV → thymine dimers → blocks replication → error-prone SOS repair.
- NTG → alkylates guanine → causes GC→AT transitions. Most potent chemical mutagen; causes **mutational clustering**.

**Procedure:**
1. Grow culture to mid-log phase.
2. Expose to mutagen at LD₇₀–LD₉₀ (70–90% kill).
3. Plate survivors on selective medium.
4. Screen colonies for improved phenotype.

#### Role of NTG Mutagenesis (1.5 marks)

- **Why NTG?** Causes multiple closely linked mutations, ideal for knocking out regulatory genes in a single step.
- **Used extensively** in *Streptomyces aureofaciens* (tetracycline), *Penicillium chrysogenum* (penicillin), *Corynebacterium glutamicum* (lysine).
- **Disadvantage:** Cannot predict the type or location of mutation; must rely on screening.

#### Analog Resistance Selection (1.5 marks)

**Principle:** Use a **toxic structural analogue** of the desired metabolite. Mutants with **deregulated, feedback-resistant** biosynthetic enzymes will overproduce the natural metabolite and **resist the toxic analogue**.

**Classic Example — L-Lysine production by *C. glutamicum*:**
- **Toxic analogue:** *S*-2-aminoethyl-L-cysteine (AEC) — analogue of lysine.
- Wild-type *C. glutamicum* is **inhibited** by AEC because lysine (true end product) normally shuts off aspartokinase → cell dies.
- **AEC-resistant mutants** have a mutated aspartokinase that **no longer responds to feedback inhibition** → they **overproduce L-lysine**.

**Other examples:**
- *Penicillium chrysogenum* selected on **phenoxyacetic acid analogues** for penicillin overproduction.
- *Streptomyces* selected on **2-deoxyglucose** for catabolite-derepressed mutants.

#### Conclusion (0.5 mark)
Classical methods, particularly **NTG + analog resistance selection**, have delivered **1000–35,000× cumulative yield improvements** in the antibiotic and amino acid industries, and remain relevant even in the recombinant DNA era.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — Alternative Choice

**(a) [7 Marks] Discuss in detail the various techniques of genetic engineering used for the improvement of industrial microorganisms. Highlight any two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer:**

#### Introduction (1 mark)
Genetic engineering enables **rational, targeted** modification of an organism's DNA to enhance metabolite production — replacing or complementing the random approach of classical mutagenesis.

#### Key Techniques (5 marks)

**1. Gene Amplification:**
- Insertion of a key biosynthetic gene on a **high-copy-number plasmid** (e.g., pUC, pBR322 derivatives).
- Increases the intracellular concentration of the rate-limiting enzyme.
- *Example:* Amplification of the *lysC* gene (aspartokinase) in *C. glutamicum* for lysine overproduction.

**2. Promoter Engineering:**
- Replacement of the native (often weak or regulated) promoter with a **strong, constitutive, or inducible promoter** — e.g., *lac*, *trc*, *tac*, *T7* phage promoter.
- Decouples product formation from native regulatory circuits.
- *Example:* *Penicillium chrysogenum* strains with the *pcbAB* and *pcbC* penicillin biosynthesis genes under the *gpdA* (glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase) strong promoter.

**3. Knockout of Repressor / Regulatory Genes:**
- Targeted deletion of negative regulators.
- *Example:* Disruption of *brpA* (a pleiotropic repressor) in *Streptomyces* → constitutive expression of antibiotic biosynthesis genes.

**4. Engineering Feedback-Resistant Enzymes:**
- **Site-directed mutagenesis** of the allosteric binding site of a feedback-inhibited enzyme.
- *Example:* Substitution of threonine → isoleucine at position 311 in *E. coli* aspartokinase III eliminates threonine-mediated feedback inhibition → enhanced lysine pathway flux.

**5. Heterologous Expression:**
- Cloning an entire biosynthetic pathway from one organism into a more tractable host.
- *Example:* Engineering *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* to produce **artemisinic acid** (anti-malarial precursor) by transferring 8 genes from *Artemisia annua*.
- *Example:* Production of **human insulin** in *E. coli* or yeast.

**6. Efflux Pump Engineering:**
- Overexpression of membrane transporters to rapidly export the product and prevent intracellular feedback inhibition.
- *Example:* Overexpression of *lysE* (lysine exporter) in *C. glutamicum*.

**7. Metabolic Engineering / Systems Approach:**
- **Flux Balance Analysis (FBA)** to identify and relieve metabolic bottlenecks.
- Application of **pull-push-block** strategies: amplify upstream genes (push), amplify downstream pathway (pull), knockout competing pathways (block).

#### Two Industrial Applications (1 mark)

1. **Penicillin:** Classical + recombinant engineering of *Penicillium chrysogenum* has pushed titers from 2 mg/L to >70,000 mg/L.
2. **L-Glutamate:** Recombinant modification of *C. glutamicum* — amplifying *gdh* (glutamate dehydrogenase) and engineering the ODHC (2-oxoglutarate dehydrogenase) complex for biotin-limited, glutamate-overproducing phenotypes.

#### Conclusion (0.5 mark)

---

**(b) [7 Marks] Compare classical mutagenesis and recombinant DNA technology as tools for strain improvement. Which approach is preferred in modern industry and why?**

**Model Answer:**

#### Comparative Table (4 marks)

| Parameter | Classical Mutagenesis | Recombinant DNA Technology |
|---|---|---|
| **Approach** | Random | Targeted / Rational |
| **Knowledge required** | None (phenotype-based) | Detailed molecular pathway knowledge |
| **Speed** | Slow (10–20 years for major gains) | Fast (months to a few years) |
| **Precision** | Low (random hits) | High (gene-specific) |
| **Regulatory concerns** | None (mutant strains generally recognized as safe) | GMO regulations apply — costly, time-consuming approval |
| **Stability of improvement** | High (chromosomal mutations) | Variable (plasmid loss is common — must integrate into chromosome) |
| **Cost** | Low | High |
| **Regulatory approval** | Easier | Stricter (especially for food/pharma) |
| **Examples** | Penicillin strain lineage, classical lysine producers | Recombinant insulin, recombinant tPA, engineered artemisinin yeast |

#### Which is Preferred in Modern Industry? (3 marks)

> **Answer: A HYBRID (integrated) approach is preferred.**

**Justification:**

1. **Starting diversity** — Classical mutagenesis is still the best way to generate **large-scale genetic diversity** quickly. Recombinant methods alone are limited by what we know — much remains unknown.
2. **Fine-tuning** — Once a high-producing variant is identified by random methods, **recombinant DNA** is used to **identify the responsible mutation**, **characterize the pathway**, and **amplify beneficial alleles**.
3. **Modern programs** (e.g., the *P. chrysogenum* and *S. cerevisiae* industrial lineages) use a **closed-loop cycle**: classical → screening → genome sequencing → targeted engineering → next round.
4. **For food-grade products** (e.g., citric acid, glutamate), classical mutants are preferred because they **avoid GMO regulations**.
5. **For pharmaceuticals** (e.g., insulin, erythropoietin), recombinant DNA is **mandatory** for purity, safety, and scalability.

#### Conclusion (0.5 mark)
> The dichotomy is artificial. Modern industrial biotechnology fuses both approaches into a single, iterative strain development platform.

---

## 5.3 Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in KTU Valuation:**
> 1. **Confusing "preservation" with "improvement":** A 14-mark question on preservation should NOT discuss mutation methods. Read the question carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting cryoprotectants:** Students often write "stored at −196 °C" but forget to mention **glycerol/DMSO** — this costs 1 mark.
> 3. **No mention of analog resistance mechanism:** When asked about analog resistance, students must explain **WHY** the mutant survives — the analogue inhibits wild-type feedback-deregulated enzyme; the mutant has a feedback-resistant enzyme that overproduces the natural metabolite, which dilutes/competes with the toxic analogue.
> 4. **Ignoring idiophase vs. trophophase:** Secondary metabolites are produced in the **idiophase** — for overproduction, the strain's growth-to-stationary transition should be extended.
> 5. **No industrial examples:** Always cite at least one industrial example (Penicillin, Lysine, Glutamate, Insulin) — KTU evaluators expect application-level answers.
> 6. **Single method bias:** When asked for "methods of preservation," list **at least 5** with **principle + application** for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-DENSITY REVISION CHECKLIST — KTU Module 1, Topic: Preservation & Improvement of Industrial Microorganisms**

### Preservation Methods (must know ≥ 5)
- ☐ **Subculturing** — periodic transfer, simple but risky
- ☐ **Refrigeration (4 °C)** — short-term, every 2–8 weeks
- ☐ **Cryopreservation (−80 °C or −196 °C)** — gold standard, with 10–15% glycerol
- ☐ **Lyophilization (freeze-drying)** — 5–20 yr shelf life, uses 20% skim milk
- ☐ **Silica gel storage** — for fungi
- ☐ **Soil/grain storage** — for actinomycetes

### Primary vs. Secondary Metabolites
- ☐ **Primary** = growth phase (trophophase); e.g., amino acids, organic acids
- ☐ **Secondary** = stationary phase (idiophase); e.g., antibiotics
- ☐ **Overproduction strategy differs** for the two classes

### Improvement Methods (must know all 4)
- ☐ **Classical Mutagenesis** — UV, NTG, EMS; random
- ☐ **Protoplast Fusion** — lysozyme + PEG; combines genomes
- ☐ **Recombinant DNA** — gene amplification, promoter engineering, knockout, heterologous expression
- ☐ **Metabolic Engineering** — FBA, pull-push-block, rational flux redirection

### Key Industrial Examples
- ☐ **Penicillin** — *P. chrysogenum* (2 mg/L → 70,000 mg/L)
- ☐ **L-Lysine** — *C. glutamicum* (analog resistance with AEC)
- ☐ **L-Glutamate** — *C. glutamicum* (biotin limitation + ODHC engineering)
- ☐ **Insulin** — recombinant in *E. coli*

### Critical Mutagens to Remember
- ☐ **UV** → thymine dimers
- ☐ **NTG** → most potent chemical mutagen → GC to AT transitions
- ☐ **EMS** → alkylates guanine
- ☐ **Acridines** → frameshift

### Selection Mechanisms
- ☐ **Analog resistance** (AEC for lysine)
- ☐ **Auxotroph selection** (minimal + growth factor)
- ☐ **Catabolite derepression** (2-deoxyglucose resistance)
- ☐ **High-throughput screening** (HTS, robotics, microtiter plates)

### Key Quantitative Formulas
- ☐ $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ — specific growth rate
- ☐ $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ — doubling time
- ☐ $Q_P = \dfrac{P}{t}$ — volumetric productivity
- ☐ $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{-\Delta S}$ — product yield
- ☐ $q_P = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ — specific productivity

### KTU Memory Hooks
- ☐ **Preservation = "Keep"**, **Improvement = "Make Better"**
- ☐ **NTG = "Super-mutagen"** (always)
- ☐ **AEC = Lysine's "twin brother"** (analogue resistance)
- ☐ **−196 °C = Forever** (storage at liquid nitrogen)
- ☐ **Penicillin = 35,000× improvement** (the poster child of strain improvement)
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Medium Requirements for Fermentation Process
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Medium Requirements for Fermentation Process

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **fermentation medium** (also called *growth medium* or *culture medium*) is a **nutrient-rich aqueous solution** that supplies all the essential elements required for the **sustained growth, metabolism, and product formation** of a microbial culture (bacteria, yeast, fungi, or mammalian/animal cells) under controlled bioprocess conditions.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (OEFTT726 — Module 1):**
> *"The fermentation medium must satisfy the elemental requirements of the microorganism — typically: Carbon, Nitrogen, Energy, Water, Minerals, Vitamins, Growth Factors, and Dissolved Oxygen — while maintaining optimum pH, temperature, and osmotic balance for the target bioprocess."*

In formal stoichiometric notation, a microbial cell of empirical formula $\mathbf{C_\alpha H_\beta O_\gamma N_\delta}$ requires a balanced supply of macronutrients and micronutrients such that the overall biomass yield ($Y_{X/S}$) and product formation rate ($r_P$) are maximized.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **cost of the medium alone contributes 30–40 %** of the total production cost in industrial fermentations (e.g., antibiotics, enzymes, recombinant proteins). Hence, *medium design is a critical economic decision* in bioprocess engineering.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — A Balanced Diet for Microbes

Imagine you are growing a tiny "biological factory" — a microorganism — inside a steel tank called a *bioreactor*. Just like a human athlete needs the right combination of **carbohydrates, proteins, vitamins, minerals, and water** to perform at peak efficiency, microorganisms also need a *precisely balanced diet* to grow, reproduce, and manufacture useful products (like antibiotics, vaccines, or industrial enzymes).

| Human Athlete Needs | Microbe Needs in Fermenter | Function |
|---|---|---|
| Carbohydrates (roti, rice) | Glucose, sucrose, molasses (C-source) | Energy + biomass building |
| Proteins (dal, eggs) | Ammonium salts, urea, yeast extract (N-source) | Protein/amino-acid synthesis |
| Vitamins (fruits) | B-complex vitamins, biotin | Enzyme cofactors |
| Minerals (iron, calcium) | MgSO₄, MnCl₂, FeSO₄, ZnSO₄ | Cofactors, structural roles |
| Water (hydration) | Deionized/distilled water | Solvent, mass transfer |
| Oxygen (breathing) | Dissolved O₂ (aeration) | Aerobic respiration |

> [!TIP]
> **Rule of thumb:** A medium is *just right* when it provides **everything the microbe needs, in the right proportion, at the right time**, and *nothing that inhibits* growth.

## 1.3 The Seven Universal Requirements of a Fermentation Medium

A KTU-board compliant fermentation medium must satisfy the following seven cardinal requirements:

1. **Carbon source** — primary energy + structural element
2. **Nitrogen source** — for proteins, nucleic acids, cell wall
3. **Minerals (macro & micro)** — for enzymes, cofactors, transport
4. **Vitamins & growth factors** — for coenzyme function
5. **Water** — solvent, mass-transfer medium (purity critical)
6. **Dissolved oxygen (DO)** — for aerobic cultures
7. **pH buffers & antifoam** — environmental stability

Additionally, industrial media may contain:
- **Precursors** (e.g., phenylalanine for penicillin production)
- **Inducers** (e.g., lactose for lac-operon in *E. coli*)
- **Antifoaming agents** (e.g., silicone oil, polypropylene glycol)
- **Selective agents** (for recombinant selection like antibiotics)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Standard physical/chemical performance metrics used by KTU examiners:
> - **pH:** typically **4.0 – 7.5** (yeasts 4–5, bacteria 6.5–7.5)
> - **Temperature:** **20 °C – 45 °C** depending on mesophile/thermophile
> - **Sterility:** *zero* contamination; achieved via autoclaving at **121 °C / 15 psi / 15–20 min**
> - **Dissolved O₂:** minimum **30 % saturation** for aerobic cultures

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Breakdown of Medium Components

### A. CARBON SOURCE (~ 50 % of medium cost)

The carbon source is the **largest mass fraction** in any fermentation medium. It serves **two simultaneous functions**:
1. **Energy generation** via catabolic pathways (glycolysis, TCA cycle, oxidative phosphorylation).
2. **Biosynthetic precursor** for cell wall, lipids, nucleic acids, polysaccharides.

| Carbon Source Type | Examples | Typical Microorganism | Cost Index |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Pure sugars** | Glucose, fructose, sucrose | *E. coli*, *S. cerevisiae* | High |
| **Industrial by-products** | Molasses, corn-steep liquor | Yeast, antibiotic producers | Low |
| **Starch / polysaccharides** | Soluble starch, dextrins | Filamentous fungi | Medium |
| **Hydrocarbons / oils** | n-Alkanes, vegetable oils | *Candida*, *Pythium* | Variable |
| **Whey / lactose** | Dairy industry waste | *Lactobacillus* | Low |
| **Cellulosic biomass** | Glucose from hydrolysis | Industrial enzymes producers | Emerging |

> [!NOTE]
> **Glucose concentration rule:** Initial glucose > **50 g/L** causes **catabolite repression** (Crabtree effect in yeast) and **overflow metabolism** (acetate formation in *E. coli*). KTU examiners often test the *glucose fed-batch* strategy to avoid this.

### B. NITROGEN SOURCE

Nitrogen is required for **proteins, nucleic acids, peptidoglycan, ATP, and chlorophyll** (in algae). Sources may be:

- **Inorganic:** Ammonium salts — $(NH_4)_2SO_4$, $NH_4Cl$, $NH_4NO_3$, $NaNO_3$
- **Organic:** Yeast extract, peptone, tryptone, corn-steep liquor (CSL), soybean meal

> The **C:N ratio** is the most important design parameter:
> - **Bacteria:** 3:1 to 5:1
> - **Yeast:** 5:1 to 10:1
> - **Filamentous fungi:** 10:1 to 20:1

### C. MINERALS — Macro & Micro

- **Macronutrients (>1 mM):** Phosphorus (K₂HPO₄), Potassium (KH₂PO₄), Magnesium (MgSO₄·7H₂O), Calcium (CaCO₃)
- **Micronutrients (μM range):** Iron (FeSO₄), Manganese (MnCl₂), Zinc (ZnSO₄), Copper (CuSO₄), Cobalt (CoCl₂), Molybdenum (NaMoO₄)

Phosphorus is provided as a **phosphate buffer** which simultaneously *adjusts pH*.

### D. VITAMINS & GROWTH FACTORS

Many microorganisms are *auxotrophs* — they cannot synthesize certain vitamins. These must be supplied externally:

- **B-complex:** Thiamine (B₁), Riboflavin (B₂), Niacin (B₃), Pantothenic acid (B₅), Pyridoxine (B₆), Biotin (B₇), Folic acid (B₉), Cobalamin (B₁₂)
- **Amino acids** (for auxotrophic mutants)
- **Fatty acids** (for some fungi)
- **Nucleobases / nucleosides** (for some mammalian cell lines)

> Commercial yeast extract supplies **almost all B-vitamins** naturally.

### E. WATER

**Water of high purity** is mandatory because trace impurities can inhibit growth or shift product profile.

- **Potable water:** Contains Cl⁻, Ca²⁺, Mg²⁺ — *unsuitable*
- **Tap water:** May contain *chlorine, fluoride* — inhibits microbes
- **Distilled / deionized water:** Standard for lab-scale
- **Reverse-osmosis + ultra-pure:** For recombinant & mammalian cell culture

### F. DISSOLVED OXYGEN (DO) REQUIREMENT

Aerobic fermentations require **continuous O₂ supply** because oxygen's *low aqueous solubility* (~ **7 mg/L at 30 °C, 1 atm**) limits transfer.

The volumetric oxygen transfer rate is given by:

$$OTR = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

Where:
- $OTR$ = Oxygen Transfer Rate (mmol O₂ / L·h)
- $k_L a$ = Volumetric mass-transfer coefficient (h⁻¹)
- $C^*$ = Equilibrium dissolved O₂ concentration (mg/L)
- $C_L$ = Actual dissolved O₂ concentration (mg/L)

### G. pH BUFFERS, ANIFOAM & PRECURSORS

- **pH buffers:** K₂HPO₄/KH₂PO₄ (phosphate), CaCO₃, MOPS, HEPES
- **Antifoams:** Silicone-based (poly-dimethylsiloxane), polypropylene glycol, vegetable oils — added at **0.01 – 0.05 % v/v**
- **Precursors:** Direct building blocks of desired product (e.g., *phenylacetic acid* → benzyl group of penicillin G)
- **Inducers:** IPTG (isopropyl β-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside), lactose, methanol (for *Pichia pastoris* AOX1 promoter)

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following formula sheet is KTU-board-certified and must be memorized for ESE (End Semester Examination).**

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ | Monod growth kinetics | h⁻¹ |
| 2 | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | Biomass yield on substrate | g biomass / g substrate |
| 3 | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | Product yield on substrate | g product / g substrate |
| 4 | $Y_{P/X} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta X}$ | Product yield on biomass | g product / g biomass |
| 5 | $r_X = Y_{X/S} \cdot r_S$ | Growth rate = yield × substrate consumption rate | g/L·h |
| 6 | $r_P = Y_{P/S} \cdot r_S$ | Product formation rate | g/L·h |
| 7 | $q_S = \dfrac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}} + m$ | Specific substrate uptake rate | g/g·h |
| 8 | $OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ | Oxygen transfer rate | mg O₂/L·h |
| 9 | $N_a = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ | Same as OTR (alternative notation) | mmol/L·h |
| 10 | $\eta_{O_2} = Y_{X/O_2} \cdot \text{actual O}_2$ | Oxygen uptake efficiency | dimensionless |
| 11 | $C:N \text{ ratio} = \dfrac{\text{Carbon source (g)}}{\text{Nitrogen source (g)}}$ | Medium design parameter | g/g |
| 12 | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ | Doubling time | h |
| 13 | $\mu_{net} = \mu - k_d$ | Net growth rate (with death) | h⁻¹ |
| 14 | $OUR = q_{O_2} \cdot X$ | Oxygen Uptake Rate | mg O₂/L·h |
| 15 | $DO_{crit} \approx 10\text{–}30\% \text{ of saturation}$ | Critical dissolved O₂ threshold | % |

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary Conditions & Limitations:**
> - Monod equation is valid only for **single-substrate, steady-state, exponential growth**.
> - $K_s$ for glucose in *E. coli* is **~ 4 mg/L** (very low — glucose is a preferred substrate).
> - $Y_{X/S}$ typical values: *E. coli* on glucose = **0.5 g/g**, *S. cerevisiae* = **0.45 g/g**, *P. chrysogenum* on lactose = **0.30 g/g**.
> - Catabolite repression occurs when **glucose > 50 g/L** in *E. coli* (acetate overflow).

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry** (e.g., insulin from *E. coli*, penicillin from *P. chrysogenum*): medium cost dominates; *defined vs complex media* choice affects downstream purification.
- **Industrial enzymes** (amylases, proteases from *Bacillus*): cheap carbon sources like CSL and wheat bran are used.
- **Beverages** (beer, wine, ethanol): molasses + ammonium salts; nitrogen-limited for ethanol vs biomass.
- **Single-cell protein (SCP):** Pruteen (ICI) used *Methylophilus methylotrophus* on methanol — high biomass yield per substrate.
- **Mammalian cell culture (vaccines, mAbs):** serum-free, chemically defined media (e.g., DMEM, RPMI-1640) with glucose, glutamine, insulin, transferrin.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Monod Growth Equation (from Mass Balance)

Starting from the **idealized assumption** that microbial growth rate is controlled by a single **rate-limiting substrate concentration** $S$:

**Step 1: Define specific growth rate** $\mu$ as the rate of biomass increase per unit biomass present.

$$\mu = \frac{1}{X} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

**Step 2:** The maximum specific growth rate $\mu_{max}$ is achieved only when substrate $S \rightarrow \infty$. For finite $S$, the growth is limited by the enzyme-mediated transport/metabolism of substrate.

**Step 3:** Apply **Michaelis–Menten-type saturation kinetics** to substrate availability:

$$\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_s + S}$$

**Step 4 (Boundary validation):**
- When $S \gg K_s$: $\mu \approx \mu_{max}$ (substrate non-limiting — exponential phase)
- When $S \ll K_s$: $\mu \approx \dfrac{\mu_{max}}{K_s} \cdot S$ (substrate-limiting — linear phase)

> **Result:** Monod (1949) growth equation — fundamental to all bioprocess engineering design.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Biomass Yield Coefficient ($Y_{X/S}$)

**Step 1: Mass balance on substrate consumption.**
Let $S_0$ = initial substrate (g/L), $S$ = residual at time $t$, $X_0$ = initial biomass, $X$ = biomass at time $t$.

$$\Delta S = S_0 - S \quad ; \quad \Delta X = X - X_0$$

**Step 2: Define yield** as biomass produced per unit substrate consumed:

$$Y_{X/S} = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} = \frac{X - X_0}{S_0 - S}$$

**Step 3: Carbon balance example —** Aerobic growth of *E. coli* on glucose:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + a\,O_2 + b\,NH_3 \rightarrow c\,CH_{1.8}O_{0.5}N_{0.2} + d\,CO_2 + e\,H_2O$$

**Step 4: For *E. coli* ($CH_{1.8}O_{0.5}N_{0.2}$, MW = 25 g/mol):**

$$Y_{X/S} = \frac{X \text{ formed (g)}}{S \text{ consumed (g)}} = \frac{180}{25} \cdot \frac{c}{1} \approx 0.5 \text{ g/g (typical)}$$

> **Note:** Theoretical maximum from carbon balance is ~ **1.0 g/g**, but losses to CO₂, maintenance energy, and by-products reduce the actual $Y_{X/S}$ to **0.4–0.6 g/g**.

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Specific Substrate Uptake Rate ($q_S$)

**Step 1: Substrate consumption rate** $r_S$ (g/L·h) is split into *growth-associated* and *maintenance-associated*:

$$r_S = \frac{1}{Y_{X/S}^{true}} \cdot r_X + m \cdot X$$

Where $m$ = maintenance coefficient (g substrate / g biomass · h).

**Step 2: Specific substrate uptake rate** $q_S$ = substrate consumed per unit biomass per unit time:

$$q_S = \frac{r_S}{X} = \frac{\mu}{Y_{X/S}^{true}} + m$$

**Step 3: For *E. coli* on glucose, $Y_{X/S} = 0.5$ g/g, $\mu = 0.5$ h⁻¹, $m = 0.05$ g/g·h:**

$$q_S = \frac{0.5}{0.5} + 0.05 = 1.05 \text{ g/g·h}$$

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR) & $k_L a$ Estimation

**Step 1: Two-film theory** — oxygen diffuses through:
- A **stagnant gas film** (resistance $1/k_G$)
- A **stagnant liquid film** (resistance $1/k_L$)

**Step 2: At steady state, flux through both films is equal:**

$$OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$$

**Step 3: Van 't Riet's correlation** (commonly tested in KTU) for **non-ionic solutions in agitated bioreactors:**

$$k_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{0.5} \cdot v_s^{0.5}$$

Where:
- $K$ = empirical constant (depends on geometry)
- $P_g / V_L$ = gassed power input per unit volume (W/m³)
- $v_s$ = superficial gas velocity (m/s)

**Step 4: Cooper's correlation** (simpler, older but asked in KTU 2019 paper):

$$k_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P_g}{V_L}\right)^{0.95} \cdot v_s^{0.67}$$

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Medium Design for Common Industrial Fermentations

| Organism | Product | Carbon Source | Nitrogen Source | Special Additives | Typical $Y_{X/S}$ (g/g) | C:N Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| *E. coli* (recomb.) | Insulin, mAbs | Glucose (fed-batch) | Yeast extract, NH₄Cl | IPTG, kanamycin | 0.50 | 4:1 |
| *S. cerevisiae* | Ethanol, bread | Glucose, molasses | (NH₄)₂SO₄, urea | Vitamins, Mg²⁺ | 0.45 | 8:1 |
| *P. chrysogenum* | Penicillin | Lactose, glucose | Corn-steep liquor (CSL) | Phenylacetic acid (precursor) | 0.30 | 12:1 |
| *A. niger* | Citric acid | Glucose, sucrose | NH₄NO₃ | Mn²⁺ limitation | 0.25 | 15:1 |
| *B. subtilis* | Amylase, protease | Starch, glucose | Peptone, yeast extract | Ca²⁺ for sporulation | 0.40 | 6:1 |
| *C. acetobutylicum* | Butanol | Glucose, molasses | Yeast extract | Fe limitation | 0.20 | 20:1 |
| *M. methylotrophus* | SCP (Pruteen) | Methanol | NH₃ | High O₂ demand | 0.50 | 5:1 |
| CHO cells | mAbs | Glucose + galactose | Glutamine | Insulin, transferrin, EGF | — | — |

> [!TIP]
> **Common KTU numerical pattern:** Given $Y_{X/S}$, $Y_{P/S}$, and substrate concentration $S_0$, find biomass $X$, product $P$, and overall mass balance closure.

---

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Hierarchy of Medium Requirements

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[FERMENTATION MEDIUM] --> B[Carbon Source]
    A --> C[Nitrogen Source]
    A --> D[Minerals]
    A --> E[Vitamins & Growth Factors]
    A --> F[Water]
    A --> G[Dissolved Oxygen]
    A --> H[Process Additives]

    B --> B1[Glucose, Molasses, Starch]
    B --> B2[Energy + Biomass]

    C --> C1[Inorganic: NH4 salts, Nitrates]
    C --> C2[Organic: Yeast extract, Peptone, CSL]

    D --> D1[Macro: P, K, Mg, Ca, S]
    D --> D2[Micro: Fe, Mn, Zn, Cu, Co, Mo]

    E --> E1[B-complex vitamins]
    E --> E2[Amino acids auxotrophs]
    E --> E3[Fatty acids, Nucleobases]

    F --> F1[Distilled]
    F --> F2[Deionized]
    F --> F3[Reverse Osmosis]

    G --> G1[Aeration agitation]
    G --> G2[OTR kLa equation]
    G --> G3[DO above critical]

    H --> H1[Antifoam agents]
    H --> H2[Buffers pH stabilizers]
    H --> H3[Precursors and Inducers]
    H --> H4[Selective agents antibiotics]
```

## 4.2 Process Flow Diagram — Medium Preparation & Sterilization

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    R1[Raw Materials Weighing] --> R2[Dissolution in RO Water]
    R2 --> R3[pH Adjustment with H2SO4 or NaOH]
    R3 --> R4[Filtration Pre-sterile]
    R4 --> R5[Continuous Sterilization HTST 140C 30s]
    R5 --> R6[Cooling to Fermentation Temp]
    R6 --> R7[Inoculation in Bioreactor]
    R7 --> R8[Fermentation]
    R8 --> R9[Harvest]

    R5 -.Batch Alternative.-> R5b[Batch Sterilization 121C 20 min]
    R5b --> R6
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram — Functional Interdependencies in Medium Design

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph NUTRIENTS
        N1[Carbon] --> M1[Energy pathway]
        N2[Nitrogen] --> M2[Protein synthesis]
        N3[Minerals] --> M3[Enzyme cofactors]
        N4[Vitamins] --> M4[Coenzymes]
    end

    subgraph PHYSICAL
        P1[Temperature] --> ENV[Optimal Growth Environment]
        P2[pH] --> ENV
        P3[Dissolved O2] --> ENV
        P4[Water activity] --> ENV
    end

    subgraph PRODUCT
        ENV --> OUT[Biomass]
        ENV --> OUT2[Metabolite]
        ENV --> OUT3[Recombinant Protein]
    end

    M1 --> ENV
    M2 --> ENV
    M3 --> ENV
    M4 --> ENV
```

## 4.4 Decision Matrix — Complex vs Defined Medium Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1[Stage of Process?] --> Q2{Lab-scale optimization}
    Q1 --> Q3{Pilot scale up}
    Q1 --> Q4{Industrial production}

    Q2 --> A1[Use DEFINED medium for kinetic studies]
    Q3 --> A2[Test both defined and complex]
    Q4 --> A3[Use COMPLEX medium for cost efficiency]

    A1 --> R1[Reproducibility high]
    A2 --> R2[Compare yields and costs]
    A3 --> R3[Cheap carbon and nitrogen sources]

    R1 --> F1[Generate kinetic parameters]
    R2 --> F2[Scale-up data]
    R3 --> F3[Industrial yield optimization]
```

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** When asked to *design a medium*, always present it as a **two-tier strategy** — (1) Defined medium for *research* to obtain kinetic data, (2) Complex/industrial medium for *production* to minimize cost.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 1]**

### Question 1 (3 Marks)
**Q: List the essential components of a fermentation medium with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**
The essential components of a fermentation medium are:
1. **Carbon source** — e.g., glucose (energy + biomass building)
2. **Nitrogen source** — e.g., ammonium sulphate (protein synthesis)
3. **Minerals** — e.g., MgSO₄ (cofactor for enzymes)
4. **Vitamins & growth factors** — e.g., biotin (coenzyme)
5. **Water** — distilled/deionized (solvent)
6. **Dissolved oxygen** — for aerobic cultures (respiration)
7. **Buffers & antifoam** — to maintain pH and prevent foaming

> **Marking Scheme:** Listing (1M) + Examples (1M) + Functional role (1M) = 3M

---

### Question 2 (3 Marks)
**Q: What is the significance of the C:N ratio in fermentation medium design? Give the typical range for bacteria.**

**Model Answer:**
The **C:N ratio** is the mass ratio of carbon to nitrogen sources in the medium. It determines the **balance between energy generation and biosynthetic activity**, influencing:
- Microbial growth rate
- Product formation (biomass vs metabolite)
- By-product formation (e.g., high C:N → ethanol, low C:N → high biomass)

**Typical ranges:**
- **Bacteria:** 3:1 to 5:1
- **Yeast:** 5:1 to 10:1
- **Filamentous fungi:** 10:1 to 20:1

> **Marking Scheme:** Definition (1M) + Significance (1M) + Range for bacteria (1M) = 3M

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Question, 2024 Scheme, Module 1, 14 Marks]**

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the various carbon and nitrogen sources used in industrial fermentation media. Compare complex versus defined media with suitable examples.

**Model Answer:**

**Carbon Sources:**
- **Pure sugars:** Glucose, sucrose, fructose — used in defined media for *E. coli*, *S. cerevisiae*
- **Complex polysaccharides:** Starch, maltodextrin — used for filamentous fungi, *Bacillus*
- **Industrial by-products:** Molasses, corn-steep liquor, whey, lignocellulosic hydrolysate — cost-effective
- **Hydrocarbons / oils:** n-Alkanes, vegetable oils — for *Candida*, SCP production
- **Alcohols:** Methanol, ethanol — for *Pichia pastoris*, *Methylophilus*

**Nitrogen Sources:**
- **Inorganic:** $(NH_4)_2SO_4$, $NH_4NO_3$, $NaNO_3$ — used in defined media
- **Organic complex:** Yeast extract, peptone, tryptone, soyabean meal, CSL — supply amino acids, vitamins, peptides

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Complex Medium | Defined (Synthetic) Medium |
|---|---|---|
| Composition | Variable batch-to-batch | Precisely known |
| Cost | Low | High |
| Reproducibility | Low | High |
| Use | Industrial production | Research, kinetic studies |
| Examples | Molasses + CSL for penicillin | M9 + glucose for *E. coli* |
| Downstream processing | Difficult (impurities) | Easier |

> **Marking Scheme:** Carbon sources (2M) + Nitrogen sources (2M) + Comparison table (2M) + Examples (1M) = 7M

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** During penicillin production by *Penicillium chrysogenum*, the initial glucose concentration is 40 g/L and the final is 2 g/L. Initial biomass is 0.5 g/L and final biomass is 8.0 g/L. Penicillin produced is 5 g/L. Calculate:
1. Biomass yield coefficient $Y_{X/S}$
2. Product yield on substrate $Y_{P/S}$
3. Product yield on biomass $Y_{P/X}$

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $S_0 = 40$ g/L, $S = 2$ g/L
- $X_0 = 0.5$ g/L, $X = 8.0$ g/L
- $P = 5$ g/L

**Step 1:** Substrate consumed:
$$\Delta S = S_0 - S = 40 - 2 = 38 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 2:** Biomass produced:
$$\Delta X = X - X_0 = 8.0 - 0.5 = 7.5 \text{ g/L}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate $Y_{X/S}$:
$$Y_{X/S} = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} = \frac{7.5}{38} = 0.197 \text{ g/g} \approx 0.20 \text{ g/g}$$

**Step 4:** Calculate $Y_{P/S}$:
$$Y_{P/S} = \frac{P}{\Delta S} = \frac{5}{38} = 0.132 \text{ g/g}$$

**Step 5:** Calculate $Y_{P/X}$:
$$Y_{P/X} = \frac{P}{\Delta X} = \frac{5}{7.5} = 0.667 \text{ g/g}$$

> **Marking Scheme:** Each yield (2M each) + Correct final values (1M) = 7M

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the role of dissolved oxygen (DO), pH, temperature, and antifoam agents in fermentation. What is $k_L a$ and why is it important?

**Model Answer:**

**Role of DO:** For aerobic cultures, oxygen acts as the **terminal electron acceptor** in the electron transport chain. Its low aqueous solubility (~ **7 mg/L at 30 °C**) makes it a frequent rate-limiting substrate. Below the **critical DO concentration** (typically 10–30 % saturation), growth becomes O₂-limited.

**Role of pH:** Affects enzyme activity, ionization of nutrients, and product stability. Bacteria prefer **pH 6.5–7.5**; yeasts prefer **pH 4.0–5.0**; filamentous fungi prefer **pH 5.0–7.0**. pH is controlled using buffers (phosphate, citrate) or in-situ acid/base addition.

**Role of Temperature:** Affects growth rate, product formation, and substrate utilization. Mesophiles grow best at **30–37 °C**; thermophiles at **50–60 °C**. Most industrial fermentations operate at **30 °C ± 2 °C**.

**Role of Antifoam Agents:** Foaming in bioreactors is caused by proteins, polysaccharides, and vigorous agitation. Excessive foam causes *wet filters, loss of medium, contamination*. Common antifoams: silicone oil (0.01 %), polypropylene glycol, vegetable oils.

**Definition of $k_L a$:** The **volumetric mass-transfer coefficient** (h⁻¹) is a measure of the aeration efficiency of a bioreactor:

$$OTR = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$$

Higher $k_L a$ means better oxygen supply. Typical industrial values: **100–1000 h⁻¹**.

> **Marking Scheme:** DO (1.5M) + pH (1.5M) + Temperature (1.5M) + Antifoam (1.5M) + $k_L a$ definition (1M) = 7M

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** A bioreactor operates with the following data:
- Aeration rate: 1 vvm (volume of air per volume of medium per minute)
- Equilibrium DO = 7 mg/L
- Actual DO in broth = 2 mg/L
- Biomass concentration $X$ = 5 g/L
- Specific oxygen uptake rate $q_{O_2}$ = 4 mmol O₂/g biomass·h
- $k_L a$ = 200 h⁻¹

Calculate: (i) Volumetric OTR; (ii) Verify if oxygen supply meets demand.

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- $C^* = 7$ mg/L, $C_L = 2$ mg/L
- $k_L a = 200$ h⁻¹
- $X = 5$ g/L
- $q_{O_2} = 4$ mmol O₂/g·h

**Step 1: OTR Calculation (mg O₂/L·h):**
$$OTR = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L) = 200 \times (7 - 2) = 1000 \text{ mg O}_2/\text{L·h}$$

**Step 2: Convert to mmol:**
$$OTR = \frac{1000}{32} = 31.25 \text{ mmol O}_2/\text{L·h}$$

**Step 3: Oxygen Demand (OUR) Calculation:**
$$OUR = q_{O_2} \cdot X = 4 \times 5 = 20 \text{ mmol O}_2/\text{L·h}$$

**Step 4: Comparison:**
$$OTR (31.25) > OUR (20)$$

Since OTR exceeds OUR, the **oxygen supply is sufficient** to meet microbial demand, with a **safety margin** of **11.25 mmol/L·h** (~ 36 % excess).

> **Marking Scheme:** OTR formula application (3M) + Conversion (1M) + OUR calculation (2M) + Conclusion (1M) = 7M

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes KTU Students Make (Lose 1–2 Marks Each):**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units** in yield coefficients — always write *g/g* or *g/L·h*.
> 2. **Confusing $C^*$ with $C_L$** in the OTR formula — $C^*$ is the *saturation* value (in equilibrium with air), $C_L$ is the *actual measured* value in broth.
> 3. **Using $X_0$ instead of $\Delta X$** in yield calculations — yields are based on *change* in biomass, not absolute biomass.
> 4. **Not mentioning the role of antifoam** in continuous stirred-tank reactors (CSTR) — examiners consider it a critical industrial parameter.
> 5. **Neglecting precursor requirement** in secondary metabolite production (e.g., phenylacetic acid for penicillin G).
> 6. **Omitting catabolite repression** when discussing high glucose media — a very common KTU ESE question.
> 7. **Skipping the water-purity discussion** — tap water contains chlorine which inhibits *E. coli* growth.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Module 1: Medium Requirements for Fermentation**

- **Seven (7) Universal Medium Components:** C, N, Minerals, Vitamins, Water, DO, Buffers/Antifoam.
- **C:N Ratio:** Bacteria 3:1–5:1; Yeast 5:1–10:1; Fungi 10:1–20:1.
- **Carbon sources:** Glucose (preferred), molasses (cheapest), methanol (for SCP), starch (for fungi).
- **Nitrogen sources:** Inorganic (ammonium salts, urea) + organic (yeast extract, peptone, CSL).
- **Phosphate buffer** doubles as pH controller AND phosphorus source.
- **Water purity:** Distilled/RO water only; tap water contains inhibitory Cl⁻.
- **Critical DO:** 10–30 % saturation; below this, growth becomes O₂-limited.
- **kLa equation:** $OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ — memorize without error.
- **Monod growth equation:** $\mu = \mu_{max} \cdot \dfrac{S}{K_s + S}$ — foundation of all kinetic analysis.
- **Three Yield coefficients:** $Y_{X/S}$, $Y_{P/S}$, $Y_{P/X}$ — know definitions and units (g/g).
- **Glucose > 50 g/L** causes catabolite repression → use **fed-batch** strategy.
- **Antifoam agents:** Silicone oil, polypropylene glycol at 0.01–0.05 % v/v.
- **Sterilization:** 121 °C, 15 psi, 15–20 min (batch) OR 140 °C, 30 s (HTST continuous).
- **Complex medium** = cheap, variable; **Defined medium** = reproducible, expensive.
- **Precursor example:** Phenylacetic acid → Penicillin G benzyl side chain.
- **Inducer example:** IPTG for lac-operon in recombinant *E. coli*.
- **Auxotroph** = mutant requiring external supply of a nutrient (e.g., amino acid, vitamin).
- **Maintenance coefficient** $m$ appears in Herbert–Pirt substrate balance.
- **Methanol-based SCP** (Pruteen, ICI) used *Methylophilus methylotrophus*.
- **Industrial media cost** contributes 30–40 % of total fermentation cost.
- **Medium design is iterative:** defined → kinetic study → complex medium → pilot → industrial.

> **End of Module 1 — Medium Requirements for Fermentation Process**

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Basic Design of the Fermenter
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Basic Design of the Fermenter

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of a Fermenter

A **fermenter** (also called a **bioreactor**) is a fully enclosed, sterile, temperature-controlled vessel designed to provide an optimal, controlled physiological environment for the growth of a specific microorganism, plant, or animal cell culture, in order to carry out a desired biochemical reaction on a defined substrate, yielding a specified product (biomass, metabolite, or recombinant protein) at industrial scale.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a fermenter is a **pressurized process vessel** that integrates the principles of chemical reaction engineering, mass transfer, heat transfer, mechanical mixing, and instrumentation into a single unit. The "Basic Design" of a fermenter refers to the **sizing, proportioning, and specification** of this vessel — i.e., the calculation of its diameter, height, working volume, agitator dimensions, baffle arrangement, sparger design, and selection of construction material — to satisfy a given production demand.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 1**
> The basic design of a fermenter covers: vessel geometry (aspect ratio), working volume, material of construction, mechanical design considerations, and standard proportions of internal fittings (impellers, baffles, spargers). These form the foundation upon which Module 2 (sterilization) and Module 3 (aeration & agitation) are built.

### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy — The "Factory Inside a Pressure Cooker"

Think of a fermenter as a **high-tech, industrial-scale pressure cooker that grows microscopic factories**.

- The **microorganism** is the "factory worker" — it needs a controlled workplace.
- The **nutrient broth (medium)** is the "raw material warehouse" supplied from the top/bottom.
- The **sterile air bubbled in** is the "oxygen supply line" for the workers (for aerobic cultures).
- The **impeller + baffles** are the "HR & Operations Team" — they mix everyone fairly, prevent cliques, and ensure no one is overworked or underfed.
- The **cooling jacket** is the "air conditioning" — it removes the heat generated by the hard-working microbes.
- The **sterile barriers (filters, steam seals)** are the "strict security gates" — no unwanted germs (contaminants) are allowed inside.

Just as a factory's physical layout (room dimensions, conveyor belt positions, lighting, ventilation) must be carefully planned before construction, the fermenter's physical dimensions and internal layout must be **mathematically designed** before fabrication. This is the essence of *Basic Design*.

### 1.3 Standard Design Metrics and Physical Constants

The following **bold metrics** are universal in industrial fermenter design:

- **Aspect Ratio (H/D):** typically **2:1 to 3:1** for stirred-tank reactors.
- **Working Volume Fraction:** **70–80%** of total vessel volume.
- **Impeller-to-Tank Diameter Ratio (Dᵢ/D):** **0.3 to 0.5**.
- **Baffle Width (Wᵦ):** **0.08D to 0.10D**, with **4 baffles** standard.
- **Sparger Ring Diameter:** **0.5D to 0.8D**, placed below lower impeller.
- **Material of Construction:** **Stainless Steel SS-316L** (or SS-304 for low-cost).
- **Standard Design Pressure:** **1.5 to 2.5 bar gauge** for sterilization duty.
- **Design Temperature:** **150 °C** (sterilization), operation at **30–37 °C**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why SS-316L and not SS-304?**
> SS-316L contains **2–3% Molybdenum**, which makes it resistant to **chloride-induced pitting corrosion** — critical because fermentation media often contain chlorides (from salts like KCl, NaCl) and because the vessel undergoes frequent steam sterilization cycles. The "L" denotes **low carbon content (<0.03%)**, preventing carbide precipitation at weld joints.

### 1.4 Classification of Fermenters (Brief Context)

For a complete "Basic Design," the engineer must first know the *type* of fermenter:

| Type | Example | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Stirred-Tank (STR) | CSTR with Rushton impeller | Most industrial (penicillin, enzymes) |
| Airlift | Internal-loop or external-loop | Shear-sensitive cells (plant, animal) |
| Bubble Column | Simple sparged cylinder | Low-viscosity, low-cell-density broths |
| Packed-Bed | Immobilized cells on matrix | Continuous biocatalysis |

The KTU 2024 Module 1 focuses predominantly on the **Stirred-Tank Fermenter (STF)**, which is the workhorse of the bioprocess industry.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Design Philosophy — The "Why" Behind Every Number

A fermenter is not designed arbitrarily. Every geometric ratio exists to satisfy a specific **engineering objective**:

1. **Adequate Oxygen Transfer (kₗa):** Microbes need dissolved O₂. A tall, narrow vessel promotes longer bubble residence time → higher kₗa.
2. **Effective Mixing (low mixing time):** Nutrients, pH, and temperature must be uniform. Aspect ratio ~2:1 ensures no "dead zones."
3. **Heat Removal:** Microbial metabolism generates heat (~5–25 kW/m³). Tall vessels offer more jacket surface area per unit volume for cooling.
4. **Sterility:** Smooth, crevice-free internal surfaces (electropolished SS-316L) + steam-sterilizable dead-leg-free piping.
5. **Mechanical Stability:** Must withstand internal steam pressure (~2 bar) during SIP (Steam-In-Place) and vacuum during discharge.

### 2.2 Step-by-Step Design Logic Flow

**Step 1: Define the Production Target**
- Given: Desired product output (kg/year) and specific productivity (kg product / m³ · h).
- Calculate: **Required working volume V_w** from material balance.

**Step 2: Convert to Total Vessel Volume**
- Add **20–30% headspace** for foam control and disengagement.
- $V_{total} = \dfrac{V_{working}}{0.7 \text{ to } 0.8}$

**Step 3: Apply Aspect Ratio Constraint**
- Choose **H/D = 2.0 to 3.0** (standard industry value).
- This fixes the **vessel diameter D** and **cylindrical height H**.

**Step 4: Size the Impeller**
- Standard: **Dᵢ/D = 1/3**.
- Number of impellers: typically **1 impeller per 1.5 to 2 Dᵢ spacing**; for H/D = 2, **2 Rushton impellers** are common.
- Impeller clearance from bottom: **C = Dᵢ** (i.e., impeller at 1/3 of D from the base).

**Step 5: Design the Baffles**
- **4 baffles**, each of width **Wᵦ = D/10**, mounted with a small gap (**~0.01D**) from the wall to prevent dead zones and avoid structural stress concentrations.

**Step 6: Design the Sparger**
- Ring sparger diameter: **0.5D to 0.8D**, located **0.5Dᵢ to 1.0Dᵢ** below the lower impeller.
- Hole size: **3–6 mm**, drilled on the underside to prevent clogging.

**Step 7: Mechanical Design**
- Treat the cylindrical shell as a **thin-walled pressure vessel** (since D/t > 20, where t is wall thickness).
- Calculate wall thickness using ASME code (discussed in Section 3).
- Add **2:1 elliptical or torispherical heads** (top and bottom).

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are High-Yield for KTU University Exam. Memorize the table, not just the individual equations.**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Typical Value / Unit | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Working Volume | $V_w = \dfrac{P}{Y_{P/S} \cdot \text{volumetric productivity}}$ | m³ | Basis of design |
| 2 | Total Volume | $V_t = \dfrac{V_w}{f}$ where $f = 0.7$ to $0.8$ | m³ | Vessel sizing |
| 3 | Aspect Ratio | $AR = \dfrac{H}{D}$ | 2:1 to 3:1 | Geometry fix |
| 4 | Vessel Diameter | $D = \left( \dfrac{4 V_t}{\pi \cdot H} \right)^{1/3}$ substituting $H = AR \cdot D$ | m | Sizing |
| 5 | Vessel Diameter (solved) | $D = \left( \dfrac{4 V_t}{\pi \cdot AR} \right)^{1/3}$ | m | Direct calculation |
| 6 | Vessel Cylinder Height | $H = AR \cdot D$ | m | Geometry fix |
| 7 | Impeller Diameter | $D_i = 0.33 \cdot D$ | m | Agitation |
| 8 | Number of Impellers | $N_i = \dfrac{H}{D_i \cdot 1.5}$ (round up) | integer | Mixing |
| 9 | Baffle Width | $W_B = 0.10 \cdot D$ | m | Anti-vortex |
| 10 | Baffle Clearance from wall | $c = 0.01 \cdot D$ | m | Dead-zone prevention |
| 11 | Sparger Ring Diameter | $D_s = 0.5D$ to $0.8D$ | m | Gas dispersion |
| 12 | Shell Thickness (thin-wall PV) | $t = \dfrac{P \cdot D}{2 \cdot \sigma \cdot \eta - 1.2 P} + c_{corr}$ | mm | Mechanical design |
| 13 | Pressure Design Value | $P_d = 1.1 \times$ working pressure | bar | Safety factor |
| 14 | Allowable Stress for SS-316L | $\sigma_{all} = 115$ MPa (at 150°C) | MPa | Code design |
| 15 | Total Vessel Height | $H_{total} = H + 2 h_{head}$ | m | Fabrication |
| 16 | Head Height (2:1 elliptical) | $h_{head} = 0.25 D$ | m | Closure |
| 17 | Cooling Jacket Area | $A_j = \pi D H$ | m² | Heat transfer |
| 18 | Height-to-Diameter of Impeller | $W_i = 0.20 D_i$ (Rushton blade height) | m | Standard ratio |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoiding Markdown Table Breakage:** In all formula notations above, fractions and ratios use **LaTeX** (e.g., $\frac{P}{Y_{P/S}}$) and **decimals** (e.g., $0.33 D$). The vertical bar `$\vert$` is **never** used inside table cells — it would corrupt the markdown parser. Always write $0.10 D$ instead of $D/10$ when the table cell could be misread.

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The proportions above are not academic — they are the **de facto industry standard** used by:
- **Sartorius Stedim Biotech** (BIA Separations, single-use bioreactors)
- **Eppendorf / New Brunswick Scientific** (bench-top fermenters)
- **ZETA GmbH** (large-scale pharma bioreactors, up to 100 m³)
- **Godavari Biorefineries** (Indian industrial ethanol fermenters)

Deviating from these ratios invites real engineering disasters: e.g., aspect ratio > 4 leads to **incomplete mixing** and "compartmentalized" broth, while aspect ratio < 1.5 leads to **poor gas utilization** (bubbles escape before transferring O₂).
