## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1: Vessel Diameter from Total Volume and Aspect Ratio

**Problem Statement:** A fermenter must hold a total volume $V_t$ with a prescribed aspect ratio $AR = H/D$. Find the vessel diameter $D$.

**Given:** $V_t$ (m³), $AR$ (dimensionless).

**Step 1:** Write the volume of the cylindrical shell (ignoring heads initially for the working volume):
$$V_{cyl} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 H$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the aspect ratio constraint $H = AR \cdot D$:
$$V_{cyl} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 (AR \cdot D) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot AR \cdot D^3$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $D$ by isolating it:
$$D^3 = \frac{4 \cdot V_{cyl}}{\pi \cdot AR}$$

**Step 4:** Take the cube root:
$$D = \left( \frac{4 \cdot V_{cyl}}{\pi \cdot AR} \right)^{1/3}$$

**Step 5 (Real Engineering Adjustment):** When the heads are included, the total height $H_{total}$ must be used. The standard approach is to first set $V_t = V_{cyl} + V_{heads}$ and solve iteratively. For a 2:1 elliptical head, $V_{head} = 0.131 \cdot D^3$. So:

$$V_t = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot AR \cdot D^3 + 2 (0.131) D^3 = D^3 \left( \frac{\pi \cdot AR}{4} + 0.262 \right)$$

$$D = \left( \frac{V_t}{\dfrac{\pi \cdot AR}{4} + 0.262} \right)^{1/3}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU Exams:** A common 14-mark question asks: *"Size a fermenter for X m³ capacity"* — and full marks are awarded only if the student accounts for the head volumes. Skipping the 0.131 D³ head term costs 2–3 marks.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2: Number of Impellers from Vessel Height

**Problem Statement:** Determine the number of Rushton impellers required for uniform mixing in a vessel of height $H$ and diameter $D_i$.

**Step 1:** Recall the design rule — for a standard Rushton disk turbine, the **axial flow zone of one impeller extends approximately 1.5 $D_i$ above and below the impeller plane**. The total zone of influence is therefore $3 D_i$ per impeller, but for **multi-impeller** mixing we use a **center-to-center spacing of $1.5 D_i$** to avoid flow interaction degradation.

**Step 2:** Express the required number of impeller intervals:
$$N_{intervals} = \frac{H}{1.5 \cdot D_i}$$

**Step 3:** The number of impellers is one more than the number of intervals if we place impellers at the top and bottom:
$$N_i = \frac{H}{1.5 \cdot D_i} + 1$$

**Step 4:** Round **up** to the next integer (you cannot have a fractional impeller!):
$$N_i = \left\lceil \frac{H}{1.5 \cdot D_i} + 1 \right\rceil$$

**Example:** For $D = 2$ m, $D_i = 0.67$ m, $H = 4$ m:
$$N_i = \frac{4}{1.5 \times 0.67} + 1 = \frac{4}{1.005} + 1 = 3.98 + 1 = 4.98 \rightarrow \lceil 4.98 \rceil = 5 \text{ impellers}$$

In practice, this is **excessive** — industry caps impeller number to **2 or 3** for a 2:1 aspect ratio, even if the math says 5. This is because each additional impeller multiplies the **sterile seal complexity** and **shaft length** (causing vibration issues). Thus a **practical rule** is used:

$$N_i = \max \left( 1, \; \text{round}\left( \frac{H}{2 D_i} \right) \right)$$

This gives a **spacing of $2 D_i$** between impellers, which is the modern industry standard (Wang et al., 1979; McFarlane & Nienow).

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3: Shell Wall Thickness (ASME BPVC Code for Thin-Walled Pressure Vessel)

**Problem Statement:** Calculate the wall thickness required for a cylindrical fermenter shell subject to internal pressure during steam sterilization.

**Step 1:** Identify the design pressure. For sterilization at 121°C, saturated steam pressure is **~2 bar gauge**. Apply a **safety factor of 1.1**:
$$P_d = 1.1 \times 2.0 = 2.2 \text{ bar} = 0.22 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Recall the **circumferential (hoop) stress** in a thin-walled cylinder (Laplace's equation):
$$\sigma_{hoop} = \frac{P_d \cdot D}{2 t}$$

**Step 3:** Rearrange to solve for the required thickness, equating hoop stress to the allowable design stress:
$$\sigma_{all} = \frac{P_d \cdot D}{2 t}$$

$$t = \frac{P_d \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{all}}$$

**Step 4:** Apply the ASME **efficiency factor** $\eta$ (accounts for welded joints, typically $\eta = 0.85$ for spot-examined welds) and a **corrosion allowance** $c_{corr}$ (typically 1.5 mm for SS-316L in food/pharma service):

$$t = \frac{P_d \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{all} \cdot \eta} + c_{corr}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute numerical values: $P_d = 0.22$ MPa, $D = 1.5$ m, $\sigma_{all} = 115$ MPa (SS-316L at 150°C), $\eta = 0.85$, $c_{corr} = 1.5$ mm:

$$t = \frac{(0.22)(1500)}{2 \times 115 \times 0.85} + 1.5$$

$$t = \frac{330}{195.5} + 1.5 = 1.688 + 1.5 = 3.19 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 6:** **Round up** to the nearest standard plate thickness — typically **4 mm or 5 mm** for a small fermenter, **8–10 mm** for industrial scale. The final design thickness is therefore:
$$t_{design} = 4 \text{ mm (for D = 1.5 m)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to convert $P_d$ from **bar to MPa** ($1$ bar $= 0.1$ MPa) and the diameter from **m to mm** in the same equation. This single dimensional error will cause a 7-mark loss in KTU 14-mark questions. Always write units beside every number.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — Design Decision Flow

| Design Input | Engineering Constraint | Resulting Geometric Output | KTU Exam Weightage |
|---|---|---|---|
| Annual production $P$ (kg/yr) | Volumetric productivity $Q_p$ (kg/m³·h) | Working volume $V_w$ | High (mandatory in Part B) |
| $V_w$ | Headspace fraction 0.7–0.8 | Total volume $V_t$ | High |
| $V_t$ | Aspect ratio $AR = 2$ | Diameter $D$, Height $H$ | Very High |
| $D$ | Sterilization pressure $P$ | Wall thickness $t$ | High (14-mark derivations) |
| $D$ | $D_i/D = 1/3$ | Impeller diameter $D_i$ | Medium |
| $D, H$ | $N_i = H/(2 D_i)$ | Number of impellers | Medium |
| $D$ | $W_B = D/10$ | Baffle width | Low (1-mark in Part A) |
| $D$ | $D_s = 0.5D$ | Sparger diameter | Low |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Fermenter Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional architecture** of a standard stirred-tank fermenter, showing all major components and their interactions. This is the standard representation expected in KTU Module 1 answers.

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[STERILE AIR INLET] --> B[AIR FILTER 0.2 MICRON]
    B --> C[SPARGER RING BELOW LOWER IMPELLER]
    C --> D[BROTH LIQUID PHASE]
    D --> E[4 RUSHTON IMPELLERS ON SHAFT]
    E --> F[4 BAFFLES WIDTH D BY 10]
    F --> G[VESSEL WALL SS316L]
    G --> H[COOLING JACKET CIRCULATING WATER]
    H --> I[CHILLER UNIT]
    I --> J[TEMPERATURE SENSOR PT100]
    J --> K[PID CONTROLLER]
    K --> I
    D --> L[DO PROBE DISSOLVED OXYGEN]
    L --> M[AIR FLOW CONTROLLER]
    M --> A
    D --> N[PH PROBE]
    N --> O[ACID AND BASE PUMPS]
    O --> D
    D --> P[FOAM SENSOR]
    P --> Q[ANTIFOAM PUMP]
    Q --> D
    D --> R[SAMPLE PORT]
    D --> S[HARVEST OUTLET BOTTOM VALVE]
    S --> T[STORAGE TANK]
    G --> U[STEAM INLET FOR SIP]
    U --> V[VENT CONDENSER]
    V --> W[EXHAUST FILTER]
    W --> X[VENT TO ATMOSPHERE]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Solid arrows indicate **mass flow** (air, broth, steam, product).
- The four probes (J, L, N, P) feed back to controllers (K, M, O, Q), forming **closed-loop control** systems.
- The `D` (Broth) node is the **central reactor volume** — every other component exists to feed, condition, monitor, or empty `D`.

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Vessel Geometry & Standard Proportions

The following block diagram captures the **standard geometric proportions** of an industrial fermenter. Memorize this layout — it appears verbatim in KTU question papers.

```mermaid
graph LR
    HEAD1[2 TO 1 ELLIPTICAL TOP HEAD HEIGHT 0.25 D] --> CYL[CYLINDRICAL SHELL HEIGHT H ASPECT RATIO H BY D EQUALS 2 TO 1]
    CYL --> HEAD2[2 TO 1 ELLIPTICAL BOTTOM HEAD HEIGHT 0.25 D]
    CYL --> BAF1[BAFFLE 1 WIDTH D BY 10]
    CYL --> BAF2[BAFFLE 2 WIDTH D BY 10]
    CYL --> BAF3[BAFFLE 3 WIDTH D BY 10]
    CYL --> BAF4[BAFFLE 4 WIDTH D BY 10]
    CYL --> IMP1[UPPER RUSHTON IMPELLER D BY 3]
    CYL --> IMP2[LOWER RUSHTON IMPELLER D BY 3]
    SPAR[SPARGER RING DIAMETER 0.5 D POSITIONED 0.5 DI BELOW LOWER IMPELLER] --> CYL
```

### 4.3 Fermenter Cross-Section — Engineering Schematic Description

Since Mermaid cannot render true physical engineering drawings (with circular cross-sections, baffles mounted at 90° intervals, and impellers on a vertical shaft), the following **textual engineering schematic** describes a cross-sectional view that you must reproduce by hand in your KTU answer scripts:

```
                        ↑ Vent + Exhaust Filter
   ┌────────────────────┴────────────────────┐
   │         2:1 Elliptical Top Head         │  ← Height = 0.25 D
   │  ┌─────────┐                            │
   │  │  Motor  │  ┌──────┐                  │
   │  │  ┌──────┴──┤Shaft │  Sample Port ──► │  ← Side entry
   │  │  │ Impeller│      │  (R)            │
   │  │  │  (top)  │      │                  │
   │  │  │    ┌────┤      │                  │
   │  │  │    │ B  │      │  Foam Probe ──►  │
   │  │  │    │ a  │      │                  │
   │  │  │    │ f  │  Impeller              │  ← Dᵢ = D/3
   │  │  │    │ f  │  (bottom)              │
   │  │  │    │ l  │      │                  │
   │  │  │    │ e  │      │                  │   Working Volume
   │  │  │    │ 4  │      │                  │   = 70% of V_t
   │  │  │    └────┤      │                  │   = (H·πD²/4)·0.7
   │  │  │         │      │                  │
   │  │  │    ◯◯◯◯◯◯◯◯   │                  │  ← Sparger ring
   │  │  │    (Sparger)   │                  │     D_s = 0.5D
   │  │  └───────────────┘                  │
   │  │        DO Probe  pH Probe           │  ← Lower side entry
   │  │         ↓          ↓                │
   │  │                                      │
   │  │   ╔══════════════════════════════╗   │
   │  │   ║  Cooling Jacket (annular)   ║   │  ← Outer shell
   │  │   ║   ↺ cooling water inlet      ║   │
   │  │   ║   ↻ cooling water outlet     ║   │
   │  │   ╚══════════════════════════════╝   │
   │  └────────────────────────────────────┘
   │         2:1 Elliptical Bottom Head       │  ← Height = 0.25 D
   │              ↓                           │
   │      Harvest Valve (sterile)             │
   └──────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Key dimensions to label in your KTU exam answer:**
1. Total height $H_t = H + 2(0.25 D) = H + 0.5 D$
2. Cylinder height $H = 2 D$ (for AR = 2)
3. Impeller position from base = $D_i$ (= 1/3 D)
4. Sparger position from base = $0.5 D_i$ below lower impeller
5. Baffle width = $D/10$, gap from wall = $0.01 D$

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
*List any three standard geometric ratios used in the basic design of a stirred-tank fermenter. State one engineering justification for each.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark per ratio):**

1. **Aspect ratio (H/D) = 2:1 to 3:1** *(1 mark)*
   *Justification:* Ensures adequate gas-liquid contact time for oxygen transfer (kₗa), provides enough jacket surface for cooling (~5–25 kW/m³ heat load), and prevents compartmentalization.

2. **Impeller-to-tank diameter ratio (Dᵢ/D) = 1:3** *(1 mark)*
   *Justification:* Maximizes power dissipation per unit volume while preventing excessive shear that would damage shear-sensitive cells (e.g., mammalian, plant).

3. **Baffle width (Wᵦ) = D/10 with 4 baffles** *(1 mark)*
   *Justification:* Suppresses vortex formation and solid-body rotation, converting ~80% of impeller power into useful mixing (radial + axial flow) rather than swirling.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often write only the ratio and forget the **justification**. The KTU 2024 marking scheme allocates exactly 1 mark for the ratio and 1 mark for the engineering reason. Stating *what* without *why* loses 50% of the marks.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Why is SS-316L (and not SS-304) the preferred material of construction for industrial fermenters? Mention any two reasons.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Chloride corrosion resistance:** SS-316L contains 2–3% Molybdenum, which resists **pitting and crevice corrosion** in chloride-rich fermentation media (e.g., media containing NaCl, KCl, HCl-based pH adjusters). *(1.5 marks)*

2. **Low-carbon "L" grade prevents sensitization:** During repeated steam sterilization (SIP) cycles at 121–150 °C, standard SS-304 forms **chromium carbide precipitates at grain boundaries**, leading to intergranular corrosion. The low carbon content (<0.03%) of SS-316L prevents this. *(1.5 marks)*

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Note:** As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations, students answer **ONE** of two 14-mark Part-B questions from each module. The full 14-mark structure is **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A pharmaceutical company requires **5,000 kg/year of an antibiotic** produced by an aerobic fermentation. The strain has a **volumetric productivity of 0.5 g/L·h**, the fermenter operates **300 days/year, 20 hours/day**. Determine:
(i) The required working volume of the fermenter.
(ii) The total vessel volume, assuming a working volume fraction of 0.75.

**(b) [7 marks]** For the fermenter in part (a), using an aspect ratio of 2:1, calculate:
(i) The vessel diameter D and the cylindrical height H.
(ii) The number of Rushton impellers required (Dᵢ = D/3, practical spacing 2 Dᵢ).

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A:**

**Part (a)(i) — Working Volume:**

**Step 1:** Total operating hours per year:
$$t_{op} = 300 \text{ days/yr} \times 20 \text{ h/day} = 6000 \text{ h/yr}$$

**Step 2:** Required production rate:
$$P_{rate} = \frac{5000 \text{ kg/yr}}{6000 \text{ h/yr}} = 0.8333 \text{ kg/h} = 833.3 \text{ g/h}$$

**Step 3:** Working volume from productivity definition $Q_p = \dfrac{P_{rate}}{V_w}$:
$$V_w = \frac{P_{rate}}{Q_p} = \frac{833.3 \text{ g/h}}{0.5 \text{ g/(L·h)}} = 1666.7 \text{ L} = 1.667 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Award 3 marks: 1 mark for hours calc, 1 mark for rate, 1 mark for V_w.**

**Part (a)(ii) — Total Vessel Volume:**

$$V_t = \frac{V_w}{f} = \frac{1.667}{0.75} = 2.222 \text{ m}^3$$

> **Award 2 marks: 1 mark formula, 1 mark answer with units. Total Part (a) = 5 marks shown — full 7-marks allocation is 1 mark setup, 4 marks calculation, 2 marks final answer with units.**

*Full 7-mark breakdown for (a):* 2 marks (operating hours), 2 marks (working volume), 2 marks (total volume), 1 mark (units and significant figures).

**Part (b)(i) — Vessel Diameter and Height:**

**Step 1:** Apply formula derived in Section 3.1, including the 2:1 elliptical head correction (0.131 D³ per head):
$$V_t = D^3 \left( \frac{\pi \cdot AR}{4} + 0.262 \right)$$

**Step 2:** Substitute $AR = 2$ and $V_t = 2.222$ m³:
$$2.222 = D^3 \left( \frac{\pi \times 2}{4} + 0.262 \right) = D^3 (1.5708 + 0.262) = D^3 \times 1.8328$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $D^3$:
$$D^3 = \frac{2.222}{1.8328} = 1.2123 \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 4:** Cube root:
$$D = (1.2123)^{1/3} = 1.066 \text{ m} \approx 1.07 \text{ m}$$

**Step 5:** Cylindrical height:
$$H = AR \cdot D = 2 \times 1.07 = 2.14 \text{ m}$$

> **Award 3.5 marks: 1 mark formula, 1 mark substitution, 0.5 mark solving, 1 mark D, 1 mark H.**

**Part (b)(ii) — Number of Impellers:**

**Step 1:** Impeller diameter:
$$D_i = \frac{D}{3} = \frac{1.07}{3} = 0.357 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** Number of impellers using practical rule (spacing = 2 Dᵢ):
$$N_i = \text{round}\left( \frac{H}{2 D_i} \right) = \text{round}\left( \frac{2.14}{2 \times 0.357} \right) = \text{round}(2.998) = 3$$

> **Award 3.5 marks: 1 mark Dᵢ, 1.5 marks formula substitution, 1 mark final answer. Total Part (b) = 7 marks. Grand Total = 14 marks.**

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative**
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Question]**

**(a) [7 marks]** Describe with a neat sketch the **basic design features of a typical stirred-tank fermenter**. Clearly label: vessel aspect ratio, baffles, sparger, impeller, cooling jacket, and the materials of construction.

**(b) [7 marks]** A fermenter of diameter **1.5 m** is to be designed for sterilization at **2.0 bar gauge** pressure. Using **SS-316L** (allowable stress = 115 MPa), **weld joint efficiency 0.85**, and a **corrosion allowance of 1.5 mm**, calculate the **minimum shell wall thickness** required.

---

#### **Model Solution to Question B:**

**Part (a) — Descriptive + Sketch (7 marks):**

*Suggested answer structure:*

| Component | Description | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| Vessel geometry | Cylindrical with 2:1 elliptical heads, H/D = 2 | 1 |
| Material | SS-316L, electropolished internal surface (Ra < 0.4 μm) | 1 |
| Baffles | 4 baffles of width D/10, 0.01D wall clearance | 1 |
| Sparger | Ring sparger 0.5D diameter, 3–6 mm holes, below lower impeller | 1 |
| Impellers | 2 Rushton disk turbines, Dᵢ = D/3, on central shaft | 1 |
| Cooling jacket | Welded outer jacket, circulating chilled water | 1 |
| Labeled sketch | Clear, correct, all 6 features identified | 1 |

**Part (b) — Wall Thickness Calculation:**

**Step 1:** Design pressure (apply 1.1 safety factor):
$$P_d = 1.1 \times 2.0 \text{ bar} = 2.2 \text{ bar} = 0.22 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 2:** Apply ASME thin-wall vessel formula (from Section 3.3):
$$t = \frac{P_d \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{all} \cdot \eta} + c_{corr}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute all values (with diameter converted to mm: D = 1500 mm; pressure already in MPa, so the product P·D is in N/mm² = MPa·mm):
$$t = \frac{(0.22)(1500)}{2 \times 115 \times 0.85} + 1.5$$

**Step 4:** Numerator:
$$(0.22)(1500) = 330$$

**Step 5:** Denominator:
$$2 \times 115 \times 0.85 = 195.5$$

**Step 6:** Divide:
$$\frac{330}{195.5} = 1.688 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 7:** Add corrosion allowance:
$$t = 1.688 + 1.5 = 3.188 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 8:** **Round up** to nearest standard plate gauge:
$$t_{design} = 4 \text{ mm (standard SS plate)}$$

> **Award 7 marks: 1 mark P_d, 1 mark formula, 1.5 marks substitution, 1 mark numerical, 1 mark addition of corrosion allowance, 0.5 mark rounding up, 1 mark final answer with units.**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls in Wall Thickness Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor (1.1×):** Loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Unit mismatch (bar vs. MPa, m vs. mm):** Loses 2 marks — the most common error.
> 3. **Omitting the corrosion allowance:** Loses 1 mark.
> 4. **Not rounding up to standard plate thickness:** Loses 1 mark.
> 5. **Forgetting the weld joint efficiency η:** Loses 1 mark.
> 
> These five errors account for **~6 of the 7 marks** lost in typical weak answers. Memorize the formula *and* the standard correction factors.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Last-Minute KTU Exam Revision Checklist for "Basic Design of the Fermenter":**

- ✅ **Aspect ratio (H/D) = 2:1 to 3:1** is the *single most important* design ratio. Always quote it first.
- ✅ **Working volume = 70–80%** of total volume. The remaining 20–30% is headspace for foam + aeration disengagement.
- ✅ **Vessel diameter formula (with heads):** $D = \left( \dfrac{V_t}{\dfrac{\pi \cdot AR}{4} + 0.262} \right)^{1/3}$
- ✅ **Vessel diameter formula (without heads, simple):** $D = \left( \dfrac{4 V_t}{\pi \cdot AR} \right)^{1/3}$
- ✅ **Dᵢ = D/3**, **Wᵦ = D/10**, **Dₛ = 0.5D to 0.8D** — these three ratios are KTU favorites.
- ✅ **4 baffles, 4 Rushton impellers** typical, with 1.5–2 Dᵢ spacing.
- ✅ **SS-316L** is mandatory for pharma/food grade. The "L" = low carbon.
- ✅ **Wall thickness formula** (ASME thin-wall, internal pressure): $t = \dfrac{P_d \cdot D}{2 \sigma_{all} \cdot \eta} + c_{corr}$
- ✅ **Design pressure** = 1.1 × working pressure. **Working pressure** for SIP = 2 bar gauge (saturated steam at 121°C).
- ✅ **Cooling jacket** is mandatory because microbial heat = 5–25 kW/m³.
- ✅ **Total vessel height** $H_{total} = H + 0.5 D$ (for two 2:1 elliptical heads of 0.25 D each).
- ✅ **Volume of one 2:1 elliptical head** = 0.131 D³ — used in total volume calculation.
- ✅ **Standard design temperature** for mechanical design = 150°C (worst-case SIP).
- ✅ **Internal finish** = electropolished to Ra < 0.4 μm for cleanability and sterility.
- ✅ **Sample port** must be **steam-blocked** (dead-leg-free) to prevent contamination.

**Master the 18-row formula table in Section 2.3 and the two derivations in Section 3 — these are the highest-weightage items in the KTU 2024 ESE for this topic.**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Industrial Processes for the Manufacture of the Following Products: Organic Acids - Citric Acid, Lactic Acid, Itaconic Acid and Acetic Acid; Production of Amino Acids - Commercially Important Amino Acids

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Alcohols: Ethanol, Acetone

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Bioethanol — The Renewable Fuel Alcohol

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Bioethanol (Ethyl alcohol, **C₂H₅OH**, CAS 64-17-5) is a primary monohydric alcohol of biogenic origin produced predominantly by the anaerobic fermentation of hexose sugars (mainly glucose, sucrose, and fructose) using *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* yeast or, alternatively, by the Zymomonas mobilis bacterial pathway. It is the most abundantly produced biochemical in the world by fermentation volume.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Bioprocess Engineering, ethanol is treated as the **canonical model product** for studying fermentation kinetics, yield stoichiometry, downstream distillation, and substrate-to-product mass balance problems.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Think of ethanol production like **bread-making on a micro-scale in a sealed bottle**. When yeast eats sugar (in bread dough it produces CO₂ for rising; in a sealed fermenter, with no oxygen, the same yeast converts that sugar into alcohol instead of CO₂ for leavening). The "trick" is simply that **no oxygen + lots of sugar + the right yeast = wine/beer that can be distilled into spirit**. In industry, the same principle is scaled up to millions of litres — the *fermenter* replaces the bottle, and the *distillation column* replaces your kitchen pot.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall):**
> - Molecular weight of ethanol: **46.07 g/mol**
> - Density at 20 °C: **0.789 g/cm³**
> - Boiling point: **78.37 °C**
> - Flash point: **16.6 °C** (highly flammable)
> - Net calorific value: **29.7 MJ/kg** (≈ 70 % of gasoline)
> - Miscibility: **Fully miscible with water** in all proportions

---

## 1.2 Acetone — The Solvent Ketone from Fermentation

**Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
Acetone (Propan-2-one, dimethyl ketone, **(CH₃)₂CO**, CAS 67-64-1) is the simplest and most industrially significant saturated ketone. In the bioprocess context, it is the principal low-boiling solvent product of the classical **Acetone–Butanol–Ethanol (ABE) fermentation** carried out by solventogenic clostridia, principally *Clostridium acetobutylicum* and *Clostridium beijerinckii*.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Acetone is rarely produced by fermentation in modern industry (it is now made almost exclusively by the cumene process from petrochemical feedstocks). However, the **ABE process is still examinable in KTU papers** as a historical / second-generation bioprocess illustrating biphasic (acidogenic → solventogenic) fermentation metabolism.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:**
Imagine a bacterium that is **"bilingual" in its chemistry**. Early in its life, when conditions are acidic and the pH drops, it speaks "acid-language" — making acetic acid and butyric acid. But when the acids reach a critical level, the bacterium **switches languages** and starts making neutral solvents — acetone and butanol — to detoxify its own environment. This switch is called the **"acid crash → solventogenesis"** metabolic shift, and it is the heart of the ABE process.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded for Recall):**
> - Molecular weight of acetone: **58.08 g/mol**
> - Density at 20 °C: **0.791 g/cm³**
> - Boiling point: **56.05 °C** (lower than ethanol → easier to strip)
> - Flash point: **−20 °C** (extremely flammable)
> - ABE typical mass ratio: **Acetone : Butanol : Ethanol = 3 : 6 : 1**

---

## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Bioprocess Engineering

Ethanol and acetone are the two textbook examples used in KTU Module 2 to introduce students to:

1. **Stoichiometric black-box yield calculations** (the Gay-Lussac equation).
2. **Real vs. theoretical yield concepts** (% theoretical yield, conversion, productivity).
3. **Product inhibition kinetics** (ethanol is a strong end-product inhibitor above ~ 100 g/L).
4. **Two-stage bioprocesses** (ABE fermentation = acidogenic + solventogenic stages).
5. **Downstream processing choices** (distillation for ethanol; distillation + decantation for butanol-water azeotrope in ABE).

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield KTU Tip:** Almost every KTU question on this module tests your ability to compute **% theoretical ethanol yield** from a given glucose feed. Memorize the Gay-Lussac equation and the conversion factor **0.511 g ethanol / g glucose**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Ethanol — Production Microbiology & Biochemistry

### 2.1.1 The Gay-Lussac Equation (Yeast Pathway, EMP → Pyruvate → Ethanol)

The overall stoichiometry for the conversion of **D-glucose to ethanol and CO₂** by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* under anaerobic conditions is:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{CO}_2
$$

**Mechanistic Logic (in plain steps):**
- **Step 1 — Glycolysis (EMP pathway):** 1 mole of glucose is split into 2 moles of pyruvate (CH₃COCOO⁻), with a net gain of 2 ATP and 2 NADH.
- **Step 2 — Pyruvate decarboxylase:** Pyruvate is decarboxylated to acetaldehyde, releasing CO₂.
- **Step 3 — Alcohol dehydrogenase:** Acetaldehyde is reduced by NADH to ethanol, regenerating NAD⁺ so that glycolysis can continue.

### 2.1.2 The Bacterial Variant — Zymomonas mobilis (Entner–Doudoroff Pathway)

The Gram-negative bacterium *Zymomonas mobilis* ferments glucose via the **Entner–Doudoroff (ED) pathway** instead of EMP:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{CO}_2
$$

**The net stoichiometry is identical** to yeast, but only **1 ATP per glucose** is produced (vs. 2 ATP for yeast). This makes *Z. mobilis* faster-growing but energetically less efficient.

### 2.1.3 Why Ethanol is a "Model Inhibitor"

Ethanol partitions into the cell membrane lipid bilayer, increasing membrane fluidity and disrupting membrane-bound transport proteins. As a result:
- Cell growth ceases at roughly **100–120 g/L ethanol**.
- Fermentation productivity drops sharply above **60–80 g/L**.
- This is why industrial ethanol fermenters operate with continuous **in-situ product removal** (e.g., vacuum fermentation, pervaporation, or stripping).

---

## 2.2 Acetone — The ABE (Acetone–Butanol–Ethanol) Process

### 2.2.1 The Weizmann Process (Historical, 1910s)

Chaim Weizmann (later the first President of Israel) isolated *Clostridium acetobutylicum* and demonstrated the production of acetone from starch during World War I — acetone was needed for cordite production. This is historically the **first large-scale industrial fermentation of a solvent** and earns a guaranteed mention in KTU essays.

### 2.2.2 Biphasic Metabolism of Clostridia

The clostridial life cycle in ABE fermentation has **two distinct physiological phases**:

| Phase | Timing | Dominant pH | Acid-Forming Enzymes | Solvent-Forming Enzymes | Major Products |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Acidogenic** | 0 – ~20 h | 5.5 → 4.5 | Ack, Ptb-Buk | Inactive | Acetic acid, Butyric acid |
| **Solventogenic** | ~20 h onward | 4.5 → rises to 5.0+ | Repressed | CoA-transferase, AADC, AdhE | Acetone, Butanol, Ethanol |

**Why the switch?**
The solventogenic switch is triggered when undissociated acid concentration (acetic acid + butyric acid) exceeds a critical threshold. The cell re-routes acetyl-CoA and butyryl-CoA to **neutral solvents** (acetone and butanol) to detoxify the intracellular environment.

### 2.2.3 The Overall ABE Stoichiometry (Simplified)

A commonly cited lumped stoichiometry for the ABE fermentation of glucose by *C. acetobutylicum* is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
&\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 0.30\,\text{CH}_3\text{COCH}_3 \;+\; 0.60\,\text{C}_4\text{H}_9\text{OH} \;+\; 0.10\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \\
&+\; 2.20\,\text{CO}_2 \;+\; 0.55\,\text{H}_2\text{O}
\end{aligned}
$$

The numbers correspond to the **3 : 6 : 1 mass ratio** of A : B : E and reflect the actual yields reported by industrial fermentations.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All formulas below are routinely asked in KTU University Examinations under OEFTT726.**

| # | Formula / Definition | Mathematical Form | Units / Typical Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Gay-Lussac Stoichiometry** (glucose → ethanol) | $\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} + 2\,\text{CO}_2$ | Dimensionless ratio |
| 2 | **Theoretical Ethanol Yield (mass basis)** | $Y_{EtOH/S}^{\,th} = \dfrac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16}$ | $\mathbf{0.511}\ \text{g EtOH / g glucose}$ |
| 3 | **% Theoretical Yield** | $\%\text{Yield} = \dfrac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{theoretical}} \times 100$ | Typically **90 – 95 %** (industrial) |
| 4 | **Volumetric Productivity** | $Q_p = \dfrac{P_f - P_i}{t}$ | $\text{g EtOH / (L·h)}$ |
| 5 | **Specific Productivity (rate)** | $q_p = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dP}{dt}$ | $\text{g product / (g cells·h)}$ |
| 6 | **Product Yield on Biomass** | $Y_{P/X} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta X}$ | g/g |
| 7 | **Substrate Conversion** | $X_S = \dfrac{S_0 - S_f}{S_0} \times 100\,\%$ | Dimensionless, % |
| 8 | **Productivity (final)** | $P_{vol} = \dfrac{[\text{Ethanol}]_{final}}{t_{ferm}}$ | g/(L·h) |
| 9 | **Energy Content of Ethanol** | $E = m \times 29.7\ \text{MJ/kg}$ | Joules |
| 10 | **ABE Mass Ratio (typical)** | $A : B : E = 3 : 6 : 1$ | Constant, empirical |
| 11 | **Maximum Theoretical Butanol Yield** | $Y_{BuOH/S}^{th} = \dfrac{1 \times 74.12}{180.16} \times \dfrac{1}{0.667}$ | $\approx 0.41\ \text{g/g}$ |
| 12 | **Pervaporation Permeate Flux** | $J = \dfrac{P}{A\cdot t}$ | kg/(m²·h) |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX Note for Exam Sheets:** Use `\vert` instead of the vertical bar `|` when writing absolute values, e.g. write $P_i - P_f \vert$ as $\vert P_i - P_f \vert$ — broken pipes will destroy your markdown table in computer-based valuation.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Ethanol** is the cornerstone of the global biofuel economy. India's **Ethanol Blending Programme (EBP)** targets **20 % blending (E20)** by 2025–26, demanding ~ 10 billion litres of bioethanol/year.
- **Acetone** from the cumene process is used to make **bisphenol-A → polycarbonates**, **methyl methacrylate → acrylics**, and **epoxy resins**.
- Both products illustrate that **"bioprocess ≠ viable at scale"** — the ABE process is a textbook case of a bioprocess displaced by cheaper petrochemistry, retained in the syllabus for pedagogical value.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Theoretical Ethanol Yield from Glucose

**Problem Statement (typical KTU-style):**
A fermentation broth contains 200 g/L glucose initially. Using the Gay-Lussac equation, derive the **maximum theoretical concentration of ethanol** achievable (in g/L) if 100 % of the glucose is converted.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

Starting with the balanced equation:
$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{CO}_2
$$

**Step 1 — Identify the stoichiometric ratio.**

From the equation, 1 mole of glucose yields 2 moles of ethanol.
$$
n_{EtOH} = 2 \times n_{glucose}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert moles to mass using molecular weights.**

- Molecular weight of glucose ($M_{glc}$): $6(12) + 12(1) + 6(16) = 180.16\ \text{g/mol}$
- Molecular weight of ethanol ($M_{EtOH}$): $2(12) + 6(1) + 16 = 46.07\ \text{g/mol}$

Therefore:
$$
m_{EtOH} = n_{EtOH} \times M_{EtOH} = (2 \times n_{glc}) \times 46.07
$$

**Step 3 — Express in terms of glucose mass.**

$$
m_{EtOH} = 2 \times \left(\dfrac{m_{glc}}{180.16}\right) \times 46.07
$$

**Step 4 — Compute the mass-to-mass yield coefficient.**

$$
Y^{\,th}_{EtOH/glc} = \dfrac{m_{EtOH}}{m_{glc}} = \dfrac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16}
$$

$$
Y^{\,th}_{EtOH/glc} = \dfrac{92.14}{180.16} = 0.5114\ \text{g EtOH per g glucose}
$$

**Step 5 — Apply to the given 200 g/L feed.**

$$
[\text{EtOH}]_{max} = 0.5114 \times 200\ \text{g/L} = 102.28\ \text{g/L}
$$

**Step 6 — Convert g/L to % v/v (for completeness, since the answer is usually asked in proof strength).**

Density of ethanol $\rho_{EtOH} = 0.789\ \text{g/cm}^3$, so:
$$
\text{Volume fraction} = \dfrac{102.28 / 0.789}{1000} = 0.1296 \;=\; 12.96\ \%\ \text{v/v}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answer:** The maximum theoretical ethanol concentration is **102.28 g/L (≈ 13.0 % v/v)**, also known as the **"Gay-Lussac ceiling"** — a hard physical upper bound that no yeast or bacterium can ever exceed.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — % Theoretical Yield, Conversion, and Productivity in a Real Fermentation

**Problem Statement:**
A 10 m³ batch fermenter is charged with a medium containing 180 g/L glucose. After 24 hours, the residual glucose is 18 g/L, and the ethanol concentration is 78 g/L. Compute:
(a) % substrate conversion, (b) % theoretical ethanol yield, (c) overall volumetric productivity, (d) mass of ethanol produced per batch (in kg).

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step (a) — % Substrate Conversion ($X_S$)**

$$
X_S = \dfrac{S_0 - S_f}{S_0} \times 100 = \dfrac{180 - 18}{180} \times 100
$$

$$
X_S = \dfrac{162}{180} \times 100 = 90.0\,\%
$$

**Step (b) — % Theoretical Ethanol Yield**

The actual yield per gram of substrate consumed:
$$
Y_{actual} = \dfrac{P_f - P_i}{S_0 - S_f} = \dfrac{78 - 0}{180 - 18} = \dfrac{78}{162} = 0.4815\ \text{g/g}
$$

Compare with the theoretical ceiling:
$$
\%\,Y = \dfrac{0.4815}{0.5114} \times 100 = 94.15\,\%
$$

**Step (c) — Volumetric Productivity ($Q_p$)**

Working volume $V = 10\ \text{m}^3 = 10{,}000\ \text{L}$:
$$
Q_p = \dfrac{P_f - P_i}{t} = \dfrac{78\ \text{g/L} - 0}{24\ \text{h}} = 3.25\ \text{g/(L·h)}
$$

**Step (d) — Mass of Ethanol per Batch**

$$
m_{EtOH} = P_f \times V = 78\ \text{g/L} \times 10{,}000\ \text{L} = 780{,}000\ \text{g} = 780\ \text{kg}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** (a) **90.0 %**, (b) **94.15 % theoretical**, (c) **3.25 g/(L·h)**, (d) **780 kg/batch**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Theoretical Butanol and Acetone Yields in ABE Fermentation

**Problem Statement:**
A 50 m³ ABE fermenter starts with 60 g/L glucose. Using the lumped ABE stoichiometry:
$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 0.30\,A + 0.60\,B + 0.10\,E + 2.20\,CO_2 + 0.55\,H_2O
$$
compute the theoretical concentrations of acetone, butanol, ethanol, and the total ABE mass.

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

**Step 1 — Theoretical mass of each product per mole of glucose consumed.**

Molecular weights:
- Glucose $M = 180.16\ \text{g/mol}$
- Acetone $M = 58.08\ \text{g/mol}$
- Butanol $M = 74.12\ \text{g/mol}$
- Ethanol $M = 46.07\ \text{g/mol}$

Mass of acetone per mole glucose:
$$
m_A = 0.30 \times 58.08 = 17.424\ \text{g}
$$

Mass of butanol per mole glucose:
$$
m_B = 0.60 \times 74.12 = 44.472\ \text{g}
$$

Mass of ethanol per mole glucose:
$$
m_E = 0.10 \times 46.07 = 4.607\ \text{g}
$$

Total ABE mass per mole glucose:
$$
m_{ABE} = 17.424 + 44.472 + 4.607 = 66.503\ \text{g/mol glucose}
$$

**Step 2 — Convert to per-gram-glucose yield coefficients.**

$$
Y^{\,th}_{A/glc} = \dfrac{17.424}{180.16} = 0.0967\ \text{g/g}
$$

$$
Y^{\,th}_{B/glc} = \dfrac{44.472}{180.16} = 0.2469\ \text{g/g}
$$

$$
Y^{\,th}_{E/glc} = \dfrac{4.607}{180.16} = 0.0256\ \text{g/g}
$$

**Step 3 — Apply to the 60 g/L feed (100 % conversion assumed).**

$$
[\text{Acetone}]_{th} = 0.0967 \times 60 = 5.80\ \text{g/L}
$$

$$
[\text{Butanol}]_{th} = 0.2469 \times 60 = 14.81\ \text{g/L}
$$

$$
[\text{Ethanol}]_{th} = 0.0256 \times 60 = 1.54\ \text{g/L}
$$

**Step 4 — Total ABE concentration.**

$$
[\text{ABE}]_{th} = 5.80 + 14.81 + 1.54 = 22.15\ \text{g/L}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Answers:** Acetone **5.80 g/L**, Butanol **14.81 g/L**, Ethanol **1.54 g/L**, Total ABE **22.15 g/L**. Industrial ABE broths rarely exceed 20–25 g/L total solvents due to butanol toxicity — this is the central process bottleneck.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Ethanol vs. Acetone Fermentation

| Parameter | **Ethanol Fermentation** | **ABE (Acetone) Fermentation** |
|---|---|---|
| Microbe | *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* / *Zymomonas mobilis* | *Clostridium acetobutylicum* / *C. beijerinckii* |
| Pathway | EMP (yeast) or ED (Zymomonas) | EMP → Acetyl-CoA → solventogenesis |
| Best substrate | Glucose, sucrose, fructose | Starch, molasses, lignocellulose hydrolysate |
| O₂ requirement | Strictly anaerobic (facultative anaerobe) | Strictly anaerobic (obligate anaerobe) |
| Operating pH | 4.0 – 5.0 (acidic) | 4.5 – 5.5 (acidogenic → solventogenic) |
| Optimum temperature | 28 – 35 °C (mesophilic) | 30 – 37 °C (mesophilic) |
| Toxic product | Ethanol (limit ≈ 100–120 g/L) | Butanol (limit ≈ 13–15 g/L) |
| Theoretical yield | **0.511 g/g glucose** | A: 0.097, B: 0.247, E: 0.026 g/g glucose |
| Maximum titre | 8 – 12 % v/v industrial | 18 – 25 g/L total ABE |
| Typical productivity | 2 – 3 g/(L·h) | 0.3 – 0.6 g/(L·h) |
| Primary recovery | Distillation (ethanol–water azeotrope) | Distillation + decantation (butanol–water) |
| Industrial status | **Dominant** (E10, E20 fuels) | Largely displaced by petrochemistry |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Process Flow — Ethanol Fermentation (Block-Level Architecture)

The following Mermaid diagram maps the **functional flow** of an industrial bioethanol plant, from raw substrate to fuel-grade ethanol. Each block represents a unit operation rather than a piece of physical equipment, making the diagram robust against mermaid rendering issues.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Raw Feedstock Sugarcane Molasses Corn Starch] --> B[Feed Preparation Milling Dilution Acid Hydrolysis]
    B --> C[Heat Sterilization 121 C for 20 min]
    C --> D[Cooling to 30 C]
    D --> E[Inoculum Preparation Yeast Seed Train]
    E --> F[Main Fermenter 30 C pH 4.5 to 5.0 48 to 72 h Anaerobic]
    F --> G[Beer Well CO2 Vent Stripping]
    G --> H[Centrifugation Yeast Cell Recycle]
    H --> I[Beer Stripping Column Removes Dissolved CO2]
    I --> J[Distillation Column 1 Beer Column 95 percent v v Ethanol]
    J --> K[Distillation Column 2 Rectification 96 to 96.5 percent v v]
    K --> L[Molecular Sieve Dehydration 99.5 to 99.9 percent v v Anhydrous]
    L --> M[Denaturant Addition for Fuel Ethanol]
    M --> N[Finished Fuel Grade Ethanol Storage]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style F fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:3px
    style L fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px
    style N fill:#f1948a,stroke:#922b21,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **green block (Main Fermenter)** is the biochemical heart. The **blue block (Molecular Sieve)** is the engineering heart — the molecular sieves (typically zeolite 3Å) adsorb the last 4–5 % water from the azeotrope, achieving anhydrous ethanol above the azeotropic limit (95.6 % v/v).

---

## 4.2 Process Flow — ABE Fermentation with Biphasic Metabolism Highlight

The diagram below illustrates the **two metabolic phases** of *Clostridium* and how they drive process design.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Spore Inoculum Heat Shock Activation] --> B[Stage 1 Acidogenic Phase 0 to 20 h pH 5.5 to 4.5]
    B --> B1[Acetic Acid Formed via Acetate Kinase]
    B --> B2[Butyric Acid Formed via Ptb Buk Pathway]
    B --> C{pH Threshold Below 4.8 Undissociated Acid Above Critical Level}
    C --> D[Stage 2 Solventogenic Phase 20 to 72 h pH rises to 5.0]
    D --> D1[Acetone via Acetoacetate Decarboxylase AADC]
    D --> D2[Butanol via Butyraldehyde Dehydrogenase AdhE]
    D --> D3[Ethanol via Alcohol Dehydrogenase]
    D --> E[Mature Beer 18 to 25 g per L Total ABE]
    E --> F[Cell Separation Centrifuge]
    F --> G[Distillation Train Beer Column Acetone Column Butanol Column]
    G --> H[Decanter Breaks Butanol Water Azeotrope]
    H --> I[Finished Solvent Storage Acetone Butanol Ethanol]

    style A fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style B fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281a,stroke-width:3px
    style C fill:#f7dc6f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:3px
    style D fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:3px
    style E fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px
    style I fill:#d2b4de,stroke:#4a235a,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The **yellow decision block (pH threshold)** is the metabolic "gear shift" of clostridia. Operators exploit this by **controlling pH** — keeping pH above 5.0 artificially maintains the cell in the acidogenic phase and *suppresses* solvent production, while letting pH drop below 4.8 *triggers* solventogenesis.

---

## 4.3 Biochemical Pathway Map — Glucose to Ethanol / Acetone / Butanol

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    G[Glucose C6H12O6] -->|Glycolysis EMP| PYR[Pyruvate CH3COCOO minus]
    PYR -->|Pyruvate Decarboxylase| ACAL[Acetaldehyde CH3CHO]
    ACAL -->|Alcohol Dehydrogenase NADH| E[Ethanol C2H5OH]

    PYR -->|Pyruvate Ferredoxin Oxidoreductase| ACCOA[Acetyl CoA]
    ACCOA -->|Thiolase| AACoA[Acetoacetyl CoA]
    AACoA -->|CoA Transferase| AA[Acetoacetate]
    AA -->|AADC| ACE[Acetone CH3COCH3]

    ACCOA -->|3 steps via Ptb Buk| BUCOA[Butyryl CoA]
    BUCOA -->|Aldehyde Dehydrogenase| BALD[Butyraldehyde]
    BALD -->|Alcohol Dehydrogenase| BUOH[Butanol C4H9OH]

    ACCOA -->|Phosphate Acetyltransferase| ACE2[Acetate]
    BUCOA -->|Phosphate Butyryltransferase| BUAC[Butyrate]

    style G fill:#f9e79f,stroke:#7d6608,stroke-width:2px
    style ACE fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:2px
    style BUOH fill:#aed6f1,stroke:#1b4f72,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#d2b4de,stroke:#4a235a,stroke-width:2px
```

> [!TIP]
> **Note for Mermaid Safety:** Every node ID above is alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (G, PYR, ACAL, etc.). No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node identifiers. All labels are plain uppercase text — no bold, italics, or HTML tables inside the quoted labels.

---

## 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Why Ethanol Wins at Scale

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R1[Substrate Cost] --> E1{Ethanol Process}
    R2[Capital Cost] --> E1
    R3[Energy Cost] --> E1
    R4[Toxicity of Product] --> E1

    R1 --> E2{ABE Process}
    R2 --> E2
    R3 --> E2
    R4 --> E2

    E1 --> O1[Low Capex Mature Technology]
    E1 --> O2[Single Product Stream Simple DSP]
    E1 --> O3[High Titre 10 percent v v]
    E1 --> O4[Strong Market E20 Mandate]

    E2 --> P1[High Capex Anaerobic Sterile]
    E2 --> P2[Three Product Streams Complex DSP]
    E2 --> P3[Low Titre 2 percent w v]
    E2 --> P4[Butanol Toxic at 13 g per L]

    O1 --> W[Ethanol Wins at Scale]
    O2 --> W
    O3 --> W
    O4 --> W

    P1 --> L[ABE Loses to Petro Cumene Process]
    P2 --> L
    P3 --> L
    P4 --> L

    style W fill:#a9dfbf,stroke:#196f3d,stroke-width:3px
    style L fill:#f5b7b1,stroke:#78281a,stroke-width:3px
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Model Paper]
**Write the Gay-Lussac equation for ethanol fermentation and state the theoretical mass yield of ethanol from glucose.**

**Model Answer:**

The Gay-Lussac equation representing the anaerobic conversion of D-glucose to ethanol and carbon dioxide is:

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{CO}_2
$$

The theoretical mass yield is computed as:

$$
Y^{\,th}_{EtOH/glc} = \dfrac{2 \times 46.07}{180.16} = 0.511\ \text{g ethanol per g glucose}
$$

This corresponds to a maximum achievable ethanol concentration of **~ 102.3 g/L** from a 200 g/L glucose feed, or equivalently **~ 13.0 % v/v** in the beer — the **Gay-Lussac ceiling**.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]
**What is the ABE fermentation? Name the microorganism and the typical mass ratio of products.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Acetone–Butanol–Ethanol (ABE) fermentation** is a strictly anaerobic, biphasic bioprocess in which a solventogenic clostridium converts a carbohydrate substrate (typically glucose, starch, or molasses) into a mixture of three neutral solvents — acetone, butanol, and ethanol.

The principal industrial microorganism is ***Clostridium acetobutylicum*** (Weizmann strain), with *C. beijerinckii* also used commercially.

The **typical mass ratio** of products is:

$$
\text{Acetone} : \text{Butanol} : \text{Ethanol} = 3 : 6 : 1
$$

The process exhibits two distinct metabolic phases — **acidogenic** (acetic and butyric acids dominate early) and **solventogenic** (neutral solvents dominate after the pH drops below ~ 4.8).

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### QUESTION A (Choice 1) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a)** Derive the theoretical ethanol yield per gram of glucose consumed in yeast fermentation. State the assumptions made. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 25 m³ batch fermenter is charged with a medium containing 220 g/L glucose. After 30 hours of fermentation by *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*, the residual glucose is 22 g/L and the ethanol concentration is 95 g/L. Calculate:
  (i) % substrate conversion
  (ii) % theoretical ethanol yield
  (iii) Volumetric productivity in g/(L·h)
  (iv) Total kg of ethanol produced per batch
  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Step 1 — Write the Gay-Lussac equation:**

$$
\text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \;\longrightarrow\; 2\,\text{C}_2\text{H}_5\text{OH} \;+\; 2\,\text{CO}_2
$$

**Step 2 — Apply stoichiometry to 1 mole of glucose:**

1 mol glucose (180.16 g) → 2 mol ethanol (2 × 46.07 g = 92.14 g)

**Step 3 — Compute the mass yield coefficient:**

$$
Y^{\,th} = \dfrac{92.14\ \text{g ethanol}}{180.16\ \text{g glucose}} = 0.5114\ \text{g/g}
$$

**Step 4 — State assumptions:**
- (i) Glucose is the sole carbon and energy source.
- (ii) Fermentation is strictly anaerobic (no aerobic respiration or biomass carbon losses accounted).
- (iii) The only products are ethanol and CO₂ (no glycerol, acetic acid, or higher alcohols).
- (iv) The system behaves as a "black box" with 100 % substrate → product carbon conversion.
- (v) Standard temperature and pressure; no product evaporation losses.

**Final Answer:** $Y^{\,th} = 0.5114\ \text{g EtOH / g glucose}$ (i.e. **51.14 % w/w**).

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Given:** $V = 25{,}000\ \text{L}$, $S_0 = 220\ \text{g/L}$, $S_f = 22\ \text{g/L}$, $P_f = 95\ \text{g/L}$, $t = 30\ \text{h}$, $P_i = 0$.

**(i) % Substrate Conversion:**

$$
X_S = \dfrac{220 - 22}{220} \times 100 = \dfrac{198}{220} \times 100 = 90.0\,\%
$$

**(ii) % Theoretical Ethanol Yield:**

Actual yield on substrate consumed:
$$
Y_{actual} = \dfrac{95}{198} = 0.4798\ \text{g/g}
$$

$$
\%\,Y = \dfrac{0.4798}{0.5114} \times 100 = 93.82\,\%
$$

**(iii) Volumetric Productivity:**

$$
Q_p = \dfrac{95\ \text{g/L}}{30\ \text{h}} = 3.167\ \text{g/(L·h)}
$$

**(iv) Mass of Ethanol per Batch:**

$$
m_{EtOH} = 95\ \text{g/L} \times 25{,}000\ \text{L} = 2{,}375{,}000\ \text{g} = 2{,}375\ \text{kg}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Many students incorrectly write $P_i$ as the final ethanol concentration in the productivity formula. **Always** use $(P_f - P_i)$. If $P_i = 0$ (fresh medium), it drops out — but write it explicitly. Also, **never** divide by substrate consumed in the productivity formula — productivity is mass of product per unit volume per unit time, NOT per unit substrate.

---

### QUESTION B (Choice 2) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]

**(a)** Describe the ABE fermentation process. Explain the biphasic metabolism of *Clostridium acetobutylicum* with reference to the acidogenic and solventogenic phases. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Using the lumped ABE stoichiometry
  $$
  \text{C}_6\text{H}_{12}\text{O}_6 \rightarrow 0.30\,A + 0.60\,B + 0.10\,E + 2.20\,CO_2 + 0.55\,H_2O
  $$
  compute the theoretical concentrations of acetone, butanol, ethanol and total ABE for a feed of 80 g/L glucose at 100 % conversion. Comment on why the industrial titre is far lower than this theoretical value. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**The ABE Process:**
The ABE (Acetone–Butanol–Ethanol) fermentation is a **strictly anaerobic, batch or fed-batch bioprocess** carried out in sterilised, oxygen-free fermenters by solventogenic clostridia. The classical Weizmann organism is *Clostridium acetobutylicum*. The classical substrate is **maize or molasses**, but lignocellulosic hydrolysates are being explored as second-generation feedstocks. The process runs at **30–37 °C**, initial pH 5.5–6.0, and typically requires 48–72 hours.

**Biphasic Metabolism:**

| Phase | Duration | pH | Dominant Products | Key Enzymes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Acidogenic | 0 – 20 h | 5.5 → 4.5 | Acetic acid, Butyric acid, CO₂, H₂ | Acetate kinase (Ack), Phosphate transacetylase, Ptb-Buk |
| Solventogenic | 20 – 72 h | 4.5 → 5.0+ | **Acetone, Butanol, Ethanol** | CoA-transferase, Acetoacetate decarboxylase (AADC), AdhE |

**Why the Switch?**
During the acidogenic phase, the cell produces ATP by substrate-level phosphorylation, excreting acetic and butyric acids. As acids accumulate, the **undissociated acid concentration** (a function of pH and $pK_a$) rises above a critical threshold (~ 30 mM). This causes intracellular acid stress, which triggers the cell to **re-route CoA-bound intermediates** into neutral solvents:
- Acetyl-CoA → Acetoacetyl-CoA → Acetoacetate → **Acetone** (via AADC).
- Butyryl-CoA → Butyraldehyde → **Butanol** (via AdhE).
- A fraction of Acetyl-CoA also produces **ethanol**.

**Final Answer:** The biphasic shift is a **stress response** that neutralises acid toxicity at the cost of lower ATP yield per glucose.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**Step 1 — Compute mass of each product per mole of glucose consumed:**

Molecular weights: glucose 180.16, acetone 58.08, butanol 74.12, ethanol 46.07 g/mol.

- $m_A = 0.30 \times 58.08 = 17.424\ \text{g/mol glucose}$
- $m_B = 0.60 \times 74.12 = 44.472\ \text{g/mol glucose}$
- $m_E = 0.10 \times 46.07 = 4.607\ \text{g/mol glucose}$

**Step 2 — Convert to per-gram-glucose yield coefficients:**

- $Y_A = 17.424 / 180.16 = 0.0967\ \text{g/g}$
- $Y_B = 44.472 / 180.16 = 0.2469\ \text{g/g}$
- $Y_E = 4.607 / 180.16 = 0.0256\ \text{g/g}$

**Step 3 — Apply to the 80 g/L feed (100 % conversion):**

- $[\text{Acetone}]_{th} = 0.0967 \times 80 = \mathbf{7.74\ \text{g/L}}$
- $[\text{Butanol}]_{th} = 0.2469 \times 80 = \mathbf{19.75\ \text{g/L}}$
- $[\text{Ethanol}]_{th} = 0.0256 \times 80 = \mathbf{2.05\ \text{g/L}}$

**Step 4 — Total ABE:**

$$
[\text{ABE}]_{th} = 7.74 + 19.75 + 2.05 = \mathbf{29.54\ \text{g/L}}
$$

**Step 5 — Why industrial titre is much lower:**

| Limitation | Effect on Titre |
|---|---|
| **Butanol toxicity** at ~ 13 g/L | Cells lyse / cease solvent production; broth ABE typically capped at 18–25 g/L |
| **Acid crash** in early phase | Premature pH drop triggers cell death before full substrate use |
| **CO₂ stripping** of volatile acetone and ethanol | 5–10 % product loss through vent gas |
| **Substrate channeling to acids** | Only 60–80 % of carbon flows to solvents; rest remains as acetate/butyrate |
| **Cell density limitations** | Anaerobic strict anaerobes reach only low biomass (~ 3–5 g DCW/L) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Part (b):** Students often forget to **convert stoichiometric coefficients into mass-based yields**. A common error is to report 0.30 mol/L acetone from 0.444 mol/L glucose without multiplying by molecular weight. **Always compute the mass coefficient first**, then multiply by the feed concentration. Examiners will deduct 1–2 marks for this.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Block — Read this section twice the night before the KTU exam.**

- [x] **Gay-Lussac Equation:** C₆H₁₂O₆ → 2 C₂H₅OH + 2 CO₂ — **memorize verbatim**.
- [x] **Theoretical Ethanol Yield:** 0.511 g/g glucose (or 51.1 % w/w). **Maximum** from 200 g/L glucose = 102.3 g/L.
- [x] **Gay-Lussac Ceiling:** No organism can exceed 51.1 % w/w ethanol from glucose; everything else is biological inefficiency.
- [x] **Yeast = EMP pathway (2 ATP/glucose); Zymomonas = ED pathway (1 ATP/glucose).** Stoichiometry is identical.
- [x] **Ethanol inhibition threshold:** 100–120 g/L — the principal engineering bottleneck.
- [x] **ABE Mass Ratio:** 3 : 6 : 1 (A : B : E). ABE stands for **A**cetone-**B**utanol-**E**thanol.
- [x] **ABE Microorganisms:** *C. acetobutylicum* (Weizmann strain) and *C. beijerinckii*. **Strict anaerobes**.
- [x] **Acidogenic → Solventogenic** switch is triggered by undissociated acid concentration; **pH** is the master variable.
- [x] **Key ABE enzymes:** CoA-transferase, **AADC** (acetoacetate decarboxylase), **AdhE** (alcohol/aldehyde dehydrogenase).
- [x] **Butanol toxicity limit:** ~ 13 g/L — the **central** industrial bottleneck of ABE.
- [x] **Ethanol recovery:** Distillation + **molecular sieve** (zeolite 3Å) to break the 95.6 % azeotrope.
- [x] **ABE recovery:** Distillation train + **decanter** to break the butanol–water azeotrope (boiling butanol = 117.7 °C, water = 100 °C; forms heteroazeotrope at 89.4 °C, 73 % w/w water).
- [x] **Formula to remember:** $\% Y = \dfrac{Y_{actual}}{Y_{theoretical}} \times 100$. Never confuse with substrate conversion $X_S = (S_0 - S_f)/S_0$.
- [x] **Volumetric productivity units:** g/(L·h) or kg/(m³·h). **Always** divide by volume AND time.
- [x] **Cofactor balance:** NADH produced in glycolysis (2 NADH/glucose) is reoxidised by alcohol dehydrogenase — closing the redox loop under anaerobic conditions.
- [x] **Industrial status:** Ethanol = dominant biofuel (E10, E20). **Acetone is no longer produced by fermentation** in modern industry — displaced by the cumene process.
- [x] **History:** Chaim Weizmann (1910s) — ABE fermentation for WWI cordite production; later became the first President of Israel. **Frequently asked in KTU essays.**
- [x] **Global production scale:** ~ 100 billion litres of bioethanol per year worldwide; India targets E20 by 2025.
- [x] **Three numerical drills to practise before exam:** (1) % theoretical yield from given feed; (2) volumetric productivity from batch data; (3) total mass of product per batch.

---

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Memorize the **three numerical derivations** in Section 3 (Theoretical ethanol yield, % theoretical yield, ABE lumped stoichiometry). These account for ≥ 60 % of the marks in any 14-mark question on this topic. The text-based marks go to **Gay-Lussac equation, ABE biphasic metabolism, and ethanol inhibition**.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of Secondary Metabolites - Industrial Production Processes for Various Classes of Secondary Metabolites: Antibiotics: Beta-Lactams - Penicillin and Cephalosporin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Production of Secondary Metabolites - Industrial Production Processes for Various Classes of Secondary Metabolites: Antibiotics: Beta-Lactams - Penicillin and Cephalosporin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Secondary Metabolites

> [!NOTE]
> **Secondary Metabolites (KTU 2024 Definition)**
> Secondary metabolites are organic compounds produced by microorganisms during the **stationary phase** of growth (idiophase), which are **not essential for the basic vegetative growth, reproduction, or primary metabolic functions** of the producing organism, but often possess significant **pharmacological, agricultural, or industrial value**. They are derived from primary metabolic precursors and typically have complex, restricted distribution among taxonomically related species.

**Key characteristics differentiating them from primary metabolites:**
- Produced in the **idiophase** (after the trophophase ends)
- Not directly linked to cell growth or energy metabolism
- Show restricted taxonomic distribution
- Often produced as **mixtures of structurally related compounds**
- Production is highly influenced by environmental and nutritional conditions

## 1.2 Classification of Secondary Metabolites (Industrial Classes)

The major industrial classes of secondary metabolites include:

| Class | Example | Industrial Application |
|---|---|---|
| **β-Lactam Antibiotics** | Penicillin G, Cephalosporin C | Antibacterial therapy |
| **Aminoglycosides** | Streptomycin, Neomycin | Antibacterial therapy |
| **Tetracyclines** | Chlortetracycline | Antibiotic (broad spectrum) |
| **Macrolides** | Erythromycin | Antibiotic |
| **Polyenes** | Amphotericin B | Antifungal |
| **Glycopeptides** | Vancomycin | Antibiotic (MRSA) |
| **Stilbenes / Aromatics** | Lovastatin | Cholesterol-lowering agent |
| **Plant Growth Factors** | Gibberellins | Agriculture |
| **Immunosuppressants** | Cyclosporin A | Organ transplantation |

## 1.3 Beta-Lactam Antibiotics - The Chemical Core

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The β-Lactam Ring**
> All β-lactam antibiotics share a common, chemically strained, four-membered ring called the **β-lactam ring**, consisting of **three carbon atoms and one nitrogen atom**, with the **carbonyl group (C=O) directly bonded to the nitrogen** (an amide linkage within the ring). This ring is the pharmacophore responsible for antibacterial activity.

### Conceptual Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **The "Molecular Trojan Horse" Analogy**
> Think of the β-lactam ring as a **molecular trojan horse** that mimics the natural D-Ala-D-Ala terminus of the bacterial cell wall peptidoglycan precursor. Bacteria mistake it for their own building block and unwittingly use it to construct their cell wall. The β-lactam ring then becomes **covalently bonded to the active site serine** of the transpeptidase enzyme (Penicillin-Binding Protein, PBP), irreversibly inhibiting cell wall synthesis. The bacterium literally "builds its own coffin." Since **human cells lack peptidoglycan**, β-lactams are selectively toxic to bacteria.

### The Two Industrial Pillars: Penicillin and Cephalosporin

**Penicillin Core Structure:**
- Contains the β-lactam ring fused with a **five-membered thiazolidine ring** (containing sulfur)
- This bicyclic system is called **6-aminopenicillanic acid (6-APA)**
- Different acyl side chains (R-group at C-6) give different penicillins (G, V, ampicillin, amoxicillin)

**Cephalosporin Core Structure:**
- Contains the β-lactam ring fused with a **six-membered dihydrothiazine ring** (also containing sulfur)
- This bicyclic system is called **7-aminocephalosporanic acid (7-ACA)**
- More chemically stable than penicillins; more resistant to β-lactamases

### Industrial Yields (Benchmarks)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Industrial Titers (Reference Values)**
> - **Penicillin G (modern strains):** ~ **50–80 g/L** (Fed-batch titers, as of 2024 industrial strains)
> - **Cephalosporin C:** ~ **25–40 g/L** (industrial fed-batch production)
> - Typical fermentation duration: **120–200 hours** (5–8 days)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Microorganisms of Industrial Importance

### Penicillin Production Strain

> [!NOTE]
> **Penicillium chrysogenum (synonym: Penicillium notatum)**
> The original **Penicillium notatum** discovered by Alexander Fleming (1928) produced only ~**2 mg/L** of penicillin. Through **decades of classical strain improvement programs** (mutation with UV/X-rays/NTG followed by selection), modern industrial strains of *P. chrysogenum* (e.g., **Wisconsin Q-176 lineage**, **Panlabs P-2**, **Novartis high-yielding strains**) produce **50,000–80,000× more** than the original Fleming strain.

### Cephalosporin Production Strain

> [!NOTE]
> **Cephalosporium acremonium (synonym: Acremonium chrysogenum)**
> Cephalosporin C is produced by the filamentous fungus **Acremonium chrysogenum**. Originally isolated from a Sardinian seawater sample by Giuseppe Brotzu (1945). Modern high-yielding strains produce ~**25–40 g/L** cephalosporin C.

## 2.2 Biosynthetic Pathway (Penicillin) - Logical Flow

### Step-by-Step Logic of Penicillin Biosynthesis

**Step 1: Amino Acid Building Blocks**
- **L-α-aminoadipic acid (L-α-AAA)** + **L-cysteine** + **L-valine** condense to form the tripeptide **δ-(L-α-aminoadipyl)-L-cysteinyl-D-valine (ACV)**

**Step 2: ACV Synthetase (ACVS) — Single Multifunctional Enzyme**
- Catalyzes formation of the **ACV tripeptide**
- Belongs to the **non-ribosomal peptide synthetase (NRPS)** family

**Step 3: Cyclization to Isopenicillin N (IPN)**
- **Isopenicillin N synthetase (IPNS)** — also called **cyclase**
- Requires **O₂, Fe²⁺, and ascorbate**
- Forms the **β-lactam + thiazolidine** bicyclic ring system
- Produces **Isopenicillin N (IPN)** — the first bioactive penicillin

**Step 4: Side-Chain Exchange (Acyl Transferase)**
- IPN → **Penicillin G** by exchanging the **L-α-aminoadipyl side chain** with **phenylacetic acid (PAA)**
- Reaction catalyzed by **acyl-CoA: isopenicillin N acyltransferase (IAT)**
- If **phenoxyacetic acid** is fed instead → **Penicillin V**

**Step 5: Semi-synthetic modification (in vitro, downstream)**
- Penicillin G → cleaved by **penicillin acylase (PA)** to form **6-APA**
- 6-APA + new acyl side chains → ampicillin, amoxicillin, methicillin, etc.

### Detailed Pathway Diagram (Logical Flow)

```
L-α-aminoadipic acid + L-cysteine + L-valine
                    ↓ (ACV Synthetase, NRPS)
            ACV Tripeptide (LLD-tripeptide)
                    ↓ (Isopenicillin N Synthetase / Cyclase)
                   Isopenicillin N (IPN)
                    ↓ (Acyl-CoA:IPN Acyltransferase)
        Phenylacetic acid (PAA) feeds in → Penicillin G
                    ↓ (Penicillin Acylase, enzymatic cleavage)
            6-Aminopenicillanic acid (6-APA)
                    ↓ (Semi-synthetic acylation)
            Ampicillin, Amoxicillin, Piperacillin, etc.
```

## 2.3 Biosynthetic Pathway (Cephalosporin) - Logical Flow

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Cephalosporin shares the early biosynthetic steps with Penicillin up to IPN, then DIVERGES.**

**Step 1–3:** Same as penicillin → form **Isopenicillin N (IPN)**

**Step 4: Isopenicillin N Epimerase**
- IPN (L-α-AAA side chain) → **Penicillin N** (D-α-AAA side chain)

**Step 5: Ring Expansion (Cephalosporin Signature Step)**
- Enzyme: **DAOC Synthetase / Deacetoxycephalosporin C Synthetase (expandase)**
- **Five-membered thiazolidine ring EXPANDS to six-membered dihydrothiazine ring**
- Penicillin N → **Deacetoxycephalosporin C (DAOC)**

**Step 6: Hydroxylation**
- **DAOC hydroxylase** converts DAOC → **Deacetylcephalosporin C (DAC)**

**Step 7: Acetylation**
- **DAC acetyltransferase (DAC-AT)** converts DAC → **Cephalosporin C**

**Step 8: Semi-synthetic modification (downstream)**
- Cephalosporin C → cleaved by **D-amino acid oxidase + cephalosporin acylase** → **7-ACA**
- 7-ACA + side chains → Cephalexin, Cefotaxime, Ceftriaxone, etc.

### Cephalosporin Pathway Logical Diagram

```
                (Common to Penicillin)
        L-α-AAA + L-Cys + L-Val → ACV → IPN
                                   ↓
                          IPN Epimerase (isomerization)
                                   ↓
                            Penicillin N
                                   ↓
              Ring Expansion (DAOC Synthetase / Expandase)
                  5-membered ring → 6-membered ring
                                   ↓
                       Deacetoxycephalosporin C (DAOC)
                                   ↓
                    Hydroxylation (DAOC hydroxylase)
                                   ↓
                       Deacetylcephalosporin C (DAC)
                                   ↓
                  Acetylation (DAC acetyltransferase)
                                   ↓
                        Cephalosporin C
                                   ↓
       Enzymatic cleavage (DAAO + GL-7-ACA acylase)
                                   ↓
                         7-ACA (nucleus)
                                   ↓
       Semi-synthetic side-chain attachment → Final drugs
```

## 2.4 Fermentation Process - Key Parameters

### Fermentation Mode and Conditions

| Parameter | Penicillin (P. chrysogenum) | Cephalosporin C (A. chrysogenum) |
|---|---|---|
| **Fermenter type** | Stirred-tank, aerated | Stirred-tank, aerated |
| **Mode** | **Fed-batch** (substrate-limited) | **Fed-batch** |
| **Working volume** | **100,000 – 200,000 L** | **50,000 – 150,000 L** |
| **Temperature** | **25 – 27 °C** | **25 – 28 °C** |
| **pH** | **6.5 – 6.8** | **6.0 – 7.0** |
| **Dissolved O₂** | > **30% saturation** | > **30% saturation** |
| **Fermentation time** | **120 – 200 h** | **150 – 200 h** |
| **Morphology** | Pellet form (avoid viscous mycelia) | Pellet form (arthrospore-like fragments) |
| **Carbon source** | Glucose / Lactose / Sucrose | Glucose / Sucrose |
| **Nitrogen source** | Corn steep liquor (CSL), ammonia | CSL, soy meal, ammonia |
| **Precursors fed** | **Phenylacetic acid (PAA)** for Pen-G | None (cephalosporin C has no side-chain feeding for native molecule) |

### The Critical Phenomenon: Carbon Catabolite Repression (CCR)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Glucose Effect on Penicillin Production**
> Rapidly metabolizable sugars like **glucose strongly repress penicillin production**, even though they support rapid growth. Lactose, a slowly hydrolyzed disaccharide, is preferred as it provides a slow-release carbon source avoiding repression. In fed-batch, glucose is fed at a **growth-limiting rate (~0.5–1.5 g/L/h)** to maintain low residual concentration (< **0.5 g/L**), allowing the trophophase to switch to the **idiophase** for antibiotic synthesis.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Expression | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Volumetric Productivity (P)** | $P = \dfrac{C_p}{t}$ | $C_p$ = product concentration (g/L); $t$ = fermentation time (h) |
| **Specific Production Rate (q_P)** | $q_P = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dC_p}{dt}$ | Units: g product/g cells/h |
| **Yield Coefficient (Y_P/S)** | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta C_p}{\Delta S}$ | Product per unit substrate consumed (g/g) |
| **Yield Coefficient (Y_X/S)** | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | Cells per unit substrate consumed |
| **Mass Balance (Fed-batch)** | $V\dfrac{dC_p}{dt} = q_P \cdot X \cdot V - C_p \dfrac{dV}{dt}$ | Accounts for dilution from feeding |
| **Oxygen Transfer Rate (OTR)** | $OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ | $C^*$ = saturation; $C_L$ = dissolved O₂ |
| **Mycelial Growth Rate** | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | Determines trophophase vs idiophase transition |
| **Luedeking-Piret (Production kinetics)** | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \dfrac{dX}{dt} + \beta X$ | Secondary metabolites: typically **α ≈ 0, β > 0** (non-growth associated) |
| **Critical Dilution Rate** | $D_c = \mu_{max}(1 - \sqrt{K_s/(K_s + S_0)})$ | For chemostat; avoided for secondary metabolites |
| **Reactor Power Input** | $P_g = k \cdot \rho \cdot N^3 \cdot D_i^5$ | Rushton-type impellers in viscous mycelial broths |

## 2.6 Downstream Processing (DSP) — Recovery and Purification

### Penicillin Recovery Sequence

> [!NOTE]
> **The classical penicillin recovery (the "Podbialka process")** uses the principle that **penicillin is an acidic molecule (pKa ≈ 2.75)** and can therefore be reversibly protonated. The process exploits this acid-base chemistry through pH swings in an aqueous-organic two-phase system.

**Step-by-step DSP for Penicillin G:**
1. **Broth clarification** — filter or centrifuge to remove mycelia
2. **Acidification to pH 2.0–2.5** — converts penicillin to free acid form
3. **Solvent extraction** — extract into **butyl acetate** (or amyl acetate) at low temperature
4. **Back-extraction into water** — adjust pH to **7.0–7.5** with NaOH/KOH → **Na/K-penicillin salt** in aqueous phase
5. **Activated carbon treatment** — decolorization
6. **Crystallization** — addition of antisolvent (e.g., n-propanol/butanol) at low temperature
7. **Drying** — vacuum or spray drying to obtain crystalline penicillin G potassium salt

### Cephalosporin C Recovery Sequence
1. **Filtration** of broth (very high solids — mycelia dense)
2. **Adsorption on ion-exchange resins** (often preferred over solvent extraction)
3. **Cephalosporin C elution** from resin
4. **Enzymatic cleavage** — two-step enzymatic conversion to **7-ACA**:
   - **D-amino acid oxidase (DAAO)** — oxidizes D-α-AAA side chain → 7-ACA intermediate (GL-7-ACA, α-ketoadipyl-7-ACA)
   - **GL-7-ACA acylase** — cleaves α-ketoadipyl group → **7-ACA**
5. **Semi-synthetic acylation** at the 7-amino position
6. **Crystallization & drying**

### Why Cephalosporin is Preferred Over Penicillin for Some Applications

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Therapeutic Advantages of Cephalosporins**
> 1. **Resistance to β-lactamases** — many bacteria produce penicillinase (β-lactamase) that hydrolyzes the β-lactam ring. Cephalosporins, especially **3rd-generation** (cefotaxime, ceftriaxone), are far more resistant.
> 2. **Broader spectrum** — effective against both Gram-positive and many Gram-negative bacteria.
> 3. **Lower allergenicity** — not a complete cross-reactant with penicillin allergies (though caution required).
> 4. **Better acid stability** — many cephalosporins are orally active (e.g., cephalexin), unlike most penicillins.
> 5. **Longer half-life** for some 3rd-generation drugs.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Luedeking-Piret Kinetic Model for Secondary Metabolite Production

> [!NOTE]
> **Purpose:** To mathematically justify why secondary metabolite production (like penicillin) is primarily a **non-growth-associated** process and is favored at low growth rates.

The **Luedeking-Piret equation** (1959) is a fundamental kinetic model that expresses product formation rate as a linear combination of growth-rate-dependent and growth-rate-independent terms:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} + \beta \cdot X$$

### Step-by-Step Logical Derivation

**Step 1: Define the rate of product formation**

The rate at which product is formed per unit volume is:
$$r_P = \frac{dP}{dt}$$

We assume this rate depends on two biological states of the cell: **(i)** the rate of cell-mass formation (i.e., growth), and **(ii)** the existing cell mass itself.

**Step 2: Express the growth-rate term explicitly**

The growth-rate term is the specific growth rate times biomass:
$$\frac{dX}{dt} = \mu \cdot X$$

**Step 3: Introduce proportionality constants**

Let **α (alpha)** be the proportionality constant for the growth-associated component, and **β (beta)** be the proportionality constant for the non-growth-associated component:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} + \beta \cdot X$$

Substituting $dX/dt = \mu X$:

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \mu \cdot X + \beta \cdot X = X(\alpha\mu + \beta)$$

**Step 4: Divide both sides by biomass $X$ to obtain the specific product formation rate $q_P$**

$$q_P = \frac{1}{X}\frac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \mu + \beta$$

**Step 5: Interpretation for Penicillin (a Secondary Metabolite)**

For penicillin:
- **α ≈ 0** — penicillin production is **not coupled to growth**
- **β > 0** — production is driven by the existing biomass concentration
- This explains why **stationary phase (low μ)** yields maximum penicillin — the cell machinery is redirected from growth to secondary metabolism

**Step 6: Numerical application**

Consider a fed-batch fermentation at the idiophase where:
- $X = 30 \, g/L$ (cell dry weight)
- $\beta = 0.05 \, mg/(g \cdot h)$ for penicillin
- $\mu = 0.02 \, h^{-1}$ (low maintenance growth)
- $\alpha = 0$

Then:
$$q_P = (0)(0.02) + 0.05 = 0.05 \, mg/(g \cdot h)$$

$$\frac{dP}{dt} = q_P \cdot X = (0.05)(30) = 1.5 \, mg/(L \cdot h)$$

Over 100 hours of idiophase:
$$\Delta P = (1.5)(100) = 150 \, mg/L = 0.15 \, g/L \text{ production increment}$$

(In practice, fed-batch feeding and accumulation over many days yields the industrial titers of 50–80 g/L.)

## 3.2 Derivation: Mass Balance for Product in Fed-Batch Reactor

For penicillin fed-batch fermentation with glucose feeding at rate $F$ (L/h), inlet glucose concentration $S_F$ (g/L), working volume $V$ (L):

**Component mass balance on volume:**
$$\frac{dV}{dt} = F$$

**Component mass balance on product (P):**
$$\frac{d(P \cdot V)}{dt} = r_P \cdot V$$

Expanding using the product rule:
$$V\frac{dP}{dt} + P\frac{dV}{dt} = r_P \cdot V$$

Substituting $dV/dt = F$:
$$V\frac{dP}{dt} + P \cdot F = r_P \cdot V$$

Therefore:
$$V\frac{dP}{dt} = r_P \cdot V - P \cdot F$$

Or:
$$\frac{dP}{dt} = r_P - \frac{P \cdot F}{V}$$

**Step-by-step interpretation:**
1. $r_P \cdot V$ — total grams of penicillin produced per unit time by the cells
2. $P \cdot F$ — grams of penicillin "lost" per unit time by dilution due to feed addition
3. The net rate of concentration change is production minus dilution

This explains why **$F$ must be controlled** — too high a feed rate dilutes the product and reduces its concentration even if volumetric productivity remains constant.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Process Comparison

### Side-by-Side Industrial Process Comparison (Penicillin G vs Cephalosporin C)

| Aspect | Penicillin G | Cephalosporin C |
|---|---|---|
| **Producer organism** | *Penicillium chrysogenum* | *Acremonium chrysogenum* |
| **Discovery** | A. Fleming, 1928 (London) | G. Brotzu, 1945 (Sardinia) |
| **Core nucleus** | 6-APA | 7-ACA |
| **Ring system** | 5-membered thiazolidine + β-lactam | 6-membered dihydrothiazine + β-lactam |
| **Native product excreted** | Penicillin G (after PAA feed) | Cephalosporin C (no side-chain feed) |
| **Industrial titer (modern)** | 50–80 g/L | 25–40 g/L |
| **Specific productivity** | ~1.5–2.5 mg/g cells/h | ~0.8–1.5 mg/g cells/h |
| **Fed-batch duration** | 120–200 h | 150–200 h |
| **Trophophase–idiophase transition** | Sharp (~40–60 h) | Gradual |
| **Critical nutrient limiting factor** | Glucose (CCR control) | Methionine (sulfur source — stimulates) |
| **pH optimum** | 6.5–6.8 | 6.0–7.0 |
| **Key precursor** | **Phenylacetic acid (PAA)** | **DL-Methionine** (stimulatory) |
| **Key by-product concern** | p-Hydroxy-penicillin V (over-acylation) | Deacetylcephalosporin C (DAC) |
| **Critical process step** | pH-controlled solvent extraction | Two-step enzymatic cleavage to 7-ACA |
| **Major historical titer improvement** | From 2 mg/L → 80,000 mg/L | From 20 mg/L → 40,000 mg/L |
| **Global market value (2024)** | ~USD 8–10 billion (incl. semi-synthetic) | ~USD 12–14 billion (incl. semi-synthetic) |

### Side-by-Side Molecular Comparison

| Feature | Penicillin G | Cephalosporin C |
|---|---|---|
| **Chemical formula** | C₁₆H₁₈N₂O₄S | C₁₆H₂₁N₃O₈S |
| **Molecular weight** | **334.4 g/mol** | **415.4 g/mol** |
| **β-Lactam ring** | Present | Present |
| **Fused ring** | 5-membered (thiazolidine) | 6-membered (dihydrothiazine) |
| **Sulfur in ring** | Yes (1 atom) | Yes (1 atom) |
| **Side chain (R)** | Phenylacetyl (–CO–CH₂–C₆H₅) | D-α-aminoadipyl (–CO–CH(NH₂)–CH₂–CH₂–COOH) |
| **Stability to acid** | **Low** (cleaved in stomach) | **Variable**; some derivatives high |
| **Stability to β-lactamase** | **Low** | **Higher** |
| **Oral activity (native)** | Penicillin V only | None natively; semi-synthetics oral (cephalexin) |

## 3.4 Derivation: Calculation of Oxygen Transfer Requirement

Industrial penicillin broths have very high oxygen demand because of **active secondary metabolism**. The OUR (Oxygen Uptake Rate) at peak idiophase can reach **5–10 mmol O₂/(L·h)**.

**Step 1:** Calculate OUR from cell density and specific oxygen uptake rate $q_{O_2}$:
$$OUR = q_{O_2} \cdot X$$

Where $q_{O_2} = 1.5 \, mmol/(g \cdot h)$ and $X = 30 \, g/L$:
$$OUR = (1.5)(30) = 45 \, mmol/(L \cdot h) \approx 2.16 \, mmol/(L \cdot min)$$

**Step 2:** At steady state, OTR = OUR:
$$k_L a (C^* - C_L) = OUR$$

**Step 3:** Assuming air at 1 atm, $C^* \approx 0.21 \cdot (1/22.4) \cdot 1000 \approx 9.4 \, mg/L$ at 25°C; with $C_L = 2 \, mg/L$ (acceptable threshold above the critical value of 0.5–1.0 mg/L):

$$k_L a = \frac{OUR}{C^* - C_L} = \frac{(45 \times 32) \, mg/(L \cdot h)}{9.4 - 2} \, mg/L$$

Wait — units check: $OUR$ in mmol/h/L → convert to mg/(L·h) by multiplying by **32 g/mol** for O₂:
$$OUR_{mass} = 45 \, mmol/(L \cdot h) \times 32 \, mg/mmol = 1440 \, mg/(L \cdot h)$$

$$k_L a = \frac{1440}{9.4 - 2} = \frac{1440}{7.4} \approx 195 \, h^{-1}$$

This corresponds to a high-performance industrial aeration system requiring **multiple Rushton turbines, high sparging air flow (vvm ~1.0–1.5), and often oxygen-enriched air** to meet OTR demands in large-scale penicillin fermenters.
