# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each)

### Question 1: Define Secondary Metabolites. List any four industrially important classes with one example each.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
Secondary metabolites are organic compounds produced by microorganisms, particularly during the **stationary phase (idiophase)** of growth, which are **not essential for vegetative growth, reproduction, or primary metabolism** of the producing organism but possess significant **pharmaceutical, agricultural, or industrial value**.

**Key features:**
- Produced in the **idiophase**
- Not required for primary growth
- Show **restricted taxonomic distribution**
- Often produced as **complex structural mixtures**

**Four Industrial Classes:**

| Class | Example |
|---|---|
| **β-Lactam Antibiotics** | Penicillin G |
| **Aminoglycosides** | Streptomycin |
| **Tetracyclines** | Chlortetracycline |
| **Macrolides** | Erythromycin |

*(Self-explanatory — 3 marks total)*

---

### Question 2: What is the significance of the β-lactam ring in penicillin and cephalosporin? Mention any two differences between penicillin and cephalosporin.

**Model Answer:**

**Significance of β-lactam ring:**
The **β-lactam ring** is the **pharmacophore** (the chemically active core) responsible for antibacterial activity. It acts as a **structural analog of D-Ala-D-Ala** terminus of bacterial peptidoglycan precursor and irreversibly acylates the **active-site serine** of the **Penicillin-Binding Protein (PBP/transpeptidase)**, inhibiting cell wall cross-linking. The **strained four-membered amide** provides the chemical reactivity.

**Two Differences:**

| Feature | Penicillin | Cephalosporin |
|---|---|---|
| **Fused ring system** | Five-membered thiazolidine ring | Six-membered dihydrothiazine ring |
| **Resistance to β-lactamases** | Low | Higher (esp. 3rd-generation derivatives) |

*(Or alternative differences: oral activity, side-chain feeding, molecular weight, etc.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> Students frequently lose marks in the following ways: (1) **Not naming the producing organism** explicitly, (2) **Mixing up penicillin G with 6-APA** when describing the semi-synthetic step, (3) **Forgetting to mention fermentation parameters** (pH, temperature, mode), (4) **Skipping DSP steps** by writing only the fermentation, (5) **Confusing carbon catabolite repression with feedback inhibition**, (6) **Not specifying that glucose is limiting in fed-batch** (CC repression context).

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Describe in detail the industrial production process of Penicillin G by fermentation. Include the producing organism, fermentation medium, process conditions, and downstream processing steps.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Producing Organism:**
- *Penicillium chrysogenum* (current industrial strain; originally *P. notatum* isolated by Alexander Fleming in 1928).
- Modern strains are derived from the **Wisconsin Q-176 lineage** and selected through **mutation-selection cycles** (UV, NTG, X-rays).

**2. Fermentation Medium (Composition):**

| Component | Role |
|---|---|
| **Glucose / Lactose (5–10%)** | Carbon source |
| **Corn steep liquor (CSL) 3–5%** | Nitrogen, vitamins, minerals |
| **Soybean meal / Yeast extract** | Supplemental nitrogen |
| **Ammonia / (NH₄)₂SO₄** | pH control and N source |
| **KH₂PO₄, MgSO₄, trace metals** | Phosphorus, sulfur, minerals |
| **Phenylacetic acid (PAA)** | Side-chain precursor (fed continuously to avoid toxicity) |
| **Antifoam (silicone/oil)** | Foam control |

**3. Fermentation Conditions:**
- **Mode:** Fed-batch (glucose-limited)
- **Volume:** 100,000–200,000 L
- **Temperature:** 25–27 °C
- **pH:** 6.5–6.8 (controlled by NH₃/NaOH/H₂SO₄)
- **Dissolved O₂:** > 30% saturation (achieved via high aeration vvm ~1.0–1.5, multiple Rushton turbines)
- **Duration:** 120–200 hours
- **Morphology:** Pellet form preferred (low viscosity, good mass transfer)

**4. Process Logic:**
- **Trophophase:** Rapid growth on glucose; biomass accumulation
- **Idiophase:** Glucose-depleted; secondary metabolism begins; PAA fed for side-chain incorporation
- **Carbon Catabolite Repression (CCR):** Glucose represses penicillin synthesis; hence lactose or low-level glucose feeding is used

**5. Downstream Processing (Podbialka Process):**
1. Broth harvest → **filtration/centrifugation** to remove mycelia
2. **Acidification to pH 2.0–2.5** (free acid form)
3. **Solvent extraction into butyl acetate** at 0–5 °C
4. **Back-extraction into water at pH 7.0–7.5** with NaOH → Na-penicillin salt
5. **Activated carbon treatment** (decolorization)
6. **Crystallization** by antisolvent addition
7. **Vacuum drying** → crystalline Penicillin G potassium salt
8. **Quality control** (HPLC potency, sterility)

*(Total: 7 marks — organism 1, medium 2, conditions 2, DSP 2 marks; distribute as appropriate)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Explain the biosynthetic pathway of Penicillin G with a clean flow diagram. Mention the role of the key enzymes.**

**Model Answer:**

**The Penicillin Biosynthetic Pathway:**

**Step 1:** Three amino acids — **L-α-aminoadipic acid (L-α-AAA), L-cysteine, L-valine** — condense to form the **ACV tripeptide**.

- **Enzyme:** **ACV Synthetase (ACVS)** — a **non-ribosomal peptide synthetase (NRPS)**; multifunctional single polypeptide

**Step 2:** ACV cyclizes to form **Isopenicillin N (IPN)** — the first bioactive β-lactam.

- **Enzyme:** **Isopenicillin N Synthetase (IPNS)** — also called **cyclase**
- **Cofactors required:** **O₂**, **Fe²⁺**, **ascorbate**
- Reaction: forms the **β-lactam ring** + the **thiazolidine ring** simultaneously

**Step 3:** **Acyl-CoA:IPN Acyltransferase (IAT)** exchanges the L-α-aminoadipyl side chain for **phenylacetyl-CoA** (derived from fed PAA), forming **Penicillin G**.

**Flow Diagram:**

```
L-α-AAA + L-Cys + L-Val
        │
        ▼ (ACV Synthetase)
   ACV Tripeptide
        │
        ▼ (IPN Synthetase + O₂, Fe²⁺)
   Isopenicillin N (IPN)
        │
        ▼ (Acyltransferase + PAA-CoA)
   Penicillin G
```

**Key Enzyme Roles:**

| Enzyme | Function |
|---|---|
| **ACV Synthetase (ACVS)** | NRPS; activates and condenses three amino acids into ACV tripeptide |
| **IPN Synthetase (IPNS)** | Cyclase; oxidative ring closure; uses O₂ and Fe²⁺ |
| **Acyltransferase (IAT)** | Side-chain exchange with phenylacetic acid |

**Industrial Significance:**
The pathway is regulated at the **transcriptional level** by **CCR** and **nitrogen catabolite repression**. **Lysine** inhibits ACVS (because lysine is derived from the same α-aminoadipate pathway branch). Hence, **lysine auxotrophs** or **lysine-insensitive mutants** show enhanced penicillin production.

*(Total: 7 marks — steps ~3 marks, diagram ~2 marks, enzyme roles ~2 marks)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Discuss in detail the industrial production of Cephalosporin C. Include the organism, fermentation conditions, and the enzymatic conversion of Cephalosporin C to 7-ACA.**

**Model Answer:**

**1. Producing Organism:**
- **Acremonium chrysogenum** (synonym: *Cephalosporium acremonium*)
- Originally isolated by **Giuseppe Brotzu (1945)** from seawater near Sardinia
- Modern strains are high-yielding via **mutation + screening** and **protoplast fusion**

**2. Fermentation Conditions:**

| Parameter | Value |
|---|---|
| **Mode** | Fed-batch |
| **Temperature** | 25–28 °C |
| **pH** | 6.0–7.0 |
| **Fermenter size** | 50,000–150,000 L |
| **Carbon source** | Glucose / sucrose (slow feeding) |
| **Nitrogen** | Soybean meal, CSL, ammonia |
| **Amino acid stimulant** | **DL-Methionine (0.5–1%)** — strongly stimulates cephalosporin C production (sulfur source for the dihydrothiazine ring) |
| **Time** | 150–200 h |
| **Aeration** | High (DO > 30%) |

**3. Distinguishing Feature from Penicillin Fermentation:**
- Methionine addition is **critical** — it serves as both **sulfur donor** and an **inducer** of ring-expansion enzyme (expandase)
- The **trophophase–idiophase transition** is more gradual than in penicillin fermentation

**4. Downstream Processing:**

| Step | Operation |
|---|---|
| 1 | Broth filtration (very dense mycelia) |
| 2 | **Activated carbon or ion-exchange adsorption** (preferred over solvent extraction due to higher polarity of cephalosporin C) |
| 3 | **Elution** from resin with buffered salt solution |
| 4 | Concentration / partial purification |
| 5 | **Enzymatic cleavage to 7-ACA** (see below) |

**5. Enzymatic Conversion of Cephalosporin C to 7-ACA:**

**Two-step enzymatic cleavage:**

- **Step 1 — Oxidative deamination:** **D-amino acid oxidase (DAAO)** oxidizes the **D-α-aminoadipyl side chain** to form an **α-keto intermediate** (α-ketoadipyl-7-ACA, also called GL-7-ACA). Requires **O₂** and produces **H₂O₂**.

- **Step 2 — Hydrolytic cleavage:** **GL-7-ACA acylase** (also called α-ketoadipyl-7-ACA acylase) cleaves the resulting α-ketoadipyl group, releasing **7-aminocephalosporanic acid (7-ACA)** — the cephalosporin nucleus.

**Significance:** 7-ACA serves as the **central building block** for all semi-synthetic cephalosporins (cephalexin, cefotaxime, ceftriaxone, ceftazidime, etc.) by attaching different acyl side chains at the 7-amino position.

**Chemical Equation Summary:**
```
Cephalosporin C
       │ DAAO + O₂
       ▼
GL-7-ACA (α-ketoadipyl-7-ACA)
       │ GL-7-ACA acylase + H₂O
       ▼
7-ACA + α-ketoadipic acid (byproduct)
```

*(Total: 7 marks — organism 1, fermentation conditions 2, DSP 1, enzymatic cleavage to 7-ACA 3 marks)*

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**Compare the biosynthetic pathways of Penicillin and Cephalosporin. Highlight the key enzymatic step that distinguishes them.**

**Model Answer:**

**Common Origin:**
Both penicillin and cephalosporin share the **early biosynthetic steps up to Isopenicillin N (IPN)**:
1. Formation of **ACV tripeptide** by ACV synthetase
2. Cyclization to **Isopenicillin N (IPN)** by IPN synthetase

**Divergence Point:**
After IPN, the pathways split:

**Penicillin Pathway:**
```
IPN ─── Acyltransferase (IAT) ───> Penicillin G (with PAA feeding)
```

**Cephalosporin Pathway:**
```
IPN ─── IPN Epimerase ───> Penicillin N
              │
              ▼ Ring Expansion (DAOC Synthetase / Expandase)
       Deacetoxycephalosporin C (DAOC)
              │
              ▼ Hydroxylation (DAOC Hydroxylase)
       Deacetylcephalosporin C (DAC)
              │
              ▼ Acetylation (DAC Acetyltransferase)
       Cephalosporin C
```

**The Key Distinguishing Enzymatic Step:**
The **ring expansion reaction** catalyzed by **DAOC Synthetase (Deacetoxycephalosporin C Synthetase / Expandase)** is the **signature step** that distinguishes cephalosporin biosynthesis. It converts the **five-membered thiazolidine ring** of penicillin N into the **six-membered dihydrothiazine ring** characteristic of cephalosporins. This reaction is:
- **Oxygen- and α-ketoglutarate-dependent** (a 2-oxoglutarate-dependent dioxygenase)
- The **only known enzyme in nature that expands a sulfur-containing ring**
- Highly **stimulated by methionine** (explains why methionine feeding is critical in cephalosporin fermentation)

**Comparative Table:**

| Step | Penicillin | Cephalosporin |
|---|---|---|
| **Common steps** | ACV → IPN | ACV → IPN |
| **Intermediate** | Direct acylation | Epimerization → Ring expansion → Hydroxylation → Acetylation |
| **Final native product** | Penicillin G | Cephalosporin C |
| **Industrial nucleus** | 6-APA (after acylase cleavage) | 7-ACA (after DAAO + acylase) |
| **Key distinguishing enzyme** | Acyltransferase | **Expandase (DAOC Synthetase)** |

**Conclusion:**
The shared origin explains why both drugs have the β-lactam pharmacophore, while the divergent enzymatic steps (especially the expandase-mediated ring expansion) give them distinct chemical, pharmacological, and clinical profiles.

*(Total: 7 marks — common steps 2 marks, divergence 2 marks, ring-expansion emphasis 2 marks, conclusion 1 mark)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD REVISION CHECKLIST — Module 3: β-Lactam Antibiotics**

### Core Definitions
- [ ] **Secondary metabolite:** non-essential organic compound produced in idiophase with pharmacological/industrial value
- [ ] **β-lactam ring:** four-membered ring (3C + 1N) with amide C=O; the pharmacophore
- [ ] **Trophophase vs Idiophase:** growth phase vs production phase (secondary metabolism)
- [ ] **NRPS (Non-ribosomal peptide synthetase):** enzymes that produce peptides without ribosomes (e.g., ACVS)

### Producer Organisms
- [ ] **Penicillin:** *Penicillium chrysogenum* (synonym *P. notatum*)
- [ ] **Cephalosporin C:** *Acremonium chrysogenum* (synonym *Cephalosporium acremonium*)

### Key Enzymes (Penicillin Pathway)
- [ ] **ACV Synthetase (ACVS)** — NRPS, forms ACV tripeptide
- [ ] **IPN Synthetase / Cyclase (IPNS)** — oxidative cyclization (O₂, Fe²⁺, ascorbate)
- [ ] **Acyltransferase (IAT)** — side-chain exchange with PAA → Penicillin G

### Key Enzymes (Cephalosporin Pathway)
- [ ] **IPN Epimerase** — L → D-α-AAA side chain (forms Penicillin N)
- [ ] **DAOC Synthetase / Expandase** — **ring expansion 5→6 membered** (signature enzyme)
- [ ] **DAOC Hydroxylase** — DAOC → DAC
- [ ] **DAC Acetyltransferase** — DAC → Cephalosporin C

### Nuclei for Semi-synthetics
- [ ] **6-APA (6-aminopenicillanic acid)** — penicillin nucleus; produced by **penicillin acylase** cleavage of Pen-G
- [ ] **7-ACA (7-aminocephalosporanic acid)** — cephalosporin nucleus; produced by **two-step enzymatic** (DAAO + GL-7-ACA acylase) cleavage of Ceph-C

### Industrial Process Parameters
- [ ] Penicillin: Fed-batch, 25–27 °C, pH 6.5–6.8, 120–200 h, PAA feeding critical
- [ ] Cephalosporin: Fed-batch, 25–28 °C, pH 6.0–7.0, 150–200 h, **methionine feeding critical**
- [ ] **Glucose repression** of penicillin (avoid high residual glucose; use lactose or low-rate glucose feeding)
- [ ] **High aeration required** (DO > 30%, multiple Rushton turbines, vvm ~1.0–1.5)

### Key Numbers to Memorize
- [ ] Fleming's original titer: **~2 mg/L**
- [ ] Modern penicillin titer: **50–80 g/L** (≈ 25,000× to 40,000× improvement)
- [ ] Penicillin G MW: **334.4 g/mol**
- [ ] Cephalosporin C MW: **415.4 g/mol**
- [ ] Penicillin pKa: **~2.75** (basis of solvent extraction at low pH)
- [ ] Industrial fermenter size: **100,000–200,000 L** for penicillin; **50,000–150,000 L** for cephalosporin

### Differences to Remember
- [ ] **Penicillin = 5-membered thiazolidine ring + β-lactam**
- [ ] **Cephalosporin = 6-membered dihydrothiazine ring + β-lactam**
- [ ] Cephalosporin is more **β-lactamase resistant** than penicillin
- [ ] Cephalosporin has **broader antibacterial spectrum**
- [ ] Penicillin G is acid-labile; cephalosporin derivatives can be **orally active**

### Industrial Process Logic
- [ ] **Strain improvement:** mutation (UV, NTG) + screening → titers improved by 4–5 orders of magnitude
- [ ] **Seed train:** spore → flask → lab fermenter → pilot → production fermenter
- [ ] **Penicillin DSP:** Mycelia removal → acidify pH 2 → butyl acetate extract → back-extract at pH 7 → crystallize → dry
- [ ] **Cephalosporin DSP:** Filter → ion-exchange adsorb → elute → DAAO + acylase → 7-ACA → semi-synthetic acylation

### Exam-Favored Topics
- [ ] Biosynthetic pathway diagrams (MUST be drawn clean with enzyme names)
- [ ] Ring expansion step (expandase) — high-yield KTU question
- [ ] Comparison tables (Pen vs Ceph)
- [ ] Significance of glucose repression / methionine stimulation
- [ ] Why solvent extraction works for penicillin (acidic pKa)
- [ ] Industrial titer improvement journey (2 mg/L → 80 g/L)

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Aminoglycosides - Streptomycin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFFT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Aminoglycosides - Streptomycin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Aminoglycosides** are a structurally related class of broad-spectrum antibiotics characterized by an **aminocyclitol nucleus** (a cyclohexane ring bearing multiple hydroxyl and amino substituents) linked to one or more **amino sugars** through **glycosidic bonds**. The clinically and industrially most prominent member is **Streptomycin**, the first aminoglycoside ever discovered (1943) by Albert Schatz, Elizabeth Bugie, and Selman Waksman from the soil actinomycete *Streptomyces griseus*.

Streptomycin is chemically designated as **2,4-diguanidino-3,5,6-trihydroxycyclohexyl 5-deoxy-3-O-formyl-α-D-pentofuranosyl-(1→4)-[6-O-(N-methyl-α-L-glucopyranosylaminyl)-α-D-streptoside]**, with the molecular formula **C₂₁H₃₉N₇O₁₂** and molecular weight **581.57 g/mol**.

| Property | Value |
|---|---|
| IUPAC Family | Aminoglycoside-aminocyclitol |
| Molecular Formula | C₂₁H₃₉N₇O₁₂ |
| Molecular Weight | **581.57 g/mol** |
| Producer Organism | *Streptomyces griseus* (also *S. bikiniensis*) |
| Discovery Year | **1943** |
| Nobel Recognition | **1952** (Selman Waksman) |
| Primary Target | **30S ribosomal subunit** of prokaryotes |
| Spectrum | Broad — Gram-negative and mycobacteria (TB) |

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Lock-and-Spanner

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The "Wrong Key" Antibiotic:**
> Imagine a bacterium's ribosome as a precision-engineered factory conveyor belt that reads genetic blueprints (mRNA) and assembles proteins. The conveyor belt uses a special cog — the **30S subunit** — which is unique to bacteria. Streptomycin acts like a **bent, slightly oversized spanner** thrown into this cog. The bacterial machine jams (translational misreading, premature termination), production halts, and the bacterium dies. Human ribosomes use a different 60S+40S architecture, so the spanner doesn't fit — that's why streptomycin is **selectively toxic** to bacteria, sparing our own cells.

## 1.3 Classification Context (Where Streptomycin Sits)

```
Aminoglycoside Antibiotics
├── 4,6-Disubstituted (2-DOS core)
│   ├── Kanamycin
│   ├── Amikacin
│   ├── Gentamicin
│   └── Tobramycin
└── 4,5-Disubstituted (streptomycin — unique)
    └── STREPTOMYCIN  ← our focus
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Architecture of Streptomycin

Streptomycin is built from **three covalently linked units** that dictate its biological activity:

- **Streptose** — a 5-deoxy-3-formyl-pentose (sugar A). The formyl (–CHO) group at C-3 is essential for activity.
- **Glucosamine** derivative (N-methyl-L-glucosamine) — sugar B. This sugar carries a methylated amine that defines the "amino" prefix of the family.
- **Streptidine** — a diguanidinated aminocyclitol (the central ring). Two guanidino groups (–C(=NH)–NH₂) flank the 3,5,6-trihydroxycyclohexane.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **glycosidic bond** linking streptose to streptidine is unusually stable in streptomycin — this is why it survives the acidic conditions of industrial fermentation and is the foundation for downstream isolation strategies.

## 2.2 Mechanism of Action (How it Kills Bacteria)

1. **Entry via Electrostatic Attraction:** Streptomycin is highly **polycationic** at physiological pH (multiple –NH₃⁺ groups). The negatively charged lipopolysaccharide (LPS) layer of Gram-negative bacteria electrostatically pulls the drug in.
2. **Energy-Dependent Uptake (EDP-I/EDP-II):** The drug is actively transported across the bacterial membrane using the proton-motive force (PMF) and an electron-transport chain. This is why streptomycin is **inactive against anaerobes** (no PMF) and **ineffective in abscesses** (low pH).
3. **Ribosomal Binding:** Binds irreversibly to the **30S ribosomal subunit**, specifically the S12 protein (encoded by *rpsL*).
4. **Translational Misreading:** Causes mispairing of tRNA–mRNA codons → incorporation of wrong amino acids → non-functional proteins.
5. **Membrane Disruption:** Inserts into the cytoplasmic membrane, causing leakage of K⁺ ions and nucleotides.
6. **Cell Death (Bactericidal):** Unlike β-lactams (bacteriostatic), streptomycin is **cidal**.

## 2.3 Industrial-Production Logic

> [!NOTE]
> **Why bioprocess engineers care:** Streptomycin is **exclusively produced by fermentation** — chemical total synthesis is impractical (low yield, 50+ steps). Thus, the *Streptomyces griseus* submerged-culture fermentation is the canonical textbook case of an **industrial secondary-metabolite antibiotic process**.

The fermentation is run as a **fed-batch, aerobic, non-sterile product-isolation, complex-medium** process.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Equation / Parameter | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Specific Growth Rate (Monod) | $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{max} \cdot S}{K_s + S}$ | h⁻¹ |
| 2 | Biomass doubling time | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | hours |
| 3 | Product formation rate (Leudeking–Piret, mixed) | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = \alpha \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt} + \beta \cdot X$ | g L⁻¹ h⁻¹ |
| 4 | Volumetric productivity | $P_v = \dfrac{P_{final} - P_{initial}}{t}$ | g L⁻¹ h⁻¹ |
| 5 | Yield coefficient (biomass on substrate) | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g g⁻¹ |
| 6 | Yield coefficient (product on substrate) | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | g g⁻¹ |
| 7 | Mass balance on substrate | $S_0 = S + \dfrac{X}{Y_{X/S}} + \dfrac{P}{Y_{P/S}} + mX$ | g L⁻¹ |
| 8 | Oxygen transfer rate (OUR) | $OTR = k_L a (C^* - C_L)$ | mmol O₂ L⁻¹ h⁻¹ |
| 9 | Critical dissolved O₂ (for *S. griseus*) | $C_{L,crit} \approx \mathbf{1.5 - 2.0}$ | mg L⁻¹ |
| 10 | Streptomycin assay (bioassay) | $\log\left( \dfrac{C_{unknown}}{C_{standard}} \right) = \dfrac{(d_{standard} - d_{unknown})}{m}$ | zone-of-inhibition method |
| 11 | pKa of streptomycin guanidino groups | ≈ **12.1, 10.5** | dictates ion-exchange behavior |
| 12 | Streptomycin potency (USP) | **1 mg free base ≡ 1000 IU** | — |
| 13 | Carbon balance check | $C_{glucose} = C_X + C_P + C_{CO_2}$ | mol C basis |
| 14 | Broth viscosity effect on $k_L a$ | $k_L a \propto (P/V)^{0.55} \cdot v_s^{0.33}$ | aerated, non-Newtonian |

> [!WARNING]
> In LaTeX tables, never write `|x|` for absolute value inside a table cell — it breaks the column delimiter. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\lvert x \rvert` instead.

## 2.5 Real-World Bioprocess Significance

- The streptomycin fermentation by *Streptomyces griseus* established the **template for all secondary-metabolite antibiotic industries** — media optimization, morphological control, fed-batch glucose feeding, and pH-shift strategies used in erythromycin, tetracycline, and rifamycin all descend from this work.
- It also pioneered **mycelial-growth control** in industrial actinomycete fermentations — a direct economic problem since over-pelletised growth limits oxygen transfer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 The Streptomycin Biosynthetic Pathway (Step-by-Step Logic)

Streptomycin is a **secondary metabolite** — its production is decoupled from growth (trophophase vs. idiophase). The pathway has ≈ 30 enzymatic steps, but the engineering-relevant core is shown below with a **textual transition row** after each block.

> [!NOTE]
> The D-glucose → streptomycin conversion is a textbook example used by KTU examiners to test whether students can draw **precursor–intermediate–product arrows** with **enzyme catalysts** and **cofactors** written above each arrow.

### Derivation Block 1 — Carbon Skeleton Origin

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{D-Glucose} 
&\xrightarrow{\text{glucose-6-P \rightarrow fructose-6-P}} 
\text{Fructose-6-P} 
\\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\text{transketolase + transaldolase (PPP)}}
\text{Ribose-5-P} \;\; \text{(early origin of streptose)}
\\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\text{isomerisation + amination (glutamine :Fru-6P amidotransferase)}}
\text{Glucosamine-6-P}
\end{aligned}
}
$$
**Conversion logic:** Glucose enters the **Pentose Phosphate Pathway (PPP)**. Pentoses (C5) provide the **streptose** pentose unit; hexose rearrangement + nitrogen insertion produces the **glucosamine** unit; the central cyclohexane (streptidine) is assembled separately from myo-inositol.

### Derivation Block 2 — Streptidine Assembly (the Central Aminocyclitol)

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucose-6-P} 
&\xrightarrow{\text{NAD}^{+}-\text{linked cyclisation}}
\text{2-Keto-myo-inositol}
\\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\text{transamination (glutamine as NH}_3\text{ donor)}}
\text{2-Inosamine}
\\[4pt]
&\xrightarrow{\text{2 sequential transamidations (L-arginine as guanidino donor, ATP)}}
\text{Streptidine} \;\; (+ 2 \text{ guanidino groups})
\end{aligned}
}
$$
**Conversion logic:** The central cyclohexane skeleton is built from glucose-6-P via the inositol-monophosphatase pathway. **L-arginine** is the specific biological guanidino donor — an important metabolic point in industrial media (excess arginine boosts streptomycin titer).

### Derivation Block 3 — Sugar Activation and Condensation

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Glucosamine} &\xrightarrow{\text{ATP, Mg}^{2+}} \text{Glucosamine-6-P}
\\[4pt]
\text{N-Methyl-glucosamine} &\xleftarrow{\text{SAM-methyltransferase (SAM donor)}} \text{Glucosamine}
\\[4pt]
\text{Streptose} &\xrightarrow{\text{NAD}^{+}+\text{formyl-THF}} \text{5-deoxy-3-formyl-pentose}
\end{aligned}
}
$$
**Conversion logic:** Each sugar is **phosphorylated** to its nucleotidyl-diphosphate (UDP/NDP) form before it can be ligated to the next unit. S-adenosyl-methionine (SAM) is the methyl donor for the N-methyl group. **Formyl-THF** donates the C1 unit for the unique formyl group on streptose.

### Derivation Block 4 — Final Coupling (Two-Step Glycosylation)

$$
\boxed{
\begin{aligned}
\text{Streptidine} + \text{UDP-streptose} 
&\xrightarrow{\text{streptomycin-6-phosphotransferase (StrA/B/C)}}
\text{Streptomycin-6-P}
\\[4pt]
\text{Streptomycin-6-P} 
&\xrightarrow{\text{streptomycin-6-phosphatase (StrK)}}
\text{Streptomycin (active)}
\end{aligned}
}
$$
**Conversion logic:** The **last step is a dephosphorylation** — without it, the product is biologically inactive. Industrial strains that hyper-produce streptomycin are often engineered to **over-express StrK** and **deregulate StrR** (the pathway-specific transcriptional activator).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Process Parameter Effects on Streptomycin Titer

| Parameter | Typical Value for *S. griseus* | Effect on Titer | KTU-level Explanation |
|---|---|---|---|
| Temperature | **28 °C** | Optimal | Above 30 °C → enzymes denature; below 25 °C → growth slow |
| pH | **6.8 – 7.3** (initial) → drops to 5.5 → rises to 8.5 | pH-shift tied to idiophase | Initial drop = growth; rise = streptomycin production |
| Dissolved O₂ | > **2 mg L⁻¹** (broth) | Essential for Krebs cycle & ATP | Hypoxia shifts metabolism to ethanol & lowers titer |
| Glucose concentration | Start 1–2 %, feed to <1 % | **Catabolite repression** by glucose | High glucose represses streptomycin synthesis |
| Soybean meal / glycine | 2–4 % | Direct precursors (amino + guanidino) | Critical nitrogen sources |
| Inoculum age | 24–30 h vegetative | Synchronous idiophase entry | Old spores → poor synchronization |
| Time to peak titer | **120 – 168 h** | Production phase length | Industrial fermenters run ≈ 7 days |
| Final titer (industrial) | **5 – 8 g L⁻¹** (modern strains > 15 g L⁻¹) | — | Through decades of strain improvement |

## 3.3 Derivation — Volumetric Productivity (Worked Numerical)

**Problem (KTU-pattern):** A 100 m³ fermenter is run for 168 h. The final streptomycin titer is 5,000 mg L⁻¹. Initial titer is negligible. Compute the volumetric productivity.

$$
\begin{aligned}
P_v &= \dfrac{P_{final} - P_{initial}}{t} \\[6pt]
    &= \dfrac{5{,}000 \; \text{mg L}^{-1} - 0 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}}{168 \; \text{h}} \\[6pt]
    &= 29.76 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1} \\[6pt]
    &= 0.0298 \; \text{g L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Total mass produced:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{total} &= P_{final} \times V \times \eta_{recovery} \\[6pt]
          &= 5.0 \; \text{g L}^{-1} \times 100{,}000 \; \text{L} \times 0.85 \\[6pt]
          &= 425 \; \text{kg of pure streptomycin sulfate}
\end{aligned}
$$

where $\eta_{recovery} \approx 0.80 - 0.90$ is the typical downstream yield (extraction + ion-exchange + crystallization).

## 3.4 Derivation — Oxygen Demand from OTR Equation

Given $k_L a = 250 \; \text{h}^{-1}$, $C^* = 8.3 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}$, and $C_L = 3.0 \; \text{mg L}^{-1}$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{OTR} &= k_L a \, (C^* - C_L) \\[4pt]
           &= 250 \times (8.3 - 3.0) \\[4pt]
           &= 1{,}325 \; \text{mg O}_2 \; \text{L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1} \\[4pt]
           &= 1.325 \; \text{g O}_2 \; \text{L}^{-1}\text{h}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

This OTR must be matched by the OUR of *S. griseus* during the trophophase (highest demand) or oxygen limitation will occur.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

## 4.3 Bioprocess Fermentation Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Working Cell Bank S. griseus] --> B[Vegetative Inoculum 28C, 24-30h]
    B --> C[Seed Fermenter 1 to 5 percent v/v]
    C --> D[Production Fermenter 100 m3]
    D --> E[Trophophase 0-48h, Rapid Growth]
    E --> F[Idiophase 48-168h, Streptomycin Synthesis]
    F --> G[Broth Harvest]
    G --> H[Rotary Vacuum Filter]
    H --> I[Filtrate with Streptomycin]
    H --> J[Cell Cake Discarded]

    M --> N[Vacuum Concentration]

    P --> Q[Drying Milling Sterile Packaging]

```

    A --> C[Glucose Catabolite Repression]

    D --> F[Streptomycin Biosynthetic Gene Cluster strA-strR]
    E --> F
    F --> G[Streptomycin Production]
    style A fill:#e0e0e0,stroke:#333
    style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#007acc
