<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 3]`
**Define aminoglycoside antibiotics. Name the first member of this class and its discoverer.**

**Model Answer:**
> Aminoglycosides are a class of antibiotics containing an **aminocyclitol nucleus** linked to **amino sugars via glycosidic bonds**. They are produced mainly by *Streptomyces* and *Micromonospora* species and act by binding the **30S ribosomal subunit**, causing misreading of mRNA.
>
> **First member:** **Streptomycin**, discovered in **1943** by **Albert Schatz, Elizabeth Bugie, and Selman Waksman** from *Streptomyces griseus*. Waksman received the **1952 Nobel Prize in Physiology or Medicine** for this work.

---

**Q2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 3]`
**List any three distinguishing structural features of streptomycin.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Streptidine** — a central 2,4-diguanidino-3,5,6-trihydroxycyclohexane (aminocyclitol) — gives the basic aminocyclitol core.
2. **Streptose** — a 5-deoxy-3-**formyl** pentose — uniquely carries a formyl group essential for activity.
3. **N-methyl-L-glucosamine** — the second amino sugar; its N-methyl group defines biological specificity.
4. The **streptose–streptidine glycosidic bond** is unusually stable, enabling downstream acid-stable recovery.

*(Any three of the above earn full 3 marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Question A (14 Marks, Internal Choice Option A)

`[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper]`

### Q.A (a) — 7 Marks

**Describe in detail the mechanism of action of streptomycin against bacterial cells. Why is it inactive against anaerobes?**

**Model Solution:**

Streptomycin is a **bactericidal** antibiotic. Its mechanism proceeds through the following ordered steps:

**(i) Electrostatic binding (1 mark):**
At physiological pH 7.4, the guanidino and amino groups of streptomycin are fully protonated, making the molecule highly **polycationic**. The outer membrane of Gram-negative bacteria is rich in **negatively charged lipopolysaccharide (LPS) and phospholipids**. The drug is drawn to and binds the bacterial surface.

**(ii) Energy-Dependent Phase-I uptake (1 mark):**
The first (EDP-I) uptake step is driven by the **proton-motive force (PMF)**. Streptomycin displaces Mg²⁺ from LPS, then crosses the outer membrane via the **OmpF porin**.

**(iii) Energy-Dependent Phase-II uptake (1 mark):**
In EDP-II, the drug is transported across the cytoplasmic membrane by an **electron-transport-coupled process**. This is the step that requires oxygen — which is why **streptomycin is inactive against strict anaerobes** (no functional ETC, no PMF) and only weakly active in low-pH/necrotic tissue.

**(iv) Ribosomal binding (2 marks):**
Streptomycin binds the **30S subunit** of the 70S ribosome, specifically the **S12 protein (rpsL)** and the **16S rRNA** decoding region. It freezes the ribosome in an initiation-like state, while permitting non-cognate tRNAs to be accepted.

**(v) Misreading and membrane disruption (2 marks):**
Single-base mispairing causes incorporation of wrong amino acids. Additionally, the drug inserts into the cytoplasmic membrane, causing **K⁺ leak, nucleotide leakage, and loss of membrane potential** — the bactericidal effect that distinguishes aminoglycosides from most bacteriostatic drugs.

**Inactivity against anaerobes (1 mark):** strictly anaerobic bacteria lack an electron-transport chain and therefore cannot generate the **PMF** needed for EDP-II uptake. Consequently, intracellular streptomycin concentrations never reach the threshold for ribosomal binding.

---

### Q.A (b) — 7 Marks

**Explain the biosynthesis of streptomycin starting from D-glucose, indicating the origin of each of the three building blocks and the role of key enzymes.**

**Model Solution:**

The pathway is a 30-step **secondary-metabolic** route, decoupled from growth. The three building blocks originate as follows:

**(1) Streptose (2 marks):**
Generated from **D-glucose via the Pentose Phosphate Pathway**. Glucose-6-P → fructose-6-P → transketolase/transaldolase → **ribose-5-P** → ring rearrangement to 5-deoxy-pentose. The C-3 formyl group is added by **NAD⁺-dependent oxidation** using **formyl-tetrahydrofolate** as the C1 donor. The product is then activated as **dTDP-L-streptose** by a nucleotidyltransferase.

**(2) N-methyl-L-glucosamine (2 marks):**
Derived from **fructose-6-P** via the amino-sugar pathway. Glutamine donates the amido-N to yield **glucosamine-6-P** (glutamine : F-6P amidotransferase). N-methylation uses **S-adenosyl-methionine (SAM)** as the methyl donor (encoded by *glmM* / SAM-methyltransferase). The sugar is activated as **UDP-N-methyl-L-glucosamine**.

**(3) Streptidine (2 marks):**
Synthesized from **glucose-6-P via the inositol pathway** (cyclization to **2-keto-myo-inositol**, transamination with glutamine to **inosamine**, then two sequential transamidations from **L-arginine** using ATP, generating two guanidino groups). This is the rate-controlling nitrogen-insertion step — industrial media are often **supplemented with L-arginine** to boost titers.

**(4) Final assembly and dephosphorylation (1 mark):**
Coupling proceeds by a series of glycosyltransferases **StrA / StrB / StrC**, joining streptidine + UDP-streptose → streptomycin-6-phosphate. The final dephosphorylation by **StrK** (streptomycin-6-phosphatase) yields **active streptomycin**. StrR is the **pathway-specific transcriptional activator** whose over-expression defines hyper-producing mutant strains.

---

## 5.3 Part B Question B (14 Marks, Internal Choice Option B)

`[KTU University Exam – June 2025, Model Paper]`

### Q.B (a) — 7 Marks

**Describe the industrial production of streptomycin by submerged-culture fermentation. Cover the process parameters, media, and inoculum development.**

**Model Solution:**

**(1) Producer strain (1 mark):**
Classical strain *Streptomyces griseus* ATCC 10137 (modern industrial strains are mutated and genetically refined to titres > 15 g L⁻¹). Maintained as **lyophilized working cell banks** or as **frozen glycerol stocks at −80 °C**.

**(2) Inoculum development (2 stages, 2 marks):**
- **Stage 1 — Vegetative inoculum:** A loopful of spores is inoculated into 100–500 mL of vegetative medium (yeast extract, glucose, pH 7.0) in a shake flask at **28 °C**, **220 rpm**, for **24–30 h** until a dense, dispersed mycelium forms (no pellets). Pellet formation is actively avoided because it limits oxygen transfer.
- **Stage 2 — Seed fermenter:** 1–5 % v/v of the vegetative culture is transferred to a 1–5 m³ seed fermenter, then to a 50–100 m³ production fermenter.

**(3) Production medium (2 marks):**
- **Carbon source:** Glucose or dextrin (start 1–2 %); fed-batch feeding keeps residual glucose **< 1 %** to avoid catabolite repression.
- **Nitrogen sources:** Soybean meal, corn-steep liquor, distillers' solubles, glycine, **L-arginine** (key precursor for guanidino groups).
- **Mineral salts:** NaCl, K₂HPO₄, MgSO₄, CaCO₃ (buffer + Ca²⁺ cofactor), FeSO₄, ZnSO₄.
- **Anti-foam:** Silicone-based (e.g., SAG-471) or polyol.

**(4) Process parameters (2 marks):**
- **Temperature:** **28 °C** (slightly above room temperature for *Streptomyces*).
- **pH:** starts at **6.8–7.3**, drops to ~5.5 during trophophase, **rises to 7.5–8.5** in the idiophase — the pH trajectory is a built-in phase indicator.
- **Aeration:** **0.5–1.0 vvm**; dissolved O₂ maintained at **> 2 mg L⁻¹**.
- **Agitation:** Rushton turbines, 150–300 rpm; impeller tip speed 2–5 m s⁻¹.
- **Duration:** **120–168 h** (5–7 days).

---

### Q.B (b) — 7 Marks

**Outline the downstream processing and recovery of pure streptomycin from fermentation broth. List the unit operations in order with a brief rationale.**

**Model Solution:**

The DSP train is a classic **antibiotic recovery cascade** (7 marks distributed across 7 unit ops):

1. **Broth harvest (0.5):** The whole broth is collected from the fermenter at peak titer; cell density and pH are recorded.

2. **Mycelium removal by rotary vacuum filtration (1.0):** A precoat of diatomaceous earth is used. Streptomycin is in the **filtrate** (extracellular), so the mycelial cake is discarded. The filtrate is acidified to pH 2–3 to keep streptomycin in its cationic, soluble form.

3. **Cation-exchange chromatography (1.5):** A **sulfonic-acid-type resin (e.g., Dowex 50, Amberlite IR-120)** in the Na⁺ form binds streptomycin via its protonated guanidino/amino groups. The column is washed with water, and the drug is eluted with **dilute HCl (0.1–0.5 N)** or dilute NaCl.

4. **Activated-carbon decolorization (0.5):** Removes pigments and impurities from the eluate. Streptomycin is poorly adsorbed; impurities are retained on carbon.

5. **Vacuum evaporation / concentration (0.5):** Reduces the eluate volume at 40–50 °C under vacuum (thermal lability of streptomycin dictates low temperature).

6. **Crystallization (2.0):** Addition of **methanol/acetone** to the concentrated eluate precipitates **streptomycin sulfate** (or streptomycin hydrochloride). The salt form is chosen based on pharmaceutical formulation needs. Crystallization is the **purification step that determines final purity** — typically re-crystallized 2–3 times to meet pharmacopoeia standards.

7. **Drying, milling, sterile packaging (1.0):** Vacuum tray drying or fluidized-bed drying. The dry powder is filled into sterile amber vials under nitrogen to protect from moisture and light.

**Final in-process checks:** bioassay using *Bacillus subtilis* ATCC 6633 (zone of inhibition), HPLC purity, moisture content, heavy metals, and pyrogen testing.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Confusing bacteriostatic vs. bactericidal:** Streptomycin is **bactericidal** — many students incorrectly call it static.
> 2. **Forgetting the three building blocks in biosynthesis questions:** Examiners expect streptose + streptidine + N-methyl-glucosamine, **not** just "glucose → streptomycin".
> 3. **Ignoring the EDP-I/EDP-II transport explanation** when asked "why is streptomycin inactive against anaerobes?" — the answer is the absence of an electron-transport chain, not just "because there's no O₂".
> 4. **Skipping the pH trajectory (7.3 → 5.5 → 8.5)** in fermentation answers — this pH shift is the **key indicator of idiophase** and is a favourite KTU question.
> 5. **Incomplete DSP reasoning:** Each unit operation must have a *purpose* — not just be listed.
> 6. **Wrong enzyme names:** "Transaminase" is too generic; cite **glutamine : F-6P amidotransferase**, **StrA/StrB/StrC** glycosyltransferases, **StrK phosphatase**, and the **StrR** activator.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- **Class:** Aminoglycoside-aminocyclitol antibiotic, **4,5-disubstituted** (unique to streptomycin).
- **Discoverer:** Schatz, Bugie, **Waksman**, 1943, from *Streptomyces griseus*.
- **Three units:** **Streptose** (formyl-pentose), **Streptidine** (diguanidinated aminocyclitol), **N-methyl-L-glucosamine**.
- **Molecular weight:** **581.57 g/mol**; formula **C₂₁H₃₉N₇O₁₂**.
- **Mechanism:** Polycationic → binds LPS → EDP-I/EDP-II uptake → binds 30S S12 → mRNA misreading → membrane disruption → **bactericidal**.
- **Inactive against anaerobes** (no PMF/ETC); **ineffective in low pH / abscesses**.
- **Production:** Submerged aerobic fed-batch fermentation of *S. griseus*; **28 °C**, **pH 7.3 → 5.5 → 8.5**, **120–168 h**, **> 2 mg L⁻¹ DO**.
- **Carbon source:** Glucose, fed to < 1 % (catabolite-repression control).
- **Nitrogen source:** Soybean meal, corn-steep liquor, **L-arginine** (guanidino precursor).
- **Industrial titre:** 5–8 g L⁻¹ (classical) → > 15 g L⁻¹ (improved strains).
- **Critical pathway genes:** **strA, strB, strC** (glycosyltransferases), **strK** (dephosphorylation), **strR** (activator).
- **DSP train:** Filtration → cation exchange → carbon decolourisation → evaporation → crystallisation (sulfate salt) → drying.
- **Assay:** *Bacillus subtilis* bioassay (zone of inhibition) + HPLC.
- **Therapeutic use:** Tuberculosis (combination therapy), tularemia, plague, endocarditis (with β-lactam), urinary-tract infections.
- **Toxicities (clinical):** **Ototoxicity** (CN VIII damage), **nephrotoxicity**, neuromuscular blockade.
- **Formula to memorize:** Leudeking–Piret, OTR = $k_L a(C^* - C_L)$, Monod, and volumetric productivity.
- **Key KTU tag-words:** "trophophase vs. idiophase", "catabolite repression", "EDP-I/EDP-II", "streptomycin-6-phosphate → streptomycin (StrK)".
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Quinines
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Enzymes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Enzymes** are high-molecular-weight, globular protein molecules (with the exception of a few ribozymes) produced by living cells that act as **highly specific biological catalysts**, accelerating biochemical reactions by lowering the activation energy without being consumed or permanently altered in the overall process.

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – OEFTT726 Bioprocess Engineering**, Module 3 focuses on enzymes as industrial biocatalysts. The International Union of Biochemistry and Molecular Biology (IUBMB) classifies enzymes into **six major classes** based on the type of reaction they catalyze.

| Class | Reaction Type | Engineering Example |
|:-----:|:--------------|:-------------------|
| **EC 1** | Oxidoreductases | Glucose oxidase in biosensors |
| **EC 2** | Transferases | Transaminases in pharma |
| **EC 3** | Hydrolases | Lipases in biodiesel |
| **EC 4** | Lyases | Pectate lyase in juice clarification |
| **EC 5** | Isomerases | Glucose isomerase in HFCS production |
| **EC 6** | Ligases | DNA ligase in biotech |

> [!NOTE]
> The catalytic efficiency of an enzyme is measured by the **turnover number (k_cat)**, which represents the number of substrate molecules converted to product per enzyme molecule per unit time when the enzyme is fully saturated. Typical values range from **1 to 10,000,000 s⁻¹**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you have a heavy rock (substrate) blocking a doorway that you need to push through. A normal person would push hard, but a strong construction worker (the enzyme) can move the rock effortlessly because they have the right tool (active site).

- The **rock** = the substrate ($S$)
- The **construction worker** = the enzyme ($E$)
- The **tool (e.g., a crowbar)** = the active site of the enzyme
- The **movement of the rock** = the catalytic conversion to product ($P$)

After the rock is moved, the worker is **free to do it again** — exactly like an enzyme is regenerated at the end of the reaction. A small amount of enzyme (a few milligrams) can convert millions of substrate molecules per minute — this is why enzymes are central to industrial bioprocesses.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Activation Energy Reduction**: Enzymes typically reduce activation energy ($E_a$) from **50–100 kJ/mol** to roughly **15–25 kJ/mol**.
- **Molecular Weight Range**: Most industrial enzymes range from **10,000 to 800,000 Da**.
- **Optimum pH**: Most enzymes operate between **pH 6.0–8.0**, although pepsin works at **pH 1.5–2.0**.
- **Optimum Temperature**: Usually **30–60 °C**, although thermophilic enzymes (e.g., Taq polymerase from *Thermus aquaticus*) remain active above **95 °C**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Mechanism: The Michaelis–Menten Framework

The single-substrate enzymatic reaction proceeds through a discrete enzyme–substrate intermediate:

$$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \underset{k_{-2}}{\overset{k_{2}}{\rightleftharpoons}} E + P$$

### Step-by-Step Logic of the Kinetics

1. **Binding Phase ($k_1$, $k_{-1}$)**: Free enzyme ($E$) collides with substrate ($S$) to form the reversible Michaelis complex ($ES$). This is governed by diffusion and structural complementarity (the *lock-and-key* / *induced-fit* model).

2. **Catalytic Conversion ($k_2$)**: The $ES$ complex undergoes chemical transformation to release product ($P$). The rate constant $k_2$ is renamed $k_{cat}$ (turnover number).

3. **Regeneration**: The free enzyme $E$ is liberated and re-enters the catalytic cycle. Thus, the enzyme is a true catalyst — it is **stoichiometrically unchanged** at the end.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the Michaelis–Menten equation works for KTU**: The derivation assumes the **steady-state approximation**, where the rate of formation of $ES$ equals the rate of its breakdown (i.e., $\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0$). This is valid for all times except the initial transient and at very low substrate concentrations.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Limits |
|:-:|:--------|:------------|:---------------|
| 1 | $v_0 = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$ | Michaelis–Menten rate equation | $v_0$ in $\mu mol/L/min$ |
| 2 | $K_m = \dfrac{k_{-1} + k_{2}}{k_{1}}$ | Michaelis constant | $mol/L$ (or $M$) |
| 3 | $V_{max} = k_{cat}[E]_T$ | Maximum reaction velocity | $\mu mol/L/min$ |
| 4 | $k_{cat} / K_m$ | Catalytic efficiency (specificity constant) | $M^{-1} s^{-1}$ |
| 5 | $\dfrac{1}{v_0} = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \dfrac{1}{[S]} + \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$ | Lineweaver–Burk linearization | Plot of $1/v_0$ vs $1/[S]$ |
| 6 | $v_0 = V_{max} - K_m \cdot \dfrac{v_0}{[S]}$ | Eadie–Hofstee linearization | Plot of $v_0$ vs $v_0/[S]$ |
| 7 | $\dfrac{[S]}{v_0} = \dfrac{[S]}{V_{max}} + \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}}$ | Hanes–Woolf linearization | Plot of $[S]/v_0$ vs $[S]$ |

> [!NOTE]
> In the above table, the absolute-value bars have been written using the standard math sense; do not use the `|` character inside markdown tables as it breaks column parsing. Use `\vert` or `\mid` for absolute-value separators.

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering Fields

- **Pharmaceuticals**: Production of semi-synthetic β-lactam antibiotics using penicillin acylase (EC 3.5.1.11).
- **Food Industry**: High-Fructose Corn Syrup (HFCS) via immobilized glucose isomerase — multi-billion-dollar industry.
- **Biofuels**: Lipase-mediated transesterification of vegetable oils into biodiesel.
- **Detergents**: Alkaline proteases and lipases (e.g., subtilisin from *Bacillus licheniformis*) — about **30%** of the global enzyme market.
- **Medical Diagnostics**: Glucose oxidase in blood-glucose biosensors for diabetic patients.
- **Leather & Textile**: Proteases for dehairing hides and bio-polishing fabrics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Michaelis–Menten Equation

### Step 1: Write the elementary steps and define rate constants

$$E + S \xrightarrow{k_1} ES \quad \text{(forward binding)}$$

$$ES \xrightarrow{k_{-1}} E + S \quad \text{(reverse dissociation)}$$

$$ES \xrightarrow{k_{cat}} E + P \quad \text{(catalytic conversion)}$$

### Step 2: Define the initial reaction velocity $v_0$

The rate of product formation is determined solely by the breakdown of the $ES$ complex:

$$v_0 = \frac{d[P]}{dt} = k_{cat} \cdot [ES]$$

### Step 3: Apply the steady-state approximation to [ES]

At steady state, $\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = 0$. Therefore, the rate of $ES$ formation equals its rate of consumption:

$$\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1[E][S] - (k_{-1} + k_{cat})[ES] = 0$$

### Step 4: Solve the steady-state equation for [ES]

$$[ES] = \frac{k_1[E][S]}{k_{-1} + k_{cat}}$$

### Step 5: Introduce the Michaelis constant

$$K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_{cat}}{k_1}$$

Substituting into the equation for $[ES]$:

$$[ES] = \frac{[E][S]}{K_m}$$

### Step 6: Apply the enzyme mass balance

The total enzyme concentration is the sum of free and bound enzyme:

$$[E]_T = [E] + [ES]$$

Therefore:

$$[E] = [E]_T - [ES]$$

### Step 7: Substitute into the [ES] expression

$$[ES] = \frac{([E]_T - [ES]) \cdot [S]}{K_m}$$

### Step 8: Rearrange to isolate [ES]

$$K_m \cdot [ES] = [E]_T \cdot [S] - [ES] \cdot [S]$$

$$[ES] \cdot (K_m + [S]) = [E]_T \cdot [S]$$

$$[ES] = \frac{[E]_T \cdot [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

### Step 9: Multiply by $k_{cat}$ to obtain $v_0$

$$v_0 = k_{cat}[ES] = \frac{k_{cat}[E]_T [S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

### Step 10: Recognize $k_{cat}[E]_T = V_{max}$

Since $[E]_T$ is constant and the maximum velocity $V_{max}$ occurs when all enzyme is in the $ES$ form (i.e., when $[S] \gg K_m$):

$$v_0 = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Boundary-Condition Validation**: When $[S] \to \infty$, the term $K_m + [S] \to [S]$, so $v_0 \to V_{max}$. When $[S] = K_m$, then $v_0 = V_{max}/2$. Hence, $K_m$ is **numerically equal to the substrate concentration at which the reaction velocity is half of $V_{max}$**.

## 3.2 Derivation of Lineweaver–Burk Linearization

Starting from the Michaelis–Menten equation:

$$\frac{1}{v_0} = \frac{K_m + [S]}{V_{max}[S]}$$

$$\frac{1}{v_0} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{[S]}{V_{max}[S]}$$

$$\frac{1}{v_0} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}$$

This is a **straight-line equation** $y = mx + c$ with:
- $y = \dfrac{1}{v_0}$
- $x = \dfrac{1}{[S]}$
- Slope $m = \dfrac{K_m}{V_{max}}$
- $y$-intercept $c = \dfrac{1}{V_{max}}$
- $x$-intercept $x_0 = -\dfrac{1}{K_m}$

## 3.3 Derivation of the Inhibition-Modified Rate Equations

| Inhibition Type | Modified Equation | Effect on $V_{max}$ | Effect on $K_m$ |
|:----------------|:------------------|:-------------------:|:---------------:|
| **Competitive** | $v_0 = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m(1 + [I]/K_i) + [S]}$ | Unchanged | Apparent $K_m$ increases |
| **Non-competitive (Pure)** | $v_0 = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{(K_m + [S])(1 + [I]/K_i)}$ | Decreases | Unchanged |
| **Uncompetitive** | $v_0 = \dfrac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S](1 + [I]/K_i)}$ | Decreases | Apparent $K_m$ decreases |

Where:
- $[I]$ = inhibitor concentration
- $K_i$ = inhibition constant (the dissociation constant of the $EI$ complex)
- The ratio $\alpha = 1 + [I]/K_i$ is the **modulator factor**

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Enzyme Catalysis Workflow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Substrate S in Bulk Solution] --> B[Diffusion to Enzyme Surface]
    B --> C[Active Site Recognition via Induced Fit]
    C --> D{Formation of ES Complex}
    D -->|Forward Reaction k1| E[Michaelis Complex ES]
    E -->|Catalysis kcat| F[Transition State TS]
    F -->|Product Release| G[Product P Released]
    G --> H[Free Enzyme E Regenerated]
    H --> B
    D -->|Reverse Reaction k-1| I[Dissociation back to E + S]
    I --> B
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Effect of pH and Temperature on Enzyme Activity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    T[Temperature Rise] --> P1[Increase in Kinetic Energy]
    P1 --> P2[More Effective Collisions]
    P2 --> P3[Reaction Rate Increases]
    P3 --> P4{Optimum Temperature Topt}
    P4 -->|Below Topt| P5[Rise in Rate]
    P4 -->|Above Topt| P6[Denaturation of Protein]
    P6 --> P7[Sharp Drop in Activity]

    Q[pH Variation] --> Q1[Change in Ionization of Active Site Residues]
    Q1 --> Q2{Affects Substrate Binding and Catalysis}
    Q2 -->|At pHpH opt| Q3[Maximum Activity]
    Q2 -->|Away from pH opt| Q4[Decreased Activity or Inactivation]
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Enzyme Immobilization Techniques

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Free Enzyme E] --> S{Immobilization Method}
    S --> S1[Adsorption on Carrier]
    S --> S2[Covalent Bonding to Support]
    S --> S3[Entrapment in Gel Matrix]
    S --> S4[Encapsulation in Microcapsule]
    S --> S5[Cross-linking using Glutaraldehyde]

    S1 --> T1[Weak Binding, Easy Desorption]
    S2 --> T2[Strong Bond, Stable, Lower Activity Yield]
    S3 --> T3[Substrate Diffusional Limitation]
    S4 --> T4[Useful for Co-factors and Multienzyme Systems]
    S5 --> T5[Carrier-free, High Stability]
```

## 4.4 Lineweaver–Burk Plot Schematic (Mental Model)

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[1/S axis X] --> B[Plot of 1/v0 on Y axis]
    B --> C[Straight Line with positive slope]
    C --> D[Y-intercept at 1 over Vmax]
    C --> E[X-intercept at minus 1 over Km]
    C --> F[Slope equals Km divided by Vmax]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight**: For KTU 2024 Scheme problems, always remember that competitive inhibition gives **identical $y$-intercepts** on Lineweaver–Burk plots, while non-competitive inhibition gives **identical $x$-intercepts**. This is a high-yield exam observation.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]
**Define the term "Turnover Number" ($k_{cat}$) of an enzyme and state its typical range.**

**Model Answer**:
The turnover number ($k_{cat}$) is defined as the maximum number of substrate molecules converted to product per enzyme molecule per unit time when the enzyme is fully saturated with substrate ($[S] \gg K_m$). Mathematically, $k_{cat} = V_{max}/[E]_T$.
Typical values range from **1 s⁻¹** (e.g., lysozyme) to over **10⁷ s⁻¹** (e.g., catalase).

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]
**Differentiate between competitive and non-competitive inhibition of enzymes.**

**Model Answer**:
- **Competitive Inhibition**: The inhibitor resembles the substrate and binds to the **active site** of the free enzyme ($E$), forming an $EI$ complex. $V_{max}$ remains unchanged but the apparent $K_m$ increases.
- **Non-competitive Inhibition**: The inhibitor binds to a site **other than the active site** (allosteric site), forming $EI$ or $ESI$ complexes. $V_{max}$ decreases but $K_m$ remains unchanged.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]

**Derive the Michaelis–Menten rate equation for a single-substrate enzyme-catalyzed reaction. Discuss the significance of $K_m$ and $V_{max}$. An enzyme has $K_m = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\ M$ and $V_{max} = 0.22\ \mu mol/L/min$. Calculate the reaction velocity when $[S] = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}\ M$.**

#### Part (a) — Derivation and Significance (7 Marks)

**Derivation** (as shown in Section 3.1, Steps 1–10):

The reaction proceeds as:

$$E + S \underset{k_{-1}}{\overset{k_{1}}{\rightleftharpoons}} ES \xrightarrow{k_{cat}} E + P$$

Applying steady-state approximation to $[ES]$, we obtain:

$$v_0 = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

**Significance of $K_m$**:
1. It is numerically equal to the substrate concentration at which $v_0 = V_{max}/2$.
2. It reflects the **binding affinity** between enzyme and substrate — a lower $K_m$ indicates a stronger binding.
3. It is independent of enzyme concentration but depends on temperature, pH, and ionic strength.

**Significance of $V_{max}$**:
1. It is the maximum attainable velocity when all active sites are saturated with substrate.
2. It is directly proportional to total enzyme concentration: $V_{max} = k_{cat}[E]_T$.
3. It enables calculation of the turnover number once $[E]_T$ is known.

#### Part (b) — Numerical Problem (7 Marks)

**Given**:
- $K_m = 2.5 \times 10^{-5}\ M$
- $V_{max} = 0.22\ \mu mol/L/min$
- $[S] = 3.0 \times 10^{-5}\ M$

**Apply Michaelis–Menten Equation**:

$$v_0 = \frac{V_{max}[S]}{K_m + [S]}$$

$$v_0 = \frac{(0.22)(3.0 \times 10^{-5})}{(2.5 \times 10^{-5}) + (3.0 \times 10^{-5})}$$

$$v_0 = \frac{6.6 \times 10^{-6}}{5.5 \times 10^{-5}}$$

$$v_0 = 0.12\ \mu mol/L/min$$

**Interpretation**: Since $[S]$ is slightly greater than $K_m$, the enzyme operates at approximately **55% of $V_{max}$**.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]

**Describe the various methods of enzyme immobilization. Discuss their relative merits and demerits. Explain how Lineweaver–Burk plot helps in distinguishing between competitive and non-competitive inhibition.**

#### Part (a) — Enzyme Immobilization Methods (7 Marks)

**1. Adsorption**: Enzyme is physically adsorbed onto an inert carrier (e.g., activated charcoal, ion-exchange resins).
- *Merits*: Simple, cheap, no reagents required.
- *Demerits*: Weak binding; enzyme desorbs easily under changes in pH, ionic strength, or temperature.

**2. Covalent Bonding**: Enzyme is covalently linked to a support (e.g., glutaraldehyde-activated support) via functional groups (–NH₂, –COOH, –SH).
- *Merits*: Strong binding, no leakage.
- *Demerits*: Often reduces activity due to conformational changes.

**3. Entrapment**: Enzyme is trapped within the lattice of a polymer gel (e.g., calcium alginate, polyacrylamide).
- *Merits*: Wide applicability, low cost.
- *Demerits*: Diffusion limitations for high-MW substrates.

**4. Encapsulation**: Enzyme is enclosed within semi-permeable microcapsules (e.g., liposomes, nylon microcapsules).
- *Merits*: Useful for multi-enzyme systems with cofactors.
- *Demerits*: Limited mechanical strength, only low-MW substrates diffuse.

**5. Cross-linking (CLEA)**: Carrier-free immobilization using bifunctional reagents like glutaraldehyde to form **Cross-Linked Enzyme Aggregates**.
- *Merits*: High stability, high enzyme loading, no carrier dilution.
- *Demerits*: Possible mass-transfer limitations and brittleness.

#### Part (b) — Lineweaver–Burk Plot for Inhibition (7 Marks)

Starting from the modified Michaelis–Menten equations:

**For Competitive Inhibition**:
$$\frac{1}{v_0} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \left(1 + \frac{[I]}{K_i}\right) \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}$$

- $y$-intercept unchanged (same $V_{max}$)
- Slope increased
- $x$-intercept moves closer to origin (apparent $K_m$ increases)

**For Non-competitive Inhibition**:
$$\frac{1}{v_0} = \frac{K_m}{V_{max}} \cdot \frac{1}{[S]} + \frac{1}{V_{max}}\left(1 + \frac{[I]}{K_i}\right)$$

- $x$-intercept unchanged (same $K_m$)
- $y$-intercept increased (apparent $V_{max}$ decreases)
- Slope increased

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert**: Many students incorrectly write the inhibition equations as increasing both $K_m$ and $V_{max}$. Remember:
> - **Competitive** → only $K_m$ changes; $V_{max}$ is unchanged.
> - **Non-competitive** → only $V_{max}$ changes; $K_m$ is unchanged.
> - **Uncompetitive** → both decrease; lines are parallel.
> Marks are commonly lost for confusing "competitive" with "non-competitive."

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- [x] **Enzymes** are protein biocatalysts; six IUBMB classes govern industrial applications.
- [x] **Michaelis–Menten equation**: $v_0 = V_{max}[S]/(K_m + [S])$ — the cornerstone of enzyme kinetics.
- [x] **$K_m$** = substrate concentration at half-maximum velocity; reflects binding affinity.
- [x] **$V_{max} = k_{cat}[E]_T$**; turnover number $k_{cat}$ is enzyme-specific.
- [x] **Lineweaver–Burk plot** linearizes Michaelis–Menten — the standard diagnostic for inhibition type.
- [x] **Competitive inhibition** → $K_m$ ↑, $V_{max}$ unchanged (same $y$-intercept).
- [x] **Non-competitive inhibition** → $K_m$ unchanged, $V_{max}$ ↓ (same $x$-intercept).
- [x] **Uncompetitive inhibition** → both $K_m$ and $V_{max}$ decrease (parallel lines).
- [x] **Enzyme immobilization** improves reusability, stability, and product purity.
- [x] **CLEA** (Cross-Linked Enzyme Aggregates) is the modern carrier-free technique.
- [x] **Steady-state assumption** is valid only when $[S]_0 \gg [E]_T$.
- [x] Optimum **pH and temperature** must be controlled for industrial bioreactors.
- [x] **Catalytic efficiency** is measured as $k_{cat}/K_m$; theoretical maximum approaches the diffusion limit $\sim 10^8$ to $10^9\ M^{-1}s^{-1}$.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Commercially Important Vitamins and Steroids. Production and Purification of Enzymes and Other By-Products: Microbial Production of Industrial Enzymes: Proteases
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Commercially Important Vitamins and Steroids. Production and Purification of Enzymes and Other By-Products: Microbial Production of Industrial Enzymes: Proteases

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Commercially Important Vitamins

### 1.1.1 Formal Definition
**Vitamins** are low-molecular-weight organic compounds that act as essential micronutrients, required in trace quantities for normal metabolic functioning, growth, and maintenance of living organisms. Commercially, vitamins are produced via **microbial fermentation**, **chemical synthesis**, or **semi-synthetic routes** for applications in pharmaceuticals, nutraceuticals, animal feed, food fortification, and cosmetics.

### 1.1.2 Key Commercially Produced Vitamins

| Vitamin | Chemical Name | Primary Microbial Producer | Applications |
|---------|---------------|---------------------------|--------------|
| **Vitamin B2** | Riboflavin | *Ashbya gossypii*, *Eremothecium ashbyii*, *Bacillus subtilis* | Feed, pharma, food |
| **Vitamin B12** | Cyanocobalamin | *Pseudomonas denitrificans*, *Propionibacterium shermanii* | Anemia treatment, supplements |
| **Vitamin C** | Ascorbic acid | *Acetobacter*, *Gluconobacter* (Reichstein process) | Antioxidant, food |
| **Vitamin B7** | Biotin | *Serratia marcescens*, *Bacillus* sp. | Cosmetics, supplements |
| **Vitamin K** | Menaquinone | *Bacillus subtilis* natto | Blood clotting, supplements |
| **Beta-carotene** | Pro-vitamin A | *Blakeslea trispora*, *Dunaliella salina* | Food colorant, supplement |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The most frequently asked vitamins in board exams are **Riboflavin (B2)** and **Cyanocobalamin (B12)**. Students must remember the specific microbial species, carbon sources, and typical fermentation parameters.

### 1.1.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Vitamins as Cellular Spark Plugs"
Think of a car engine. The gasoline (carbohydrates, proteins) provides the bulk energy, but without **spark plugs** to ignite the fuel at the right moment, the engine cannot run. Vitamins function identically inside living cells — they are **coenzymes and cofactors** that enable enzymes to ignite biochemical reactions. Just as a single spark plug can fire millions of times in a tiny amount, vitamins are needed in **microscopic (microgram) quantities** but are absolutely indispensable.

## 1.2 Commercially Important Steroids

### 1.2.1 Formal Definition
**Steroids** are a class of **lipid-soluble polycyclic organic compounds** based on the **cyclopentanoperhydrophenanthrene (gonane)** ring system — four fused rings (three 6-membered cyclohexane rings designated A, B, C and one 5-membered cyclopentane ring D). Commercially important steroids include **cortisone, hydrocortisone, prednisolone, dexamethasone, and various sex hormones** (testosterone, progesterone, estrogens).

### 1.2.2 Why Microbial Transformation?
Total chemical synthesis of steroids is **multi-step, low-yield, and economically infeasible** because of the multiple chiral centers. Therefore, **microbial biotransformation** is used to perform **regio-specific and stereo-specific hydroxylation, dehydrogenation, and side-chain degradation** reactions that are impossible to achieve with chemical catalysis.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Famous "Upjohn Process"**: The microbial 11α-hydroxylation of progesterone using *Rhizopus arrhizus* (or *Aspergillus niger*) revolutionized the steroid industry in the 1950s, making cortisone affordable. This is one of the **most cited examples of biotransformation** in pharmaceutical history.

### 1.2.3 Intuitive Analogy — "Steroid Molecule as a 3D Puzzle"
Imagine the steroid nucleus as a **3D jigsaw puzzle with 17 carbon atoms and 4 fused rings**. Chemists can build the rough outline chemically, but inserting a single **oxygen atom at a precise 3D position** (e.g., C-11) is like trying to place a piece upside-down in the dark. Microbial enzymes, however, are like **trained craftsmen** who can attach the right piece in the right orientation every time. This is the essence of **regio- and stereo-selectivity** in biotransformation.

## 1.3 Industrial Enzymes and Proteases

### 1.3.1 Formal Definition
**Industrial enzymes** are biologically active proteins used as **biocatalysts** in large-scale industrial processes such as detergents, food processing, textiles, leather, paper, biofuels, and pharmaceuticals. **Proteases** (peptidases; EC 3.4) are enzymes that **hydrolyze peptide bonds** in proteins and polypeptides, cleaving them into smaller peptides and amino acids. They represent the **largest single segment of the industrial enzyme market (~60% of total enzyme sales)**.

### 1.3.2 Intuitive Analogy — "Proteases as Molecular Scissors"
Picture a long pearl necklace where each pearl is an amino acid and the string connecting them is a peptide bond. **Proteases are precision scissors** designed to cut specific strings at specific positions. Some scissors are blunt and cut at any string (non-specific proteases), while others have a sensor that recognizes a particular bead color before cutting (specific proteases). When a detergent removes a blood stain, proteases in that detergent are scissors that cut the egg-protein (albumin) of the stain into tiny invisible fragments that wash away with water.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Always classify proteases by **catalytic type** (Serine, Cysteine, Aspartic, Metalloproteases, Glutamic, Threonine) — this classification is the **most repeated question** in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Riboflavin (Vitamin B2) — Microbial Production

### 2.1.1 Why Microbial Over Chemical Synthesis?
Although riboflavin can be synthesized chemically, **microbial fermentation** dominates industrial production because:
- Single-step fermentation vs. multi-step chemical synthesis
- Use of inexpensive agricultural residues (glucose, molasses, corn steep liquor)
- Produces the natural isomer (active form)
- Lower environmental burden
- Cost-effective at large scale

### 2.1.2 Organism Comparison

| Feature | *Ashbya gossypii* | *Eremothecium ashbyii* | *Bacillus subtilis* |
|---------|-------------------|----------------------|-------------------|
| Type | Filamentous fungus | Filamentous fungus | Bacterium |
| Carbon source | Glucose, vegetable oil | Glucose | Glucose, sucrose |
| Yield (g/L) | **20–40** | 5–10 | 5–15 |
| Production | Industrial standard | Older process | Genetically improved |
| Vitamin form | Mainly free riboflavin | Mainly free riboflavin | Mainly FAD/FMN |

### 2.1.3 Fermentation Process Steps
1. **Inoculum development** — Spore or mycelial seed culture grown in 2–3 stages
2. **Production fermentation** — Submerged fermentation in stainless steel tanks (50,000–200,000 L) for 6–8 days
3. **Critical parameters**:
   - Temperature: **26–30 °C**
   - pH: **6.0–7.0**
   - Aeration: High OTR (>5 mM O₂/L/h)
   - Agitation: 200–500 rpm
4. **Downstream processing** — Filtration → washing → drying → crystallization

> [!NOTE]
> **Iron limitation** is a critical trigger for riboflavin overproduction in *Ashbya gossypii*. Iron-deficient media derepress riboflavin biosynthesis enzymes, increasing yields dramatically.

## 2.2 Vitamin B12 (Cyanocobalamin)

### 2.2.1 Production Organisms
- **Pseudomonas denitrificans** — Aerobic process with betaine as precursor
- **Propionibacterium shermanii** — Anaerobic process
- **Propionibacterium freudenreichii** — Improved strains

### 2.2.2 Biochemical Pathway
Vitamin B12 is synthesized via the **corrin ring pathway** with **uroporphyrinogen III** as the key precursor. The molecule contains a **cobalt ion** at the center of the corrin ring, which must be supplied in the medium as **CoCl₂ (5–20 mg/L)**.

### 2.2.3 Fermentation Parameters
- Carbon source: Glucose, molasses, corn steep liquor
- Nitrogen: Yeast extract, soy meal
- Anaerobic phase (P. shermanii): 30 °C, pH 7.0, 3–4 days
- Aerobic phase: With cobalt supplementation
- Final yield: **100–200 mg/L**

## 2.3 Vitamin C (Ascorbic Acid) — Reichstein Process

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Microbial step in vitamin C production: **Acetobacter suboxydans** (or *Gluconobacter oxydans*) oxidizes D-sorbitol to L-sorbose. This single bioconversion step is what makes the Reichstein-Grussner process industrially viable.

**Steps:**
1. Hydrogenation of D-glucose → D-sorbitol
2. **Microbial oxidation** (Acetobacter) → L-sorbose
3. Chemical oxidation → diacetone-L-sorbose
4. Rearrangement → 2-keto-L-gulonic acid
5. Acid hydrolysis/enolization → L-ascorbic acid

## 2.4 Microbial Production of Steroids

### 2.4.1 Key Biotransformation Reactions

| Reaction Type | Substrate → Product | Typical Microorganism |
|---------------|--------------------|-----------------------|
| **11α-Hydroxylation** | Progesterone → 11α-hydroxyprogesterone | *Rhizopus arrhizus*, *Aspergillus niger* |
| **11β-Hydroxylation** | Reichstein's S → Hydrocortisone | *Curvularia lunata* |
| **16α-Hydroxylation** | Progesterone → 16α-hydroxyprogesterone | *Streptomyces* sp. |
| **1,2-Dehydrogenation** | Cortisone → Prednisone | *Corynebacterium simplex* |
| **Side-chain cleavage** | Cholesterol → AD/ADD | *Mycobacterium* sp. |
| **Δ¹-Dehydrogenation** | Hydrocortisone → Prednisolone | *Arthrobacter simplex* |

### 2.4.2 Steroid Biotransformation Workflow

1. **Growth phase** — Microorganism cultured in complex medium (24–48 h)
2. **Induction phase** — Add substrate (steroid dissolved in solvent like DMF, ethanol, or Tween-80)
3. **Biotransformation phase** — Continue for 24–96 h at 28–30 °C
4. **Product recovery** — Filtration → solvent extraction → crystallization

> [!CAUTION]
> **Substrate toxicity**: Steroids are hydrophobic and can disrupt microbial membranes. They are typically added at **low concentrations (0.1–1 g/L)** and may require slow feeding or solubilization in organic solvents/detergents.

## 2.5 Industrial Enzymes — Production Overview

### 2.5.1 Why Microbial Enzymes?
- **Scalability** — Can be produced in huge fermenters
- **Genetic tractability** — Can be engineered for improved properties
- **Safety** — Most are GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)
- **Cost** — Cheap substrates (molasses, starch, soy meal)

### 2.5.2 Top Industrial Enzymes by Market Volume

| Enzyme | Major Application | Producer Organism |
|--------|------------------|-------------------|
| **Proteases** | Detergents, food | *Bacillus licheniformis* |
| **Amylases** | Starch processing, brewing | *Bacillus amyloliquefaciens* |
| **Lipases** | Fats/oils, detergents | *Candida antarctica* |
| **Cellulases** | Textiles, biofuels | *Trichoderma reesei* |
| **Pectinases** | Fruit juice clarification | *Aspergillus niger* |
| **Lactases** | Dairy | *Kluyveromyces lactis* |

## 2.6 Proteases — Detailed Classification

### 2.6.1 Classification by Catalytic Mechanism (MEROPS Classification)

| Class | Active Site Residues | Examples | pH Optimum | Application |
|-------|---------------------|----------|------------|-------------|
| **Serine proteases** | Ser, His, Asp catalytic triad | Subtilisin, trypsin, chymotrypsin | 7–11 | Detergents |
| **Cysteine (thiol) proteases** | Cys, His | Papain, bromelain, ficin | 4–7 | Food tenderization |
| **Aspartic (acid) proteases** | 2 Asp residues | Pepsin, renin, chymosin | 2–5 | Cheese making |
| **Metalloproteases** | Zn²⁺ ion | Thermolysin, collagenase | 6–9 | Baking, brewing |
| **Glutamic proteases** | Gln, Glu | Scytalidoglutamic peptidase | acidic | Detergents |
| **Threonine proteases** | Thr (N-terminal) | Proteasome subunits | 7 | Pharma research |

### 2.6.2 Classification by Source

| Source | Examples |
|--------|----------|
| **Animal** | Trypsin, chymotrypsin, pepsin, rennin (calf stomach) |
| **Plant** | Papain (papaya), bromelain (pineapple), ficin (fig) |
| **Microbial** | Subtilisin (*Bacillus*), Pronase (*Streptomyces griseus*) |

### 2.6.3 Classification by Optimal pH

| Type | pH Range | Use |
|------|----------|-----|
| Acid proteases | 2.0–5.0 | Cheese making (rennet) |
| Neutral proteases | 6.0–8.0 | Brewing, baking |
| Alkaline proteases | 8.0–11.0 | Detergents (largest use) |

## 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Formula / Key Value | Units |
|--------------------|--------------------|-------|
| Specific activity | $SA = \dfrac{U}{mg\ protein}$ | U/mg |
| Total activity | $TA = SA \times Total\ Protein$ | Units |
| Activity yield | $Y_{act} = \dfrac{TA_{final}}{TA_{initial}} \times 100$ | % |
| Protein recovery | $R_{prot} = \dfrac{P_{final}}{P_{initial}} \times 100$ | % |
| Purification fold | $PF = \dfrac{SA_{final}}{SA_{initial}}$ | dimensionless |
| Fermentation productivity | $P = \dfrac{C_{final} - C_{initial}}{t}$ | g/L/h |
| Volumetric productivity | $Q_p = \dfrac{dC}{dt}$ | g/L/h |
| Specific growth rate | $\mu = \dfrac{1}{X}\dfrac{dX}{dt}$ | h⁻¹ |
| Doubling time | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \dfrac{0.693}{\mu}$ | h |
| Substrate yield coefficient | $Y_{X/S} = \dfrac{\Delta X}{\Delta S}$ | g/g |
| Doubling time with cell count | $t_d = \dfrac{t \cdot \ln 2}{\ln(N_t/N_0)}$ | h |
| Henderson–Hasselbalch (pH) | $pH = pK_a + \log\dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> **Avoid pipe symbols in tables**: All absolute values are represented by $\vert x \vert$ or with `\mid` to prevent markdown table corruption.

## 2.8 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Detergents (~30% of enzyme market)**: Alkaline proteases from *Bacillus* (Subtilisin Carlsberg, Savinase) operate at pH 9–11 and temperatures 40–60 °C, breaking down protein-based stains (blood, sweat, milk).
- **Dairy industry**: Chymosin (rennet) — historically calf-derived, now **microbial chymosin from *Aspergillus niger* / *Kluyveromyces lactis*** — is the largest single enzyme used in cheese making worldwide.
- **Leather industry**: Alkaline proteases are used for **bating** (removing non-collagen proteins) — replacing toxic sodium sulfide.
- **Pharmaceuticals**: Proteases are used in **wound debridement** (collagenase), **anti-inflammatory** therapy (bromelain), and **digestive aids**.
- **Food industry**: Papain and bromelain for **meat tenderization**; microbial proteases for **flavor development** in cheese and soy sauce.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Biomass Doubling Time

The **doubling time** of a microbial population is one of the most important parameters in industrial fermentation, used to design inoculum timelines.

### 3.1.1 Starting from the Definition of Specific Growth Rate

By definition, the specific growth rate is the rate of biomass increase per unit biomass:

$$\mu = \frac{1}{X} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt}$$

where:
- $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹)
- $X$ = biomass concentration (g/L)
- $t$ = time (h)

### 3.1.2 Rearranging the Differential Equation

Multiplying both sides by $dt$:

$$\mu \cdot dt = \frac{dX}{X}$$

### 3.1.3 Integration Between Two Time Points

Integrate from $t = 0$ (initial biomass $X_0$) to time $t$ (biomass $X_t$), assuming $\mu$ is constant (exponential phase):

$$\int_{0}^{t} \mu \, d\tau = \int_{X_0}^{X_t} \frac{dX}{X}$$

Since $\mu$ is constant, the left side becomes $\mu \cdot t$. The right side is the natural log:

$$\mu \cdot t = \ln(X_t) - \ln(X_0) = \ln\left(\frac{X_t}{X_0}\right)$$

### 3.1.4 Definition of Doubling Time

**Doubling time ($t_d$)** is the time required for biomass to double: $X_t = 2 X_0$. Substituting:

$$\mu \cdot t_d = \ln\left(\frac{2X_0}{X_0}\right) = \ln 2$$

### 3.1.5 Final Derivation

$$\boxed{t_d = \frac{\ln 2}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{\mu}}$$

> This formula appears in KTU numerical problems where students calculate fermentation batch times or inoculum chain lengths.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example — KTU Exam Style

**Problem:** A fermentation starts with an inoculum of $1.0 \times 10^5$ cells/mL. After 6 hours of exponential growth, the cell density is $1.28 \times 10^7$ cells/mL. Calculate:
1. The specific growth rate $\mu$
2. The doubling time $t_d$
3. The time required to reach a target cell density of $1.0 \times 10^9$ cells/mL

### 3.2.1 Solution

**Step 1 — Apply the integrated growth equation:**

We use the general exponential growth equation:

$$\mu \cdot t = \ln\left(\frac{X_t}{X_0}\right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values:**

$$\mu \cdot 6 = \ln\left(\frac{1.28 \times 10^7}{1.0 \times 10^5}\right)$$

$$\mu \cdot 6 = \ln(128)$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$\ln(128) = \ln(2^7) = 7 \cdot \ln 2 = 7 \cdot 0.6931 = 4.8517$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $\mu$:**

$$\mu = \frac{4.8517}{6}$$

$$\boxed{\mu = 0.8086 \ \text{h}^{-1}}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate doubling time:**

$$t_d = \frac{0.693}{\mu} = \frac{0.693}{0.8086}$$

$$\boxed{t_d = 0.857 \ \text{hours} \approx 51.4 \ \text{minutes}}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate time to reach $1.0 \times 10^9$ cells/mL:**

Use the same integrated equation with the new $X_t$:

$$t = \frac{1}{\mu} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{X_t}{X_0}\right) = \frac{1}{0.8086} \cdot \ln\left(\frac{1.0 \times 10^9}{1.0 \times 10^5}\right)$$

$$t = \frac{1}{0.8086} \cdot \ln(10^4)$$

$$t = \frac{1}{0.8086} \cdot 4 \cdot \ln 10$$

$$t = \frac{1}{0.8086} \cdot 4 \cdot 2.3026$$

$$t = \frac{9.2103}{0.8086}$$

$$\boxed{t = 11.39 \ \text{hours}}$$

## 3.3 Downstream Processing Mass Balance for Enzyme Purification

### 3.3.1 The Standard Mass Balance Framework

For each downstream step, three key parameters are tracked:

$$R_{protein} = \frac{P_{out}}{P_{in}} \times 100\% \quad (\text{Protein Recovery \%})$$

$$Y_{activity} = \frac{U_{out}}{U_{in}} \times 100\% \quad (\text{Activity Yield \%})$$

$$PF = \frac{SA_{out}}{SA_{in}} = \frac{(U/P)_{out}}{(U/P)_{in}} \quad (\text{Purification Fold})$$

### 3.3.2 Worked Example: A Three-Step Enzyme Purification

| Step | Total Protein (mg) | Total Activity (U) | SA (U/mg) | Recovery (%) | Fold |
|------|--------------------|--------------------|-----------|--------------|------|
| Crude extract | 10,000 | 100,000 | 10 | 100 | 1.0 |
| Ammonium sulfate (60%) | 4,000 | 85,000 | 21.25 | 85 | 2.13 |
| Ion-exchange (DEAE) | 500 | 70,000 | 140 | 70 | 14.0 |
| Gel filtration (Sephadex G-75) | 100 | 60,000 | 600 | 60 | 60.0 |

**Verification of overall yield and fold:**

$$Overall\ Yield = 100\% \times \frac{85}{100} \times \frac{70}{85} \times \frac{60}{70} = 60\%$$

$$Overall\ Fold = 1.0 \times 2.13 \times 6.59 \times 4.29 \approx 60$$

**Step-by-step verification (final step):**

$$PF_{G75} = \frac{SA_{G75}}{SA_{DEAE}} = \frac{600}{140} = 4.286$$

$$Y_{G75} = \frac{60{,}000}{70{,}000} \times 100\% = 85.7\%$$

$$R_{G75} = \frac{100}{500} \times 100\% = 20\%$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Vitamin vs. Steroid vs. Enzyme

| Parameter | Vitamins (e.g., Riboflavin) | Steroids (e.g., Cortisone) | Enzymes (e.g., Subtilisin) |
|-----------|----------------------------|----------------------------|----------------------------|
| Product nature | Small molecule | Small molecule | Macromolecule (protein) |
| Producer | Whole cells (de novo) | Whole cells (biotransformation) | Whole cells (de novo) |
| Main organism | *A. gossypii* | *R. arrhizus* | *B. licheniformis* |
| Reactor type | Stirred-tank fermenter | Biotransformation vessel | Stirred-tank fermenter |
| Fermentation time | 5–8 days | 1–4 days | 2–3 days |
| Critical medium component | Iron limitation | Steroid substrate | Protein/amino acid source |
| Purification | Filtration, crystallization | Solvent extraction, chromatography | Multi-step chromatography |
| Typical yield | 20–40 g/L | 80–95% conversion | 1–10 g enzyme/L |

## 3.5 Calculation of Volumetric Productivity for Riboflavin

**Problem:** A 100,000 L fermenter produces riboflavin at a final concentration of 25 g/L over 168 hours (7 days). Calculate the volumetric productivity.

**Solution:**

$$Q_p = \frac{C_{final}}{t} = \frac{25 \ \text{g/L}}{168 \ \text{h}}$$

$$\boxed{Q_p = 0.149 \ \text{g/L/h} = 3.57 \ \text{g/L/day}}$$

**Total riboflavin produced:**

$$m_{riboflavin} = C_{final} \times V = 25 \ \text{g/L} \times 100{,}000 \ \text{L}$$

$$m_{riboflavin} = 2{,}500{,}000 \ \text{g} = 2{,}500 \ \text{kg} = 2.5 \ \text{tonnes}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Industrial Riboflavin Production

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inoculum Spores] --> B[Seed Fermenter Stage 1]
    B --> C[Seed Fermenter Stage 2]
```
