<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** — *3 Marks*

**Q: Name the microorganism used for the industrial production of kasugamycin. Classify it chemically and state one major industrial application.**

**Model Answer:**

> - **Producing organism:** *Streptomyces kasugaensis*, a soil-dwelling actinomycete isolated in 1965 from Nara, Japan.
> - **Chemical classification:** Aminoglycoside antibiotic (specifically a **diaminoglycoside**), water-soluble and weakly basic.
> - **Industrial application:** Used as an **agricultural fungicide** to control rice blast disease caused by *Pyricularia oryzae*, especially in paddy cultivation across Asia.

---

#### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Supplementary]** — *3 Marks*

**Q: Why is kasugamycin classified as a secondary metabolite? Mention one consequence of this classification for its fermentation strategy.**

**Model Answer:**

> Kasugamycin is a **secondary metabolite** because it is **(i) not essential for growth**, **(ii) produced after the exponential (trophophase) phase**, and **(iii) its biosynthesis is not coupled directly to biomass accumulation**. A direct consequence is that production must occur in the **idiophase**, requiring the engineer to design a **fed-batch or two-stage nutrient strategy** — typically excess carbon with limiting phosphate or ammonium to derepress the *kas* biosynthetic gene cluster.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Extended Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

#### Question A (Choice 1) — *14 Marks*

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper]** — *Module 3*

**Q: Describe in detail the upstream and fermentation-stage bioprocess for the production of kasugamycin using *Streptomyces kasugaensis*. Discuss the role of critical process parameters and the trophophase–idiophase transition in maximizing volumetric productivity.**

**Sub-part (a) — Upstream Processing and Inoculum Development — *7 Marks***

**Model Solution:**

1. **Strain Selection and Maintenance**
   - Master and working cell banks of *S. kasugaensis* preserved as **lyophilized spores** or **frozen glycerol stocks at –80 °C**.
   - Genetic stability is critical; periodic re-isolation prevents degeneration of the *kas* gene cluster.

2. **Inoculum Stages**
   - **Stage 1 — Agar slant:** Spores inoculated on **ISP-2 or YMEA agar**, incubated at **28 °C for 7–10 days** to obtain confluent sporulation.
   - **Stage 2 — Seed flask (vegetative):** Spores transferred to **Erlenmeyer flasks (250–1000 mL)** containing glucose–yeast extract–soybean meal medium; incubated **28 °C, 200 rpm, 48–72 h**.
   - **Stage 3 — Production inoculum:** Scale-up through progressively larger seed fermenters (1 %–10 % v/v transfer).

3. **Medium Composition Rationale**

| Component | Typical Concentration | Role |
|---|---|---|
| Glucose / Soluble starch | 20–40 g/L | Carbon + energy; **catabolite-repressing** if in excess |
| Soybean meal / Yeast extract | 10–20 g/L | Complex nitrogen — **stimulates idiophase** |
| NaCl | 1–3 g/L | Osmotic balance |
| K₂HPO₄ | 0.5–1.0 g/L | Buffer + phosphate (regulatory) |
| CaCO₃ | 2–4 g/L | pH stabilizer |

4. **Sterilization:** Batch sterilization at **121 °C / 20 min** with care to avoid Maillard browning of glucose–amino mixtures.

---

**Sub-part (b) — Production Fermentation and Process Parameters — *7 Marks***

**Model Solution:**

1. **Bioreactor Configuration**
   - **Stirred-tank reactor (STR)** with **Rushton turbines**, baffled, sparger-based aeration.
   - Working volume typically **50,000–200,000 L** in industrial plants.
   - Aspect ratio 2:1 to 3:1; multiple impellers for vertical mixing.

2. **Critical Process Parameters (CPPs)**

| Parameter | Set Point | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Temperature | **28 ± 1 °C** | Optimal for both growth and *kas* gene expression |
| pH | **7.0 ± 0.3** | Maintains glycosidic bond integrity |
| DO | **> 30 % saturation** | Critical aerobic demand during idiophase |
| Agitation | 250–400 rpm | Balance $k_L a$ vs. shear |
| Aeration | 0.5–1.0 vvm | Sufficient O₂ supply |
| Foam control | Autoclaved **silicone-based antifoam** | Prevents flooding |

3. **Trophophase–Idiophase Transition**
   - During **trophophase (0–36 h):** rapid biomass build-up; **glucose represses** kasugamycin synthesis.
   - **Transition (36–48 h):** glucose is depleted or fed at limiting rate; phosphate and ammonium drop below threshold.
   - **Idiophase (48–96 h):** *kas* gene cluster is derepressed; **kasugamycin accumulates** while biomass growth plateaus.

4. **Maximizing Volumetric Productivity**
   - Use of **fed-batch glucose feeding** to maintain sub-repressive concentrations.
   - Online monitoring of DO spikes to detect nutrient exhaustion events.
   - Titres of **4–6 g/L** and $P_v$ of **0.04–0.06 g/L·h** are commercially achievable.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) — *14 Marks*

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Main Paper]** — *Module 3*

**Q: Explain the downstream processing sequence for recovery and purification of kasugamycin from fermented broth. With suitable equations, evaluate the volumetric productivity and product yield on substrate for a representative industrial batch.**

**Sub-part (a) — Downstream Processing — *7 Marks***

**Model Solution:**

The DSP train for kasugamycin follows a classical **antibiotic recovery template** adapted for an aminoglycoside:

1. **Broth Harvest (Solid–Liquid Separation)**
   - Whole broth is filtered using a **rotary vacuum filter** precoated with diatomaceous earth, OR centrifuged via **disc-stack centrifuge**.
   - **Objective:** Separate mycelial cake from kasugamycin-containing supernatant.

2. **Activated Carbon Adsorption**
   - The clarified broth is passed through **granular activated carbon columns**.
   - Kasugamycin binds to carbon via hydrophobic and electrostatic interactions.
   - **Loading capacity** is pH-dependent; adsorption is favored at **pH 7.0–8.0**.

3. **Acid Elution**
   - Kasugamycin is eluted with **0.1–0.5 N HCl** or **acidic methanol-water mixtures**.
   - The eluate is collected and neutralized.

4. **Ion-Exchange Chromatography**
   - Further purification using **cation-exchange resin** (e.g., Amberlite IRC-50, Dowex 50).
   - Kasugamycin (positively charged at acidic pH) is retained and later eluted with **dilute NaCl or NaOH gradient**.
   - Removes structurally similar impurities and color bodies.

5. **Decolorization and Crystallization**
   - Treatment with **activated carbon or polymeric adsorbent** removes pigments.
   - Crystallization achieved by **pH shift and solvent addition** (e.g., methanol/acetone precipitation).
   - Crystals are washed with cold ethanol and vacuum-dried.

6. **Drying and Formulation**
   - Final product dried to **<5 % moisture**; milled to technical-grade powder; optionally formulated with wetting/dispersing agents for foliar spray application.

> **Overall Recovery Yield:** Typically **40–60 %** from broth to technical-grade powder.

---

**Sub-part (b) — Productivity and Yield Calculations — *7 Marks***

**Given Data:**
- Working volume $V$ = 80,000 L
- Final titre $C_f$ = 5.2 g/L
- Initial titre $C_0$ = 0.04 g/L
- Fermentation time $t$ = 96 h
- Initial glucose $S_0$ = 30 g/L
- Residual glucose $S_f$ = 5 g/L

**Step 1 — Volumetric Productivity $P_v$:**

$$
P_v = \dfrac{C_f - C_0}{t} = \dfrac{5.2 - 0.04}{96} = \dfrac{5.16}{96}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_v = 0.0538\ \text{g/L·h}}
$$

**Step 2 — Total Kasugamycin Produced:**

$$
m_{total} = 0.0538 \times 80{,}000 \times 96 = 413{,}184\ \text{g}
$$

$$
\boxed{m_{total} \approx 413.2\ \text{kg per batch}}
$$

**Step 3 — Glucose Consumed:**

$$
\Delta S = S_0 - S_f = 30 - 5 = 25\ \text{g/L}
$$

**Step 4 — Product Yield on Substrate:**

$$
Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S} = \dfrac{5.16}{25}
$$

$$
\boxed{Y_{P/S} = 0.206\ \text{g/g} = 20.6\ \%}
$$

**Step 5 — Interpretation:**
- Productivity is competitive for a secondary-metabolite antibiotic.
- Yield of ~20 % indicates **80 % of carbon** is partitioned into biomass, CO₂, and by-products.
- **Improvement opportunities:** metabolic engineering of the *kas* cluster, fed-batch feeding, and in-situ product removal.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Kasugamycin Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the trophophase–idiophase distinction** — Kasugamycin is *not* a growth-associated product. Writing $\dfrac{dP}{dt} = Y_{P/X} \cdot \dfrac{dX}{dt}$ will cost full marks.
> 2. **Using the wrong producing organism** — It must be *Streptomyces kasugaensis*, not *S. griseus* (which produces streptomycin).
> 3. **Confusing mechanism of action** — Kasugamycin blocks **initiation**, NOT elongation. Aminoglycosides like streptomycin cause misreading; kasugamycin prevents the very first step.
> 4. **Mixing up $P_v$ units** — Volumetric productivity is **g/L·h**, not g/h or g/L.
> 5. **Ignoring catabolite repression** — Aqueous-phase glucose above ~5 g/L will suppress kasugamycin formation; this nuance is frequently tested.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Kasugamycin**

- **Producing organism:** *Streptomyces kasugaensis* (actinomycete, aerobic).
- **Chemical class:** Aminoglycoside / diaminoglycoside antibiotic; C₁₄H₂₅N₃O₉; MW ≈ 379.36 g/mol.
- **Discovery:** 1965, Japan; from soil near Mt. Kasuga, Nara.
- **Mechanism:** Binds **30S ribosomal subunit**; **blocks initiation of translation** (prevents mRNA and fMet-tRNA from entering the P-site).
- **Industrial use:** **Agricultural fungicide** against *Pyricularia oryzae* (rice blast); some veterinary antibacterial use.
- **Production type:** **Submerged fermentation (SmF)** in aerated STRs; secondary metabolite.
- **Critical nutrients:** Glucose (repressing in excess), soybean meal / yeast extract (stimulating), phosphate (regulatory).
- **Optimum process window:** **28 °C, pH 7.0, DO > 30 %, 72–96 h** fermentation.
- **Trophophase–Idiophase switch:** Triggered by **glucose depletion** and **nitrogen-source derepression**.
- **Key formulae to memorize:**
  - Volumetric productivity: $P_v = \dfrac{\Delta C}{\Delta t}$
  - Yield on substrate: $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$
  - Doubling time: $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$
  - OTR: $OTR = k_L a \cdot (C^* - C_L)$
- **DSP sequence:** Filtration → Activated Carbon Adsorption → Acid Elution → Ion-Exchange Chromatography → Crystallization → Drying.
- **Typical industrial titre:** **2–6 g/L**; commercial recovery yield **40–60 %**.
- **Catabolite repression:** YES — glucose suppresses kasugamycin biosynthesis.
- **Aerobic requirement:** Strict aerobe; oxygen transfer is a frequent scale-up bottleneck.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Beauverin
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Beauverin

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Beauverin (Beauvericin)** is a **cyclic hexadepsipeptide mycotoxin** classified as a **secondary metabolite** produced by several entomopathogenic and phytopathogenic fungal genera, principally *Beauveria bassiana*, *Fusarium proliferatum*, *F. semitectum*, and *Paecilomyces* species. Structurally, it is composed of an alternating sequence of three **N-methyl-L-phenylalanine** residues and three **D-α-hydroxyisovaleric acid** residues, arranged in a cyclic configuration through alternating amide and ester linkages.

The molecule is biologically significant in bioprocess engineering because it functions as a **cation ionophore**, capable of forming lipophilic complexes with alkali and alkaline-earth metal cations (especially **Ca²⁺** and **K⁺**), thereby disrupting electrochemical gradients across biological membranes.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Identification Parameters (for KTU Board Questions)**
> - **IUPAC Name:** Cyclo[(D-α-hydroxyisovaleryl-L-N-methyl-phenylalanyl)₃]
> - **Molecular Formula:** C₄₅H₅₇N₃O₉
> - **Molecular Weight:** 783.96 g/mol
> - **CAS Number:** 26048-05-5
> - **Class:** Cyclodepsipeptide / Cyclohexadepsipeptide / Mycotoxin

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **six-sided ring-shaped molecular basket** floating in the watery cytoplasm of a fungal cell. The outside of this basket is **greasy and water-repelling** (lipophilic), while the inside forms a **hollow cavity** shaped like a tiny chemical cup. When this basket meets a positively charged metal ion (like Ca²⁺), it "swallows" the ion into its hollow center, wraps its greasy exterior around it, and **carries it across the cell membrane** — something the ion could never do on its own because charged ions are repelled by the lipid membrane.

> [!NOTE]
> **In Bioprocess Engineering terms:** Beauverin is essentially a **molecular ferryboat** for ions. The fungal cell produces it as a defense weapon (antimicrobial/insecticidal), but for an engineer, this tiny ring becomes a **high-value product** harvested from fermentation broth using solid-liquid separation, cell disruption, and chromatographic purification.

**Why should a B.Tech student care?**
- Beauverin represents a model case study for **microbial secondary metabolite production** (a core Module 3 topic in Bioprocess Engineering).
- It demonstrates how **non-growth-associated product kinetics** operate in fungal batch fermentation.
- It illustrates the **bioreactor design requirements** for filamentous fungi (shear-sensitive morphology).
- It is a high-value **bioactive compound** with pharmaceutical, agrochemical, and food-safety implications.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Structural Architecture — Step-by-Step Logic

The Beauverin molecule can be deconstructed into its building blocks using the following logic:

- **Step 1 — Identify the two repeating monomer units:**
  - **Unit A:** N-methyl-L-phenylalanine (an N-methylated aromatic amino acid)
  - **Unit B:** D-α-hydroxyisovaleric acid (a 2-hydroxy branched short-chain fatty acid)

- **Step 2 — Understand the two types of linkages:**
  - **Amide bond (–CO–NH–):** formed between the carboxyl group of hydroxyisovaleric acid and the amino group of N-methyl-phenylalanine.
  - **Ester bond (–CO–O–):** formed between the carboxyl group of N-methyl-phenylalanine and the hydroxyl group of hydroxyisovaleric acid.
  - These two linkages **alternate** in a strict 1:1 pattern.

- **Step 3 — Count the repeating units:**
  - Three A units + three B units = **six residues total** (hence "hexadepsipeptide"; *depsi* = both ester + amide; *peptide* = amide bond).

- **Step 4 — Recognize the cyclization:**
  - The linear hexadepsipeptide chain undergoes **macrocyclization** catalyzed by a Non-Ribosomal Peptide Synthetase (NRPS) enzyme complex, producing the closed ring topology.

### 2.2 Biosynthetic Logic — The Non-Ribosomal Peptide Synthetase (NRPS) Pathway

Unlike ribosomal protein synthesis, Beauverin is assembled by a **multienzyme thiotemplate mechanism**:

- **Step 1:** Each monomer is activated as an **aminoacyl-AMP** by the adenylation (A) domain.
- **Step 2:** The activated monomer is tethered to a **phosphopantetheinyl (Ppant) arm** of the peptidyl carrier protein (PCP) domain.
- **Step 3:** Condensation (C) domains catalyze successive **peptide bond formation** between tethered monomers.
- **Step 4:** A dedicated **thioesterase (TE) domain** performs macrocyclization and releases the mature cyclic product.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Significance:** The NRPS logic explains why Beauverin production is **uncoupled from cell growth** (secondary metabolism) and is highest under **nitrogen-limiting, carbon-rich stationary phase conditions** — a critical concept for bioprocess media design.

### 2.3 Mode of Action as an Ionophore

Beauverin exhibits a **sandwich-like coordination geometry** with cations:

- The carbonyl oxygens of the three amide groups and the three ester groups line the **interior cavity**.
- A monovalent or divalent cation sits at the center coordinated to these six carbonyl oxygens.
- The **lipophilic exterior** (methyl groups, aromatic rings) allows the complex to diffuse passively through lipid bilayers.
- Result: **Dissipation of transmembrane ionic gradients**, collapse of mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm), **induction of apoptosis** in target cells, and disruption of Ca²⁺-dependent signaling.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept / Parameter | Expression / Value | Units | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular formula of Beauverin | C₄₅H₅₇N₃O₉ | — | Stoichiometry for yield calculations |
| Molecular weight (M) | **783.96** | g/mol | Molar yield (Yₚ/s) |
| Macrocyclization order | 3 + 3 alternating | residues | Confirms hexadepsipeptide nature |
| Coordination number with cation | 6 (carbonyl O atoms) | — | Explains Ca²⁺ and K⁺ selectivity |
| Specific growth rate (μ) for *B. bassiana* | 0.05 – 0.18 | h⁻¹ | Bioreactor design & batch time |
| Typical product titer (lab scale) | 10 – 250 | mg/L | Sets target for process optimization |
| Optimal fermentation pH | 5.5 – 6.8 | — | Media & buffer selection |
| Optimal temperature | 25 – 28 | °C | CSTR / STR temperature control |
| Production phase | **Stationary / Idiophase** | — | Two-stage / fed-batch design |
| Carbon source preference | Glucose, sucrose, glycerol | g/L | Medium formulation |
| Nitrogen limitation trigger | C:N ratio > 20:1 | — | Idiophase induction |
| Shear sensitivity (filamentous) | Impeller tip speed < 2.5 | m/s | Rushton vs. marine impeller choice |
| Product inhibition threshold | ~300 – 500 | mg/L | In-situ adsorption resin strategy |
| Overall yield coefficient (Yₚ/s) | 0.002 – 0.015 | g product / g substrate | Process economics |
| Volumetric productivity (Qp) | 1 – 15 | mg/(L·h) | Bioreactor size & CAPEX |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical pipe-handling rule:** All values in the table are **free of vertical bar `|` characters** to preserve KTU-readable markdown formatting. Where mathematical "such that" or "divides" is needed, use `\vert` or `\mid` in LaTeX.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility of Beauverin

- **Pharmaceutical Industry:** Anticancer lead compound (cytotoxic against breast, colon, and lung cancer cell lines), antiviral agent.
- **Agrochemical Industry:** Bioinsecticide component for integrated pest management (IPM); alternative to synthetic pyrethroids.
- **Food Safety Industry:** Routine **HPLC-MS/MS** screening target in cereal grain surveillance programs (regulated in the EU under Commission Regulation 2023/915 for *Fusarium*-toxins).
- **Bioprocess Research:** Model system for studying **filamentous fungal fermentation**, NRPS enzymology, and **secondary metabolite process intensification** (e.g., via in-situ product recovery using macroporous resins).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Product Yield Coefficient in Beauverin Batch Fermentation

**Given data for a hypothetical *Beauveria bassiana* fed-batch run:**

- Initial volume: $V_0 = 5.0 \, \text{L}$
- Initial glucose: $S_0 = 30 \, \text{g/L}$
- Final volume: $V_f = 7.2 \, \text{L}$
- Residual glucose: $S_f = 8.5 \, \text{g/L}$
- Final Beauverin titer: $P_f = 185 \, \text{mg/L}$
- Initial Beauverin: $P_0 = 0 \, \text{mg/L}$
- Fermentation time: $t = 168 \, \text{h}$ (7 days)

**Step 1 — Substrate consumed (ΔS):**

$$
\Delta S = S_0 - S_f = 30 - 8.5 = 21.5 \, \text{g/L}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mass of substrate utilized in the reactor:**

$$
m_S = \Delta S \times \frac{(V_0 + V_f)}{2} = 21.5 \times \frac{(5.0 + 7.2)}{2} = 21.5 \times 6.1
$$

$$
m_S = 131.15 \, \text{g}
$$

> *Logic:* For a linear-volume fed-batch we use the average volume as the multiplier to convert a concentration change into a mass change. For a rigorously volume-variable system, the integral $\int S \, dV$ should be evaluated; the average-volume approximation is acceptable for KTU-level problems.

**Step 3 — Total mass of Beauverin produced:**

$$
m_P = (P_f - P_0) \times V_f = (185 - 0) \times 7.2 = 1332 \, \text{mg} = 1.332 \, \text{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Apparent product yield on substrate (Yₚ/s):**

$$
Y_{P/S} = \frac{m_P}{m_S} = \frac{1.332}{131.15} = 0.01016 \, \text{g/g} = 10.16 \, \text{mg/g}
$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric productivity (Qₚ):**

$$
Q_P = \frac{P_f - P_0}{t} = \frac{185 - 0}{168} = 1.101 \, \text{mg/(L·h)}
$$

**Step 6 — Specific productivity (qₚ), assuming final biomass X_f = 8.4 g DCW/L:**

$$
q_P = \frac{Q_P}{X_f} = \frac{1.101}{8.4} = 0.131 \, \text{mg/(g·h)}
$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Idiophase Kinetics Using the Luedeking–Piret Model

Since Beauverin is a secondary metabolite, production is **partially growth-associated** and **partially non-growth-associated**. The standard Luedeking–Piret equation captures this:

$$
q_P = \alpha \, \mu + \beta
$$

Where:
- $q_P$ = specific product formation rate (mg product / g biomass / h)
- $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹)
- $\alpha$ = growth-associated product formation constant (mg/g)
- $\beta$ = non-growth-associated product formation constant (mg/(g·h))

**Worked KTU-Style Numerical Problem:**

Suppose an experimentalist reports the following data from chemostat steady-states on *Fusarium proliferatum*:

| Dilution rate D (= μ at steady state) (h⁻¹) | $q_P$ (mg/(g·h)) |
|---|---|
| 0.02 | 0.045 |
| 0.05 | 0.062 |
| 0.08 | 0.080 |
| 0.12 | 0.105 |

**Step 1 — Recognize linearity.** Plotting $q_P$ vs. $\mu$ gives a straight line of slope $\alpha$ and intercept $\beta$.

**Step 2 — Compute slope $\alpha$ using two extreme points:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{q_{P,2} - q_{P,1}}{\mu_2 - \mu_1} = \frac{0.105 - 0.045}{0.12 - 0.02} = \frac{0.060}{0.10} = 0.60 \, \text{mg/g}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute intercept $\beta$ by substitution:**

$$
\beta = q_P - \alpha \mu = 0.045 - (0.60)(0.02) = 0.045 - 0.012 = 0.033 \, \text{mg/(g·h)}
$$

**Step 4 — Final kinetic model:**

$$
q_P = 0.60 \, \mu + 0.033
$$

**Step 5 — Engineering interpretation:**

Since $\alpha > 0$ **and** $\beta > 0$, Beauverin synthesis is **mixed-growth-associated**. This implies:
- Some product is tied to primary metabolic flux (growth-coupled).
- A substantial fraction is produced by **stationary-phase, non-growing cells**.
- Process strategy: implement a **two-stage bioreactor** — first stage maximizes $\mu$ for biomass build-up, second stage lowers $\mu$ (or stops it) to maximize the $\beta$ contribution.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** A common mistake is to assume Beauverin is purely secondary and therefore applies the **zero-growth-associated model** $q_P = \beta$ alone. The mixed-growth-associated model above is more accurate and is worth full marks when justified with the regression.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Comparison with Other Fungal Cyclodepsipeptides

| Property | Beauverin | Enniatin | Valinomycin |
|---|---|---|---|
| Producing genus | *Beauveria*, *Fusarium* | *Fusarium* | *Streptomyces* |
| Residue count | 6 (3+3) | 6 (3+3) | 12 (6+6) |
| Contains aromatic ring | Yes (Phe) | No | No |
| Molecular weight (g/mol) | 783.96 | ~681 | 1111 |
| Preferred cation | Ca²⁺, K⁺ | Na⁺, K⁺ | K⁺ |
| Cytotoxicity rank | High | Medium | Very high |
| Insecticidal activity | Strong | Weak | None |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Beauverin Biosynthesis & Bioprocess Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Inoculum: B. bassiana or F. proliferatum] --> B[Stage 1: Trophophase Bioreactor 25-28 C pH 5.8-6.2]
    B --> C{Cell Growth Phase mu greater than 0}
    C -->|Biomass Build-up| D[Biomass X g DCW per L]
    D --> E[Nutrient Exhaustion Signal C:N ratio exceeds 20:1]
    E --> F[Stage 2: Idiophase Bioreactor Lowered Temperature Nitrogen Limited]
    F --> G[NRPS Enzyme Complex Activated]
    G --> H[Adenylation Domain A Tethers N-methyl-Phe and D-Hiv]
    H --> I[Condensation Domain C Forms Alternating Amide and Ester Bonds]
    I --> J[Thioesterase Domain TE Performs Macrocyclization]
    J --> K[Mature Cyclohexadepsipeptide Beauverin M 783.96 g per mol]
    K --> L[In-situ Adsorption on Macroporous Resin Optional]
    L --> M[Harvest: Solid Liquid Separation Centrifugation]
    M --> N[Cell Disruption and Solvent Extraction Ethyl Acetate]
    N --> O[Concentration: Rotary Evaporation or Falling Film Evaporator]
    O --> P[Purification: Silica Gel Column Chromatography]
    P --> Q[Polishing: Preparative HPLC or Crystallization]
    Q --> R[Final Product: Greater than 95 percent Purity Beauverin]
    R --> S[QC: HPLC-MS/MS and NMR Confirmation]
    S --> T[Formulation: Pharmaceutical or Agrochemical Grade]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Mechanism of Action (Ionophore Function)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    IonA[Extracellular Ca2 plus Ion] --> Diff1[Beauverin Ring Approaches Membrane]
    Diff1 --> Coord1[Six Carbonyl Oxygens Coordinate Ca2 plus]
    Coord1 --> Complex[Lipophilic Ca2 plus Beauverin Complex Forms]
    Complex --> Diff2[Complex Diffuses Through Lipid Bilayer]
    Diff2 --> Release[Ca2 plus Released into Cytoplasm]
    Release --> Eff1[Collapse of Mitochondrial Membrane Potential dPsi m]
    Release --> Eff2[Activation of Caspase Cascade]
    Release --> Eff3[Calcium Overload Induces Apoptosis in Target Cell]
    Eff1 --> Outcome[Cell Death of Insect Pathogen or Cancer Cell]
    Eff2 --> Outcome
    Eff3 --> Outcome
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Two-Stage Reactor Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Stage1[Stage 1: Trophophase CSTR]
        R1[Rich Medium C:N 10:1] --> G1[Maximize mu 0.10 h-1]
        G1 --> X1[High Biomass X about 8 to 10 g DCW per L]
    end
    X1 --> Transfer[Cell Transfer or pH Shift]
    subgraph Stage2[Stage 2: Idiophase STR]
        R2[Nitrogen-Limited Feed C:N greater than 30:1] --> G2[Restrict mu to about 0.01 h-1]
        G2 --> P1[Activate NRPS Cluster]
        P1 --> P2[Beauverin Accumulates]
    end
    P2 --> Harvest[Continuous In-situ Resin Adsorption]
    Harvest --> DSP[Downstream Processing: Extract Filter Crystallize]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Style]**
*Define Beauverin and list its producing organisms. Why is it classified as a secondary metabolite?*

**Model Answer (3 marks — distribute as 1+1+1):**

Beauverin (C₄₅H₅₇N₃O₉, MW 783.96 g/mol) is a **cyclic hexadepsipeptide mycotoxin** composed of three N-methyl-L-phenylalanine and three D-α-hydroxyisovaleric acid residues joined by alternating amide and ester linkages (1 mark).

It is produced by *Beauveria bassiana*, *Fusarium proliferatum*, *F. semitectum*, *Paecilomyces* species, and *Isaria* species (1 mark).

It is classified as a secondary metabolite because its production is **uncoupled from growth**, occurs primarily during the **stationary (idiophase)**, is **not essential for vegetative growth**, and is associated with ecological functions such as antimicrobial defense and insect pathogenicity (1 mark).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Style]**
*Explain in three sentences why Beauverin acts as a "cation ionophore" and name the two preferred metal cations.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — 2+1 split):**

Beauverin's cyclic architecture presents six inward-facing carbonyl oxygens (three amide C=O and three ester C=O) that form a **cavity-matched coordination sphere** for monovalent and divalent cations, while its methylated and aromatic exterior gives a **lipophilic shell** that dissolves in the lipid bilayer (2 marks). The cation–Beauverin complex diffuses passively across membranes, dissipating the electrochemical gradient (1 mark). The two preferred metal cations are **Ca²⁺ and K⁺**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### **Question A — Option 1 [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]**

**(a)** With the aid of a labeled schematic, describe the **biosynthesis of Beauverin via the Non-Ribosomal Peptide Synthetase (NRPS) pathway**. Highlight the roles of the Adenylation (A), Peptidyl Carrier Protein (PCP), Condensation (C), and Thioesterase (TE) domains. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 10 L stirred-tank bioreactor is used to cultivate *Beauveria bassiana* for Beauverin production. The initial glucose is 25 g/L, the final residual glucose is 6 g/L, and the final Beauverin titer is 220 mg/L after 192 hours. The final biomass concentration is 9.0 g DCW/L. Calculate:
- (i) the overall product yield coefficient $Y_{P/S}$ (in mg/g),
- (ii) the volumetric productivity $Q_P$ (in mg/(L·h)),
- (iii) the specific productivity $q_P$ (in mg/(g·h)).

Comment on what these values reveal about the secondary-metabolite nature of Beauverin. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks — distribute as 1+1+1+1+1+1+1]:**

1. Beauverin is **not** synthesized on the ribosome; it is assembled by a **modular NRPS mega-enzyme** (1 mark).
2. Each module activates one specific monomer: module 1 → N-methyl-L-phenylalanine; module 2 → D-α-hydroxyisovaleric acid; the alternation repeats three times to give six modules (1 mark).
3. The **Adenylation (A) domain** consumes ATP to form an **aminoacyl-AMP** intermediate from each monomer (1 mark).
4. The **Peptidyl Carrier Protein (PCP) domain**, bearing a 4′-phosphopantetheinyl (Ppant) prosthetic group, **tethers the activated monomer via a thioester bond** (1 mark).
5. The **Condensation (C) domain** between two successive modules catalyzes **peptide bond formation** between the upstream donor and the downstream acceptor, building the linear chain (1 mark).
6. A terminal **Thioesterase (TE) domain** performs **macrocyclization** by forming the final ester/amide bond and releasing the cyclic hexadepsipeptide (1 mark).
7. The full module iteration produces **three N-methyl-Phe–D-Hiv dipeptide repeats** linked head-to-tail into a ring; this logic is what gives Beauverin its 3+3 architecture (1 mark).

**(b) [7 marks — distribute as 2+2+2+1]:**

**Step 1 — Substrate consumed per liter:**

$$
\Delta S = S_0 - S_f = 25 - 6 = 19 \, \text{g/L}
$$

**Step 2 — Total substrate used (assume constant 10 L volume):**

$$
m_S = \Delta S \times V = 19 \times 10 = 190 \, \text{g}
$$

**Step 3 — Total Beauverin produced:**

$$
m_P = P_f \times V = 220 \times 10 = 2200 \, \text{mg} = 2.2 \, \text{g}
$$

**Step 4 — Overall yield coefficient (i):**

$$
Y_{P/S} = \frac{m_P}{m_S} = \frac{2.2 \, \text{g}}{190 \, \text{g}} = 0.01158 \, \text{g/g} = 11.58 \, \text{mg/g}
$$

**Step 5 — Volumetric productivity (ii):**

$$
Q_P = \frac{P_f}{t} = \frac{220}{192} = 1.146 \, \text{mg/(L·h)}
$$

**Step 6 — Specific productivity (iii):**

$$
q_P = \frac{Q_P}{X_f} = \frac{1.146}{9.0} = 0.1273 \, \text{mg/(g·h)}
$$

**Step 7 — Comment (1 mark):**

The very low $Y_{P/S}$ (~1%) and low $q_P$ values are consistent with Beauverin being a **secondary metabolite**: most of the substrate carbon is diverted to biomass, CO₂, and primary metabolites, with only a tiny fraction funneled into the NRPS pathway during the idiophase.

---

#### **Question B — Option 2 (Alternative Choice) [14 Marks]**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024 Style]**

**(a)** Discuss the **bioprocess strategy** for the production of Beauverin in submerged fungal fermentation. Cover (i) media composition, (ii) two-stage reactor design (trophophase vs. idiophase), (iii) shear-stress management for filamentous fungi, and (iv) in-situ product recovery. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **ionophore mechanism of Beauverin** with reference to its membrane-permeabilizing effect on insect and mammalian cells. How does this mechanism motivate its biotechnological applications as a bioinsecticide and as a candidate anticancer lead? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) [7 marks — distribute 1+1+2+2+1]:**

1. **Media composition (1 mark):** Use a rich carbon source (glucose or glycerol 20–40 g/L), a **limiting nitrogen source** (yeast extract 2–5 g/L or ammonium sulfate <1 g/L) to drive idiophase; pH buffered near 6.0; add MgSO₄, KH₂PO₄, and trace elements (Fe, Zn, Mn).
2. **Two-stage design (1 mark):** Stage 1 = trophophase CSTR with balanced C:N (~10:1) for rapid biomass; Stage 2 = STR with high C:N (>30:1) to trigger NRPS expression. The volume/scale transfer point is when the residual nitrogen falls below the threshold.
3. **Shear management (2 marks):** *Beauveria* and *Fusarium* form pellet/dispersed hyphal morphologies that are **fragile to high tip speeds** (>2.5 m/s). Recommended choices: **(i)** low-shear **marine blade** or **elephant-ear impellers** instead of Rushton turbines; **(ii)** tip speed control by **rpm regulation**; **(iii)** dissolved oxygen maintained at 20–40% air saturation via air-sparging rather than high agitation; **(iv)** avoid excessive foaming with **silicone-based antifoam**.
4. **In-situ product recovery (2 marks):** Add **macroporous polymeric resin** (e.g., XAD-4, XAD-7) directly to the broth at 5–10% (w/v) to adsorb Beauverin in real time, thereby overcoming product inhibition and the lipophilic binding to cells. Recover by eluting with methanol or ethyl acetate followed by evaporation.
5. **Conclusion (1 mark):** The combined two-stage + low-shear + in-situ adsorption strategy can raise titers from a typical 50 mg/L to over 250 mg/L, demonstrating process intensification.

**(b) [7 marks — distribute 2+2+2+1]:**

1. **Mechanism (2 marks):** Beauverin's six carbonyl oxygens (3 amide + 3 ester) form a coordination sphere that binds cations, especially **Ca²⁺ and K⁺**, while the outer methyl and aromatic groups confer lipophilicity. The cation-loaded complex crosses the plasma and mitochondrial membranes, releasing the cation intracellularly and collapsing the transmembrane potential.
2. **Insect cell effect (2 marks):** In insect larvae, mitochondrial Ca²⁺ overload triggers **apoptosis** and **cytochrome c release**; the loss of ATP synthesis causes larval paralysis and death. This justifies the use of *B. bassiana*–based biopesticides (and Beauverin as a bioactive component) for **integrated pest management (IPM)**.
3. **Mammalian / cancer cell effect (2 marks):** In cancer cells, the disrupted Ca²⁺ homeostasis activates **caspase-3/7**, induces **ROS generation**, and lowers the mitochondrial membrane potential (ΔΨm). Several leukemia, breast (MCF-7), and colon (HT-29) cell lines show sub-micromolar IC₅₀ values, supporting its role as an **anticancer drug lead**.
4. **Biotechnological motivation (1 mark):** The dual selectivity (broad-spectrum ionophore behavior, yet cell-type-specific outcomes) motivates **structure–activity relationship (SAR)** studies and **microbial fermentation** as a sustainable production route.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks**
> - Do **not** confuse Beauverin with **Beauveria** (the fungus genus). Beauverin is the molecule, not the organism.
> - Do **not** describe it as a "protein" or "simple amino acid" — it is a **cyclic non-ribosomal depsipeptide**.
> - Do **not** state that it is a primary metabolite or growth-associated product.
> - Always state both the **molecular formula (C₄₅H₅₇N₃O₉)** and the **molecular weight (~784 g/mol)** for full credit.
> - When answering bioprocess questions, do **not** ignore **shear sensitivity** of filamentous fungi — this is a classic 2-mark deduction trap.
> - In numerical problems, always show the **unit of every intermediate**; missing units are the single biggest source of partial-credit loss on KTU answer sheets.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- Beauverin = **cyclic hexadepsipeptide mycotoxin**; C₄₅H₅₇N₃O₉; MW ≈ **784 g/mol**.
- Structure = **3 N-methyl-L-phenylalanine + 3 D-α-hydroxyisovaleric acid**, linked by alternating amide and ester bonds in a closed ring.
- Producing organisms: ***Beauveria bassiana***, ***Fusarium proliferatum***, ***F. semitectum***, ***Paecilomyces***, ***Isaria***.
- Biosynthesis: **Non-Ribosomal Peptide Synthetase (NRPS)** with A, PCP, C, and TE domains — **ribosome-independent**.
- Biological role: **Cation ionophore** (Ca²⁺, K⁺); membrane potential dissipation; apoptosis induction.
- Industrial relevance: **bioinsecticide (IPM)**, **anticancer lead molecule**, **food-safety mycotoxin (EU regulated)**.
- Bioprocess classification: **Secondary metabolite** — produced in the **idiophase** (stationary phase) under **nitrogen limitation**.
- Growth kinetics: **Luedeking–Piret mixed model** $q_P = \alpha \mu + \beta$ — both $\alpha$ and $\beta$ > 0.
- Typical lab-scale titer: **10 – 250 mg/L**; volumetric productivity: **1 – 15 mg/(L·h)**.
- Reactor: **Submerged STR / CSTR**, 25–28 °C, pH 5.5–6.8, 20–40% DO, low-shear impeller (tip speed < 2.5 m/s).
- Process intensification levers: **two-stage reactor**, **in-situ resin adsorption (XAD series)**, **fed-batch C:N manipulation**.
- Downstream processing: centrifugation → solvent extraction (ethyl acetate) → silica column → preparative HPLC / crystallization → **HPLC-MS/MS** confirmation.
- Don't confuse with: **Beauveria** (fungus), **Beauveria bassiana** conidia, or **cyclic lipopeptides** (iturin, surfactin).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Devine
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Devine

> [!NOTE]
> **Topic Clarification Note:** The input topic "Devine" appears to be a typographical/autocorrect artifact. Based on the KTU 2024 Scheme OEFTT726 (Bioprocess Engineering) Module 3 syllabus mapping, this section comprehensively covers the most probable intended topic — **"Design" of Bioprocess Equipment** (encompassing Fermenter/Bioreactor Mechanical Design, Agitator Design, Aeration System Design, and Sterilizer Design). The notes below are aligned to the standard KTU Module 3 learning outcomes.

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Design of Bioprocess Equipment** is the systematic engineering procedure of specifying the geometry, materials, dimensions, and operating parameters of a fermenter (bioreactor), its ancillary systems (agitator, sparger, heat exchanger, sterilization loop), and upstream/downstream units such that the vessel delivers a predefined mass-transfer, heat-transfer, and sterility performance for a given microbial/enzymatic process.

A **fermenter** (or **bioreactor**) is a closed, aseptic vessel in which a biologically active substance is produced by the controlled growth of microorganisms, plant cells, or animal cells in a defined nutrient medium under controlled environmental conditions (pH, temperature, dissolved oxygen, agitation).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 3 Learning Outcome (LO):**
> *Apply the principles of mass transfer, heat transfer, fluid mechanics, and sterilization kinetics to size and design a fermenter and its subsystems (agitator, sparger, heat exchanger, sterilizer).*

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are building a **high-tech fish-aquarium where you grow fish (microorganisms) for food (product)**. Before the fish are introduced, you must guarantee the water is **sterile** (no rival microbes). Once seeded, you need to:

- **Bubble air (oxygen)** uniformly from below → a *sparger*.
- **Stir** the water gently so no fish sits in a "dead zone" → an *agitator/impeller*.
- **Maintain a constant temperature** → a *cooling jacket/coil*.
- **Know the size of the tank** based on how many fish and how much food → *volumetric design*.
- **Ensure the tank walls don't poison the fish** → *material of construction (SS 316 L)*.

Bioprocess design is essentially that — but with **sterility, GMP, and process control** layered on top.

### 1.3 Standard Design Metrics & Constants

| Symbol | Parameter | Typical Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| $H/D$ | Aspect ratio (height-to-diameter) | **1.5 – 3.0** |
| $D_i/D_T$ | Impeller-to-tank diameter ratio | **0.3 – 0.5** |
| $N_P$ | Power number | **5 – 6 (Rushton turbine)** |
| $K_L a$ | Volumetric mass-transfer coeff. | **100 – 1000 h⁻¹** |
| $P/V$ | Power input per unit volume | **0.5 – 5 kW/m³** |
| $v_s$ | Superficial gas velocity | **0.01 – 0.05 m/s** |
| $C^*$ | Saturation DO concentration | **~7 mg/L at 30 °C, 1 atm** |
| $D_{ab}$ | Diffusivity of O₂ in water | **2.1 × 10⁻⁹ m²/s (25 °C)** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The "**Rule of Thumb**" triad for any working fermenter design: **(1)** Sterile, **(2)** Mixed, **(3)** Aerated/Heat-controlled. If any one fails, the design is rejected.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Design Sequence (Structured Logic)

A KTU-compliant fermenter design follows this deterministic sequence:

1. **Define the process need** → Product (e.g., penicillin, ethanol, enzyme), titre, yield, production scale (g, kg, t/yr).
2. **Select operating mode** → Batch / Fed-batch / Continuous.
3. **Estimate working volume $V_w$** from mass balance:
   $$V_w = \frac{\text{Annual production} \times \text{Cycle time}}{\text{Titre} \times \text{Yield} \times \eta \times 8760}$$
4. **Fix aspect ratio $H/D$** and compute tank diameter $D_T$ and height $H$ from $V_w$.
5. **Design the agitator system** → type (Rushton, pitched-blade, hydrofoil), number, diameter, speed, power.
6. **Design the sparger / aeration system** → ring/porous, hole size, gas flow rate, $v_s$.
7. **Design the heat-transfer surface** → jacket area $A_j$ from $Q = UA_j \Delta T_{LMTD}$.
8. **Design the sterilization system** → batch or continuous (hold-time → Del factor).
9. **Specify materials, baffles, and ports.**

### 2.2 Agitator Design (Power Correlation)

Power drawn by an impeller is correlated via the dimensionless **Power Number**:

$$N_P = \frac{P}{\rho \, N^3 \, D_i^5}$$

For **Rushton (disc) turbine** in turbulent flow ($Re > 10^4$):

$$N_P \approx 5.5 \text{ (constant)}$$

For **laminar / transitional** flow, use the **Metzner–Otto correlation** for effective viscosity of non-Newtonian broths:

$$N_{P_{eff}} = f\!\left(Re_{eff}\right), \quad Re_{eff} = \frac{\rho \, N^2 \, D_i^2}{\mu_{app}(k_s N^{n-1})}$$

Under **gassed conditions**, power drops due to gas cavity formation on the impeller blades. The **van't Riet / Michel–Miller** correlation:

$$\frac{P_g}{P_0} = 0.10 \left(\frac{Q_g}{N V_w}\right)^{-0.25} \left(\frac{N^2 D_i^4}{g \, W_i \, V_w^{2/3}}\right)^{-0.20}$$

where $W_i$ is the impeller blade width.

### 2.3 Aeration / Mass-Transfer Design (kLa)

The **Kumar–Tagawa–Miyahara (KTM)** correlation is the workhorse for $K_L a$ estimation in KTU papers:

$$\frac{K_L a \, D_i^2}{D_{ab}} = 0.060 \left(\frac{d_b \, N \, D_i^2}{D_{ab}}\right)^{1.51} \left(\frac{d_b \, g}{u_g^2}\right)^{0.17} \left(\frac{u_g}{N \, D_i}\right)^{0.31} (1 + 2.0 \lambda_L^{0.5})^{-0.67}$$

Simpler **Van't Riet (1979)** form often used:

$$K_L a = K \cdot \left(\frac{P_g}{V_w}\right)^{0.4} \cdot u_s^{0.5}$$

where $K$ is a constant: **K ≈ 0.026 (non-coalescent)** or **0.002 (coalescent)** broth.

**Oxygen transfer rate (OTR)** then becomes:

$$OTR = K_L a \, (C^* - C_L)$$

### 2.4 Sterilizer Design — Batch

For **batch sterilization**, with $N_0$ initial contaminants and thermal death kinetics (first-order):

$$\ln N = \ln N_0 - k_d \, t \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \frac{\ln(N_0/N)}{k_d}$$

$$k_d = A \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right)$$

To relate to a *reference* condition, the **Del factor (Design Criterion)** is used:

$$\nabla = \int_0^t k_d(t)\, dt = A \int_0^t \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT(t)}\right) dt = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)$$

Heating and cooling legs are accounted for:

$$\nabla_{total} = \nabla_{heat} + \nabla_{hold} + \nabla_{cool}$$

For heating/cooling at linear $T$ ramp, change variable $T = T_0 + \alpha t$:

$$\nabla_{ramp} = \frac{A}{\alpha}\int_{T_0}^{T_1}\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT}\right) dT = \frac{A R T_1^2}{\alpha E_a}\left[\exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT_1}\right) - \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_a}{RT_0}\right)\right] \cdot (\text{approx.})$$

### 2.5 Sterilizer Design — Continuous (Plug Flow)

For a **continuous sterilizer** (holding coil), with flow rate $F$ and holding volume $V$:

$$\tau = \frac{V}{F} \quad \text{(residence time)}$$

$$N_{out} = N_{in} \exp(-\nabla_{hold})$$

Required holding length for sterilization at $T_{hold}$:

$$L = \frac{V}{\pi r^2} = \frac{F \cdot \tau}{\pi r^2}$$

### 2.6 Heat-Transfer Surface

For a jacket-side vessel:

$$Q = U \, A_j \, \Delta T_{LMTD}$$

$$A_j = \pi D_T H \quad \text{(for full side jacket, neglecting domes)}$$

For internal coil:

$$A_c = \pi d_c L_c, \quad d_c = 0.6 D_T \text{ (typical)}$$

Overall $U$ values:

| Service | U (W/m²·K) |
|---|---|
| Jacket (cooling) | 200 – 350 |
| Internal coil | 500 – 900 |
| External exchanger | 800 – 1500 |

### 2.7 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Use |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_P = P / (\rho N^3 D_i^5)$ | Impeller power |
| 2 | $P/V_w = \text{specific power input}$ | kLa driver |
| 3 | $K_L a = K(P_g/V)^{0.4} u_s^{0.5}$ | Oxygen transfer |
| 4 | $OTR = K_L a(C^* - C_L)$ | O₂ supply rate |
| 5 | $\nabla = A \int \exp(-E_a/RT) dt$ | Del factor |
| 6 | $k_d = A \exp(-E_a/RT)$ | Thermal death rate |
| 7 | $\tau = V/F$ | Residence time |
| 8 | $A_j = \pi D_T H$ | Jacket area |
| 9 | $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$ | Heat duty |
| 10 | $v_s = Q_g / A_T$ | Superficial gas velocity |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-world utility:** These equations are used by design engineers at companies like Sartorius, Eppendorf, GEA, and ABEC to scale-up bioreactors from 1 L lab-scale to 200 m³ production vessels. The $K_L a$ equation is the single most-asked parameter in industrial scale-up audits.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I — Working Volume from Mass Balance

**Statement:** A plant must produce 100 t/yr of an antibiotic at a titre of 25 g/L. The cycle time (including turnaround) is 8 days. Assume yield factor $\eta = 0.95$. Find the working volume $V_w$.

**Step 1 — Convert annual production to per-cycle demand:**

$$\text{Production per cycle} = \frac{100{,}000 \text{ kg}}{8760 \text{ h}/8 \text{ d/cycle}} = \frac{100{,}000}{365} \approx 274 \text{ kg/cycle}$$

**Step 2 — Relate titre to required broth volume:**

$$V_w = \frac{\text{kg/cycle}}{\text{titre} \cdot \eta} = \frac{274 \text{ kg}}{25 \text{ g/L} \cdot 0.95} = \frac{274{,}000 \text{ g}}{23.75 \text{ g/L}}$$

$$V_w \approx 11{,}537 \text{ L} \approx 11.5 \text{ m}^3$$

This is the *working* volume. Total vessel volume $V_T = V_w / 0.7 \approx 16.5$ m³ (since working volume is typically **70%** of nominal).

**Step 3 — Fix $H/D = 2.0$ (typical) and solve geometry:**

$$V_T = \frac{\pi}{4} D_T^2 H = \frac{\pi}{4} D_T^2 (2 D_T) = \frac{\pi D_T^3}{2}$$

$$D_T^3 = \frac{2 V_T}{\pi} = \frac{2 \times 16.5}{3.1416} = 10.51 \text{ m}^3$$

$$D_T = (10.51)^{1/3} \approx 2.19 \text{ m}, \quad H = 2 D_T \approx 4.38 \text{ m}$$

### 3.2 Derivation II — Power Input for Agitation

**Statement:** For the above fermenter, design a two-Rushton-impeller system with $D_i = 0.4 D_T$, $N = 120$ rpm, broth density $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³. Find $P$ and $P/V_w$.

**Step 1 — Compute $D_i$:**

$$D_i = 0.4 \times 2.19 = 0.876 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2 — Convert rotational speed:**

$$N = 120/60 = 2.0 \text{ rev/s}$$

**Step 3 — Reynolds number check (assume water-like, $\mu = 1$ cP = 10⁻³ Pa·s):**

$$Re = \frac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 2.0 \times (0.876)^2}{10^{-3}} = \frac{1534.7}{10^{-3}} = 1.53 \times 10^6$$

$$Re \gg 10^4 \Rightarrow \text{Turbulent}, \quad N_P \approx 5.5$$

**Step 4 — Compute Power:**

$$P = N_P \, \rho \, N^3 \, D_i^5 = 5.5 \times 1000 \times (2.0)^3 \times (0.876)^5$$

$$P = 5.5 \times 1000 \times 8.0 \times 0.516 \approx 22{,}704 \text{ W} \approx 22.7 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 5 — Specific power input (per impeller pair; here 2 impellers ⇒ double):**

$$\frac{P_{total}}{V_w} = \frac{2 \times 22{,}704}{11.5} \approx 3{,}950 \text{ W/m}^3 = 3.95 \text{ kW/m}^3$$

This sits comfortably in the typical $0.5 - 5$ kW/m³ range — **design acceptable**.

### 3.3 Derivation III — Aeration / $K_L a$ & OTR

**Statement:** For the same vessel, with $u_s = 0.02$ m/s and a non-coalescent broth, find $K_L a$ and OTR at $C_L = 2$ mg/L, $T = 30$ °C, 1 atm.

**Step 1 — Apply Van't Riet correlation:**

$$K_L a = 0.026 \times (P_g/V)^{0.4} \times u_s^{0.5}$$

Take $P_g \approx 0.6 P$ under gassed conditions (Michel–Miller heuristic): $P_g/V = 0.6 \times 3{,}950 = 2{,}370$ W/m³.

$$(P_g/V)^{0.4} = (2370)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 2370} = e^{0.4 \times 7.77} = e^{3.108} \approx 22.4$$

$$u_s^{0.5} = (0.02)^{0.5} = 0.1414$$

$$K_L a = 0.026 \times 22.4 \times 0.1414 \approx 0.0823 \text{ s}^{-1} = 296 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — OTR calculation:**

$C^*$ at 30 °C in water at 1 atm ≈ **7.6 mg/L** (standard table).

$$OTR = K_L a (C^* - C_L) = 0.0823 \times (7.6 - 2) \text{ mg/(L·s)} = 0.461 \text{ mg/(L·s)}$$

Total oxygen transfer to the broth per second:

$$\dot{m}_{O_2} = OTR \times V_w = 0.461 \times 11{,}500 \text{ L} = 5{,}300 \text{ mg/s} = 5.3 \text{ g/s}$$

This must equal the **OUR (oxygen uptake rate)** of the cells. If it does, the aeration design is consistent.

### 3.4 Derivation IV — Batch Sterilizer Hold Time

**Statement:** A 10 m³ medium has $N_0 = 10^5$ spores/mL of *Bacillus stearothermophilus* and must reach $N = 10^{-3}$ spores/mL (i.e., $10^{12}$-fold kill). Sterilize at 121 °C. Use $A = 1.0 \times 10^{36}$ min⁻¹, $E_a = 283$ kJ/mol, $R = 8.314$ J/mol·K.

**Step 1 — Compute $k_d$ at 121 °C (= 394 K):**

$$k_d = 1.0 \times 10^{36} \times \exp\!\left(-\frac{283{,}000}{8.314 \times 394}\right)$$

$$\frac{E_a}{RT} = \frac{283{,}000}{3{,}275.7} = 86.39$$

$$k_d = 1.0 \times 10^{36} \times e^{-86.39} = 1.0 \times 10^{36} \times 2.50 \times 10^{-38} \approx 0.025 \text{ min}^{-1}$$

(The well-known textbook value is $k_d \approx 0.025$ min⁻¹ at 121 °C ✓)

**Step 2 — Compute required Del factor:**

$$\nabla = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right) = \ln\!\left(\frac{10^5}{10^{-3}}\right) = \ln(10^8) = 8 \times 2.303 = 18.42$$

**Step 3 — Hold time at constant 121 °C:**

$$t_{hold} = \frac{\nabla}{k_d} = \frac{18.42}{0.025} = 736.8 \text{ min} \approx 12.3 \text{ hours}$$

This is the **hold time at constant temperature**. Heating and cooling ramps would contribute additional $\nabla$ but require numerical integration.

### 3.5 Derivation V — Continuous Sterilizer Coil Length

**Statement:** Media flows at $F = 5$ m³/h through a continuous sterilizer holding coil of 50 mm ID at 130 °C. $k_d$ at 130 °C = 0.20 min⁻¹. Required $\nabla = 20$. Find coil length.

**Step 1 — Required residence time at 130 °C:**

$$\tau = \frac{\nabla}{k_d} = \frac{20}{0.20} = 100 \text{ min}$$

**Step 2 — Cross-sectional area:**

$$A_c = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 3 — Coil length:**

$$L = \frac{F \cdot \tau}{A_c} = \frac{(5/3600) \times 100}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{0.1389}{1.963 \times 10^{-3}} = 70.7 \text{ m}$$

A long helical coil — typical industrial sterilizer configuration.

### 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — When to Use What

| Condition | Choose | Why |
|---|---|---|
| Heat-sensitive media (vitamins) | Continuous sterilizer | Short exposure |
| Heat-stable, low cost (salts) | Batch sterilizer | Simple, cheap |
| Newtonian broth | Rushton turbine | High $N_P$, good gas dispersion |
| Shear-sensitive (mammalian cells) | Pitched-blade / marine | Low shear |
| High viscosity broth (mycelial) | Multiple impellers + helical | Better bulk mixing |
| Coalescent broth (salt-rich) | $K = 0.002$ in $K_L a$ corr. | Matches physical reality |
