## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — Short Answer (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **Power Number** for an agitated vessel. What is its significance in bioreactor design?

**Model Answer:**
The Power Number $N_P$ is a dimensionless group defined as:

$$N_P = \frac{P}{\rho N^3 D_i^5}$$

It represents the ratio of inertial drag forces to inertial forces on the impeller. Its significance in bioreactor design is twofold:
1. It allows **scale-up** — once $N_P$ is known, the power $P$ can be calculated for any impeller diameter/speed combination while preserving flow regime.
2. It links directly to **mass transfer** ($K_L a \propto (P/V)^{0.4}$) and hence to oxygen delivery to cells.

**[3 marks: 1 mark definition + 2 marks significance]**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the **Del factor** in batch sterilization? Why is it called a "design criterion"?

**Model Answer:**
The **Del factor** $\nabla$ is defined as:

$$\nabla = \int_0^t k_d(T)\, dt = \ln\!\left(\frac{N_0}{N}\right)$$

It quantifies the **integrated lethal effect** of a time-temperature sterilization cycle. It is called a "design criterion" because the designer specifies a required $\nabla$ (typically **18–22** for industrial nutrient media) that guarantees a pre-defined spore inactivation ratio (e.g., $10^{12}$), and then sizes the holding time / sterilizer geometry to deliver this $\nabla$ reliably.

**[3 marks: 1 mark formula + 2 marks reasoning]**

---

### Part B — Long Answer (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3]**

> **(a)** A 15 m³ working-volume fermenter is fitted with two Rushton turbines of diameter $D_i = 0.9$ m, operating at 150 rpm. The broth density is 1050 kg/m³ and viscosity 2 cP. Compute (i) Reynolds number, (ii) power drawn per impeller, and (iii) gassed power assuming a gas flow of 0.5 vvm. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Explain the **van't Riet correlation** for $K_L a$ estimation in a non-coalescent broth. Using $P_g/V = 3000$ W/m³ and $u_s = 0.025$ m/s, compute $K_L a$ and the OTR at $C_L = 1.5$ mg/L. Take $C^* = 7.5$ mg/L. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Step-by-step:**

*Step 1 — Convert units:*
- $N = 150/60 = 2.5$ rev/s
- $\mu = 2 \text{ cP} = 2 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s
- $V_w = 15$ m³

*Step 2 — Reynolds number:*

$$Re = \frac{\rho N D_i^2}{\mu} = \frac{1050 \times 2.5 \times (0.9)^2}{2 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2126.25}{0.002} = 1.06 \times 10^6$$

*Step 3 — Since $Re > 10^4$ (turbulent), $N_P \approx 5.5$ for Rushton turbine:*

$$P = N_P \rho N^3 D_i^5 = 5.5 \times 1050 \times (2.5)^3 \times (0.9)^5$$

$$P = 5.5 \times 1050 \times 15.625 \times 0.5905 \approx 55{,}890 \text{ W} \approx 55.9 \text{ kW per impeller}$$

*Step 4 — Gassed power using Michel–Miller:*

$$\frac{P_g}{P_0} = 0.10 (Q_g/NV)^{-0.25} (N^2 D_i^4 / gW_i V^{2/3})^{-0.20}$$

With $Q_g = 0.5 \times 15/60 = 0.125$ m³/s and assuming $W_i = 0.2 D_i = 0.18$ m:
- $Q_g/NV = 0.125/(2.5 \times 15) = 3.33 \times 10^{-3}$ → $(...)^{-0.25} \approx 4.16$
- $N^2 D_i^4 = 6.25 \times 0.656 = 4.10$ m³/s²
- $g W_i V^{2/3} = 9.81 \times 0.18 \times (15)^{2/3} = 9.81 \times 0.18 \times 6.08 = 10.74$
- Ratio = 4.10/10.74 = 0.382 → $(...)^{-0.20} \approx 1.22$

$$\frac{P_g}{P_0} = 0.10 \times 4.16 \times 1.22 \approx 0.51$$

$$P_g \approx 0.51 \times 55.9 \times 2 = 57 \text{ kW total (2 impellers)}$$

*Step 5 — Specific power:*

$$\frac{P_g}{V_w} = \frac{57{,}000}{15} = 3{,}800 \text{ W/m}^3 = 3.8 \text{ kW/m}^3$$

✔ Within the typical $0.5 - 5$ kW/m³ band.

**[Part (a): 7 marks — 2 for Re, 3 for P, 2 for $P_g$]**

**Part (b) — Step-by-step:**

*Step 1 — Explain van't Riet correlation (write 3-4 lines):*
The van't Riet (1979) correlation is an empirical relation linking the volumetric mass-transfer coefficient $K_L a$ to the **specific power input** $(P_g/V)$ and the **superficial gas velocity** $u_s$:

$$K_L a = K (P_g/V)^{0.4} u_s^{0.5}$$

For **non-coalescent** broths (e.g., those rich in salts, antifoam, or with high cell density), $K = 0.026$. For **coalescent** broths, $K = 0.002$. The 0.4 exponent on $P_g/V$ reflects the importance of mechanical agitation in breaking/coalescing bubbles; the 0.5 exponent on $u_s$ reflects the direct effect of gas holdup on interfacial area.

*Step 2 — Numerical evaluation:*

$$(P_g/V)^{0.4} = (3000)^{0.4} = e^{0.4 \ln 3000} = e^{0.4 \times 8.006} = e^{3.203} = 24.6$$

$$u_s^{0.5} = (0.025)^{0.5} = 0.1581$$

$$K_L a = 0.026 \times 24.6 \times 0.1581 = 0.1011 \text{ s}^{-1} = 364 \text{ h}^{-1}$$

*Step 3 — OTR:*

$$OTR = K_L a (C^* - C_L) = 0.1011 \times (7.5 - 1.5) = 0.607 \text{ mg/(L·s)}$$

*Step 4 — Total O₂ supply:*

$$\dot{m}_{O_2} = 0.607 \text{ mg/(L·s)} \times 15{,}000 \text{ L} = 9{,}105 \text{ mg/s} \approx 9.1 \text{ g/s}$$

This must meet the **OUR** (oxygen uptake rate) of the culture. If OUR > 9.1 g/s, the system is **oxygen-limited** and the design must be revised (higher $N$ or larger $D_i$).

**[Part (b): 7 marks — 3 for theory, 2 for $K_L a$, 2 for OTR]**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024 Scheme, Module 3, Internal Choice]**

> **(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **design features of a typical stirred-tank fermenter**. List **five key components** and state their design functions. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A nutrient medium with initial spore load $N_0 = 5 \times 10^4$ spores/mL is to be batch-sterilized at 121 °C to achieve a final count of $N = 10^{-3}$ spores/mL. Use $k_d(121°C) = 0.025$ min⁻¹. Compute (i) the required Del factor, (ii) the hold time at constant temperature, and (iii) the additional $\nabla$ contributed by a 15-minute linear heating ramp from 110 °C to 121 °C (assume the ramp integral is approximated as $1.5 \times k_d(121°C) \times 15$ min). Comment on whether the cooling ramp should also be considered. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Part (a) — Descriptive + Sketch (text equivalent):**

A **stirred-tank fermenter (STF)** has the following key design features:

1. **Cylindrical vessel with dished heads** — $H/D$ typically 1.5–3.0. Material: **SS-316L** (low-carbon, corrosion-resistant, GMP-compliant).
2. **Agitator system** — 2 or 3 Rushton disc turbines on a single shaft, with $D_i = 0.3 - 0.5 D_T$. Provides radial mixing and gas dispersion.
3. **Baffles** — 4 vertical flat strips, width = $0.1 D_T$, attached to vessel wall. Prevents vortexing, promotes turbulent mixing.
4. **Ring sparger** — perforated ring located just below the lower impeller, with holes of 5–10 mm diameter. Distributes sterile air uniformly.
5. **Heat-transfer jacket (or coil)** — dimpled jacket on outer wall for cooling/heating. Maintains broth at optimum temperature (typically 30–37 °C).
6. **Instrumentation ports** — DO, pH, temperature, foam, level probes.
7. **Sample port, harvest line, inoculation port** — all sterilizable (steam-block) valves.

*Functions:*
- Sterility, mixing, gas–liquid mass transfer, temperature control, monitoring & control.

**Part (b) — Step-by-step calculation:**

*Step 1 — Del factor:*

$$\nabla_{req} = \ln(N_0/N) = \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^4}{10^{-3}}\right) = \ln(5 \times 10^7)$$

$$\nabla_{req} = \ln 5 + 7 \ln 10 = 1.609 + 16.12 = 17.73$$

*Step 2 — Hold time at 121 °C:*

$$t_{hold} = \frac{\nabla_{req} - \nabla_{ramp}}{k_d} = \frac{17.73 - 0.5625}{0.025} = \frac{17.17}{0.025} = 686.7 \text{ min} \approx 11.45 \text{ h}$$

*Step 3 — Additional ∇ from heating ramp:*

$$\nabla_{ramp} = 1.5 \times 0.025 \times 15 = 0.5625$$

*Step 4 — Cooling ramp comment:*
The cooling ramp (from 121 °C to ~80 °C) also contributes additional $\nabla$ (often 1–3 units) but reduces the **nutrient damage** because the medium is hot for less time. Designers usually **credit** this cooling $\nabla$ toward $\nabla_{req}$, but the **cooling medium design (chilled water rate, jacket area)** must ensure the broth is cooled to growth temperature within the design time.

**[Part (b): 7 marks — 1.5 for ∇, 1.5 for $t_{hold}$, 2 for ramp ∇, 2 for cooling comment]**

---

### ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 places where students LOSE marks in Module 3 Design questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert rpm → rev/s** in the power formula. Always write $N$ in rev/s, NOT rpm. A 60× error is a guaranteed 3-mark loss.
> 2. **Using $N_P = 5.5$ in laminar regime** ($Re < 10$). The Metzner–Otto approach or a curve lookup is needed.
> 3. **Mixing up $P$ vs $P_g$**. Use the **un-gassed** $P$ in $K_L a$ correlations only if the original correlation was developed that way; otherwise use $P_g$.
> 4. **Omitting the "kPa / atm / m water column" unit** when writing $k_d$. KTU expects **consistent SI units** throughout.
> 5. **Not commenting on the design decision** (e.g., "specific power is in the acceptable range, design is feasible") — KTU awards 1–2 marks for the **interpretive sentence** at the end.

---

### 🧠 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

- ✅ **Power Number** $N_P = P / (\rho N^3 D_i^5)$ — use **5.5** for Rushton turbine in turbulent flow.
- ✅ **Aspect ratio $H/D = 1.5 - 3.0$** — pick **2.0** if not specified.
- ✅ **Working volume = 70%** of nominal vessel volume.
- ✅ **Reynolds number $Re = \rho N D_i^2 / \mu$** — turbulent if $Re > 10^4$.
- ✅ **Gassed power $P_g \approx 0.4 - 0.7 \, P$** (heuristic from Michel–Miller).
- ✅ **Van't Riet $K_L a$**: $K = 0.026$ (non-coalescent), $K = 0.002$ (coalescent).
- ✅ **OTR = $K_L a (C^* - C_L)$** — must equal OUR for an oxygen-balanced design.
- ✅ **Del factor** $\nabla = \ln(N_0/N)$ — typical industrial target = **18 – 22**.
- ✅ **$k_d(121°C) = 0.025$ min⁻¹** for *B. stearothermophilus* (memorize this number).
- ✅ **Continuous sterilizer** $L = F\tau/A_c$ — holds the medium at sterilization T.
- ✅ **Heat transfer:** $Q = U A \Delta T_{LMTD}$; jacket $U$ ≈ **250 W/m²·K**; coil $U$ ≈ **700 W/m²·K**.
- ✅ **Material:** **SS-316L** is the gold standard for aseptic fermenter construction.
- ✅ **Baffles = 4**, width = $0.1 D_T$ — prevents vortex, increases $K_L a$.
- ✅ **Sparger** ring located **just below the lower impeller** at $0.5 D_i$ from centre.
- ✅ **Aseptic design rule:** every line entering/leaving the vessel has a **steam block** valve.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Biopreservatives
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Biopreservatives # 🧬 Module 4 — Biopreservatives: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition** > A **Biopreservative** is a natural, biologically-derived antimicrobial compound (typically produced by microorganisms such as lactic acid bacteria, yeast, or fungi via fermentation) that is used to inhibit the growth of spoilage-causing and pathogenic microorganisms, thereby extending the shelf-life and enhancing the safety of food, pharmaceutical, and cosmetic products without compromising their nutritional or sensory quality. In simpler terms, biopreservatives are **"nature's own food guardians"** — they are the same substances that friendly microbes secrete to outcompete harmful bacteria in their ecological niche. Instead of using harsh synthetic chemicals (like BHA, BHT, or sodium benzoate at high doses), we harness these natural weapons through controlled **bioprocess engineering**. ## 💡 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a crowded apartment building (your packaged food) where unwanted guests (pathogenic bacteria like *Listeria* or *Salmonella*) want to break in. Normally, you'd hire a security guard (synthetic preservatives). But here, you instead release a **"friendly guard dog"** (a biopreservative like nisin) that was raised and trained by another friendly neighbor (a lactic acid bacterium). The guard dog doesn't harm you or the apartment — it only targets the bad guests. This is exactly what **nisin** does: it's a bacteriocin produced by *Lactococcus lactis* that punches holes in the membranes of Gram-positive pathogens. ## 📘 Standard Terminology & Key Metrics > [!NOTE] > **Critical Terminology for KTU Examinations** > - **Bacteriocin**: Ribosomally synthesized antimicrobial peptides produced by bacteria. > - **Lantibiotic**: A subclass of bacteriocins containing unusual amino acids like lanthionine (e.g., Nisin). > - **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)**: The U.S. FDA designation required for food-grade biopreservatives. > - **Minimum Inhibitory Concentration (MIC)**: The lowest concentration of preservative that visibly inhibits microbial growth, typically expressed in **µg/mL** or **IU/mL** (International Units per milliliter for nisin). > - **Shelf-life Extension Factor**: Ratio of shelf life with preservative to shelf life without, dimensionless. > - **Antimicrobial Spectrum**: Range of microorganisms inhibited — narrow (specific) or broad. > - **Biopreservative Yield ($Y_{P/S}$)**: Grams of preservative produced per gram of substrate consumed. ## 🧪 Why Biopreservatives Over Chemical Preservatives? | Property | Chemical Preservatives | Biopreservatives | |----------|------------------------|------------------| | Source | Synthetic | Microbial / Natural | | Consumer perception | Negative (allergies, toxicity concerns) | Positive (clean-label) | | Toxicity | Higher at elevated doses | Generally recognized as safe | | Digestibility | Poor | Easily digestible (proteins/peptides) | | Mode of action | Often single target | Multi-target (pore formation, pH drop) | | Regulatory acceptance | Restricted in some products | Widely accepted (GRAS status) | | Cost | Low | Moderate to high (downstream processing cost) | # ⚙️ Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 🏛️ Classification of Biopreservatives Biopreservatives are classified based on their origin, chemical nature, and mechanism of action. This classification is **highly examinable** in KTU ESE. ### 1️⃣ By Origin (Most Common Classification for KTU) **(a) Microbial Origin** - **Bacteriocins** — Nisin (from *Lactococcus lactis*), Pediocin (from *Pediococcus acidilactici*), Sakacin (from *Lactobacillus sakei*). - **Organic acids** — Lactic acid, Acetic acid, Propionic acid. - **Ethanol** — Produced by yeasts (*Saccharomyces*). - **Hydrogen peroxide** — Produced by *Lactobacillus* spp. **(b) Plant Origin (Phytochemicals)** - Essential oils (thymol, carvacrol, eugenol). - Polyphenols (resveratrol). **(c) Animal Origin** - **Lysozyme** (from hen egg white). - **Lactoferrin** (from milk). - **Chitosan** (from crustacean shells — chitin deacetylation). **(d) Fungal Origin** - Natamycin (from *Streptomyces natalensis*) — used in cheese, sausages. ### 2️⃣ By Chemical Nature | Class | Examples | Molecular Nature | |-------|----------|------------------| | Bacteriocins | Nisin, Pediocin | Cationic peptides (3–6 kDa) | | Organic acids | Lactic, Acetic | Weak acids (pKa 3–5) | | Antibiotics | Natamycin | Polyene macrolide | | Enzymes | Lysozyme | Glycoside hydrolase (14.3 kDa) | | Phenolics | Catechins | Aromatic secondary metabolites | ### 3️⃣ By Mechanism of Action (Critical for 14-Mark Questions) | Mechanism | Example | Target Site | |-----------|---------|-------------| | **Pore formation in cytoplasmic membrane** | Nisin | Lipid II + membrane | | **pH reduction / proton gradient disruption** | Lactic acid | Cytoplasm acidification | | **Cell wall hydrolysis** | Lysozyme | Peptidoglycan β-1,4 bond | | **Inhibition of protein synthesis** | Natamycin | Ergosterol-binding in fungi | | **Chelation of essential metals** | Lactoferrin | Fe³⁺ sequestration | | **Disruption of enzymatic systems** | Essential oils | SH-groups, ATPase | ## 📐 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these equations — they appear repeatedly in ESE numericals and derivations.** **Table 1 — Core Equations for Biopreservative Engineering** | Concept | Formula | Variables & Units | Significance | |---------|---------|-------------------|--------------| | Michaelis–Menten Inhibition Kinetics | $v = \dfrac{V_{max} \cdot S}{K_m \cdot \left(1 + \dfrac{I}{K_i}\right) + S}$ | $v$ = reaction rate (µmol/min), $V_{max}$ = max rate, $K_m$ = substrate affinity (mM), $I$ = inhibitor conc., $K_i$ = inhibition constant | Predicts how bacteriocins slow pathogen growth | | Henderson–Hasselbalch Equation | $pH = pK_a + \log \dfrac{[A^-]}{[HA]}$ | $pK_a$ = acid dissociation constant | Determines fraction of undissociated organic acid available to penetrate microbial cells | | Logistic Growth (Limited by Biopreservative) | $\dfrac{dN}{dt} = \mu_{max} \cdot N \cdot \left(1 - \dfrac{N}{N_{max}}\right)$ | $N$ = cell density (CFU/mL), $\mu_{max}$ = max specific growth rate (h⁻¹) | Models microbial inhibition over time | | Biopreservative Yield Coefficient | $Y_{P/S} = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\Delta S}$ | $\Delta P$ = product formed (g/L), $\Delta S$ = substrate consumed (g/L) | Quantifies bioprocess efficiency | | Volumetric Productivity | $Q_P = \dfrac{P}{t_f}$ | $P$ = product concentration (g/L), $t_f$ = fermentation time (h) | Measures process throughput (g/L·h) | | Decimal Reduction Time (D-value) | $\log N_t = \log N_0 - \dfrac{t}{D}$ | $N_0$ = initial count, $N_t$ = count at time $t$, $D$ = time for 90% kill (min) | Quantifies preservative potency | | Shelf-life Extension Ratio | $\eta = \dfrac{SL_{preserved}}{SL_{control}}$ | $SL$ = shelf life (days), dimensionless | Demonstrates commercial value | **Boundary Conditions & Critical Constants (to memorize for KTU):** - **Nisin molecular weight**: ~3,350 Da (34 amino acids, 5 lanthionine rings). - **Nisin commercial form**: Nisaplin (2.5% pure nisin w/w in NaCl). - **Nisin typical dose**: 100–250 IU/g in cheese (1 IU ≈ 0.025 µg pure nisin). - **Lactic acid pKa**: **3.86** at 25 °C. - **Acetic acid pKa**: **4.76** at 25 °C. - **Typical fermentation pH for nisin production**: 6.0–6.5. - **Optimum temperature for *Lactococcus lactis* growth**: 30 °C. > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility** > In the dairy industry, nisin is used at ~**10 mg/kg** in processed cheese to inhibit *Clostridium botulinum* spores. The bioprocess engineer must calculate the fermentation time, substrate consumption, and downstream recovery cost to deliver nisin at a competitive price (~$0.10 per IU) — making bioprocess optimization economically vital. # 🔬 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 🧮 Derivation 1 — Optimum Preservation Dose Using D-value Concept The **D-value** (Decimal Reduction Time) is the time required at a given condition to reduce the microbial population by **90% (1 log cycle)**. Combining the D-value with target shelf-life gives the **required biopreservative dose**. ### Step-by-Step Derivation: Let $N_0$ = initial microbial count (CFU/g) and $N_t$ = count after time $t$. By definition of D-value, every $D$ minutes reduces the population by a factor of 10: $$ \frac{N_t}{N_0} = 10^{-t/D} $$ Taking the logarithm of both sides (base 10): $$ \log N_t - \log N_0 = -\frac{t}{D} $$ Rearranging for $t$ (the required preservation time): $$ t = D \cdot \left(\log N_0 - \log N_t\right) $$ If we know the initial contamination $N_0$ and the maximum acceptable count $N_t$ at the end of shelf-life, we can determine the required $D$ value, which is inversely related to biopreservative concentration $C$: $$ D = \frac{D_{ref}}{10^{(C - C_{ref})/z}} $$ where $D_{ref}$ is the D-value at reference concentration $C_{ref}$, and $z$ is the concentration change needed to alter D by a factor of 10 (analogous to the z-value in thermal sterilization). Substituting: $$ t = \frac{D_{ref}}{10^{(C - C_{ref})/z}} \cdot \left(\log N_0 - \log N_t\right) $$ Solving for the **required concentration $C$**: $$ C = C_{ref} + z \cdot \log\left[\frac{D_{ref} \cdot (\log N_0 - \log N_t)}{t}\right] $$ ### Worked Numerical Example: A dairy product contains $N_0 = 10^4$ CFU/g of *Listeria monocytogenes*. The maximum acceptable count after storage is $N_t = 10$ CFU/g. The shelf-life requirement is $t = 30$ days. At a reference nisin concentration of $C_{ref} = 100$ IU/g, the D-value is $D_{ref} = 12$ days. The $z$-value for nisin is $z = 80$ IU/g. **Step 1**: Compute the log reduction required. $$ \log N_0 - \log N_t = \log(10^4) - \log(10) = 4 - 1 = 3 \text{ log cycles} $$ **Step 2**: Substitute into the master equation. $$ C = 100 + 80 \cdot \log\left[\frac{12 \cdot 3}{30}\right] $$ **Step 3**: Evaluate the bracket. $$ \frac{12 \cdot 3}{30} = \frac{36}{30} = 1.2 $$ **Step 4**: Compute the logarithm. $$ \log(1.2) = 0.0792 $$ **Step 5**: Final concentration. $$ C = 100 + 80 \cdot 0.0792 = 100 + 6.33 = 106.33 \text{ IU/g} $$ **Engineering Interpretation**: A minimum of ~106 IU/g nisin is required to ensure the product remains safe for 30 days. Industrial practice rounds up to **110 IU/g** with a safety factor of 1.04. --- ## 🧮 Derivation 2 — Bioprocess Mass Balance for Nisin Production Consider a fed-batch fermentation for nisin production by *L. lactis* on a glucose-based medium. ### Mass Balance Derivation: Let: - $X$ = biomass concentration (g/L) - $S$ = substrate (glucose) concentration (g/L) - $P$ = nisin concentration (g/L) - $V$ = broth volume (L) **Substrate consumption rate** (assuming Monod kinetics with maintenance): $$ -\frac{dS}{dt} = \frac{\mu_{max}}{Y_{X/S}} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S} \cdot X + m_S \cdot X $$ where $Y_{X/S}$ is the biomass yield on substrate, $m_S$ is the maintenance coefficient (g substrate/g biomass·h), and $K_S$ is the Monod constant. **Product formation** (nisin is a primary metabolite, growth-associated): $$ \frac{dP}{dt} = Y_{P/X} \cdot \frac{dX}{dt} = Y_{P/X} \cdot \mu_{max} \cdot \frac{S}{K_S + S} \cdot X $$ **Step 1**: Integrating biomass growth from 0 to time $t_f$: $$ \int_0^{X_f} \frac{dX}{X} = \mu_{max} \int_0^{t_f} \frac{S}{K_S + S} \, dt $$ Assuming pseudo-steady-state with $S \gg K_S$ (typical when initial glucose is high, ~20 g/L): $$ \ln\left(\frac{X_f}{X_0}\right) \approx \mu_{max} \cdot t_f $$ **Step 2**: Solving for fermentation time: $$ t_f = \frac{1}{\mu_{max}} \ln\left(\frac{X_f}{X_0}\right) $$ **Step 3**: Substituting into product formation (with $Y_{P/X}$ constant): $$ P_f = Y_{P/X} \cdot (X_f - X_0) $$ **Step 4**: Volumetric productivity: $$ Q_P = \frac{P_f}{t_f} = \frac{Y_{P/X} \cdot (X_f - X_0) \cdot \mu_{max}}{\ln(X_f/X_0)} $$ ### Worked Numerical Example: Given: $\mu_{max} = 0.5 \text{ h}^{-1}$, $X_0 = 0.1 \text{ g/L}$, $X_f = 2.5 \text{ g/L}$, $Y_{P/X} = 0.05 \text{ g nisin/g biomass}$. **Step 1**: Compute $t_f$. $$ t_f = \frac{1}{0.5} \ln\left(\frac{2.5}{0.1}\right) = 2 \cdot \ln(25) = 2 \cdot 3.2189 = 6.44 \text{ h} $$ **Step 2**: Compute $P_f$. $$ P_f = 0.05 \cdot (2.5 - 0.1) = 0.05 \cdot 2.4 = 0.12 \text{ g/L} = 120 \text{ mg/L} $$ **Step 3**: Compute $Q_P$. $$ Q_P = \frac{0.12}{6.44} = 0.0186 \text{ g/L·h} = 18.6 \text{ mg/L·h} $$ This corresponds to an industrial-grade nisin titer in **6.4 hours**, which is economically favorable for a commercial bioprocess. --- ## 📊 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Evaluation of Major Biopreservatives > [!IMPORTANT] > **This comparative table is a KTU board-exam favorite. Memorize the structure.** | Parameter | Nisin | Natamycin | Lysozyme | Pediocin | |-----------|-------|-----------|----------|----------| | Producer organism | *Lactococcus lactis* | *Streptomyces natalensis* | Hen egg white (natural) | *Pediococcus acidilactici* | | Chemical class | Lantibiotic (Class I bacteriocin) | Polyene macrolide | Glycoside hydrolase | Class IIa bacteriocin | | Molecular weight | 3,350 Da | 665 Da | 14,300 Da | 4,628 Da | | Spectrum | Gram-positive | Fungi (yeasts/molds) | Gram-positive | *Listeria* spp. | | Application | Cheese, canned food | Cheese surface, sausages | Wine, cheese | Meat products | | Approved dose | 100–250 IU/g | 1–10 mg/kg | 250–500 mg/L | 100–500 AU/g | | Regulatory status | GRAS, E234 | GRAS, E235 | GRAS | GRAS | **Engineering Insight**: Pediocin's narrow spectrum makes it ideal for **targeted pathogen control** in ready-to-eat meats without disturbing the natural microbiota — a key advantage over broad-spectrum antibiotics. # 🗺️ Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 🔄 Mermaid Diagram 1 — Classification of Biopreservatives ```mermaid graph TD A[Biopreservatives]:::root --> B1[Microbial Origin] A --> B2[Plant Origin] A --> B3[Animal Origin] A --> B4[Fungal Origin] B1 --> C1[Bacteriocins] B1 --> C2[Organic Acids] B1 --> C3[Ethanol] B1 --> C4[Hydrogen Peroxide] C1 --> D1[Lantibiotics
Nisin] C1 --> D2[Non-lantibiotics
Pediocin] C1 --> D3[Large proteins
Helveticin] C2 --> E1[Lactic Acid] C2 --> E2[Acetic Acid] C2 --> E3[Propionic Acid] B2 --> F1[Essential Oils] B2 --> F2[Polyphenols] B3 --> G1[Lysozyme] B3 --> G2[Lactoferrin] B3 --> G3[Chitosan] B4 --> H1[Natamycin] B4 --> H2[Citrinin] classDef root fill:#1f77b4,stroke:#000,stroke-width:3px,color:#fff classDef sub fill:#ff7f0e,stroke:#000,color:#fff ``` ## 🏭 Mermaid Diagram 2 — Bioprocess Flow for Nisin Production ```mermaid graph TD S1[Inoculum Preparation
Lactococcus lactis
30 degC, 18 h]:::stage --> S2 S2[Fermenter
Glucose 20 g/L
pH 6.5, 30 degC
6-8 h]:::stage --> S3 S3[Cell Separation
Microfiltration
or Centrifugation]:::stage --> S4 S4[Concentration
Ultrafiltration
or Ammonium Sulfate Precipitation]:::stage --> S5 S5[Purification
Ion Exchange
Chromatography]:::stage --> S6 S6[Formulation
Nisaplin 2.5%
w/w in NaCl]:::stage --> S7 S7[Quality Control
HPLC, Bioassay
IU/mL determination]:::stage S7 --> P1[Cheese and Dairy]:::product S7 --> P2[Canned Foods]:::product S7 --> P3[Meat Products]:::product classDef stage fill:#2ca02c,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef product fill:#d62728,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px ``` ## 🧫 Mermaid Diagram 3 — Mechanism of Nisin Action on Gram-Positive Bacteria ```mermaid graph LR A[Gram-positive
Pathogen Cell] --> B[Lipid II
Recognition]:::step B --> C[Pore Formation
in Cytoplasm]:::step C --> D[Ion Efflux
K+ H+ Mg2+]:::step D --> E[ATP Depletion]:::step E --> F[Membrane
Depolarization]:::step F --> G[Cell Death]:::step classDef step fill:#9467bd,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px ``` > [!NOTE] > **Diagram Interpretation** > Nisin binds specifically to **Lipid II**, the membrane-bound precursor of peptidoglycan. This binding is highly selective — which is why nisin predominantly kills Gram-positive bacteria (they have exposed peptidoglycan, while Gram-negatives have an outer LPS shield protecting Lipid II). ## 🧬 Mermaid Diagram 4 — Mechanism Comparison Across Biopreservatives ```mermaid graph TD M[Biopreservative
Action] --> MA[Pore Formation
Nisin Sakacin]:::mech M --> MB[pH Disruption
Lactic Acid]:::mech M --> MC[Wall Hydrolysis
Lysozyme Chitosan]:::mech M --> MD[Protein Synthesis
Natamycin]:::mech M --> ME[Metal Chelation
Lactoferrin]:::mech MA --> R[Membrane Damage]:::result MB --> R MC --> R MD --> R2[Cellular Dysfunction]:::result ME --> R2 R --> OUT[Microbial
Inhibition]:::final R2 --> OUT classDef mech fill:#8c564b,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef result fill:#e377c2,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px classDef final fill:#bcbd22,stroke:#000,color:#fff,stroke-width:2px ``` # 📚 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## ✍️ Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) > [!NOTE] > **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` > **Q: Define biopreservatives. List any four examples with their microbial sources.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > Biopreservatives are naturally occurring antimicrobial compounds produced by microorganisms, plants, or animals that inhibit spoilage and pathogenic organisms in food and pharmaceutical products. > > Examples: > 1. **Nisin** — produced by *Lactococcus lactis* > 2. **Pediocin** — produced by *Pediococcus acidilactici* > 3. **Natamycin** — produced by *Streptomyces natalensis* > 4. **Lactic acid** — produced by *Lactobacillus* spp. > > (1 mark for definition + 2 marks for four examples with sources — KTU valuation key format) > [!NOTE] > **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024]` > **Q: Differentiate between bacteriocins and antibiotics as biopreservatives.** > > **Model Answer (3 marks):** > > | Parameter | Bacteriocin | Antibiotic | > |-----------|-------------|------------| > | Source | Ribosomally synthesized by bacteria | Secondary metabolites (non-ribosomal) | > | Spectrum | Narrow (often species-specific) | Broad | > | Toxicity | Low | Higher, may cause resistance | > | Mode | Pore formation, membrane disruption | Various (wall, protein, DNA) | > | Examples | Nisin, Pediocin | Natamycin | > > (3 marks awarded as 1 mark per correct comparative point — KTU pattern) --- ## ✍️ Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Module Internal Choice Format: Answer ANY ONE full question (a) + (b)** ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4]` **(a)** Describe the production of nisin by *Lactococcus lactis* using a batch fermentation process. Include the fermentation conditions, media requirements, and downstream processing steps. **[7 marks]** **(b)** Explain the mechanism of action of nisin on Gram-positive bacteria. How does it differ in effectiveness against Gram-negative bacteria? Discuss the role of Lipid II. **[7 marks]** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a): **Step 1 — Organism Selection (1 mark):** *Lactococcus lactis* subsp. *lactis* is the primary nisin-producing strain. It is **GRAS (Generally Recognized As Safe)** and grows optimally at **30 °C, pH 6.5**. **Step 2 — Media Formulation (2 marks):** - Carbon source: Glucose (10–20 g/L) or sucrose. - Nitrogen source: Yeast extract, peptone, tryptone. - Phosphate buffer to maintain pH 6.0–6.5. - *Note*: Nisin production is **highest when carbon source becomes limiting** (catabolite-controlled). **Step 3 — Fermentation Conditions (1.5 marks):** - Mode: Batch or Fed-batch. - Temperature: 30 °C. - pH: 6.0–6.5 (controlled by NaOH/HCl). - Agitation: 100–200 rpm (gentle, since LAB are anaerobic/microaerophilic). - Time: 6–10 hours for typical nisin titer of 100–150 mg/L. **Step 4 — Downstream Processing (2.5 marks):** 1. Cell removal by centrifugation or microfiltration. 2. Concentration by ultrafiltration (10 kDa membrane) or ammonium sulfate (60% saturation) precipitation. 3. Purification by ion-exchange chromatography (cation-exchange). 4. Formulation into **Nisaplin** (2.5% w/w nisin in NaCl) for commercialization. #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b): **Step 1 — Nisin Structure (1 mark):** Nisin is a **34-amino-acid lantibiotic** containing 5 lanthionine and methyllanthionine rings formed via post-translational modifications. **Step 2 — Mechanism of Action on Gram-Positives (3 marks):** 1. Nisin binds to **Lipid II** (peptidoglycan precursor) in the cytoplasmic membrane. 2. This binding forms a **transmembrane pore** (1.5–2 nm diameter). 3. Pore formation causes **efflux of K⁺, H⁺, Mg²⁺** and influx of Na⁺. 4. Result: **ATP depletion, membrane depolarization, cell death**. **Step 3 — Gram-Negative Ineffectiveness (2 marks):** Gram-negative bacteria possess an **outer membrane composed of lipopolysaccharide (LPS)** that prevents nisin from accessing Lipid II in the inner membrane. Hence, nisin is **ineffective against E. coli, Salmonella** unless the outer membrane is disrupted (e.g., by EDTA or high-pressure processing). **Step 4 — Role of Lipid II (1 mark):** Lipid II is the **specific docking receptor**. This explains nisin's targeted action and low toxicity to human cells (which lack peptidoglycan). --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice > `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 - Module 4 - Alternative]` **(a)** Classify biopreservatives by origin, giving two examples for each class with their applications. **[7 marks]** **(b)** With a neat schematic, describe the upstream and downstream processing of natamycin from *Streptomyces natalensis*. Discuss its application and mode of action. **[7 marks]** --- #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a): **Classification by Origin (7 marks — distribute 1.75 marks per class):** | Class | Example 1 (with source & application) | Example 2 (with source & application) | |-------|---------------------------------------|---------------------------------------| | **Microbial** | Nisin from *L. lactis* — used in processed cheese | Pediocin from *P. acidilactici* — used in meat | | **Plant** | Thymol from thyme — used in baked goods | Eugenol from cloves — used in confectionery | | **Animal** | Lysozyme from egg white — used in wine | Lactoferrin from milk — used in infant formula | | **Fungal** | Natamycin from *Streptomyces natalensis* — used on cheese surface | — | (0.5 marks per example, 0.5 marks for application = 7 marks total) #### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b): **Step 1 — Upstream Processing (2.5 marks):** - Inoculum: *Streptomyces natalensis* grown on agar slants. - Medium: Soybean meal, glucose, yeast extract; pH 6.5–7.0. - Fermenter: Submerged aerobic fermentation at **28 °C, 200–300 rpm, 5–7 days**. - Production: Natamycin is a **secondary metabolite**, peaking after exponential phase. **Step 2 — Downstream Processing (2.5 marks):** 1. Filtration to remove mycelia. 2. Extraction with organic solvents (methanol/ethanol). 3. Crystallization and drying. 4. Formulation as aqueous suspension (50% w/v) for cheese surface application. **Step 3 — Mode of Action (1 mark):** Natamycin is a **polyene macrolide** that binds to **ergosterol** in fungal cell membranes, forming pores that cause leakage and cell death. It is **inactive against bacteria** (they have no ergosterol). **Step 4 — Application (1 mark):** Used at **1–10 mg/kg** on cheese surfaces to inhibit *Penicillium*, *Aspergillus*, and *Candida* without affecting bacterial starter cultures. --- > [!WARNING] > **🚨 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls** > > 1. **Forgetting Lipid II**: When explaining nisin's mechanism, students often just say "forms pores" without mentioning **Lipid II** — examiners specifically deduct 1–2 marks for this omission. > 2. **Confusing nisin with natamycin**: Nisin is **antibacterial**, natamycin is **antifungal**. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks. > 3. **Wrong producer**: Nisin is from *Lactococcus*, **not** *Lactobacillus*. This is a very common error. > 4. **Skipping the pKa**: For organic acid biopreservatives, the Henderson–Hasselbalch equation and pKa are crucial — examiners expect students to explain **why undissociated form penetrates cells**. > 5. **Ignoring downstream processing**: KTU weightage for DSP (downstream processing) is **at least 40%** of any production question. Skipping steps = losing 2–3 marks. > 6. **Units confusion**: Nisin activity in **IU/mL**, not mg/mL. Natamycin in **mg/kg**, not IU. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **📌 Rapid Revision Checklist — Memorize before KTU ESE** - **Definition**: Biopreservatives = natural antimicrobials from biological sources (microbes/plants/animals). - **Classification**: Four origins — Microbial, Plant, Animal, Fungal. - **Nisin** = Lantibiotic bacteriocin from *Lactococcus lactis*; binds **Lipid II**; effective against Gram-positive only. - **Natamycin** = Polyene from *Streptomyces natalensis*; binds **ergosterol**; effective against fungi only. - **Pediocin** = Class IIa bacteriocin; narrow spectrum against *Listeria*. - **Lysozyme** = Hydrolyzes β-1,4 glycosidic bond in peptidoglycan; used in wine. - **Lactic acid** = Weak acid, pKa = 3.86; works via undissociated form crossing membrane. - **GRAS status** is mandatory for all food-grade biopreservatives. - **Key equations**: D-value equation, Henderson–Hasselbalch, Logistic growth, $Y_{P/S}$, $Q_P$. - **Dose ranges**: Nisin 100–250 IU/g, Natamycin 1–10 mg/kg, Lysozyme 250–500 mg/L. - **Fermentation conditions for nisin**: 30 °C, pH 6.5, 6–10 h, glucose-limiting. - **Downstream steps**: Cell removal → Concentration (UF/NH₄₂SO₄) → Purification (IEC) → Formulation. - **Compare with**: Chemical preservatives (BHA, BHT, benzoate) — biopreservatives are cleaner-label, safer, but costlier. - **Industrial commercial forms**: Nisaplin (2.5% nisin), Delvocid (50% natamycin). - **Regulatory codes**: Nisin = E234; Natamycin = E235; Lysozyme = E1105. - **Bioprocess economics**: Volumetric productivity $Q_P$ and yield $Y_{P/S}$ determine commercial viability. > [!NOTE] > **🎯 Last-Minute Tip for KTU 2024 ESE** > In any 14-mark question, use the **3-2-2 split** of marks: **3 marks** for definition/classification, **2 marks** for mechanism, **2 marks** for production process, plus adequate diagrams. Always end with an applications and GRAS status statement. This single pattern covers ~85% of past KTU questions on bioprocess products.

topics.title: Beverages - Production of Beverages
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module Module 4
> - **Topic:** Beverages - Production of Beverages

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Introduction to Beverages

A **beverage** is any potable liquid specifically formulated for human consumption, primarily serving the physiological functions of hydration, nourishment, and refreshment. In the context of **Bioprocess Engineering**, beverages represent one of the most industrially significant product categories where **fermentation technology**, **enzymatic conversion**, **downstream processing**, and **microbial biotechnology** converge to produce commercially viable, shelf-stable products at massive scales.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Beverages are classified under the broader category of **fermented and non-fermented food products**, where bioprocess engineering principles govern the conversion of raw agricultural substrates (grains, fruits, milk, sugar) into value-added consumable liquids through controlled biochemical transformations.

### Classification of Beverages (Engineering Perspective)

From a bioprocess standpoint, beverages are classified into two fundamental categories based on whether **microbial fermentation** is the central unit operation:

| Category | Sub-Category | Examples | Key Bioprocess Step |
|----------|--------------|----------|---------------------|
| **Non-Alcoholic Beverages** | Non-Fermented | Soft drinks, packaged water, tea, coffee | Extraction, pasteurization, carbonation |
| | Fermented | Kombucha, kefir | Lactic acid / acetic acid fermentation |
| **Alcoholic Beverages** | Fermented (Low Alcohol) | Beer (~4–6% ABV), Wine (~9–14% ABV), Cider | Yeast-based anaerobic fermentation |
| | Distilled (High Alcohol) | Whisky, Rum, Vodka, Brandy (~40% ABV) | Fermentation followed by distillation |

> [!NOTE]
> **ABV (Alcohol By Volume)** is the standard engineering metric used globally to express alcohol concentration. The term **"Proof"** (mostly used in the US/UK) equals **2 × ABV %**. In India, excise regulations typically classify beverages with **less than 42.8% ABV** as "Country Liquor" and above as "Indian Made Foreign Liquor (IMFL)".

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Bioreactor of a Brewery

Imagine a **giant stainless-steel bubble bath** controlled by an army of tiny, single-celled workers (yeast) — that is essentially what a brewery fermenter is. Just like how dough rises because yeast eats sugar and exhales CO₂ inside a closed bowl, an industrial fermenter is a **temperature-controlled, oxygen-deprived, stainless-steel version of that dough bowl**, scaled up to thousands of liters. The sugar solution (wort) is the "food," the yeast (e.g., *Saccharomyces cerevisiae*) is the "worker," and the products are alcohol, CO₂, and a spectrum of flavor compounds (esters, higher alcohols) that make beer taste like beer.

The downstream steps — **filtering, aging, carbonating, packaging** — are analogous to a chef *clarifying a soup*, *letting it rest in the fridge*, and *garnishing it before serving*. Every step matters because each is a **rate-limiting biological or physical process** that determines final product quality.

## 1.3 Significance in KTU 2024 Scheme

The production of beverages is mapped to multiple **Course Outcomes (COs)** in the Bioprocess Engineering syllabus, particularly:

- **CO4:** Apply principles of fermentation technology to industrial food and beverage production.
- **CO5:** Design and analyze downstream processing operations for biological products.
- **CO2:** Evaluate microbial kinetics and bioreactor performance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Bioprocess Constants to Memorize:**
> - **Specific gravity of ethanol at 20 °C = 0.789 g/mL**
> - **Ethanol yield from glucose (theoretical) = 0.51 g ethanol / g glucose (Gay-Lussac equation)**
> - **Carbonation pressure for soft drinks = 2.5–4.0 volumes CO₂**
> - **Pasteurization equivalent for beer = 60 °C for 20 minutes (or 71 °C for 30 seconds for HTST)**
> - **Standard brewing pH range = 4.0–4.5 (post-fermentation)**
> - **Beer color unit = °SRM (Standard Reference Method) or °EBC (European Brewery Convention)**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 Beer Production — The Flagship Bioprocess

Beer is the world's most widely consumed **alcoholic fermented beverage**, produced from four primary raw materials: **malt (germinated barley), hops, water, and yeast**. The entire process is a textbook application of **enzymatic hydrolysis followed by anaerobic microbial fermentation**.

### 2.1.1 Process Flow — Step-by-Step Engineering Logic

**Step 1: Malting (Controlled Germination)**
- Barley grains are steeped in water at **12–18 °C for 2–3 days**, then allowed to germinate under controlled humidity.
- During germination, the embryo releases the plant hormone **gibberellic acid**, which triggers the **aleurone layer** to synthesize hydrolytic enzymes:
  - **α-Amylase** and **β-Amylase** (starch-degrading)
  - **Proteases** (protein-degrading)
  - **β-Glucanases** (cell wall-degrading)
- **Kilning** at **50–85 °C** halts germination, dries the grain, and develops color/flavor via the **Maillard reaction**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why kilning matters:** Higher kilning temperatures produce darker malts (Munich, Vienna, Chocolate) by generating melanoidins. This is a **non-enzymatic browning reaction** between reducing sugars and amino acids.

**Step 2: Milling and Mashing (Enzymatic Hydrolysis)**
- Milled malt (grist) is mixed with water at a **mash-in ratio of ~2.5–3.0 L water per kg grist**.
- **Mash tun temperatures** are programmed in steps called *rests*:
  - **Acid rest (35 °C)** — lowers pH, activates phytase
  - **Proteolytic rest (45–55 °C)** — breaks down proteins to amino acids
  - **Saccharification rest (62–67 °C)** — β-amylase peak activity → maltose
  - **α-Amylase rest (71–78 °C)** — dextrin formation
- The resulting sugary liquid is called **wort**.

**Step 3: Lautering (Solid-Liquid Separation)**
- Wort is separated from spent grains in a **lauter tun** (filter bed) using the grain husk as the filter medium.
- **Sparging** with hot water (76–78 °C) extracts residual sugars.

**Step 4: Boiling with Hops**
- Wort is boiled for **60–90 minutes** in a **copper brew kettle**.
- **Hops (Humulus lupulus)** contribute:
  - **α-Acids (humulone, cohumulone, adhumulone)** — isomerize during boiling → iso-α-acids (bitterness)
  - **Essential oils** — aroma (myrcene, humulene, farnesene)
  - **Polyphenols** — natural antioxidants and antimicrobial agents

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Iso-α-acid formation** follows first-order kinetics with respect to α-acid concentration and boiling time. Isomerization reaches ~70% completion after 60 minutes of boiling.

**Step 5: Cooling and Aeration**
- Wort is cooled rapidly to **fermentation temperature (8–22 °C depending on yeast strain)** via a **plate heat exchanger**.
- **Why aeration?** Yeast requires dissolved oxygen in the initial phase for:
  - Synthesis of **ergosterol** and **unsaturated fatty acids** (cell membrane components)
  - ATP generation for cell proliferation
- After ~12 hours, dissolved O₂ drops to ~0 as yeast switches to anaerobic respiration.

**Step 6: Fermentation (The Heart of Bioprocess)**
- Inoculated with **pitching yeast** at **~10⁶–10⁷ cells/mL**.
- Two major yeast types:
  - **Saccharomyces cerevisiae (ale yeast)** — top-fermenting at 18–22 °C
  - **Saccharomyces pastorianus (lager yeast)** — bottom-fermenting at 8–15 °C

> [!NOTE]
> **"Top-fermenting" vs "Bottom-fermenting"** are historical terms. Ale yeasts flocculate and rise due to CO₂ buoyancy; lager yeasts form dense flocs that sediment. Modern genetic analysis shows lager yeast is a hybrid of *S. cerevisiae* and *S. eubayanus*.

**Step 7: Maturation (Aging/Conditioning)**
- **Primary maturation** — 7–14 days at 0–4 °C for lagers (called *lagering*, German for "storage")
- Diacetyl (2,3-butanedione, a buttery off-flavor) is re-absorbed by yeast and reduced to **2,3-butanediol**.
- **Secondary maturation** — flavor harmonization, chill-proofing (tannin-protein complexes precipitate).

**Step 8: Filtration and Packaging**
- **Kieselguhr (diatomaceous earth) filtration** or **membrane filtration (0.45 µm)** clarifies the beer.
- **Carbonation** — CO₂ injected to **2.5–3.5 volumes**.
- Packaging into bottles, cans, or kegs under **counter-pressure** to avoid foam.

## 2.2 Wine Production

Wine is produced by fermenting the **must** (crushed grape juice) using *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* (or indigenous yeasts for natural wines).

### 2.2.1 Process Steps

1. **Harvesting & Crushing** — Grapes destemmed, crushed to release juice.
2. **Must Preparation** — Sulfiting with **SO₂ (50–100 ppm)** to suppress wild microbes and prevent oxidation.
3. **Primary Fermentation** — 7–14 days at 15–25 °C; sugars converted to ethanol.
4. **Pressing** — For red wines, fermentation occurs with skins (maceration) to extract **anthocyanins** and **tannins**. White wines are pressed before fermentation.
5. **Malolactic Fermentation (MLF)** — Conversion of harsh **L-malic acid** to soft **L-lactic acid** by *Oenococcus oeni*. Reduces total acidity and adds complexity.
6. **Aging** — In stainless steel tanks or **oak barrels** (Quercus spp.). Oak contributes **vanillin, lactones, tannins**.
7. **Bottling** — After filtration and possible fining (using egg whites, bentonite, or isinglass).

## 2.3 Distilled Spirits — Whisky, Rum, Vodka, Brandy

Distillation separates ethanol (boiling point **78.37 °C**) from water (BP 100 °C) based on **vapor-liquid equilibrium**.

### Key Distillation Concepts

- **Pot Still (Batch)** — Simple, retains congeners, produces flavorful spirits (Scotch malt whisky, Cognac).
- **Patent Still (Coffey Still, Continuous)** — Multi-column, produces lighter, higher-proof spirits (grain whisky, vodka, gin).
- **Heads, Hearts, Tails** — Fractionation:
  - **Heads** (foreshots) — Methanol, acetaldehyde, low BP (toxic, discarded)
  - **Hearts** — Desired ethanol fraction
  - **Tails** — Fusel alcohols, fatty acids (re-distilled)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Gay-Lussac Equation (Theoretical Ethanol Yield):**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{yeast}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2$$

**Molecular weights:** Glucose = 180 g/mol, Ethanol = 46 g/mol, CO₂ = 44 g/mol

Theoretical yield = **2 × 46 / 180 = 0.511 g ethanol / g glucose** = **51.1% by mass**

In practice, only **~90–95%** of theoretical yield is achieved due to:
- Biomass synthesis (yeast growth consumes ~5% of sugar)
- By-product formation (glycerol, succinic acid, higher alcohols)
- Evaporation losses

## 2.4 Non-Alcoholic Fermented Beverages

### 2.4.1 Kombucha
- Symbiotic culture of **bacteria and yeast (SCOBY)** — primarily *Acetobacter xylinum*, *Gluconobacter*, and *Saccharomyces*.
- Fermentation of sweetened tea (sucrose 50–100 g/L) for **7–14 days at 22–30 °C**.
- Produces **acetic acid, gluconic acid, lactic acid, ethanol (<0.5% ABV), and a cellulose pellicle** (the "mother").

### 2.4.2 Kefir
- **Kefir grains** — symbiotic culture of lactic acid bacteria (*Lactobacillus*, *Lactococcus*) and yeasts (*Kluyveromyces*, *Saccharomyces*) embedded in a **polysaccharide matrix (kefiran)**.
- Fermented milk with **0.5–2.0% lactic acid** and minor ethanol (<0.5%).
- pH drops to **4.2–4.6**.

## 2.5 Soft Drinks and Carbonated Beverages

Soft drinks are **non-fermented beverages** where bioprocess engineering intersects with:
- **Water treatment** (deionization, reverse osmosis, UV sterilization)
- **Sugar syrup preparation** (high-fructose corn syrup, sucrose at **10–13° Brix**)
- **Carbonation** (CO₂ injection at **~3.0–4.0 volumes**, ~5–7 g CO₂/L at 4 °C)
- **Cold aseptic filling** to prevent microbial contamination

> [!NOTE]
> **°Brix** is the mass percent of dissolved sucrose equivalent. For example, 10° Brix = 10 g sucrose per 100 g solution. Most colas are formulated at **11–13° Brix**.

---

## 2.6 KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

| # | Formula / Concept | Equation / Value | Engineering Application | Standard Units |
|---|-------------------|------------------|--------------------------|----------------|
| 1 | **Gay-Lussac Theoretical Yield** | $Y_{E/S} = \dfrac{2 \times M_{ethanol}}{M_{glucose}} = \dfrac{92}{180} = 0.511$ | Maximum ethanol from sugar | g ethanol / g glucose |
| 2 | **Actual Ethanol Yield** | $Y_{practical} = 0.90 \times 0.511 = 0.460$ | Industrial fermentation | g/g |
| 3 | **ABV Conversion** | $\% ABV = \dfrac{V_{ethanol}}{V_{solution}} \times 100$ | Beverage labelling | % (v/v) |
| 4 | **Proof Conversion** | $\text{Proof} = 2 \times \% ABV$ | US/UK regulation | — |
| 5 | **Plato to Specific Gravity** | $SG = 1 + \dfrac{°P}{250}$ | Wort density measurement | dimensionless |
| 6 | **Attenuation (Apparent)** | $\text{Attenuation} = \dfrac{°P_{initial} - °P_{final}}{°P_{initial}} \times 100$ | Fermentation efficiency | % |
| 7 | **Carbonation Volume** | $V_{CO_2} = \dfrac{(P + 1.033)}{1.033} \times \left(1 - 1.97 \times 10^{-4} \times T\right)$ | Soft drink / beer CO₂ content | volumes |
| 8 | **Starch Hydrolysis Mass Balance** | $m_{maltose} = m_{starch} \times 1.056$ | Mashing efficiency | kg |
| 9 | **Mash Tun Water-to-Grist Ratio** | $\text{L:W} = 2.5 - 3.0 \;\vert\; \text{L water / kg grist}$ | Brewing recipe design | L/kg |
| 10 | **Pasteurization Units (PU)** | $PU = 1.393^{(T-60)} \times t_{min}$ | Thermal processing | dimensionless |
| 11 | **Beer Color (SRM)** | $SRM = 1.355 \times A_{430}^{1.0} \times 10$ | Quality control | °SRM |
| 12 | **Maltose Equivalent** | $1 \text{ kg starch} \rightarrow 1.056 \text{ kg maltose}$ | Mashing theoretical max | kg/kg |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - The **Gay-Lussac equation** is the foundation for sizing **fermenter vessels** in distilleries. Knowing the yield allows engineers to compute required fermenter volume: $V_f = \dfrac{M_{ethanol,required}}{\rho_{ethanol} \times Y \times C_{sugar} \times V_{eff}}$.
> - **Carbonation volume calculations** are critical for **counter-pressure filling line design** in beverage packaging plants.
> - **Pasteurization Units (PU)** help in **F₀ value calculations** for thermal microbial inactivation (similar to canned food sterilization).

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation: Theoretical Ethanol Yield (Gay-Lussac Equation)

### Problem Context
A KTU examiner typically asks: *"Calculate the theoretical and practical (90% efficient) ethanol yield from 1000 kg of glucose in a fermentation batch."*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Write the stoichiometric fermentation equation**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \xrightarrow{\text{Zymase (yeast)}} 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 + \text{Energy (ATP)}$$

**Step 2: Determine molecular weights**

- Glucose ($C_6H_{12}O_6$): MW = (6×12) + (12×1) + (6×16) = **180 g/mol**
- Ethanol ($C_2H_5OH$): MW = (2×12) + (6×1) + (1×16) = **46 g/mol**
- CO₂: MW = (1×12) + (2×16) = **44 g/mol**

**Step 3: Set up the mass balance**

From 1 mole (180 g) of glucose, 2 moles (2 × 46 = 92 g) of ethanol are theoretically produced.

**Step 4: Calculate theoretical yield on a mass basis**

$$Y_{theoretical} = \frac{\text{Mass of ethanol produced}}{\text{Mass of glucose consumed}} = \frac{92 \text{ g}}{180 \text{ g}} = 0.5111$$

$$\boxed{Y_{theoretical} = 0.511 \; \text{g ethanol / g glucose} \; (\text{or } 51.1\%)}$$

**Step 5: Apply practical efficiency (90%)**

$$Y_{practical} = 0.511 \times 0.90 = 0.460 \; \text{g/g}$$

**Step 6: Calculate ethanol from 1000 kg glucose**

$$m_{ethanol} = 1000 \text{ kg} \times 0.460 = 460 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7: Convert mass to volume**

Using $\rho_{ethanol}$ = **0.789 g/mL** at 20 °C:

$$V_{ethanol} = \frac{460{,}000 \text{ g}}{789 \text{ g/L}} = 583.0 \text{ L}$$

**Step 8: Final answer**

$$\boxed{V_{ethanol} = 583.0 \; \text{L pure ethanol} \;\vert\; \text{from 1000 kg glucose}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation:** This 583 L of pure ethanol, when diluted to 40% ABV (typical whisky strength), yields **~1457 L of finished spirit**. This calculation is the basis for **distillery capacity planning**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Carbonation Volume for Beer at 4 °C

### Problem Context
*"A beverage plant operator wants to achieve 2.8 volumes of CO₂ in finished beer at 4 °C. If the keg is sealed and counter-pressure filled at what pressure must the CO₂ tank be set?"*

### Derivation

The **carbonation volume** $V_{CO_2}$ in a beverage is defined as the volume of CO₂ (at STP, 0 °C, 1 atm) dissolved per unit volume of beverage.

**Standard Carbonation Formula:**

$$V_{CO_2} = \left(\frac{P + 1.033}{1.033}\right) \times \left[1 - 0.000197 \times (T)\right]$$

Where:
- $V_{CO_2}$ = volumes of CO₂ (dimensionless)
- $P$ = gauge pressure (bar)
- $T$ = temperature (°C)
- $1.033$ = atmospheric pressure (bar)
- $0.000197$ = empirical temperature correction factor per °C

**Given:** $V_{CO_2} = 2.8$ volumes, $T = 4$ °C

**Step 1: Calculate the temperature correction term**

$$1 - 0.000197 \times 4 = 1 - 0.000788 = 0.99921$$

**Step 2: Substitute into the main equation**

$$2.8 = \left(\frac{P + 1.033}{1.033}\right) \times 0.99921$$

**Step 3: Isolate the pressure ratio**

$$\frac{P + 1.033}{1.033} = \frac{2.8}{0.99921} = 2.8022$$

**Step 4: Solve for P**

$$P + 1.033 = 1.033 \times 2.8022 = 2.8947$$

$$P = 2.8947 - 1.033 = 1.8617 \text{ bar (gauge)}$$

**Step 5: Convert to common units**

$$P = 1.86 \text{ bar (gauge)} = 26.97 \text{ psi} = 0.184 \text{ MPa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 1.86 \text{ bar gauge}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Practical Plant Insight:** At higher temperatures, more pressure is needed to achieve the same carbonation level. For example, at 25 °C, achieving 2.8 volumes requires ~3.5 bar — explaining why **cold carbonation (~0–4 °C)** is industrial standard.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Mash Tun Mass Balance — Wort Production

### Problem Context
*"A craft brewery uses 500 kg of malt with a starch content of 58%. Assuming 95% starch conversion efficiency during mashing, calculate the maltose produced and the expected original gravity (°Plato)."*

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Calculate starch mass available**

$$m_{starch} = 500 \text{ kg} \times 0.58 = 290 \text{ kg starch}$$

**Step 2: Apply hydrolysis stoichiometry**

Starch hydrolysis (overall): 

$$(C_6H_{10}O_5)_n + (n-1)\,H_2O \rightarrow n\,C_6H_{12}O_6$$

- Mass ratio: 162 g starch (per anhydroglucose unit) → 180 g glucose
- Conversion factor: $180/162 = 1.111$ (mass gain due to water of hydrolysis)

**Step 3: Convert starch to glucose**

$$m_{glucose} = 290 \text{ kg} \times 1.111 = 322.2 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 4: Convert glucose to maltose** (β-amylase cleaves maltose from starch chains)

$$m_{maltose} = m_{starch} \times 1.056 = 290 \times 1.056 = 306.2 \text{ kg (theoretical)}$$

**Step 5: Apply 95% conversion efficiency**

$$m_{maltose,actual} = 306.2 \times 0.95 = 290.9 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 6: Calculate total fermentable sugars (maltose ≈ glucose equivalent)**

For simplicity, treat all maltose as glucose equivalent:

$$m_{sugar,total} = 322.2 \times 0.95 = 306.1 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 7: Calculate Original Gravity (°Plato)**

°Plato is defined as grams of sucrose equivalent per 100 g of wort.

Assume mash water = **1500 L** (3.0 L/kg grist ratio)

Total wort mass ≈ 1500 kg (approximating density = 1 kg/L)

$$°P = \frac{306.1}{1500 + 500_{malt}} \times 100 = \frac{306.1}{2000} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{°P \approx 15.3° \text{Plato}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This corresponds to **Original Gravity (OG) ≈ 1.064 SG**, typical of a **strong pale ale**. The final extract at the end of fermentation would be **~4° Plato**, yielding an **attenuation of ~74%** — typical of a balanced ale yeast.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Major Fermented Beverages

| Parameter | Beer | Wine | Cider | Sake | Kombucha |
|-----------|------|------|-------|------|----------|
| **Substrate** | Malted barley wort | Grape must | Apple juice | Polished rice | Sweetened tea |
| **Microorganism** | *S. cerevisiae / S. pastorianus* | *S. cerevisiae* (+ MLF bacteria) | *S. cerevisiae* | *Aspergillus oryzae* + *S. cerevisiae* | SCOBY |
| **ABV (%)** | 4–6 | 9–14 | 3–8 | 14–20 | <0.5 |
| **pH (final)** | 4.0–4.5 | 3.0–4.0 | 3.2–4.0 | 4.0–4.5 | 2.5–3.5 |
| **Fermentation Temp** | 8–22 °C | 15–25 °C | 15–25 °C | 15–20 °C (parallel) | 22–30 °C |
| **Fermentation Type** | Batch (anaerobic) | Batch (anaerobic) | Batch | Multi-step (solid → liquid) | Static batch |
| **Key Downstream Step** | Filtration, carbonation | Malolactic, aging | Filtration, carbonation | Pressing, pasteurization | Cellulose removal |
| **Shelf Life** | 6–12 months | 2–10 years | 6–12 months | 12+ months | 30–90 days |
