# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A QUESTIONS (3 MARKS EACH)

### Question 1: Short Answer Concept
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**

**Q:** Define the term "attenuation" in brewing. Distinguish between **apparent attenuation** and **real attenuation**.

**Model Answer:**

**Attenuation** is the percentage of wort sugars that have been converted to ethanol and CO₂ by yeast during fermentation. It is a critical measure of **fermentation efficiency** and yeast strain performance.

- **Apparent Attenuation (AA)** — Measured using a hydrometer in the fermenter, before CO₂ release:

$$AA = \frac{SG_{original} - SG_{apparent}}{SG_{original} - 1} \times 100$$

- **Real Attenuation (RA)** — Measured after degassing the beer (to remove residual CO₂) and accounts for the volume contribution of alcohol (which lowers SG):

$$RA = \frac{SG_{original} - SG_{real}}{SG_{original} - 1} \times 100$$

RA is always **higher** than AA by **~1.5–2.0%** because alcohol is less dense than water, lowering the real SG further than apparent.

---

### Question 2: Short Answer Concept
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024]**

**Q:** What is **malolactic fermentation (MLF)**? Name the organism responsible and explain its significance in wine production.

**Model Answer:**

**Malolactic fermentation (MLF)** is a secondary microbial fermentation carried out by the lactic acid bacterium ***Oenococcus oeni*** (formerly *Leuconostoc oenos*). In this process, the sharp, dicarboxylic **L-malic acid** is decarboxylated to the milder monocarboxylic **L-lactic acid** and CO₂:

$$L\text{-malic acid} \rightarrow L\text{-lactic acid} + CO_2$$

**Significance in wine production:**
1. **De-acidification** — Reduces total acidity by ~1–3 g/L, softening the wine.
2. **Microbial stability** — Once malic acid is consumed, spoilage organisms have less substrate.
3. **Flavor modification** — Adds buttery, nutty, or yeasty complexity (especially in Chardonnay).
4. **Naturally occurring** — Spontaneous in red wines and most white wines after primary fermentation.

---

## 5.2 PART B QUESTIONS (14 MARKS — INTERNAL CHOICE)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**

**Q:** Describe in detail the **manufacture of beer** from malt. Include a flow diagram and discuss the role of each major unit operation. *(14 marks)*

#### (a) Explain the steps of malting and mashing with their biochemical significance. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Malting** is the controlled germination of barley that activates hydrolytic enzymes. It comprises three sub-stages:

1. **Steeping (2–3 days at 12–18 °C)** — Hydration to 42–46% moisture; air-rest cycles maintain embryo respiration.
2. **Germination (4–6 days at 15–20 °C)** — Embryo releases **gibberellic acid (GA₃)**, which diffuses to the **aleurone layer** and induces synthesis of:
   - **α-Amylase** — cleaves α-1,4 glycosidic bonds randomly → dextrins
   - **β-Amylase** — cleaves maltose units from non-reducing ends
   - **Limit dextrinase** — cleaves α-1,6 branch points
   - **Proteases** — degrade hordein storage proteins → amino acids
   - **β-Glucanases** — degrade cell wall β-glucans
3. **Kilning (24–48 hours, 50–85 °C)** — Stops germination; develops color/flavor; reduces moisture to <5%.

**Mashing** is the enzymatic extraction phase:

| Temperature Rest | Enzyme Activity | Function |
|------------------|-----------------|----------|
| 35–45 °C | Phytase | pH reduction to 5.2–5.5 |
| 45–55 °C | Protease | Free amino nitrogen (FAN) release |
| 62–67 °C | β-Amylase (optimum) | Maltose production (fermentable) |
| 71–78 °C | α-Amylase (optimum) | Dextrin production (non-fermentable) |
| 78 °C | Mash-out | Stops enzyme activity, reduces viscosity |

The resulting **sweet wort** contains ~60–65% maltose, 15–20% maltotriose, 5–10% glucose, and ~20–25% dextrins.

#### (b) Describe fermentation, maturation, and packaging operations. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Fermentation:**

The cooled, aerated wort (~10 °C for lager, ~20 °C for ale) is pitched with yeast:

- **Ale fermentation (*S. cerevisiae*)** — 18–22 °C, 3–5 days, top-cropping yeast, produces fruity esters (isoamyl acetate — banana).
- **Lager fermentation (*S. pastorianus*)** — 8–15 °C, 7–10 days, bottom-cropping, cleaner profile.

**Key biochemical events:**
1. **Lag phase (0–12 h)** — Yeast metabolizes O₂; synthesizes sterols.
2. **Growth phase (12–48 h)** — Rapid cell division; sugar uptake.
3. **Stationary/fermentation phase (2–5 days)** — Anaerobic ethanol production.
4. **Flocculation phase** — Yeast cells aggregate and sediment.

**Maturation (Conditioning):**
- **Primary maturation (lagering)** — 14–28 days at 0–4 °C.
- **Diacetyl reduction** — Yeast metabolizes the buttery off-flavor diacetyl to 2,3-butanediol.
- **Chill-proofing** — Cold stabilization precipitates tannin-protein complexes that would cause haze.

**Filtration:**
- **Kieselguhr (DE)** filtration removes yeast and haze particles.
- **PVPP** (polyvinylpolypyrrolidone) removes polyphenols.
- **Membrane filtration (0.45 µm)** for premium products.

**Carbonation:**
- Achieved through **natural conditioning** (priming sugar) or **forced carbonation** (CO₂ injection at 1.8–2.5 bar in bright beer tank).
- Target: **2.5–3.5 volumes CO₂**.

**Packaging:**
- **Counter-pressure filling** into bottles, cans, or kegs prevents foaming and CO₂ loss.
- **In-package pasteurization** (tunnel pasteurizer: 60 °C for 20 min) for shelf stability.
- Alternatively, **flash pasteurization** (HTST: 71 °C for 30 s) before filling.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 | Module 4 | 14 Marks]**

**Q:** Discuss the production of **distilled spirits**. Explain the biochemistry of fermentation, the principles of distillation, and the maturation process in detail. *(14 marks)*

#### (a) Explain the fermentation biochemistry and pre-distillation steps. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Raw materials for major spirits:**

| Spirit | Substrate | Microorganism |
|--------|-----------|---------------|
| Whisky | Malted barley (Scotch) or mixed grains (Bourbon) | *S. cerevisiae* |
| Rum | Sugarcane molasses or juice | *S. cerevisiae* |
| Vodka | Grain, potato, or beet sugar | *S. cerevisiae* |
| Brandy | Wine (grape) | *S. cerevisiae* |
| Gin | Neutral spirit + botanicals | Flavoring only |

**Fermentation Process:**

1. **Milling/Mashing** — Starch substrates are gelatinized and hydrolyzed (similar to brewing but to a higher extract).
2. **Cooling** — Wort cooled to 20–30 °C.
3. **Pitching** — Yeast inoculated at 5–10% v/v.
4. **Fermentation** — 48–96 hours, no oxygen limitation since ethanol will be distilled anyway.
5. **End-product: "Wash" or "Beer"** — Typically 7–10% ABV.

**Stoichiometry (Gay-Lussac):**

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 \rightarrow 2\,C_2H_5OH + 2\,CO_2 \;\vert\; \Delta G^0 = -218 \text{ kJ/mol}$$

$$Y_{theoretical} = 0.511 \text{ g/g} \;\vert\; Y_{practical} = 0.46 \text{ g/g}$$

#### (b) Explain distillation principles and maturation. *(7 marks)*

**Model Solution:**

**Principles of Distillation:**

Distillation exploits the **difference in volatility** between ethanol (BP = 78.37 °C) and water (BP = 100 °C).

**Raoult's Law for the liquid phase:**

$$P_i = x_i \cdot P_i^*$$

**Relative volatility of ethanol over water:**

$$\alpha = \frac{P_{ethanol}^*}{P_{water}^*} \approx 2.5 - 3.5$$

**Types of Stills:**

1. **Pot Still (Batch)** — Used for Scotch malt whisky, Cognac, Irish whiskey.
   - Two or three-fold distillation.
   - Retains **congeners** (higher alcohols, esters, aldehydes) — gives flavor.
   - Operating principle: simple batch differential distillation.

2. **Patent/Coffey Still (Continuous)** — Used for grain whisky, vodka, neutral spirits.
   - Multi-column design (analyzer + rectifier).
   - Produces near-pure ethanol (~96% ABV).
   - **Azeotrope at 95.63% ABV** — cannot be exceeded by simple distillation; requires **molecular sieves (3 Å zeolites)** to produce absolute alcohol.

**Fractionation (Heads, Hearts, Tails):**

| Fraction | Components | Action |
|----------|------------|--------|
| Heads (foreshots) | Methanol, acetaldehyde, ethyl acetate | Discard or re-distill |
| Hearts | Ethanol + desirable congeners | Retain for product |
| Tails | Fusel alcohols (isoamyl, isobutyl), fatty acids | Re-distill or discard |

**Maturation:**

Aged in **oak barrels** (*Quercus alba* for American; *Quercus robur* for European).

- **Legal minimum aging** (varies by spirit): Scotch whisky ≥ 3 years; Bourbon ≥ 2 years; Cognac ≥ 2 years.
- **Chemical changes during maturation:**
  - **Extraction** — Vanillin, lactones (oak), tannins, color compounds.
  - **Evaporation** — "Angel's share" — water + ethanol loss (3–5% per year in Scotland).
  - **Oxidation** — Slow reactions produce esters and aldehydes.
  - **Char layer (Bourbon)** — New charred American oak barrels contribute caramelized sugar and smoky notes.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units and molecular weights** — Always state MW values in Gay-Lussac calculations.
> 2. **Conflating "top-fermenting" with modern genetics** — Use *S. cerevisiae* (ale) and *S. pastorianus* (lager) correctly.
> 3. **Missing the "angel's share"** in whisky — Examiners specifically test maturation losses.
> 4. **Forgetting the azeotrope at 95.63%** in distillation — Common trap question.
> 5. **Not mentioning MLF** when discussing wine flavor — A frequent 2-mark loss.
> 6. **Skipping flow diagrams** in 14-mark answers — Always include a labeled block diagram; it secures 1–2 easy marks.
> 7. **Confusing °Brix with °Plato** — °Brix is sucrose-equivalent (used in wine); °Plato is sucrose-equivalent (used in brewing). They are nearly identical numerically but conventionally distinct.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Points to Memorize Before KTU Exam:**

- ☐ Beverages are classified as **alcoholic** (fermented + distilled) and **non-alcoholic** (fermented + non-fermented).
- ☐ The four ingredients of beer: **malt, hops, water, yeast**.
- ☐ The **three key enzymes in mashing**: α-amylase, β-amylase, limit dextrinase.
- ☐ **Mash rest temperatures**: 45 °C (protease), 65 °C (β-amylase), 72 °C (α-amylase).
- ☐ **Hops** contribute α-acids (bitterness), essential oils (aroma), polyphenols (antimicrobial).
- ☐ **Ale yeast** = *S. cerevisiae*, ferments at 18–22 °C.
- ☐ **Lager yeast** = *S. pastorianus*, ferments at 8–15 °C, requires cold maturation (*lagering*).
- ☐ **Wine yeast** = *S. cerevisiae*; **MLF** by *Oenococcus oeni* converts malic → lactic acid.
- ☐ **Gay-Lussac equation**: Glucose → 2 Ethanol + 2 CO₂; theoretical yield = **0.511 g/g**.
- ☐ **Industrial yield** ≈ **0.46 g/g** (~90% efficiency).
- ☐ **Azeotrope of ethanol-water** at **95.63% ABV at 78.2 °C**.
- ☐ **Distillation types**: Pot still (batch, flavorful) vs Patent still (continuous, neutral).
- ☐ **Spirit maturation** in oak barrels → "Angel's share" loss.
- ☐ **Carbonation volume formula** for beer/soft drinks at given P and T.
- ☐ **Soft drink °Brix** typically **11–13°**.
- ☐ **Kombucha** = SCOBY fermentation of sweet tea → acetic acid + cellulose pellicle.
- ☐ **Kefir** = symbiotic grains in milk → lactic acid + trace ethanol.
- ☐ **Pasteurization Units (PU)** for beer = 60 °C × 20 min equivalent.
- ☐ **Attenuation (apparent vs real)** — RA > AA by ~1.5–2%.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For any 14-mark question on beverage production, always structure your answer as:
> 1. **Introduction** (2 marks) — Definition + classification
> 2. **Raw materials** (2 marks) — With scientific names where applicable
> 3. **Process flow diagram** (2 marks) — Mandatory block diagram
> 4. **Step-by-step unit operations** (6 marks) — With biochemistry + engineering
> 5. **Quality control + packaging** (2 marks) — Microbiology + downstream
> Total = 14 marks ✓

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Production of Baker's Yeast. Bioremediation - Microbes in Mining
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Production of Baker's Yeast. Bioremediation - Microbes in Mining # 📘 MODULE 4 — INDUSTRIAL BIOPRODUCTS & ENVIRONMENTAL BIOTECHNOLOGY ## 1.1 Production of Baker's Yeast ### 1.1.1 Core Definition **Baker's Yeast** is the commercially produced, dehydrated, viable cell mass of *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* used as a biological leavening agent in baking. The industrial production involves **aerobic fed-batch fermentation** of molasses-based media, where the objective is **biomass (cell mass) maximization**, not ethanol production. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Baker's yeast production is the *classic industrial case study* of aerobic biomass production where **catabolite repression (Crabtree effect)** and **glucose concentration control** dictate the entire process strategy. The yield coefficient **Yx/s ≈ 0.5 g/g** under fully aerobic conditions. **Key Terminology Table:** | Term | Definition | |---|---| | **Crabtree Effect** | Repression of oxidative metabolism by high glucose; yeast ferments even in presence of O₂ | | **Pasteur Effect** | Inhibition of fermentation by oxygen; yeast respires when O₂ available | | **Fed-Batch** | Cultivation where nutrients are added incrementally without removing broth | | **Yx/s** | Yield coefficient — grams of biomass per gram of substrate consumed (**≈ 0.5 g/g**) | | **Active Dry Yeast (ADY)** | Yeast with >95% dry matter, vacuum/drum dried, long shelf life | ### 1.1.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Sugar Buffet" Imagine a dinner buffet. If you let a person sit at an all-you-can-eat table with unlimited desserts, they will eat *too fast, become sluggish, and produce waste (ethanol)*. But if you give them **small controlled portions every 15 minutes**, they will **eat efficiently, convert food into muscle (biomass)**, and stay healthy. This is exactly what happens in **fed-batch baker's yeast production**: - **High glucose at start → Crabtree effect → ethanol formed (waste)** - **Controlled glucose feed → no repression → all sugar converted to cell mass** - The feed rate of molasses is therefore the **master variable** of the entire process. ### 1.1.3 Microorganism of Choice **Saccharomyces cerevisiae** (strain selection criteria): - High growth rate (μmax ≈ 0.45–0.55 h⁻¹) - High protein content (~50% w/w dry weight) - Tolerance to low pH (3.5–4.5) - Robust in high-sugar environments - Stable genetic traits after drying ### 1.1.4 Raw Materials & Nutritional Requirements > [!NOTE] > **Industrial Molasses** (the standard substrate) is a by-product of sugar refining containing: > - **Sucrose:** 45–55% w/v > - **Nitrogen sources:** Added as (NH₄)₂SO₄ or NH₄H₂PO₄ > - **Phosphorus:** Critical for ATP and nucleic acid synthesis > - **Vitamins:** Biotin, thiamine, pantothenate (typically from corn-steep liquor) > - **Minerals:** Mg²⁺, Zn²⁺, Ca²⁺, Fe²⁺ as trace elements --- ## 1.2 Bioremediation — Microbes in Mining (Biomining) ### 1.2.1 Core Definition **Biomining (Bioleaching)** is the extraction of metals from low-grade ores, mineral concentrates, or mine tailings using **chemolithotrophic microorganisms** that catalyze the oxidation of metal sulfides into water-soluble metal sulfates. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Board Favorite:** Copper, gold (from refractory ores), uranium, cobalt, and zinc are the principal metals extracted via biomining. The economics favor **low-grade ores (0.1–0.5% metal)** which are uneconomical for smelting. ### 1.2.2 Intuitive Analogy — "The Microscopic Mining Crew" Think of biomining as **biological drilling**: instead of using high-temperature furnaces and toxic chemicals, you sprinkle invisible microbes onto a rock pile, give them air and water, and they **chew away the rock**, releasing the trapped metal like workers dissolving a lock with acid. The acid they produce (H₂SO₄) and the iron they recycle act as their mining tools. ### 1.2.3 Key Microorganisms | Microbe | Optimum pH | Role | |---|---|---| | **Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans** | 1.5–3.0 | Oxidizes Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ and sulfide minerals | | **Acidithiobacillus thiooxidans** | 1.0–3.0 | Oxidizes elemental sulfur → H₂SO₄ | | **Leptospirillum ferrooxidans** | 1.0–2.0 | Specialist Fe²⁺ oxidizer (tolerant to high Fe³⁺) | | **Acidithiobacillus caldus** | 1.5–3.0 | Sulfur oxidation at moderate temperatures | | **Sulfolobus metallicus** *(archaea)* | 60–75 °C | High-temperature leaching | > [!NOTE] > These microbes are **chemolithoautotrophs** — they derive energy from inorganic oxidation reactions and CO₂ is their carbon source. They operate in **extremely acidic** (pH 1–3) and **metal-rich** environments where most life cannot survive. # 📗 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & FORMULA SHEET ## 2.1 Baker's Yeast — Process Engineering Logic ### 2.1.1 Why Fed-Batch? — The Crabtree Effect Logic Chain The entire process strategy hinges on **maintaining substrate concentration below the critical glucose threshold (S₍crit₎ ≈ 0.5–1.0 g/L)**. **Logical Step-by-Step Breakdown:** 1. **Yeast under aerobic conditions** has two metabolic pathways for glucose: - **Respiration:** Glucose → CO₂ + H₂O (high biomass yield, Yx/s ≈ 0.5) - **Fermentation:** Glucose → Ethanol + CO₂ (low biomass yield, Yx/s ≈ 0.05) 2. **At high glucose concentrations (S > S₍crit₎):** - Glucose is taken up faster than it can be respired - Excess pyruvate is converted to ethanol via **pyruvate decarboxylase** - Biomass yield collapses 3. **At low glucose concentrations (S < S₍crit₎):** - All glucose enters **respiratory pathway** - Maximum biomass yield achieved - **No ethanol produced** → all carbon goes to cells 4. **Hence:** Feed molasses *slowly* so that consumption rate ≈ feeding rate → S remains low → pure respiration → maximum cells. ### 2.1.2 Process Stages **Stage 1 — Inoculum Development (Train):** - Stock culture → Test tube → Erlenmeyer flask → Laboratory fermenter → Pilot scale → Production - Each stage increases volume by **5–10×** **Stage 2 — Production Fermenter (Fed-Batch, 100–200 m³):** - **Phase A (Batch Start):** Small volume of molasses at **~4% w/v sugar**, full nutrients - **Phase B (Fed-Batch):** Molasses feed started once initial sugar depleted; feed rate gradually increased as biomass builds - **pH control:** **5.0 ± 0.2** (controlled by NH₃ addition which also supplies N) - **Temperature:** **30 ± 1 °C** - **Dissolved Oxygen (DO):** >30% saturation maintained by agitation + aeration - **Duration:** **15–20 hours** to reach 40–50 g DCW/L **Stage 3 — Downstream Processing:** - Centrifugation → Washing (with chilled water to remove impurities, color) → Cream (~20% solids) → Filtration → Drying (Drum dryer or Fluidized bed at **30–40 °C**) → Packaging > [!WARNING] > **Drying is the most critical downstream step.** Yeast viability drops sharply if temperature exceeds **45 °C**. The drying rate must be tightly controlled to retain >75% viability. ### 2.1.3 Material Balance — Theoretical Foundation For a fed-batch yeast fermentation: - **Biomass produced:** $P = V \cdot X$ - **Substrate consumed:** $\Delta S = S_0 V_0 + F_{in} t - S V$ (where $S \approx 0$ in fed-batch) - **Biomass yield:** $Y_{x/s} = \dfrac{X V - X_0 V_0}{S_0 V_0 + F_{in} t}$ For purely aerobic respiration: $$Y_{x/s}^{max} = \dfrac{\text{biomass formed}}{\text{substrate consumed}} \approx 0.50 \text{ g g}^{-1}$$ For purely fermentative metabolism: $$Y_{x/s}^{ferm} \approx 0.10 \text{ g g}^{-1}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** The Crabtree effect can cost up to **80% of the theoretical biomass yield** if glucose is uncontrolled — a 5× economic loss. --- ## 2.2 Biomining — Process Engineering Logic ### 2.2.1 Two Complementary Mechanisms **Mechanism 1 — Direct (Contact Leaching):** - Microbes attach to mineral surface via **extracellular polymeric substances (EPS)** - They directly oxidize metal sulfides using enzymes (e.g., rusticyanin) **Mechanism 2 — Indirect (Non-Contact Leaching):** - Microbes generate **ferric iron (Fe³⁺)** in solution - Fe³⁺ chemically attacks the sulfide mineral → Fe²⁺ + dissolved metal - Microbes re-oxidize Fe²⁺ back to Fe³⁺ (cyclic catalyst) > [!NOTE] > In practice, **both mechanisms operate simultaneously**. The indirect route dominates because Fe³⁺ is a powerful oxidant (E° = +0.77 V) that dissolves many sulfide minerals rapidly. ### 2.2.2 The Core Stoichiometric Equations **Iron oxidation (microbial):** $$4 \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{bacteria}} 4\text{Fe}^{3+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **Sulfur oxidation (microbial):** $$2\text{S}^0 + 3\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \xrightarrow{\text{bacteria}} 2\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}^+$$ **Pyrite oxidation (overall):** $$4\text{FeS}_2 + 15\text{O}_2 + 14\text{H}_2\text{O} \longrightarrow 4\text{Fe}^{3+} + 8\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 28\text{H}^+$$ **Chalcopyrite leaching (copper extraction):** $$\text{CuFeS}_2 + 4\text{Fe}^{3+} \longrightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 5\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{S}^0$$ The elemental sulfur formed is oxidized by *A. thiooxidans* to sulfuric acid, regenerating protons. ### 2.2.3 Leaching Methods Comparison | Method | Scale | Ore Type | Time | |---|---|---|---| | **Heap Leaching** | 10⁴–10⁶ tonnes | Crushed low-grade ore piled on impermeable pad | 6 months – 3 years | | **Dump Leaching** | >10⁶ tonnes | Run-of-mine waste rock, no crushing | 5–20 years | | **Tank/Shake Flask** | kg – tonnes | High-grade concentrate | hours – days | | **In-situ Leaching** | Underground ore body | Deep deposits, fractured rock | years – decades | --- ## 2.3 📊 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Typical Value / Unit | |---|---|---| | Biomass yield (respiration) | $Y_{x/s}$ | **0.5 g/g** | | Biomass yield (fermentation) | $Y_{x/s}$ | **0.10 g/g** | | Specific growth rate (max) | $\mu_{max}$ | **0.45–0.55 h⁻¹** | | Doubling time | $t_d = \dfrac{\ln 2}{\mu}$ | **1.3–1.5 h** | | Specific O₂ uptake rate | $q_{O_2}$ | **5–8 mmol g⁻¹ h⁻¹** | | Critical dissolved O₂ | $DO_{crit}$ | **>30% saturation (~1 mg/L)** | | Critical glucose | $S_{crit}$ | **0.5–1.0 g/L** | | Final biomass conc. | $X_{final}$ | **40–50 g DCW/L** | | Fermenter volume (industrial) | $V$ | **100–200 m³** | | Process time | $t$ | **15–20 h** | | Yield on molasses | — | **~0.25–0.30 kg yeast/kg molasses** | | Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺ redox potential | $E$ | **+600 to +850 mV** (vs Ag/AgCl) | | Optimum leaching pH | pH | **1.5–2.5** | | Iron oxidation rate | $r_{Fe}$ | **0.5–5 g L⁻¹ day⁻¹** | | Copper recovery (heap) | — | **70–90%** over 1–3 years | | Yield: Cu²⁺ per ton ore | — | **2–8 kg** (typical low-grade) | | Acid generation | $pH \downarrow$ via $\text{H}^+$ release | **up to 50 kg H₂SO₄/tonne ore** | ### 2.3.1 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Baker's yeast:** Global market > **1.2 million tonnes/year**; top producers are AB Mauri, Lesaffre, Lallemand. - **Biomining:** ~**20–25% of global copper** is now bioleached; this percentage is increasing as high-grade ores deplete. - **Gold biomining (BIOX® process):** Used at Ashanti Goldfields (Ghana) — pretreats refractory ore to liberate gold trapped inside pyrite arsenopyrite matrix. # 🧮 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX ## 3.1 Numerical Problem — Baker's Yeast Fed-Batch Yield ### Problem Statement A 150 m³ industrial fed-batch fermenter is inoculated with **2000 kg of molasses** (initial sugar 50% w/w) at $X_0 = 2$ g DCW/L. During 18 hours of fed-batch, an additional **25,000 kg of molasses** (50% w/w sugar) is fed. Final biomass = **45 g DCW/L**. Final volume = **130 m³** (water lost to evaporation and aeration accounted). Calculate: (a) Total substrate consumed (kg sugar) (b) Overall yield coefficient Yx/s (c) Productivity (g L⁻¹ h⁻¹) (d) Comment on whether respiration or fermentation dominated. ### 3.1.1 Solution — Step-by-Step **Given Data:** - $V_f = 130$ m³ = 130,000 L - $X_0 = 2$ g/L, $X_f = 45$ g/L - Total molasses added = 2000 + 25000 = 27000 kg - Sugar fraction = 50% → Total sugar introduced = **13,500 kg** - Residual sugar at end ≈ **0.5 g/L** (negligible vs initial loading) --- **Part (a) — Total Substrate Consumed (kg sugar)** $$S_{total\,in} = (2000 + 25000) \times 0.50 = 13500 \text{ kg}$$ $$S_{residual} = 0.5 \text{ g/L} \times 130{,}000 \text{ L} = 65 \text{ kg}$$ $$ \Delta S = S_{total\,in} - S_{residual} = 13500 - 65 = 13435 \text{ kg} $$ $$ \boxed{\Delta S \approx 13435 \text{ kg of sugar consumed}} $$ --- **Part (b) — Overall Biomass Yield Yx/s** Biomass produced: $$ \Delta X \cdot V_f = (45 - 2) \text{ g/L} \times 130{,}000 \text{ L} = 5{,}590{,}000 \text{ g} = 5590 \text{ kg} $$ Yield: $$ Y_{x/s} = \frac{\Delta X}{\Delta S} = \frac{5590}{13435} $$ $$ \boxed{Y_{x/s} \approx 0.416 \text{ g g}^{-1}} $$ --- **Part (c) — Volumetric Productivity (g L⁻¹ h⁻¹)** $$ P_v = \frac{X_f V_f - X_0 V_0}{V_f \cdot t} $$ Initial volume approximated as $V_0 = 2000 \text{ kg}/(\rho \cdot 0.50) \approx 2000 \text{ L}$ (assuming 50% w/w density ≈ 1.0): $$ P_v = \frac{5590000 - 4000}{130000 \times 18} = \frac{5586000}{2340000} $$ $$ \boxed{P_v \approx 2.387 \text{ g L}^{-1} \text{ h}^{-1}} $$ --- **Part (d) — Metabolic Regime Analysis** Theoretical maximum aerobic yield: $$ Y_{x/s}^{max} = 0.50 \text{ g g}^{-1} $$ The ratio: $$ \eta = \frac{Y_{x/s}^{observed}}{Y_{x/s}^{max}} = \frac{0.416}{0.50} = 0.832 \; (83.2\%) $$ Since η > 0.80 and Yx/s > 0.40 g/g, the process was **predominantly respiratory** with minor Crabtree-related ethanol spillover. > **Engineering Verdict:** A well-controlled fed-batch with ~83% efficiency — typical of a commercial plant. The 17% loss accounts for maintenance metabolism, cell lysis products, and minor fermentative episodes at peak feed rates. --- ## 3.2 Numerical Problem — Biomining Copper Heap Leaching ### Problem Statement A copper heap leach pad contains **800,000 tonnes** of run-of-mine ore with **0.40% Cu** content, primarily as chalcopyrite (CuFeS₂). Microbial leaching extracts **75% of the copper** over 18 months. The pregnant leach solution (PLS) has **Fe³⁺/Fe²⁺ redox potential of 720 mV**, pH 2.0. Calculate: (a) Total copper dissolved (kg) (b) Average daily copper extraction rate (kg/day) (c) Moles of sulfuric acid generated per kg Cu if FeS₂ co-extraction occurs. (d) Stack the molar stoichiometry of overall pyrite-chalcopyrite leaching. ### 3.2.1 Solution — Step-by-Step **Given:** - Ore mass = $8 \times 10^5$ tonnes - Cu grade = 0.40% = 0.004 kg Cu / kg ore - Recovery = 75% --- **Part (a) — Total Copper Dissolved** $$ M_{Cu} = 8 \times 10^5 \times 1000 \times 0.0040 = 3.2 \times 10^6 \text{ kg Cu in ore} $$ $$ M_{Cu, dissolved} = 3.2 \times 10^6 \times 0.75 $$ $$ \boxed{M_{Cu, dissolved} = 2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ kg Cu (2400 tonnes)}} $$ --- **Part (b) — Average Daily Extraction Rate** Time = 18 months = 540 days: $$ r_{Cu} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ kg}}{540 \text{ days}} $$ $$ \boxed{r_{Cu} \approx 4444 \text{ kg Cu / day} \;\; (\approx 4.4 \text{ t/day})} $$ --- **Part (c) — H₂SO₄ Stoichiometry per kg Cu** Coupled chalcopyrite-pyrite leaching, combining: - $\text{CuFeS}_2 + 4\text{Fe}^{3+} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 5\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{S}^0$ - $4\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ \rightarrow 4\text{Fe}^{3+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$ - $2\text{S}^0 + 3\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}^+$ Overall (per mol Cu): $$ \text{CuFeS}_2 + 2\text{Fe}^{3+} + 1.75\text{O}_2 + 1.5\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 3\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}^+ $$ *(Net proton generation: 2 H⁺ produced per Cu²⁺ released, going into 1 H₂SO₄.)* Therefore: - 1 mol Cu → 1 mol H₂SO₄ net - Per kg Cu: $n_{H_2SO_4} = \dfrac{1000}{63.55} = 15.73$ mol - Mass: $15.73 \times 98 = 1542$ g = **1.54 kg H₂SO₄ / kg Cu** $$ \boxed{m_{H_2SO_4} = 1542 \text{ g H}_2\text{SO}_4 \text{ per kg Cu}} $$ > **Engineering Implication:** The acid produced *self-fuels* the leach — no external acid needed once pyrite is co-leached. --- ## 3.3 Derivation — Fed-Batch Mass Balance Framework The general fed-batch mass balance for biomass: $$ \frac{d(XV)}{dt} = \mu X V $$ The volume balance: $$ \frac{dV}{dt} = F_{in} - F_{out} $$ For yeast fed-batch (no outflow, evaporation neglected): $$ \frac{dV}{dt} = F_{in} $$ Defining cumulative biomass: $$ \frac{d(XV)}{dt} = \mu \cdot XV \quad \Rightarrow \quad \frac{dX}{dt} + X\frac{d\ln V}{dt} = \mu X $$ If the feed is exponentially programmed at rate: $$ F_{in}(t) = F_0 \cdot e^{\mu_{set} t} $$ Then for quasi-steady growth, $X(t) = $ const, and $d\ln V/dt = \mu_{set}$, so $\mu = \mu_{set}$. This is **exponential fed-batch**, the gold-standard protocol for industrial yeast production. # 🗺️ 4.
