KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK ## 5.1 📝 Part A — 3-Mark Short Answer Questions ### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **Explain the Crabtree effect and its significance in industrial baker's yeast production.** **Model Answer:** The Crabtree effect describes the **repression of aerobic respiration** by yeast (*S. cerevisiae*) when exposed to **high glucose concentrations** (>1 g/L). Under such conditions, the cells preferentially ferment glucose to ethanol, even in the presence of sufficient dissolved oxygen. **Significance in baker's yeast production:** - It is the **central problem** of yeast biomass production — fermentation (Yx/s = 0.10) wastes 80% of the carbon as ethanol instead of biomass. - It is overcome by **fed-batch cultivation** where glucose concentration is kept **below the critical threshold (S₍crit₎ ≈ 0.5–1.0 g/L)** so that cells use the **respiratory pathway (Yx/s ≈ 0.50)** exclusively. - Therefore, the **molasses feed rate** is the master control variable. --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **List any four chemolithotrophic microorganisms used in biomining. State the role of *Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans*.** **Model Answer:** **Four biomining microorganisms:** 1. *Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans* 2. *Acidithiobacillus thiooxidans* 3. *Leptospirillum ferrooxidans* 4. *Sulfolobus metallicus* (archaea, thermophilic) **Role of *A. ferrooxidans*:** - A **chemolithoautotrophic, acidophilic** bacterium - Oxidizes **ferrous iron (Fe²⁺)** to **ferric iron (Fe³⁺)** at pH 1.5–3.0 - Also oxidizes **reduced sulfur compounds** to sulfuric acid - The regenerated Fe³⁺ acts as the **chemical leaching agent** that dissolves metal sulfide ores (chalcopyrite, pyrite) — the indirect leaching mechanism. --- ## 5.2 📝 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) > **KTU Exam Pattern:** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. Solve **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### ✅ QUESTION A — (14 Marks) — Baker's Yeast Focus **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, Dec 2024 Pattern]** #### (a) [7 Marks] — Industrial Process Description **Describe in detail the industrial production of baker's yeast by fed-batch fermentation. Mention the microorganism, raw materials, process parameters, and downstream processing steps.** **Model Answer:** **(i) Microorganism:** *Saccharomyces cerevisiae* — selected for high μmax, robust growth, and tolerance to drying. **(ii) Raw Materials:** - **Substrate:** Beet or cane molasses (45–55% sucrose, also contains minerals, vitamins) - **Nitrogen source:** (NH₄)₂SO₄, NH₄H₂PO₄ (also serves as pH titrant) - **Phosphorus:** NH₄H₂PO₄ or Na₂HPO₄ - **Vitamins:** Biotin, thiamine, pantothenate — supplied via corn-steep liquor - **Trace elements:** Mg²⁺, Zn²⁺, Ca²⁺, Fe²⁺ **(iii) Process Stages:** **A. Inoculum Train:** - Stock (lyophilized) → Test tube (YPD broth) → 500 mL flask → 10 L lab fermenter → 500 L pilot → Production fermenter (100–200 m³) **B. Fed-Batch Production (18–20 h):** - **Initial batch:** ~4% w/v sugar, full nutrients, all-at-once - **Feed phase:** Molasses added gradually; feed rate follows **exponential fed-batch profile** F(t) = F₀·e^(μt) - **pH:** Maintained at **5.0 ± 0.2** using **NH₃ titration** (also supplies nitrogen) - **Temperature:** **30 ± 1 °C** - **DO:** Maintained > **30% saturation** through aeration + agitation - **Anti-foam:** Polyglycol/silicone additions - **Final biomass:** 40–50 g DCW/L **C. Downstream Processing:** 1. **Centrifugation** → Yeast cream (~20% solids) 2. **Washing** with chilled water (≤ 8 °C) to remove color, residual substrate 3. **Filtration** (rotary vacuum) → Cake ~30% solids 4. **Drying** → Drum dryer or fluidized bed at **30–40 °C** (critical for viability) 5. **Packaging** → Active Dry Yeast (ADY), vacuum-packed or nitrogen-flushed **(iv) Yield:** ~0.25–0.30 kg yeast / kg molasses; Yx/s ≈ 0.45–0.50 g/g achieved. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] — Critical Analysis of Crabtree Effect **Explain the Crabtree and Pasteur effects with their industrial implications. Discuss why fed-batch is preferred over batch cultivation for baker's yeast.** **Model Answer:** **Crabtree Effect:** - **Definition:** Repression of oxidative metabolism by high glucose; yeast ferments to ethanol even in presence of O₂ when glucose > S₍crit₎ (~1 g/L) - **Cause:** Hexokinase and pyruvate decarboxylase not repressed; TCA cycle repressed by catabolite inactivation - **Industrial consequence:** Yx/s drops from 0.50 → 0.10, losing 80% of substrate to ethanol **Pasteur Effect:** - **Definition:** Inhibition of fermentation (glycolysis) by oxygen; anaerobic fermentation rate is much higher than aerobic fermentation rate - **Industrial consequence:** Once ethanol is formed (during a Crabtree episode), oxygen cannot re-route the metabolism back to respiration — **ethanol is "locked in"** **Why Fed-Batch is Preferred:** | Aspect | Batch | Fed-Batch | |---|---|---| | Initial sugar concentration | High (10–20%) | Low (4%) | | Crabtree triggered? | **Yes — ethanol produced** | **No — sugar kept low** | | Biomass yield | 0.10 g/g (poor) | 0.50 g/g (theoretical max) | | Final biomass | 8–12 g/L | **40–50 g/L** | | Substrate inhibition at high initial sugar | Yes (substrate inhibits growth) | Avoided | | Productivity | ~0.5 g/L/h | **~2.4 g/L/h** | > **Conclusion:** Fed-batch is **the only viable industrial mode** for baker's yeast because it alone maintains glucose below the Crabtree threshold throughout cultivation, allowing exclusively respiratory metabolism and maximal biomass yield. --- ### ✅ QUESTION B — (14 Marks) — Biomining Focus **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, May 2024 Pattern]** #### (a) [7 Marks] — Biomining Principles & Mechanisms **Describe the mechanism of bioleaching of copper from low-grade ores. Explain direct and indirect leaching mechanisms with suitable equations.** **Model Answer:** **Microorganisms:** *Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans*, *A. thiooxidans*, *Leptospirillum ferrooxidans* — all chemolithoautotrophic, acidophilic (pH 1.5–3.0). **(i) Direct Mechanism:** - Bacterial cells attach to mineral surface via **EPS (extracellular polymeric substances)** - Direct enzymatic oxidation of **metal sulfide (MS)** bonds by surface-bound enzymes (rusticyanin, cytochrome oxidase) - Reactions occur at the cell-mineral interface - **Equation:** $$ \text{CuFeS}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{cells}} \text{Cu}^{2+} + \text{Fe}^{2+/3+} + 2\text{S}^0 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} $$ - **Equation (pyrite):** $$\text{FeS}_2 + \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{bacteria}} \text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{S}^0 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ **(ii) Indirect Mechanism (dominant in practice):** - Microbes generate Fe³⁺ (the chemical oxidant) and H₂SO₄ (the acid) - Fe³⁺ then **chemically** attacks the mineral without direct microbial contact - **Equation:** $$\text{CuFeS}_2 + 4\text{Fe}^{3+} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 5\text{Fe}^{2+} + 2\text{S}^0$$ - Microbes re-oxidize the produced Fe²⁺ to Fe³⁺ (regeneration): $$4\text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{O}_2 + 4\text{H}^+ \rightarrow 4\text{Fe}^{3+} + 2\text{H}_2\text{O}$$ - Elemental sulfur formed is oxidized to H₂SO₄: $$2\text{S}^0 + 3\text{O}_2 + 2\text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 2\text{SO}_4^{2-} + 4\text{H}^+$$ > **Net effect:** Fe³⁺ acts as a **biological catalyst** — it is consumed in attacking the ore and regenerated by bacterial action. --- #### (b) [7 Marks] — Heap Leaching & Industrial Operations **Explain the operation of a copper bioleaching heap. Discuss its advantages and limitations.** **Model Answer:** **Heap Leaching Process Steps:** 1. **Pad Construction:** Impermeable clay/HDPE-lined pad with drainage channels 2. **Ore Loading:** Crushed ore (~2 cm) stacked into heaps of 4–8 m height (100,000s of tonnes) 3. **Inoculation:** Acidified Fe³⁺-rich pregnant leach solution (PLS) recirculated; contains indigenous + added *A. ferrooxidans* 4. **Irrigation:** Drip emitters spray solution from top; gravity-driven percolation 5. **Aeration:** Forced air injection via aeration pipes (O₂ is essential electron acceptor) 6. **PLS Collection:** Cu²⁺-rich solution drains at base → sent to SX/EW plant 7. **Metal Recovery:** Solvent extraction (LIX reagents) → Electrowinning → 99.99% Cu cathode 8. **Refreshing:** Acidified Fe³⁺ solution recycled back to heap; cycle continues 9. **Cycle time:** 6 months – 3 years per heap 10. **Raffinate:** Stripped organic phase returned for re-extraction **Advantages:** - Processes **low-grade (0.1–0.5%) ores** uneconomical for smelting - **Low capital cost** vs smelting - **Environmentally benign** vs pyrometallurgy (no SO₂ emissions) - **Self-acidifying** via pyrite co-leaching - Operates at ambient temperature (no fossil fuel heating) - Suitable for remote locations; simple to scale **Limitations:** - **Slow kinetics** (months to years) - **Lower recovery** (~75%) vs smelting (~95%) - **Sensitive to temperature** — slows in winter, accelerates in summer - **Passivation** — sulfur film on chalcopyrite can halt leaching - **Highly acidic waste solutions** requiring careful handling - **Open-system** — risk of uncontrolled microbial succession - Requires **continuous irrigation** — water scarcity a constraint in arid regions > **Real-World Example:** Escondida (Chile), world's largest copper mine — produces >1 million tonnes Cu/year using combined bioleaching + SX/EW operations. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Where Students Lose Marks** > 1. **Confusing Pasteur & Crabtree effects** — Pasteur = O₂ inhibits fermentation; Crabtree = glucose inhibits respiration. **Mnemonic:** "Crabtree is about **C**arbohydrate (glucose)." > 2. **Writing "yeast produces ethanol" without context** — Always specify "due to the Crabtree effect under high glucose." Without this, answer looks half-baked. > 3. **In biomining, students write "bacteria eat rocks"** — examiners reject this. Always emphasize **Fe³⁺ regeneration** as the true mechanism. > 4. **Forgetting stoichiometric equations** — Examiner explicitly asks for reactions; incomplete equations lose 2–3 marks. > 5. **Not mentioning DOWNSTREAM processing in yeast production** — Drying temperature control is **a favorite 2-mark sub-question**. > 6. **Mixing up heap vs tank vs dump leaching** — Note the **scale and time** differences clearly. > 7. **Forgetting to give numbers/units** — Yx/s = 0.5 g/g, pH = 5.0, T = 30 °C. **Memorize these constants.** --- ## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist ### 🔹 Baker's Yeast — Key Recap - Microbe: ***Saccharomyces cerevisiae*** (eukaryotic budding yeast) - Process: **Aerobic fed-batch** with exponential molasses feed - **Crabtree Effect** = repression of respiration by high glucose; overcome by **fed-batch** - Yx/s (respiration) = **0.5 g/g**; Yx/s (fermentation) = **0.1 g/g** - Final biomass: **40–50 g DCW/L** in 15–20 h - Process parameters: **pH 5.0, 30 °C, DO > 30%** - DSP: Centrifuge → Wash → Filter → Dry (≤ 40 °C) → Pack - Product: **Active Dry Yeast (ADY)** for bakeries - Molasses yield: ~**0.25–0.30 kg yeast/kg molasses** ### 🔹 Biomining — Key Recap - Definition: **Microbial extraction of metals from low-grade ores** - Key microbes: ***A. ferrooxidans***, ***A. thiooxidans***, ***L. ferrooxidans***, *Sulfolobus* - They are **chemolithoautotrophs**, acidophilic, operating at **pH 1.5–2.5** - Two mechanisms: **Direct (contact)** + **Indirect (Fe³⁺ mediated, dominant)** - Core equation: Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ (microbial), Fe³⁺ attacks MS → Cu²⁺ + Fe²⁺ - Target metals: **Cu, Au (refractory ores), U, Co, Zn, Ni** - Methods: **Heap > Dump > Tank** (scale descending) - Global copper bioleaching share: ~**20–25%** and rising - Advantages: Low capex, low-grade feasible, no SO₂ emissions - Limitations: Slow kinetics, passivation issues ### 🔹 Cross-Cutting Concepts (High-Yield) - **Catabolite Repression** — Common to both topics (Glucose effect on yeast; not on acidophiles though) - **Fed-batch vs Continuous vs Batch** — Yeast uses fed-batch; biomining is essentially a **solid-state plug-flow bioreactor** - **Yx/s** — Mass-yield coefficient is central to *both* processes - **Steady-state vs Unsteady-state** — Yeast = unsteady (fed-batch); biomining = quasi-steady over years - **Industrial organisms** must be **robust, safe, genetically stable, recoverable** — common selection criterion > [!TIP] > **Last-Minute Exam Strategy:** Draw the **Crabtree Decision Tree** (Section 4.3) on the back of your answer booklet. Examiners reward flowcharts. Then for biomining, always present the **indirect leaching cycle as a 3-arrow loop** — bio-regeneration of Fe³⁺ is the heart of every answer.

topics.title: Ore Leaching
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Ore Leaching

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# ORE LEACHING – Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Ore Leaching** is a *hydrometallurgical extractive process* in which a **valuable metal** is selectively dissolved from its parent ore (or concentrate) by contacting the solid matrix with a suitable **lixiviant** (aqueous chemical solution), allowing the metal ions to enter the solution phase as a soluble compound, from which it is later recovered by **electrowinning, precipitation, or solvent extraction**.

In the context of **Bioprocess Engineering (OEFTT726)**, ore leaching specifically includes:

- **Chemical Leaching** – using inorganic lixiviants (H₂SO₄, NaCN, NH₃, HCl).
- **Bioleaching / Biomining** – using *microorganisms* (e.g., *Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans*, *A. thiooxidans*) to catalyze the oxidation of sulfide ores or facilitate the regeneration of ferric iron (Fe³⁺) which then acts as the primary oxidant.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 4 – High-Yield Definition Box**
> **Leaching** = Selective dissolution of a soluble component from a solid (ore) by a liquid solvent (lixiviant).
> **Extraction efficiency (E)** = (Mass of metal dissolved / Mass of metal in ore) × **100 %**

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Real-World Intuition)

Think of ore leaching exactly like making **tea in a hot water bath**:

| Tea-Making Step | Ore Leaching Step |
|-----------------|-------------------|
| Tea leaves (solid) | Crushed ore (solid) |
| Hot water (solvent) | Lixiviant (H₂SO₄, NaCN, bacterial culture) |
| Tea diffuses into water | Metal ions diffuse into solution |
| Tea bag remains as residue | Gangue / unleached residue |
| Strength of tea | Pregnant leach solution (PLS) concentration |

Just as gentle heating and stirring release the tea compounds faster, in ore leaching, parameters like **temperature, particle size, pH, agitation, and microbial activity** accelerate the release of metal ions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight – KTU Board Exam Frequency**
> Direct questions on *"Define leaching and differentiate between chemical and bioleaching"* appear almost every semester. Memorize the table in §1.2 style.

## 1.3 Classification of Ore Leaching (At a Glance)

Ore leaching is broadly classified into **four** industrial modes based on ore handling:

1. **Heap Leaching** – crushed ore piled on an impermeable pad; lixiviant sprinkled on top.
2. **Dump Leaching** – low-grade waste rock dumped on a slope; natural rainfall + lixiviant percolation.
3. **In-Situ Leaching** – lixiviant injected directly into the underground ore body via boreholes.
4. **Vat / Tank Leaching** – ore placed in a sealed vessel; lixiviant agitated and recycled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Metric to Remember**
> Typical metal recovery via heap leaching of low-grade copper ore: **60 – 85 %** over **90 – 250 days** operation.
> Typical particle size: **6 – 25 mm** crushed ore.
> Typical lixiviant: **H₂SO₄ at pH 1.5 – 2.5** for oxide copper ores.

## 1.4 Why Ore Leaching is a Bioprocess Engineering Topic

Bioprocess Engineering emphasizes **microbial kinetics, bioreactor design, and mass transfer in solid–liquid systems**. Ore leaching is a perfect example because:

- The *rate-limiting step* is identical to that in **enzyme catalysis and microbial growth** (Monod-type kinetics with substrate = Fe²⁺ or S⁰).
- The *solid–liquid mass transfer* equations are **mathematically identical** to those in **microbial film reactors** and **immobilized cell systems**.
- The *shrinking core model* used to describe ore leaching is also used to describe **bioparticle degradation** in anaerobic digesters.

> [!TIP]
> **Linkage to Module 1, 2 & 3 of OEFTT726**
> Students often miss the connection. Recall:
> Module 1 – Stoichiometry of microbial reactions.
> Module 2 – Monod kinetics → applied here for *A. ferrooxidans* on Fe²⁺ substrate.
> Module 3 – Mass transfer → applied here for oxygen/nutrient diffusion into ore heaps.
> Module 4 – Ore Leaching (current topic) integrates all three.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# ORE LEACHING – Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fundamental Mechanism of Leaching

The leaching of a metal *M* from its ore involves a sequence of **five sequential steps**:

1. **External Diffusion** – lixiviant transports from bulk solution to the ore surface.
2. **Internal Diffusion (Pore Diffusion)** – lixiviant penetrates pores of the ore particle.
3. **Surface Chemical Reaction** – the metal (or its compound) reacts chemically with the lixiviant.
   - Example: CuO + H₂SO₄ → CuSO₄ + H₂O
   - Example: 4 Au + 8 NaCN + O₂ + 2 H₂O → 4 Na[Au(CN)₂] + 4 NaOH
4. **Product Diffusion Out** – dissolved metal ions diffuse back through pores to the surface.
5. **Bulk Mixing** – metal ions mix into the bulk pregnant leach solution (PLS).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The slowest step = the rate-controlling step.**
> In heap leaching → **Internal diffusion** is usually controlling.
> In agitated tank leaching → **Surface chemical reaction** is usually controlling.
> In dump/in-situ leaching → **External diffusion** is often controlling.

## 2.2 Bioleaching Mechanism (Microbial Catalysis)

Two main mechanisms are described in the **KTU-recommended literature**:

### (A) Direct Mechanism
Microbes attach to the mineral surface via extracellular polymeric substances (EPS) and **directly oxidize** the sulfide mineral using their enzymatic machinery (rusticyanin, cytochrome oxidase, etc.).

$$
\text{CuS (covellite)} \; + \; 2 \, \text{O}_2 \; \xrightarrow{\text{A. ferrooxidans}} \; \text{CuSO}_4
$$

### (B) Indirect Mechanism (most accepted)
The microbe oxidizes **ferrous iron (Fe²⁺) to ferric iron (Fe³⁺)** in solution. The Fe³⁺ then acts as a *chemical oxidant* to attack the sulfide mineral, regenerating Fe²⁺ in a closed cycle.

$$
4 \, \text{Fe}^{2+} \; + \; \text{O}_2 \; + \; 4 \, \text{H}^+ \; \xrightarrow{\text{A. ferrooxidans}} \; 4 \, \text{Fe}^{3+} \; + \; 2 \, \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{CuS} \; + \; 2 \, \text{Fe}^{3+} \; \longrightarrow \; \text{Cu}^{2+} \; + \; 2 \, \text{Fe}^{2+} \; + \; \text{S}^0
$$

The regenerated Fe²⁺ is re-oxidized by the microbe, making the cycle **self-sustaining**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Board Exam Trick Question**
> *"Which is more industrially relevant – direct or indirect bioleaching?"*
> **Answer: Indirect mechanism**, because it is not limited by physical contact between microbe and mineral. It operates in the bulk solution and works even on finely disseminated ore.

## 2.3 Microorganisms Used in Bioleaching (KTU Important Table)

| Microorganism | Type | Substrate Oxidized | Optimum pH | Temperature |
|---------------|------|--------------------|------------|-------------|
| *Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans* | Chemolithoautotroph | Fe²⁺, S⁰, sulfides | 1.5 – 2.5 | 30 °C |
| *Acidithiobacillus thiooxidans* | Chemolithoautotroph | S⁰, thiosulfate | 1.0 – 3.0 | 30 °C |
| *Leptospirillum ferrooxidans* | Chemolithoautotroph | Fe²⁺ only | 1.5 – 2.0 | 35 °C |
| *Sulfolobus* spp. | Archeon (thermophile) | Fe²⁺, S⁰, sulfides | 1.5 – 3.0 | 65 – 80 °C |

## 2.4 Heap Leaching – Process Architecture

A heap leach operation involves:

1. **Pad preparation** – impermeable HDPE liner on compacted earth.
2. **Ore stacking** – crushed ore (6 – 25 mm) piled 4 – 10 m high.
3. **Irrigation system** – drip emitters or wobblers spray lixiviant on top.
4. **Percolation** – lixiviant trickles down by gravity through the heap.
5. **Collection sump** – Pregnant Leach Solution (PLS) collected at the base.
6. **Metal recovery** – solvent extraction (SX) followed by electrowinning (EW).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Memory Point**
> **PLS** = Pregnant Leach Solution (metal-rich solution exiting the heap).
> **Raffinate** = Lixiviant after metal extraction, recycled back to the heap.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Variables & Units | Applicable To |
|---|-------------------|-------------------|---------------|
| 1 | $E = \dfrac{m_{\text{dissolved}}}{m_{\text{ore}}} \times 100$ | $E$ = Extraction efficiency (%); $m$ = mass (kg) | All leaching modes |
| 2 | $R_{\text{obs}} = k \cdot A \cdot C^n$ | $R$ = leaching rate; $A$ = surface area (m²); $C$ = lixiviant conc. (mol/m³); $n$ = reaction order | Surface-reaction controlled |
| 3 | $1 - (1-X)^{1/3} = k_c \, t$ | $X$ = fractional conversion; $k_c$ = reaction rate constant (s⁻¹); $t$ = time (s) | Surface chemical reaction control (shrinking core) |
| 4 | $1 - (1-X)^{2/3} = k_d \, t$ | $k_d$ = external (film) diffusion constant (s⁻¹) | Liquid film diffusion control |
| 5 | $1 - 3(1-X)^{2/3} + 2(1-X) = k_p \, t$ | $k_p$ = product (ash) layer diffusion constant (s⁻¹) | Diffusion through product layer control |
| 6 | $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{\max} \, C_s}{K_s + C_s}$ | Monod kinetics for *A. ferrooxidans* on Fe²⁺ substrate | Bioleaching kinetics |
| 7 | $\text{Da} = \dfrac{k \, L}{u}$ | Damköhler number; $L$ = heap height (m); $u$ = superficial velocity (m/s) | Heap leach reactor analysis |
| 8 | $\text{Pe} = \dfrac{u \, d_p}{D_{\text{eff}}}$ | Peclet number; $d_p$ = particle diameter (m); $D_{\text{eff}}$ = effective diffusivity (m²/s) | Dispersion in heap |
| 9 | $\eta = 1 - \dfrac{1}{\cosh(\phi)}$ | Internal effectiveness factor; $\phi$ = Thiele modulus | Pore-diffusion in ore particle |
| 10 | $\phi = R_p \sqrt{\dfrac{k}{D_{\text{eff}}}}$ | Thiele modulus; $R_p$ = particle radius (m) | Coupled reaction-diffusion in ore |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Exam Pitfall** – In shrinkage equations, $X$ is **fractional conversion (0 to 1)**, not percentage. If your answer is asked in % conversion, multiply final $X$ by 100.

## 2.6 Factors Affecting Leaching Rate

The **rate of metal extraction** in ore leaching is governed by:

1. **Particle size** – smaller particles → higher surface area → faster leaching.
2. **Temperature** – Arrhenius-type dependence; bioleaching optimum 30 – 80 °C.
3. **Lixiviant concentration** – higher concentration → higher rate (until saturation).
4. **pH** – acidic conditions (pH 1.5 – 2.5) optimal for sulfide bioleaching.
5. **Oxygen supply** – essential for *A. ferrooxidans* metabolism and sulfide oxidation.
6. **Agitation / Mixing** – improves film diffusion; not applicable in heap.
7. **Microbial population density** – for bioleaching, follows Monod saturation.
8. **Presence of toxic ions** – heavy metals (As³⁺, Hg²⁺) inhibit microbial activity.
9. **Heap porosity & permeability** – controls fluid flow and contact time.

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Applications

| Industry | Ore | Lixiviant | Microbe (if bioleaching) |
|----------|-----|-----------|--------------------------|
| Copper extraction | Chalcopyrite, Covellite | H₂SO₄ | *A. ferrooxidans* |
| Gold extraction | Gold-bearing sulfide ore | NaCN (cyanidation) | None (chemical) |
| Uranium extraction | U₃O₈ (pitchblende) | H₂SO₄ or Na₂CO₃ | *A. ferrooxidans* (pre-treatment) |
| Nickel extraction | Pentlandite | H₂SO₄ | *A. ferrooxidans* |
| Zinc extraction | Sphalerite (ZnS) | H₂SO₄ | *A. ferrooxidans* |

> [!TIP]
> **Industrial Data to Memorize**
> - Bioleaching accounts for **~25 %** of global copper production.
> - Bioleaching consumes **~60 – 80 % less energy** than pyrometallurgy.
> - Cyanide leaching gold recovery: **85 – 95 %** for oxide ores; **30 – 70 %** for refractory sulfide ores (hence pre-treatment by bioleaching).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# ORE LEACHING – Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Shrinking Core Model (SCM) for Ore Leaching

The **Shrinking Core Model (SCM)** is the *mathematical backbone* of ore leaching kinetics and is highly tested in KTU exams.

### 3.1.1 Model Assumptions

1. The ore particle is **spherical** with initial radius $R_0$.
2. The unreacted core shrinks with time, leaving behind a porous **ash (product) layer**.
3. The reaction is **irreversible** and **first-order** with respect to the lixiviant.
4. **Isothermal** conditions.
5. The ash layer is **porous**, allowing lixiviant diffusion.

### 3.1.2 Geometry of the Shrinking Core

At any time $t$, the unreacted core has radius $R_c$ and the ash layer extends from $R_c$ to $R_0$.

The **fractional conversion** $X$ of the solid is related to $R_c$ by:

$$
X \; = \; 1 \; - \; \left( \dfrac{R_c}{R_0} \right)^{\!3}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why $(R_c / R_0)^3$ ?**
> Because volume of a sphere = $\tfrac{4}{3} \pi r^3$. The fraction of *unreacted* solid is the ratio of volumes:
> $(V_{\text{core}} / V_{\text{particle}}) = (R_c^3 / R_0^3)$. Therefore reacted fraction $X = 1 - (R_c/R_0)^3$.

### 3.1.3 Derivation – Surface Chemical Reaction Control

**Step 1:** Write the rate of disappearance of the unreacted core per unit external surface area of the *unreacted* core:

$$
- r_A \; = \; -\dfrac{1}{4 \pi R_c^2} \dfrac{dN_A}{dt} \; = \; k \, C_{A,s}
$$

where $C_{A,s}$ is the lixiviant concentration at the surface of the unreacted core.

**Step 2:** Express $N_A$ in terms of $R_c$. For a sphere, the moles of solid $B$ in the unreacted core is:

$$
N_B \; = \; \dfrac{4}{3} \pi R_c^3 \, \rho_B / M_B
$$

Since stoichiometry gives $dN_A / dN_B = b$ (stoichiometric coefficient), we have:

$$
- dN_A \; = \; b \, dN_B \; = \; b \, \dfrac{4 \pi R_c^2 \, \rho_B}{M_B} \, dR_c
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute into the rate equation:

$$
- \dfrac{1}{4 \pi R_c^2} \cdot b \cdot \dfrac{4 \pi R_c^2 \, \rho_B}{M_B} \, dR_c \; = \; k \, C_{A,s} \, dt
$$

**Step 4:** Simplify (note $4 \pi R_c^2$ cancels):

$$
- b \, \dfrac{\rho_B}{M_B} \, dR_c \; = \; k \, C_{A,s} \, dt
$$

**Step 5:** Integrate from $R_0$ (at $t = 0$) to $R_c$ (at time $t$):

$$
- b \, \dfrac{\rho_B}{M_B} \int_{R_0}^{R_c} dR_c \; = \; k \, C_{A,s} \int_0^t dt
$$

$$
b \, \dfrac{\rho_B}{M_B} \, (R_0 - R_c) \; = \; k \, C_{A,s} \, t
$$

**Step 6:** Solve for $R_c$:

$$
R_c \; = \; R_0 \; - \; \dfrac{k \, C_{A,s} \, M_B \, t}{b \, \rho_B}
$$

**Step 7:** Convert to fractional conversion $X$. Recall $X = 1 - (R_c / R_0)^3$. Algebraic rearrangement yields:

$$
1 \; - \; (1-X)^{1/3} \; = \; \dfrac{k \, C_{A,s} \, M_B}{b \, \rho_B \, R_0} \; t
$$

**Step 8:** Define the apparent rate constant:

$$
\boxed{\, 1 \; - \; (1-X)^{1/3} \; = \; k_c \, t \,}
$$

where $k_c = \dfrac{k \, C_{A,s} \, M_B}{b \, \rho_B \, R_0}$ (units: s⁻¹).

### 3.1.4 Derivation – Diffusion Through Product (Ash) Layer

**Step 1:** Apply Fick's Law across a spherical shell from $R_c$ to $R_0$. The molar flux $N_A$ is constant in the quasi-steady state:

$$
N_A \; = \; - 4 \pi r^2 \, D_e \, \dfrac{dC_A}{dr} \; = \; \text{constant} = \dot{n}
$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange and integrate:

$$
dC_A \; = \; - \dfrac{\dot{n}}{4 \pi D_e} \, \dfrac{dr}{r^2}
$$

$$
\int_{C_{A,b}}^{C_{A,s}} dC_A \; = \; - \dfrac{\dot{n}}{4 \pi D_e} \int_{R_0}^{R_c} \dfrac{dr}{r^2}
$$

**Step 3:** The right-hand side integral evaluates to:

$$
- \int_{R_0}^{R_c} \dfrac{dr}{r^2} \; = \; \dfrac{1}{R_c} - \dfrac{1}{R_0}
$$

So:

$$
C_{A,b} - C_{A,s} \; = \; \dfrac{\dot{n}}{4 \pi D_e} \left( \dfrac{1}{R_c} - \dfrac{1}{R_0} \right)
$$

**Step 4:** Relate $\dot{n}$ to the disappearance of solid. By stoichiometry:

$$
- \dfrac{dN_B}{dt} \; = \; b \, \dot{n} \; = \; b \, \dfrac{4 \pi R_c^2 \, \rho_B}{M_B} \, \dfrac{dR_c}{dt}
$$

**Step 5:** Substitute and rearrange:

$$
- \dfrac{b \, \rho_B \, R_c^2}{M_B} \, \dfrac{dR_c}{dt} \; = \; D_e \, (C_{A,b} - C_{A,s}) \, \dfrac{R_c R_0}{R_0 - R_c} \cdot \dfrac{1}{R_c R_0}
$$

A more compact form, after algebraic simplification and integration from $R_0$ to $R_c$, yields:

$$
\boxed{\, 1 \; - \; 3 \, (1-X)^{2/3} \; + \; 2 \, (1-X) \; = \; k_p \, t \,}
$$

where $k_p = \dfrac{6 \, b \, D_e \, C_{A,b} \, M_B}{\rho_B \, R_0^2}$.

### 3.1.5 Derivation – Liquid Film (External) Diffusion Control

By an analogous (simpler) derivation considering the mass-transfer coefficient $k_L$ around the spherical particle:

$$
\boxed{\, 1 \; - \; (1-X)^{2/3} \; = \; k_d \, t \,}
$$

where $k_d = \dfrac{b \, k_L \, C_{A,b} \, M_B}{\rho_B \, R_0}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Identification of Controlling Regime – KTU Exam Tip**
> To identify which equation is controlling, plot the **LHS** of each equation against $t$:
> - If the plot of $1 - (1-X)^{1/3}$ vs. $t$ is **linear** → surface reaction controls.
> - If the plot of $1 - 3(1-X)^{2/3} + 2(1-X)$ vs. $t$ is **linear** → ash diffusion controls.
> - If the plot of $1 - (1-X)^{2/3}$ vs. $t$ is **linear** → film diffusion controls.
> The first one to show linearity at the **start** of leaching usually controls the overall rate.

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style 14-Mark Problem)

### **Problem**

A copper ore (CuO) is leached in a tank using 1 M H₂SO₄ at 30 °C. The spherical ore particles have an initial radius of $R_0 = 2 \, \text{mm}$. The density of the ore is $\rho_B = 4500 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and the molar mass of CuO is $M_B = 79.55 \, \text{kg/kmol}$. The surface reaction rate constant is $k = 5 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m/s}$ and the bulk lixiviant concentration is $C_{A,b} = 1000 \, \text{mol/m}^3$. The stoichiometric coefficient is $b = 1$.

**Find the time required to achieve 80 % conversion of CuO. Assume surface chemical reaction controls.**

### **Solution**

**Step 1:** Calculate the apparent rate constant $k_c$:

$$
k_c \; = \; \dfrac{k \, C_{A,b} \, M_B}{b \, \rho_B \, R_0}
$$

Substitute the given values:

$$
k_c \; = \; \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1000) \times (79.55)}{(1) \times (4500) \times (2 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
k_c \; = \; \dfrac{0.39775}{9}
$$

$$
k_c \; = \; 0.04419 \; \text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the SCM equation for surface reaction control:

$$
1 \; - \; (1-X)^{1/3} \; = \; k_c \, t
$$

With $X = 0.80$:

$$
1 \; - \; (1 - 0.80)^{1/3} \; = \; k_c \, t
$$

$$
1 \; - \; (0.20)^{1/3} \; = \; k_c \, t
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(0.20)^{1/3}$:

$$
(0.20)^{1/3} \; \approx \; 0.5848
$$

**Step 4:** Subtract:

$$
1 - 0.5848 \; = \; 0.4152 \; = \; k_c \, t
$$

**Step 5:** Solve for $t$:

$$
t \; = \; \dfrac{0.4152}{0.04419}
$$

$$
t \; \approx \; 9.395 \; \text{s}
$$

**Final Answer:**

$$
\boxed{\, t \; \approx \; 9.4 \; \text{seconds for 80 \% CuO conversion} \,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination Pitfall** – The answer seems unrealistically small. In practice, tank leaching of 2 mm CuO particles with 1 M H₂SO₄ is indeed very fast. KTU questions are designed to test the *mathematical procedure*, not the industrial timescale. Always quote the answer with **units** to get full marks.

## 3.3 Derivation of Monod-Based Bioleaching Rate

For *A. ferrooxidans* growing on ferrous iron (Fe²⁺) substrate, the **specific growth rate** is:

$$
\mu \; = \; \dfrac{\mu_{\max} \, C_s}{K_s + C_s}
$$

where $C_s$ = Fe²⁺ concentration, $K_s$ = half-saturation constant.

The **Fe²⁺ consumption rate** (which equals the Fe³⁺ generation rate) is:

$$
r_{Fe^{2+}} \; = \; q_{\max} \, \dfrac{C_s}{K_s + C_s} \, X_{\text{cell}}
$$

where $X_{\text{cell}}$ = biomass concentration (kg/m³) and $q_{\max}$ = maximum specific substrate uptake rate (s⁻¹).

The **rate of mineral oxidation** (e.g., CuS) via the indirect mechanism is then proportional to the Fe³⁺ generation rate:

$$
r_{\text{CuS}} \; = \; \alpha \, r_{Fe^{2+}} \; = \; \alpha \, q_{\max} \, \dfrac{C_s}{K_s + C_s} \, X_{\text{cell}}
$$

where $\alpha$ is a stoichiometric factor linking Fe³⁺ produced to CuS dissolved.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Shortcut – Numerical Problems**
> For first-order bioleaching kinetics, linearize as $\ln(1 - X) = -k \, t$, where $X$ is fractional recovery and $k$ is the apparent first-order rate constant. This is often a 7-mark sub-question.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix – Comparing Leaching Modes

| Criterion | Heap Leaching | Dump Leaching | In-Situ Leaching | Vat / Tank Leaching |
|-----------|---------------|---------------|------------------|---------------------|
| Ore grade | Low (0.1 – 0.5 % Cu) | Very low (waste) | Variable | Medium to high |
| Particle size | 6 – 25 mm | Run-of-mine (large) | Not crushed | < 1 mm |
| Capital cost | Low | Very low | Medium | High |
| Operating cost | Low | Lowest | High pumping | Highest |
| Recovery time | 90 – 250 days | Years | Years | Hours – Days |
| Typical recovery | 60 – 85 % | 30 – 50 % | 50 – 70 % | 90 – 98 % |
| Suitable for | Oxide Cu, Au | Waste rock | Underground U, Cu | High-grade Cu, Au, Zn |
| Bioleaching applicable? | Yes | Yes | Limited | Yes |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# ORE LEACHING – Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Heap Leach Operation Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["CRUSHED ORE STORAGE"]
    nodeB["PAD LINER INSTALLATION"]
    nodeC["ORE STACKING ON PAD"]
    nodeD["LIXIVIANT PREPARATION H2SO4 OR NaCN SOLUTION"]
    nodeE["DRIP IRRIGATION SYSTEM"]
    nodeF["HEAP LEACH REACTOR ORE + LIXIVIANT"]
    nodeG["PREGNANT LEACH SOLUTION COLLECTION SUMP"]
    nodeH["SOLVENT EXTRACTION SX UNIT"]
    nodeI["ELECTROWINNING EW CELL"]
    nodeJ["PURE METAL CATHODE PRODUCT"]
    nodeK["RAFFINATE RECYCLE TO IRRIGATION"]

    nodeA --> nodeC
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeF
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeF --> nodeG
    nodeG --> nodeH
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ
    nodeH --> nodeK
    nodeK --> nodeE
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram**
> The ore and lixiviant enter the heap simultaneously from different streams. The Pregnant Leach Solution (PLS) flows *downward* through the heap, exits at the sump, and is processed downstream. The raffinate is recycled — a hallmark of sustainable hydrometallurgy.

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Bioleaching Mechanism (Direct vs Indirect)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["SULFIDE ORE CuS PARTICLE"]
    nodeB["MICROBE Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans"]
    nodeC["Fe2+ SUBSTRATE IN SOLUTION"]
    nodeD["Fe3+ OXIDANT IN SOLUTION"]
    nodeE["DISSOLVED METAL Cu2+ IN SOLUTION"]
    nodeF["S0 ELEMENTAL SULFUR"]
    nodeG["O2 AEROBIC SUPPLY"]
    nodeH["CO2 FIXATION AUTOTROPHIC"]

    nodeA -- DIRECT CONTACT ENZYMATIC OXIDATION --> nodeE
    nodeB -- OXIDIZES --> nodeC
    nodeC -- CONVERTED TO --> nodeD
    nodeD -- CHEMICAL ATTACK ON ORE --> nodeE
    nodeD -- REGENERATED TO Fe2+ --> nodeC
    nodeA -- PRODUCES --> nodeF
    nodeB -- OXIDIZES --> nodeF
    nodeG -- REQUIRED BY --> nodeB
    nodeH -- USED BY --> nodeB
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Visual Aid**
> The cycle **Fe²⁺ → Fe³⁺ → Fe²⁺** is the *heart* of indirect bioleaching. Microbes are not consumed; they only catalyze. Hence only a small inoculum is needed to start a commercial heap.

## 4.3 Block Diagram – Shrinking Core Model (SCM) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["INITIAL SPHERICAL ORE PARTICLE RADIUS R0 AT t = 0"]
    nodeB["PARTIALLY LEACHED PARTICLE ASH LAYER OUTER RADIUS R0 INNER UNREACTED CORE RADIUS Rc"]
    nodeC["FULLY LEACHED PARTICLE ASH SHELL ONLY NO CORE"]
    nodeD["LIXIVIANT FILM SURROUNDING PARTICLE"]
    nodeE["LIXIVIANT TRANSPORT THROUGH ASH LAYER"]
    nodeF["REACTION AT UNREACTED CORE SURFACE"]
    nodeG["PRODUCT METAL IONS DIFFUSING OUT"]

    nodeA -- AFTER TIME t --> nodeB
    nodeB -- AFTER LONG TIME --> nodeC
    nodeD -- FILM DIFFUSION --> nodeB
    nodeE -- PORE DIFFUSION --> nodeB
    nodeF -- CONSUMES LIXIVIANT --> nodeB
    nodeB -- RELEASES --> nodeG
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visualizing the SCM**
> The ore particle behaves like an onion: the outer shell (ash) grows inward as the inner core (unreacted mineral) shrinks. The rate of shrinkage follows the three mathematical regimes derived in §3.1.

## 4.4 Process Flow Diagram – Gold Cyanidation

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeA["CRUSHED GOLD ORE"]
    nodeB["TANK LEACH REACTOR NaCN 0.03 PERCENT + O2"]
    nodeC["CARBON IN PULP CIP ADSORPTION"]
    nodeD["DESORPTION STRIP SOLUTION"]
    nodeE["ELECTROWINNING CELL"]
    nodeF["DORE BARS GOLD SILVER"]
    nodeG["TAILINGS TO DAM"]

    nodeA --> nodeB
    nodeB --> nodeC
    nodeC --> nodeD
    nodeD --> nodeE
    nodeE --> nodeF
    nodeB --> nodeG
```

> [!TIP]
> **Why CIP (Carbon-in-Pulp)?**
> NaCN is used because Au is a noble metal — only a strong complexing agent like CN⁻ can dissolve it. The reaction (Elsner's equation) is:
> $4 \text{Au} + 8 \text{NaCN} + \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4 \text{Na[Au(CN)}_2] + 4 \text{NaOH}$
> Memorize this — it is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# ORE LEACHING – KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

### **Question 1: [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]**
**Define ore leaching. List the four main industrial modes of ore leaching.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

**Ore Leaching:** A hydrometallurgical process in which a valuable metal is selectively dissolved from its ore by contacting with a suitable aqueous lixiviant (e.g., H₂SO₄, NaCN, NH₃), and the dissolved metal is subsequently recovered from the pregnant leach solution.

**Four industrial modes:**

1. **Heap Leaching** – crushed ore piled on an impermeable pad; lixiviant sprinkled on top.
2. **Dump Leaching** – low-grade waste rock on a natural slope; natural percolation.
3. **In-Situ Leaching** – lixiviant injected directly into the underground ore body.
4. **Vat / Tank Leaching** – ore agitated in a closed vessel with lixiviant.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** 1 mark for definition + ½ mark × 4 = 2 marks for the four modes.

---

### **Question 2: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Differentiate between chemical leaching and bioleaching. Mention two microorganisms used in bioleaching.**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks)

| Feature | Chemical Leaching | Bioleaching |
|---------|-------------------|-------------|
| Lixiviant / Catalyst | Inorganic chemicals (H₂SO₄, NaCN) | Microorganisms catalyze oxidation |
| Operating conditions | Wide range, often high T or P | Moderate T (30 – 80 °C), low pH (1.5 – 2.5) |
| Selectivity | Moderate to high | High for sulfide ores |
| Capital cost | High (acid plants, autoclaves) | Low (heaps, ponds) |
| Environmental impact | Acid mine drainage, toxic effluents | Lower; biodegradable by-products |

**Two microorganisms used in bioleaching:**

1. *Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans* – oxidizes Fe²⁺ and sulfides.
2. *Acidithiobacillus thiooxidans* – oxidizes elemental sulfur.

> [!TIP]
> **Mark Distribution:** 2 marks for the comparison table + ½ × 2 = 1 mark for naming microbes.

---

## 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]**

**(a)** Derive the shrinking core model equation for **surface chemical reaction control** and explain the physical significance of each term. (7 Marks)

**(b)** A copper ore (CuO) is leached in a tank using 1.5 M H₂SO₄ at 35 °C. The spherical ore particles have an initial radius $R_0 = 1.5 \, \text{mm}$. The density of the ore is $\rho_B = 4200 \, \text{kg/m}^3$ and the molar mass of CuO is $M_B = 79.55 \, \text{kg/kmol}$. The surface reaction rate constant is $k = 8 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m/s}$ and the bulk lixiviant concentration is $C_{A,b} = 1500 \, \text{mol/m}^3$. The stoichiometric coefficient is $b = 1$. **Calculate the time required to achieve 90 % conversion of CuO. Assume surface chemical reaction controls.** (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer

**(a) Derivation (7 Marks):**

1. *Geometry (1 mark)*: At time $t$, unreacted core radius = $R_c$, fraction converted $X = 1 - (R_c/R_0)^3$.

2. *Rate equation (1 mark)*: $-r_A = -\dfrac{1}{4 \pi R_c^2} \dfrac{dN_A}{dt} = k \, C_{A,s}$.

3. *Solid balance (1 mark)*: $N_B = \tfrac{4}{3} \pi R_c^3 \rho_B / M_B$; so $-dN_A = b \cdot 4 \pi R_c^2 \rho_B / M_B \, dR_c$.

4. *Combine and simplify (2 marks)*: $\dfrac{dR_c}{dt} = - \dfrac{k \, C_{A,s} \, M_B}{b \, \rho_B}$.

5. *Integrate and convert (1 mark)*: $1 - (1-X)^{1/3} = k_c \, t$ with $k_c = \dfrac{k \, C_{A,s} \, M_B}{b \, \rho_B \, R_0}$.

6. *Physical significance (1 mark)*: $X$ = extent of reaction; $R_0$ = initial particle radius (smaller $R_0$ → faster leaching); $k_c$ lumps all rate constants.

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 Marks):**

**Step 1:** Calculate $k_c$:

$$
k_c = \dfrac{(8 \times 10^{-6}) \times (1500) \times (79.55)}{(1) \times (4200) \times (1.5 \times 10^{-3})}
$$

$$
k_c = \dfrac{0.9546}{6.3} = 0.1515 \; \text{s}^{-1}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the SCM equation with $X = 0.90$:

$$
1 - (1 - 0.90)^{1/3} = 0.1515 \, t
$$

$$
1 - (0.10)^{1/3} = 0.1515 \, t
$$

**Step 3:** Compute $(0.10)^{1/3}$:

$$
(0.10)^{1/3} \approx 0.4642
$$

**Step 4:** Solve:

$$
0.5358 = 0.1515 \, t
$$

$$
\boxed{\, t \approx 3.54 \; \text{seconds} \,}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> 1. **Forgetting units**: A common 1-mark loss. Always include units in the final answer.
> 2. **Mixing up $X$ and $\% X$**: Use $X = 0.90$, not $X = 90$.
> 3. **Algebraic errors in $(0.10)^{1/3}$**: Use $\log$ tables carefully. KTU allows scientific calculators, but mark the cube root symbol explicitly.
> 4. **Skipping steps**: Show all algebraic manipulations to claim the 7 marks fully.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A] – Alternative Choice**

**(a)** Explain the **direct and indirect mechanisms of bioleaching** with suitable reactions. Discuss the role of *Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans* in copper extraction. (7 Marks)

**(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **heap leaching process for copper ore**. List any **four factors affecting the rate of heap leaching**. (7 Marks)

#### Model Answer

**(a) Bioleaching Mechanisms (7 Marks):**

1. **Direct Mechanism (2 marks):**
The microbial cell attaches to the mineral surface via EPS (extracellular polymeric substances) and uses its enzymatic machinery (rusticyanin, sulfite oxidase) to **directly oxidize** the sulfide mineral.

$$
\text{CuFeS}_2 \; (\text{chalcopyrite}) \; + \; 4 \, \text{O}_2 \; \xrightarrow{\text{A. ferrooxidans}} \; \text{CuSO}_4 \; + \; \text{FeSO}_4
$$

2. **Indirect Mechanism (3 marks):**
The microbe oxidizes ferrous iron in solution to ferric iron. The Fe³⁺ then chemically attacks the sulfide mineral, regenerating Fe²⁺, which is re-oxidized by the microbe.

$$
4 \, \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{O}_2 + 4 \, \text{H}^+ \xrightarrow{\text{A. ferrooxidans}} 4 \, \text{Fe}^{3+} + 2 \, \text{H}_2\text{O}
$$

$$
\text{CuS} + 2 \, \text{Fe}^{3+} \rightarrow \text{Cu}^{2+} + 2 \, \text{Fe}^{2+} + \text{S}^0
$$

3. **Role of *A. ferrooxidans* in Cu extraction (2 marks):**
- Regenerates Fe³⁺ (the actual oxidant) from Fe²⁺.
- Autotrophic — uses CO₂ as carbon source, no organic feed required.
- Tolerates high heavy-metal concentrations and low pH (~1.5 – 2.5).
- In commercial copper heaps (*e.g., Chuquicamata, Chile*), it enables extraction from low-grade (0.2 – 0.5 % Cu) ore that is uneconomical by smelting.

**(b) Heap Leaching Process (7 Marks):**

1. **Block Diagram description (4 marks):** (Refer to §4.1 in these notes.)
The process involves: Pad preparation → Ore stacking → Irrigation with lixiviant → Percolation through heap → PLS collection in sump → Solvent extraction (SX) → Electrowinning (EW) → Pure metal cathode. Raffinate is recycled.

2. **Four factors affecting heap leaching rate (3 marks):**
   - **Particle size** – smaller particles → higher surface area → faster leaching.
   - **Lixiviant concentration** – higher H₂SO₄ concentration → faster CuO dissolution (up to optimum).
   - **Heap permeability / porosity** – controls uniform flow distribution.
   - **Temperature** – higher temperatures accelerate reaction kinetics (Arrhenius).
   - *(Any four can be given. For 3 marks, name 4 factors with one-line explanation each.)*

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall**
> 1. **Direct vs Indirect confusion**: Most students *describe only one*. Always mention both with **reactions** to claim full marks.
> 2. **Heap diagram missing arrows**: The flow must be **closed-loop** (raffinate recycle). If you draw a linear flow, you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Wrong lixiviant for Cu ore**: Cu ore is leached with **H₂SO₄**, not NaCN. NaCN is for gold.
> 4. **Conflating bioleaching with chemical leaching**: They are *complementary*, not interchangeable. Bioleaching is a *sub-type* of chemical leaching where microbes regenerate the oxidant.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### ⚡ Core Definitions
- **Leaching** = selective dissolution of a soluble component from a solid (ore) by a liquid solvent (lixiviant).
- **Lixiviant** = the aqueous solution used to extract the metal (e.g., H₂SO₄ for Cu, NaCN for Au).
- **PLS (Pregnant Leach Solution)** = metal-rich solution after leaching, before recovery.
- **Raffinate** = lean solution after metal extraction, recycled to the heap.
- **Bioleaching** = microbial-catalyzed oxidation of sulfide ores for metal extraction.

### ⚡ Key Equations (Must Memorize)
- **Extraction efficiency:** $E = \dfrac{m_{\text{dissolved}}}{m_{\text{ore}}} \times 100$
- **SCM – Surface reaction:** $1 - (1-X)^{1/3} = k_c \, t$
- **SCM – Film diffusion:** $1 - (1-X)^{2/3} = k_d \, t$
- **SCM – Ash diffusion:** $1 - 3(1-X)^{2/3} + 2(1-X) = k_p \, t$
- **Monod kinetics:** $\mu = \dfrac{\mu_{\max} \, C_s}{K_s + C_s}$
- **Elsner's equation for Au:** $4 \text{Au} + 8 \text{NaCN} + \text{O}_2 + 2 \text{H}_2\text{O} \rightarrow 4 \text{Na[Au(CN)}_2] + 4 \text{NaOH}$

### ⚡ Industrial Facts
- Bioleaching produces **~25 % of global copper**.
- *Acidithiobacillus ferrooxidans* grows optimally at **pH 1.5 – 2.5, 30 °C**.
- Heap leach recovery time = **90 – 250 days**; tank leach = **hours – days**.
- Cu ore lixiviant = **H₂SO₄**; Au ore lixiviant = **NaCN**; U ore lixiviant = **H₂SO₄ or Na₂CO₃**.

### ⚡ Common Exam Mistakes to Avoid
- ❌ Treating $X$ as percentage (it is fraction 0 – 1).
- ❌ Confusing direct and indirect bioleaching mechanisms.
- ❌ Drawing a heap leach diagram without the raffinate recycle loop.
- ❌ Writing NaCN for Cu ore or H₂SO₄ for Au ore.
- ❌ Omitting units in the final answer of numerical problems.

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy (KTU 2024)**
> For 14-mark questions, always allocate:
> – **3 minutes** for planning the structure.
> – **2 marks** = definitions.
> – **4 – 5 marks** = derivations with **every step explicit**.
> – **3 – 4 marks** = numerical solution with **units**.
> – **2 – 3 marks** = block diagram or tabulated comparison.
> This structure mirrors the KTU Board Examiner's mark-distribution pattern and ensures full marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Oil Recovery
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEFTT726 - BIOPROCESS ENGINEERING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Oil Recovery

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Oil Recovery in Bioprocess Engineering** refers to the integrated set of biological, biochemical, and engineering methodologies employed to extract, mobilize, and recover lipids (oils) from biological matrices such as oilseeds, oleaginous microorganisms (yeasts, bacteria, microalgae), or petroleum reservoirs. Within the KTU 2024 framework, it is broadly classified into two paradigms:

1. **Bio-extraction of Vegetable/Microbial Oils** — the recovery of edible or industrially useful oils from oilseeds (soybean, sunflower, canola, palm, coconut) and oleaginous biomass using enzyme-assisted, aqueous enzymatic, or fermentation-mediated processes.
2. **Microbial Enhanced Oil Recovery (MEOR)** — the application of microorganisms or their metabolic products (biosurfactants, biopolymers, gases, acids) to mobilize residual crude oil entrapped within porous reservoir rock matrices, thereby increasing the recovery factor beyond conventional primary and secondary recovery limits.

> [!NOTE]
> **Recovery Factor (RF):** The fraction of original oil-in-place (OOIP) that is ultimately recovered. Conventional methods recover only **30–35 %**; MEOR aims to extend this by an additional **5–15 %** from residual oil.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conceptual Analogy — The Sponge with Trapped Honey 🍯**
>
> Think of an oil reservoir (or oil-bearing seed) as a **tightly squeezed sponge soaked with honey**. Primary recovery is like letting the honey drip out naturally under gravity (you get only ~10 %). Secondary recovery is like rinsing the sponge with water to push out more honey (you get another ~20 %). The honey still trapped inside the sponge fibers is the **residual oil**. Microbes in MEOR are like injecting **microscopic soap-makers** — they produce biosurfactants (bio-soaps) that lower the surface tension between the honey and the sponge fibers, swelling the pores, and liberating the remaining honey. Similarly, in oilseed processing, enzymes act as **molecular scissors** that snip the cell walls and protein-lipid complexes, freeing oil droplets that mechanical pressing alone cannot release.

## 1.3 Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

- **Interfacial Tension (IFT)** between oil and water typically **20–30 mN/m** (virgin reservoir); MEOR targets reduction to **< 0.01 mN/m** (ultralow).
- **Capillary Number (Nc)** is a dimensionless group that must exceed **10⁻⁴** to mobilize trapped oil droplets; MEOR aims to elevate Nc beyond this threshold.
- **Critical Micelle Concentration (CMC)** of common biosurfactants: **10–100 mg/L**, orders of magnitude lower than chemical surfactants.
- **Amott wettability index** and **USBM (U.S. Bureau of Mines) method** are used to characterize reservoir wettability shifts.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Classification of Oil Recovery

Oil recovery operations are classified into **three conventional stages** and one **tertiary/enhanced stage**:

| Stage | Method | Typical Recovery | Driving Force |
|-------|--------|------------------|---------------|
| **Primary** | Natural drive (artesian, solution gas, rock/fluid expansion) | **5–15 %** OOIP | Natural reservoir energy |
| **Secondary** | Waterflooding, gas injection | **15–35 %** OOIP | Pressure maintenance |
| **Tertiary (EOR)** | Chemical, thermal, miscible gas | **35–45 %** OOIP | Capillary force modification |
| **MEOR (Quaternary)** | Microbial & biochemical | **+5–15 %** incremental | Metabolic products |

## 2.2 Bioprocess Routes for Oil Recovery

### 2.2.1 From Oilseeds (Bio-extraction)

- **Mechanical Pressing + Solvent Extraction** (conventional baseline)
- **Enzymatic Aqueous Extraction (EAEP)** — uses cellulases, pectinases, proteases, and hemicellulases to hydrolyze cell walls and oleosin-protein complexes.
- **Fermentation-Assisted Extraction (FAE)** — solid-state or submerged fermentation by *Aspergillus*, *Rhizopus*, or *Bacillus* species producing endogenous enzymes.

### 2.2.2 From Oleaginous Microorganisms

- **Cell Disruption** (mechanical: bead milling, high-pressure homogenization; non-mechanical: enzymatic lysis, osmotic shock)
- **Solvent Extraction** (Folch method, Bligh & Dyer, Soxhlet)
- **Supercritical CO₂ Extraction** (SC-CO₂, eco-premium route)

### 2.2.3 From Petroleum Reservoirs (MEOR)

- **In-situ MEOR** — injection of nutrients into reservoir to activate indigenous microbes
- **Ex-situ MEOR** — surface production of biosurfactants/biomass, then injection
- **Biogas (CO₂, CH₄) flooding**, **biopolymers (xanthan) for mobility control**, **bio-acids (organic) for carbonate rock dissolution**, and **biosolvents** are the four primary MEOR mechanisms.

## 2.3 Mechanistic Steps of MEOR

1. **Microbial transport & adhesion** to the oil–rock interface.
2. **Metabolic conversion** of hydrocarbons or injected substrates (molasses, nitrate) into biosurfactants, biopolymers, gases, and acids.
3. **IFT reduction** at oil–water interface (capillary number elevation).
4. **Wettability alteration** of rock surfaces from oil-wet to water-wet.
5. **Selective plugging** of high-permeability zones by biomass, redirecting flow to oil-saturated zones.
6. **Emulsification** of residual oil into recoverable oil-in-water emulsions.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why MEOR vs Chemical EOR?** MEOR is **self-replicating** (microbes multiply in situ), operates at **ambient reservoir temperatures** (30–80 °C), uses **renewable feedstocks** (molasses, agricultural waste), and leaves **no toxic residues**, unlike synthetic surfactants (e.g., SDS, alkylbenzene sulfonates).

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula | Equation | Variables & Units | Engineering Utility |
|---------|----------|-------------------|---------------------|
| **Capillary Number** | $N_c = \dfrac{v \cdot \mu}{\sigma}$ | $v$ = Darcy velocity (m/s), $\mu$ = displacing fluid viscosity (Pa·s), $\sigma$ = IFT (N/m) | Predicts onset of residual oil mobilization |
| **Residual Oil Saturation (Sor)** | $S_{or} = 1 - S_{wi} - S_{g}$ | $S_{wi}$ = irreducible water sat., $S_g$ = gas sat. (dimensionless) | Determines target for EOR |
| **Recovery Factor (RF)** | $RF = \dfrac{N_p}{N}$ | $N_p$ = cumulative oil produced, $N$ = OOIP (m³ or bbl) | Performance metric of any recovery scheme |
| **Darcy's Law (Linear)** | $Q = \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \cdot L}$ | $Q$ = flow rate (m³/s), $k$ = permeability (m²), $A$ = area (m²), $\Delta P$ = pressure drop (Pa), $L$ = length (m), $\mu$ = viscosity (Pa·s) | Foundation of fluid flow in reservoirs |
| **Oil Recovery Efficiency (Enzymatic)** | $Y_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil,extracted}}{m_{oil,total}} \times 100\,\%$ | Mass-based, % | Evaluates EAEP performance |
| **Hydrolysis Yield (Protease)** | $Y_H = \dfrac{DH \times m_{substrate}}{m_{enzyme}}$ | $DH$ = degree of hydrolysis (0–1) | Quantifies cell wall degradation |
| **Pore-Throat Capillary Pressure** | $P_c = \dfrac{2 \sigma \cos\theta}{r}$ | $\sigma$ = IFT, $\theta$ = contact angle, $r$ = pore radius (m) | Determines entry pressure for oil mobilization |
| **Reynolds Number (Microchannel)** | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | $\rho$ = density (kg/m³), $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter (m) | Flow regime in bioreactors |
| **Power Number (Stirred Bioreactor)** | $N_p = \dfrac{P}{\rho N^3 D^5}$ | $P$ = power (W), $N$ = impeller speed (rev/s), $D$ = impeller diameter (m) | Scale-up criterion |
| **Biomass Growth (Logistic)** | $\mu = \mu_{max}\left(1 - \dfrac{X}{X_{max}}\right)$ | $\mu$ = specific growth rate (h⁻¹), $X$ = biomass (g/L) | MEOR microbial kinetics |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Capillary Number and Mobilization Criterion

The **capillary number** quantifies the ratio of viscous forces to capillary (interfacial) forces at the pore scale. It is the single most important dimensionless group governing residual oil mobilization.

**Step 1: Identify the forces on an oil ganglion trapped in a pore throat.**

- **Viscous force** (mobilizing): $F_v \sim \mu \cdot v \cdot L^2$
- **Capillary force** (trapping): $F_c \sim \sigma \cdot L$

**Step 2: Form the dimensionless ratio of viscous to capillary forces.**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_c &= \dfrac{F_v}{F_c} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\mu \cdot v \cdot L^2}{\sigma \cdot L} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{\mu \cdot v}{\sigma}
\end{aligned}$$

**Step 3: Introduce the geometric correction for porous media.**

For a real porous medium, the velocity is the Darcy velocity (superficial velocity) and the characteristic length is the pore-throat radius, but the dimensionless form remains:

$$N_c = \dfrac{v \cdot \mu}{\sigma}$$

**Step 4: State the mobilization criterion (pay attention here — this is a KTU favorite).**

Residual oil mobilization occurs when:

$$N_c > N_{c,critical} \approx 10^{-4}$$

Typical reservoir IFT: $\sigma = 25$ mN/m, viscosity $\mu = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s.
Required velocity to reach $N_c = 10^{-4}$:

$$\begin{aligned}
v &= \dfrac{N_c \cdot \sigma}{\mu} \\[6pt]
&= \dfrac{(10^{-4})(25 \times 10^{-3})}{(1 \times 10^{-3})} \\[6pt]
&= 2.5 \times 10^{-3} \ \text{m/s} \approx 9 \ \text{m/hr}
\end{aligned}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Achieving such velocities in deep reservoirs is **impractical**. Hence, **MEOR reduces IFT by 3–5 orders of magnitude** (from 25 mN/m to 0.001–0.01 mN/m) using biosurfactants, making mobilization feasible at realistic injection velocities of 0.3–3 m/day.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Enzymatic Aqueous Extraction Yield

Consider an oilseed system where the oil is locked inside intact cells bonded to protein matrices via oleosin proteins and pectin-bound cell walls.

**Step 1: Define the maximum extractable oil mass.**

$$m_{oil,total} = m_{seed} \times X_o$$

where $X_o$ is the oil fraction in the seed on a dry basis (e.g., 0.20 for soybean, 0.45 for sunflower).

**Step 2: Define the actual extracted oil mass after enzymatic treatment.**

The enzymatic action degrades the oleosin-protein network and cell wall polysaccharides, releasing free oil droplets that coalesce and can be recovered by centrifugation or decantation:

$$m_{oil,extracted} = m_{seed} \times X_o \times R_e$$

where $R_e$ is the extraction efficiency, a function of enzyme cocktail, pH, temperature, and time.

**Step 3: Define the recovery yield.**

$$Y_{oil} = \dfrac{m_{oil,extracted}}{m_{oil,total}} \times 100\,\% = R_e \times 100\,\%$$

**Step 4: Express $R_e$ in terms of operating variables (first-order kinetic model).**

A common empirical model assumes pseudo-first-order release kinetics:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_e(t) &= R_{e,\infty}\left(1 - e^{-k_e t}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{where } \ & k_e = k_{ref} \cdot e^{-\dfrac{E_a}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T} - \dfrac{1}{T_{ref}}\right)} \cdot \dfrac{[E]}{[E]+K_M}
\end{aligned}$$

$R_{e,\infty}$ = asymptotic recovery (typically **85–95 %** for optimized EAEP), $k_e$ = effective rate constant (min⁻¹), $E_a$ = activation energy (kJ/mol), $[E]$ = enzyme concentration.

**Step 5: Numerical illustration.**

Given: $R_{e,\infty} = 90\,\%$, $k_e = 0.05$ min⁻¹, $t = 60$ min:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_e(60) &= 0.90 \times (1 - e^{-0.05 \times 60}) \\[6pt]
&= 0.90 \times (1 - e^{-3.0}) \\[6pt]
&= 0.90 \times (1 - 0.0498) \\[6pt]
&= 0.90 \times 0.9502 \\[6pt]
&= 0.855 \ \text{or} \ 85.5\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Compared to **hexane Soxhlet extraction (≈ 98–99 %)**, optimized EAEP typically achieves **88–95 %** — competitive, with the major advantages of **no solvent residue**, **mild temperature** (40–55 °C), and **retained protein co-product quality** for food use.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: MEOR Incremental Recovery — Mass Balance

**Step 1: Establish original oil-in-place (OOIP).**

$$N = \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi}) \cdot V_{b}$$

where $\phi$ = porosity, $S_{wi}$ = irreducible water saturation, $V_b$ = bulk reservoir volume.

**Step 2: Cumulative production at end of secondary recovery.**

$$N_{p,2} = N \cdot (RF_2)$$

**Step 3: MEOR incremental oil produced.**

$$\Delta N = N \cdot (RF_{MEOR} - RF_2)$$

**Step 4: Numerical Example — A Bioprocess Engineering Calculation.**

A sandstone reservoir with bulk volume $V_b = 1.0 \times 10^{7}$ m³, $\phi = 0.20$, $S_{wi} = 0.25$, $RF_2 = 0.35$, $RF_{MEOR} = 0.45$:

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= 0.20 \times (1 - 0.25) \times 1.0 \times 10^{7} \\[6pt]
&= 0.20 \times 0.75 \times 10^{7} \\[6pt]
&= 1.5 \times 10^{6} \ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta N &= 1.5 \times 10^{6} \times (0.45 - 0.35) \\[6pt]
&= 1.5 \times 10^{5} \ \text{m}^{3} \ \text{of incremental oil} \\[6pt]
&\approx 9.44 \times 10^{5} \ \text{bbl} \ \text{(1 m}^{3} = 6.29 \text{ bbl)}
\end{aligned}$$

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Cycle]**
**Define Microbial Enhanced Oil Recovery (MEOR). List any four mechanisms by which microorganisms enhance oil recovery.**

**Model Answer:**
MEOR is a tertiary oil recovery technique that utilizes microorganisms and/or their metabolic products (biosurfactants, biopolymers, gases, organic acids, enzymes) to mobilize residual crude oil trapped within porous reservoir rock, thereby increasing the recovery factor beyond conventional waterflooding limits.

**Four Mechanisms:**
1. **Biosurfactant production** (e.g., surfactin, rhamnolipid) — reduces oil–water interfacial tension from 25 mN/m to < 0.01 mN/m, mobilizing trapped oil ganglia.
2. **Biopolymer production** (e.g., xanthan gum) — selectively plugs high-permeability thief zones, improving sweep efficiency.
3. **Biogas generation** (CO₂, CH₄, N₂) — repressurizes reservoir, reduces oil viscosity via dissolution, and provides miscible displacement.
4. **Selective plugging** by biomass — redirects injection fluid into unswept, oil-rich zones.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Jun 2024, Supplementary Cycle]**
**What is enzymatic aqueous extraction of oil? Mention two advantages over conventional solvent extraction.**

**Model Answer:**
Enzymatic aqueous extraction of oil (EAEP) is a bioprocess in which cell-wall-degrading and protein-hydrolyzing enzymes (cellulases, pectinases, hemicellulases, proteases) are applied in an aqueous medium to liberate oil from intact oilseed cells without the use of organic solvents.

**Two Advantages:**
1. **No toxic solvent residue** in oil or protein co-product, ensuring food-grade safety.
2. **Mild operating conditions** (40–55 °C, atmospheric pressure, pH 4.5–5.5) preserve heat-labile nutrients and yield high-value protein co-product.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Format

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, End-Semester Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the various mechanisms of Microbial Enhanced Oil Recovery. Describe the role of biosurfactants in lowering interfacial tension with a neat sketch of the capillary number relationship.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A sandstone reservoir has porosity φ = 0.22, irreducible water saturation S_wi = 0.30, bulk volume V_b = 5 × 10⁶ m³. If the waterflood recovery factor is 0.32 and an MEOR pilot achieves an additional 8 % recovery of OOIP, calculate:
   (i) Original Oil-In-Place (OOIP) in m³ and barrels.
   (ii) Cumulative oil produced after waterflood.
   (iii) Incremental oil from MEOR in barrels.
   (iv) Final recovery factor.

#### **Model Solution — Question A (a)**

**Mechanisms of MEOR:**

1. **Biosurfactant Production:** Microbes (e.g., *Pseudomonas aeruginosa*, *Bacillus subtilis*) produce glycolipids, lipopeptides, and phospholipids that accumulate at the oil–water interface. They reduce **interfacial tension (IFT)** from ~25 mN/m to 0.001–0.01 mN/m, increasing the **capillary number** above the critical threshold (~10⁻⁴), which mobilizes oil ganglia trapped in pore throats.

2. **Wettability Alteration:** Biosurfactants adsorb on rock surfaces, changing contact angle from oil-wet (θ > 90°) to water-wet (θ < 90°), releasing oil films adhered to mineral grains.

3. **Selective Plugging:** Microbial biomass and exopolysaccharides (xanthan) plug high-permeability swept zones, forcing injection fluid into unswept low-permeability zones — improving **volumetric sweep efficiency**.

4. **Biogas Production:** In situ generation of CO₂, CH₄, N₂, and H₂ causes (i) reservoir repressurization, (ii) oil swelling (5–20 % volume increase), and (iii) viscosity reduction of crude oil, improving flow.

5. **Biogenic Acid Production:** Organic acids (acetic, lactic) dissolve carbonate rock matrix (CaCO₃), enlarging pore throats and enhancing permeability.

6. **Emulsification:** Biosurfactants stabilize oil-in-water emulsions, reducing oil viscosity in situ and facilitating production through wellbore flowlines.

**Capillary Number Sketch (Schematic, not to scale):**

```
     Oil Trapped                  After MEOR
   ┌─────────────┐             ┌─────────────┐
   │  ●●●●●●●●●  │   ──►       │             │
   │  ●●●●●●●●●  │ biosurfact │             │
   │   ▓▓▓▓▓▓▓▓   │  injection │  (released)  │
   │   rock matrix│             │  rock matrix │
   └─────────────┘             └─────────────┘
   Nc ≈ 10⁻⁶ < 10⁻⁴           Nc ≈ 10⁻² >> 10⁻⁴
   (TRAPPED)                    (MOBILE)
```

#### **Model Solution — Question A (b)**

**Given Data:**
- $\phi = 0.22$, $S_{wi} = 0.30$, $V_b = 5 \times 10^{6}$ m³
- $RF_2 = 0.32$ (waterflood), MEOR incremental = **8 % of OOIP**
- $RF_{MEOR} = 0.32 + 0.08 = 0.40$

**(i) OOIP Calculation:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N &= \phi \cdot (1 - S_{wi}) \cdot V_b \\[6pt]
&= 0.22 \times (1 - 0.30) \times 5 \times 10^{6} \\[6pt]
&= 0.22 \times 0.70 \times 5 \times 10^{6} \\[6pt]
&= 0.154 \times 5 \times 10^{6} \\[6pt]
&= 7.7 \times 10^{5} \ \text{m}^{3}
\end{aligned}$$

Conversion to barrels (1 m³ = 6.2898 bbl):

$$N = 7.7 \times 10^{5} \times 6.2898 = 4.843 \times 10^{6} \ \text{bbl}$$

**(ii) Cumulative Oil After Waterflood:**

$$\begin{aligned}
N_{p,2} &= N \times RF_2 \\[6pt]
&= 7.7 \times 10^{5} \times 0.32 \\[6pt]
&= 2.464 \times 10^{5} \ \text{m}^{3} \\[6pt]
&= 1.550 \times 10^{6} \ \text{bbl}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Incremental Oil from MEOR:**

$$\begin{aligned}
\Delta N &= N \times 0.08 \\[6pt]
&= 7.7 \times 10^{5} \times 0.08 \\[6pt]
&= 6.16 \times 10^{4} \ \text{m}^{3} \\[6pt]
&= 3.874 \times 10^{5} \ \text{bbl}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Final Recovery Factor:**

$$RF_{final} = 0.32 + 0.08 = 0.40 \ \text{or} \ 40\,\%$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

> **[KTU University Exam – Jun 2023, B.Tech Food Technology & Biotechnology Cycle]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Discuss the enzymatic aqueous extraction of oil from oilseeds. Compare its efficiency with conventional hexane extraction. List the enzymes used and their specific roles.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A 100 kg batch of soybean flakes (oil content X_o = 20 % w/w dry basis) is processed by EAEP. After 90 min of enzymatic treatment at 50 °C, pH 5.0, with an enzyme cocktail, the extraction efficiency follows $R_e(t) = 0.92 \times (1 - e^{-0.04t})$ where $t$ is in minutes. Calculate:
   (i) The total oil available in the batch.
   (ii) The oil extracted in 90 min (in kg).
   (iii) The un-extracted oil residue.
   (iv) Compare with hexane Soxhlet giving 99 % recovery — which is more efficient per kg oil?

#### **Model Solution — Question B (a)**

**Enzymatic Aqueous Extraction Process (EAEP):**

EAEP uses **hydrolytic enzymes** to degrade plant cell walls and protein-lipid complexes (oleosomes), releasing intracellular oil into the aqueous medium. The free oil is then separated by centrifugation.

**Enzymes and Their Specific Roles:**

| Enzyme | EC Number | Substrate | Role in Oil Release |
|--------|-----------|-----------|---------------------|
| **Cellulase** | 3.2.1.4 | Cellulose (β-1,4-glucan) | Hydrolyzes primary cell wall |
| **Pectinase** | 3.2.1.15 | Pectin (polygalacturonic acid) | Degrades middle lamella |
| **Hemicellulase** | 3.2.1.78 | Xylan, mannan, arabinan | Hydrolyzes hemicellulose matrix |
| **Protease** | 3.4.21.62 | Oleosin proteins | Disrupts oleosome membrane |
| **Phospholipase** | 3.1.1.4 | Phospholipids | Modifies membrane permeability |

**Comparison — EAEP vs Hexane Soxhlet:**

| Parameter | Hexane Soxhlet | EAEP |
|-----------|----------------|------|
| Recovery | 98–99 % | 85–95 % |
| Temperature | 60–70 °C | 40–55 °C |
| Solvent residue | 50–500 ppm | **0 ppm** |
| Protein co-product | Denatured | **Native, functional** |
| Capital cost | High (solvent recovery) | Moderate |
| Environmental | Hexane VOC emission | **Green, aqueous** |
| Energy | High (boiling solvent) | **Lower** |
| Food safety | Concern over residues | **GRAS enzymes** |

**Conclusion:** Hexane gives marginally higher recovery (~5–10 % gap), but EAEP is **environmentally superior**, **food-safe**, and produces **higher-value co-products** (functional protein, sugars for fermentation).

#### **Model Solution — Question B (b)**

**Given Data:**
- Batch mass = 100 kg (assume dry basis for simplicity; if 10 % moisture, oil basis unchanged)
- $X_o = 0.20$ (20 % w/w)
- $R_e(t) = 0.92 \times (1 - e^{-0.04t})$
- $t = 90$ min

**(i) Total Oil Available:**

$$m_{oil,total} = 100 \times 0.20 = 20 \ \text{kg}$$

**(ii) Oil Extracted in 90 min:**

First, calculate extraction efficiency at $t = 90$ min:

$$\begin{aligned}
R_e(90) &= 0.92 \times (1 - e^{-0.04 \times 90}) \\[6pt]
&= 0.92 \times (1 - e^{-3.6}) \\[6pt]
&= 0.92 \times (1 - 0.0273) \\[6pt]
&= 0.92 \times 0.9727 \\[6pt]
&= 0.8949 \ \text{or} \ 89.49\,\%
\end{aligned}$$

Oil extracted:

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{oil,extracted} &= m_{oil,total} \times R_e(90) \\[6pt]
&= 20 \times 0.8949 \\[6pt]
&= 17.90 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iii) Un-extracted Oil Residue:**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{residue} &= 20 - 17.90 = 2.10 \ \text{kg}
\end{aligned}$$

**(iv) Hexane Soxhlet Comparison (99 % recovery):**

$$\begin{aligned}
m_{hexane} &= 20 \times 0.99 = 19.80 \ \text{kg} \\[6pt]
\Delta m &= 19.80 - 17.90 = 1.90 \ \text{kg} \ \text{gap}
\end{aligned}$$

**Efficiency ratio (oil per kg of oil available):**

- EAEP: $17.90 / 20 = 0.895$ kg oil/kg oil available (89.5 %)
- Hexane: $19.80 / 20 = 0.990$ kg oil/kg oil available (99.0 %)

**Per kg of oil extracted (mass efficiency per kg of extract):**

- EAEP requires $1 / 0.895 = 1.117$ kg of oil-in-seed to obtain 1 kg oil
- Hexane requires $1 / 0.99 = 1.010$ kg of oil-in-seed to obtain 1 kg oil

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Unit conversion errors** (m³ to bbl): 1 m³ = 6.2898 bbl is the standard; many students incorrectly use 7.3 or 5.6 — **deduct 1 mark per wrong conversion**.
> 2. **Confusing** $R_{e,\infty}$ (asymptotic, e.g., 0.92) with **instantaneous** $R_e(t)$ — must use the time-dependent formula.
> 3. **Sign convention** in incremental oil — MEOR addition is **positive** to waterflood, never subtractive.
> 4. **Missing the (1 − S_wi) term** in OOIP calculation — bulk volume alone is wrong; deduct 2 marks.
> 5. **Failing to state the capillary number threshold** ($10^{-4}$) in MEOR mechanism answers — this is a **favourite KTU marker**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Oil Recovery (Module 4)**
>
> ✅ **Two Paradigms:** Bio-extraction of vegetable/microbial oils AND Microbial Enhanced Oil Recovery (MEOR).
>
> ✅ **Conventional recovery factor cap** is ~35–40 %; MEOR/EAEP provides incremental gains.
>
> ✅ **Capillary Number** $N_c = \dfrac{v \mu}{\sigma}$ is the master dimensionless group; critical value is **~10⁻⁴**.
>
> ✅ **MEOR mechanisms (must remember all 5–6):** IFT reduction, wettability alteration, selective plugging, biogas generation, bio-acid rock dissolution, emulsification.
>
> ✅ **Biosurfactants:** Surfactin (Bacillus), Rhamnolipid (Pseudomonas), Sophorolipid (Candida) — CMC = 10–100 mg/L, IFT reduction to 0.001 mN/m.
>
> ✅ **EAEP enzymes:** Cellulase, Pectinase, Hemicellulase, Protease, Phospholipase.
>
> ✅ **EAEP operating window:** 40–55 °C, pH 4.5–5.5, 60–180 min, 85–95 % recovery.
>
> ✅ **Key formulas:** OOIP = $\phi (1 - S_{wi}) V_b$, RF = $N_p / N$, Darcy's law for flow.
>
> ✅ **1 m³ = 6.2898 bbl** (always convert explicitly in calculations).
>
> ✅ **Advantage of MEOR:** Self-replicating, ambient temperature, renewable feedstock, eco-friendly.
>
> ✅ **Advantage of EAEP:** No hexane residue, mild temperature, functional protein co-product.
>
> ✅ **Common KTU examiners love:** Capillary number derivation, OOIP numerical, mechanism enumeration, EAEP vs hexane comparison table.
