# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Define a biosensor. List its main components with one example each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **biosensor** is an analytical device that integrates a **biological recognition element** with a **physicochemical transducer** to produce a measurable signal proportional to the concentration of an analyte.

**Main Components (any 3 × 1 mark each):**

| Component | Function | Example |
|-----------|----------|---------|
| **Biological Recognition Element** | Selectively binds the analyte | Glucose oxidase enzyme |
| **Transducer** | Converts biological event into a signal | Platinum electrode |
| **Signal Processor** | Amplifies and conditions the signal | Operational amplifier circuit |
| **Display Unit** | Shows the quantitative result | LCD display |

**Example of complete biosensor:** Glucose meter — uses glucose oxidase (recognition) + amperometric electrode (transducer) → current → displayed as mg/dL of glucose.

---

### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**What are monoclonal antibodies? How are they produced?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Monoclonal antibodies (mAbs) are **identical antibody molecules** produced by a **single clone** of hybridoma cells, all binding to the **same specific epitope** on an antigen.

**Production (2 marks):**
1. A mouse is immunized with the target antigen.
2. Antibody-producing B-lymphocytes are isolated from the spleen.
3. B-cells are fused with immortal myeloma cells using **polyethylene glycol (PEG)** to form **hybridomas**.
4. Hybridomas are selected using **HAT medium** and screened for the desired antibody specificity using ELISA.
5. Positive hybridomas are cloned by limiting dilution to ensure monoclonality.
6. mAbs are produced at scale either in vivo (mouse ascites) or in vitro (bioreactor).

---

## 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks) [Choose this OR Question B]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **construction, working principle, and applications** of an **amperometric glucose biosensor**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **principle, procedure, and applications** of **Sandwich ELISA** in clinical diagnostics. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution A(a): Amperometric Glucose Biosensor (7 marks)

**1. Construction (3 marks):**

The amperometric glucose biosensor consists of three layers:
- **Outer membrane (polycarbonate):** Controls diffusion of glucose into the sensor
- **Enzyme layer (immobilized glucose oxidase + glutaraldehyde):** Recognition element
- **Inner electrode assembly:**
  - **Platinum (Pt) working electrode:** Where H₂O₂ is oxidized
  - **Ag/AgCl reference electrode:** Maintains constant potential
  - **Pt counter electrode:** Completes the circuit

**2. Working Principle (3 marks):**

**Step 1:** Glucose from the blood sample diffuses through the outer membrane into the enzyme layer.

**Step 2:** Glucose oxidase (GOD) catalyzes:

$$\text{Glucose} + O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{GOD}} \text{Gluconic Acid} + H_2O_2$$

**Step 3:** H₂O₂ diffuses to the platinum working electrode held at +0.7 V vs. Ag/AgCl:

$$H_2O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{+0.7 V}} O_2 + 2H^+ + 2e^-$$

**Step 4:** The resulting current is proportional to glucose concentration:

$$I = n \cdot F \cdot A \cdot k \cdot [\text{Glucose}]$$

Where $n$ = 2 electrons, $F$ = Faraday constant (**96,485 C/mol**), $A$ = electrode area, $k$ = rate constant.

**3. Applications (1 mark):**
- Self-monitoring of blood glucose in diabetic patients
- Continuous glucose monitoring systems (CGMS) — subcutaneous sensors
- Glucose monitoring in bioreactors and fermentation broth

---

### Solution A(b): Sandwich ELISA (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
Sandwich ELISA is an immunoassay that uses **two antibodies** — a *capture antibody* and a *detection antibody* — both binding to **different epitopes** on the target antigen. The antigen gets "sandwiched" between the two antibodies, hence the name. The detection antibody is conjugated to an enzyme (HRP or alkaline phosphatase) that catalyzes a chromogenic reaction.

**2. Step-by-Step Procedure (4 marks):**

| Step | Action | Duration |
|------|--------|----------|
| 1 | Coat 96-well plate with **capture antibody**, incubate at 4°C | Overnight |
| 2 | Wash with PBS-Tween; block with **BSA** to prevent non-specific binding | 1–2 hours |
| 3 | Add **sample/antigen**; antigen binds to capture antibody | 1–2 hours |
| 4 | Wash thoroughly to remove unbound material | 3 washes |
| 5 | Add **HRP-conjugated detection antibody**; binds to second epitope | 1–2 hours |
| 6 | Wash to remove unbound detection antibody | 3 washes |
| 7 | Add **TMB substrate** (3,3',5,5'-tetramethylbenzidine); HRP converts it to **blue color** | 15–30 min |
| 8 | Add **stop solution (H₂SO₄)**; color changes to yellow | — |
| 9 | Measure **absorbance at 450 nm** using ELISA reader | — |

**3. Applications (1 mark):**
- Diagnosis of HIV, Hepatitis B, Dengue
- Detection of cancer biomarkers (PSA, CA-125)
- Pregnancy tests (hCG detection)
- Quantification of cytokines and hormones

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice]**
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the **principle of hybridoma technology** for monoclonal antibody production. Describe the role of **HAT selection medium** with relevant biochemical pathways. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Discuss the major **therapeutic enzymes** used in medicine, their **mechanisms of action**, and the **bioprocess considerations** for their large-scale production. **(7 marks)**

---

### Solution B(a): Hybridoma Technology (7 marks)

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
Hybridoma technology is the method developed by **Köhler and Milstein (1975)** to produce monospecific (monoclonal) antibodies by fusing antibody-producing B-lymphocytes with immortal myeloma cells. The resulting hybrid cell — the **hybridoma** — has two key properties: (1) it produces a single specific antibody (from the B-cell), and (2) it can divide indefinitely (from the myeloma).

**2. Procedure (2 marks):**

1. Immunize a BALB/c mouse with the target antigen
2. Harvest spleen; isolate B-lymphocytes producing antigen-specific antibodies
3. Culture myeloma cells (HGPRT-deficient)
4. Fuse B-cells and myeloma cells using **40% PEG** for 1–2 minutes
5. Centrifuge and resuspend in HAT medium
6. Distribute into 96-well plates; incubate at 37°C, 5% CO₂
7. Screen supernatants by ELISA for desired antibody
8. Clone positive hybridomas by limiting dilution (0.3 cells/well)
9. Scale up — in vivo (ascites) or in vitro (bioreactor)

**3. HAT Selection — Biochemical Mechanism (3 marks):**

HAT medium contains **H**ypoxanthine, **A**minopterin, and **T** hymidine.

**Three possible cell populations after fusion:**

| Cell Type | Survival in HAT? | Reason |
|-----------|------------------|--------|
| **Unfused myeloma cells** | **Die** | Aminopterin blocks *de novo* nucleotide synthesis; HGPRT-deficient, so cannot use salvage pathway |
| **Unfused B-cells** | **Die** | Cannot divide indefinitely (limited lifespan) |
| **Hybridomas (fused)** | **Survive** | Receive functional HGPRT gene from B-cell, can use hypoxanthine via salvage pathway; also get immortality from myeloma |

**Two biochemical pathways:**

- **De novo pathway:** Synthesizes nucleotides from scratch — blocked by aminopterin (inhibits dihydrofolate reductase)
- **Salvage pathway:** Reuses hypoxanthine and thymidine — works *only* in cells with functional **HGPRT (hypoxanthine-guanine phosphoribosyltransferase)**

Myeloma cells are **HGPRT-deficient** (selected using 8-azaguanine), so they cannot use the salvage pathway. In HAT medium:
- De novo is blocked (aminopterin)
- Salvage fails (no HGPRT in unfused myeloma)
- Only **hybridomas** have HGPRT (from B-cell) → survive ✓

---

### Solution B(b): Therapeutic Enzymes (7 marks)

**1. Definition (1 mark):**
Therapeutic enzymes are enzymes used as **drugs** to treat specific diseases by catalyzing biochemical reactions in the human body that are deficient or absent due to genetic disorders, infections, or cancers.

**2. Major Therapeutic Enzymes (4 marks):**

| Enzyme | Disease Treated | Mechanism of Action | Source |
|--------|-----------------|---------------------|--------|
| **Tissue Plasminogen Activator (tPA / Alteplase)** | Acute myocardial infarction, ischemic stroke | Converts plasminogen → plasmin → dissolves fibrin clots | Recombinant CHO cells |
| **DNase I / Dornase alfa (Pulmozyme®)** | Cystic fibrosis | Cleaves extracellular DNA in thick mucus → thins secretions | Recombinant CHO cells |
| **Glucocerebrosidase / Imiglucerase (Cerezyme®)** | Gaucher's disease | Replaces deficient enzyme; degrades glucocerebroside in macrophages | Recombinant CHO cells |
| **L-Asparaginase (Elspar®)** | Acute lymphoblastic leukemia (ALL) | Depletes asparagine → kills leukemic cells (which cannot synthesize it) | *E. coli* / *Erwinia chrysanthemi* |
| **Urate Oxidase / Rasburicase (Elitek®)** | Tumor lysis syndrome | Converts uric acid → allantoin (more soluble) | Recombinant yeast |
| **Alpha-Galactosidase A (Fabrazyme®)** | Fabry disease | Replaces deficient lysosomal enzyme | Recombinant CHO cells |
| **Collagenase (Santyl®)** | Chronic dermal ulcers | Digests collagen in necrotic tissue | Bacterial fermentation |
| **Pancrelipase (Creon®)** | Pancreatic insufficiency | Supplies lipase/protease/amylase for digestion | Porcine pancreas |

**3. Bioprocess Considerations (2 marks):**

1. **Expression system choice:** 
   - *E. coli* — cheaper but cannot perform glycosylation (used for asparaginase)
   - Yeast (*Pichia pastoris*) — moderate glycosylation (used for rasburicase)
   - Mammalian cells (CHO, HEK293) — proper folding and glycosylation for therapeutic glycoproteins

2. **Post-translational modifications (PTMs):** Most therapeutic enzymes are glycoproteins; correct glycosylation is critical for in vivo half-life and efficacy.

3. **Purification:** Multi-step chromatography (Protein A, ion exchange, size exclusion) to achieve >99% purity.

4. **Quality control:** SDS-PAGE, HPLC, mass spectrometry, ELISA, endotoxin testing (LAL assay), bioactivity assays.

5. **Formulation & Stability:** Lyophilization, stabilizing excipients (sucrose, trehalose), cold-chain storage at 2–8°C.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Pitfall Callout:**
>
> **Common Mistakes Students Make (Costing Valuable Marks):**
>
> 1. **Confusing recognition element and transducer:** Students often describe the enzyme as the transducer. **Correct:** Enzyme is the *recognition element*; transducer is the *electrode/optical system* that converts the signal.
>
> 2. **In ELISA diagrams, drawing the wrong antibody order:** The **capture antibody** is immobilized *first* (on the plate), not the detection antibody. Reversing the order will lose 2–3 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting PEG in hybridoma fusion:** The cell fusion reagent is **polyethylene glycol (PEG)**, not electric current (that's electroporation, a different technique).
>
> 4. **Mixing up HAT components:** HAT = **H**ypoxanthine + **A**minopterin + **T**hymidine. Confusing these will lose 2 marks.
>
> 5. **Missing the role of aminopterin in HAT:** Aminopterin *blocks de novo synthesis*, but does not kill cells directly. Cells die because they cannot use the salvage pathway due to lack of HGPRT.
>
> 6. **In biosensor numericals, forgetting units:** Always include units in the final answer (e.g., **µA/mM** for sensitivity, **mM** or **µM** for LOD). Missing units = -1 mark per KTU convention.
>
> 7. **Wrong color in ELISA stop reaction:** TMB + HRP = **blue color**; adding H₂SO₄ (stop solution) = **yellow color**. Many students write the wrong sequence.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final High-Density Revision Checklist:**

### Analytical Applications — Must-Know Concepts
- [ ] Biosensor = Recognition element + Transducer + Signal processor + Display
- [ ] Glucose oxidase (GOD) catalyzes: Glucose + O₂ → Gluconic acid + H₂O₂
- [ ] H₂O₂ oxidation at Pt electrode (+0.7 V) generates current ∝ glucose concentration
- [ ] Four ELISA formats: **Direct, Indirect, Sandwich, Competitive** (Sandwich is most common for antigen)
- [ ] Sandwich ELISA uses **two antibodies** binding to **two different epitopes**
- [ ] HRP enzyme + TMB substrate = blue color; after H₂SO₄ = yellow (read at 450 nm)
- [ ] **Lineweaver-Burk:** y-intercept = 1/V_max, x-intercept = -1/K_m, slope = K_m/V_max
- [ ] **LOD formula:** LOD = 3σ/S, where σ = blank SD, S = calibration slope
- [ ] Sensitivity = ΔSignal/Δ[Analyte] (slope of calibration curve)
- [ ] BOD biosensors use *Pseudomonas*; pesticide biosensors use acetylcholinesterase

### Medical Therapy — Must-Know Concepts
- [ ] Monoclonal antibody = antibody from a *single clone* of hybridoma cells
- [ ] Hybridoma = B-lymphocyte (specificity) + Myeloma (immortality)
- [ ] Fusion agent = **Polyethylene glycol (PEG)**
- [ ] Selection agent = **HAT medium** (Hypoxanthine + Aminopterin + Thymidine)
- [ ] HGPRT-deficient myeloma cells die in HAT; hybridomas survive via salvage pathway
- [ ] Limiting dilution (0.3 cells/well) ensures monoclonality
- [ ] Murine mAbs cause **HAMA response** (Human Anti-Mouse Antibody) → use chimeric/humanized mAbs
- [ ] **Therapeutic enzymes:** tPA (clot dissolution), DNase I (CF), Asparaginase (ALL), Glucocerebrosidase (Gaucher's)
- [ ] Biopharmaceuticals require mammalian (CHO) cells for proper glycosylation
- [ ] Recombinant insulin (Humulin, 1982) was the first FDA-approved biopharmaceutical

### High-Yield Mnemonics
- [ ] **"EATS"** for ELISA order — **E**nzyme, **A**ntibody-bound, **T**MB substrate, **S**top solution
- [ ] **"BFMH"** for Hybridoma — **B**-cell, **F**usion with PEG, **M**yeloma, **H**AT selection
- [ ] **"HAT kills M not H"** — HAT medium kills Myeloma but keeps Hybridoma alive

### Common Question Patterns
- [ ] "Explain with diagram the working of [enzyme] biosensor" — 7 marks
- [ ] "Discuss the principle and steps of [ELISA format]" — 7 marks
- [ ] "Describe monoclonal antibody production by hybridoma technology" — 14 marks
- [ ] "List any 5 therapeutic enzymes with applications" — 5–7 marks
- [ ] "Explain HAT selection with biochemical basis" — 7 marks
